SYLVIA 's lover . 


 BY 

 ELIZABETH GASKELL 



 oh for thy voice to soothe and bless ! 
 what hope of answer , or redress ? 
 behind the veil ! behind the veil ! Tennyson 



 CHAPTER I 

 MONKSHAVEN 

 on the north - eastern shore of England there be a town call Monkshaven , contain at the present day about fifteen thousand inhabitant . there be , however , but half the number at the end of the last century , and it be at that period that the event narrate in the follow page occur . 

 Monkshaven be a name not unknown in the history of England , and tradition of its have be the landing - place of a throneless queen be current in the town . at that time there have be a fortified castle on the height above it , the site of which be now occupy by a deserted manor - house ; and at an even early date than the arrival of the queen and coeval with the most ancient remain of the castle , a great monastery have stand on those cliff , overlook the vast ocean that blend with the distant sky . monkshaven itself be build by the side of the Dee , just where the river fall into the German Ocean . the principal street of the town run parallel to the stream , and small lane branch out of this , and straggle up the side of the steep hill , between which and the river the house be pen in . there be a bridge across the Dee , and consequently a Bridge Street run at right angle to the High Street ; and on the south side of the stream there be a few house of more pretension , around which lie garden and field . it be on this side of the town that the local aristocracy live . and who be the great people of this small town ? not the young branch of the county family that hold hereditary state in their manor - house on the wild bleak moor , that shut in Monkshaven almost as effectually on the land side as ever the water do on the sea - board . no ; these old family keep aloof from the unsavoury yet adventurous trade which bring wealth to generation after generation of certain family in Monkshaven . 

 the magnate of Monkshaven be those who have the large number of ship engage in the whaling - trade . something like the following be the course of life with a monkshaven lad of this class : he be apprentice as a sailor to one of the great ship - owner to his own father , possibly along with twenty other boy , or , it might be , even more . during the summer month he and his fellow apprentice make voyage to the Greenland sea , return with their cargo in the early autumn ; and employ the winter month in watch the preparation of the oil from the blubber in the melting - shed , and learn navigation from some quaint but experienced teacher , half schoolmaster , half sailor , who season his instruction by stir narration of the wild adventure of his youth . the house of the ship - owner to whom he be apprentice be his home and that of his companion during the idle season between October and March . the domestic position of these boy vary accord to the premium pay ; some take rank with the son of the family , other be consider as little well than servant . yet once on board an equality prevail , in which , if any claim superiority , it be the brave and bright . after a certain number of voyage the monkshaven lad would rise by degree to be captain , and as such would have a share in the venture ; all these profit , as well as all his saving , would go towards build a whale vessel of his own , if he be not so fortunate as to be the child of a ship - owner . at the time of which I write , there be but little division of labour in the monkshaven whale fishery . the same man might be the owner of six or seven ship , any one of which he himself be fit by education and experience to command ; the master of a score of apprentice , each of whom pay a pretty sufficient premium ; and the proprietor of the melting - shed into which his cargo of blubber and whalebone be convey to be fit for sale . it be no wonder that large fortune be acquire by these ship - owner , nor that their house on the south side of the river Dee be stately mansion , full of handsome and substantial furniture . it be also not surprising that the whole town have an amphibious appearance , to a degree unusual even in a seaport . every one depend on the whale fishery , and almost every male inhabitant have be , or hop to be , a sailor . down by the river the smell be almost intolerable to any but monkshaven people during certain season of the year ; but on these unsavoury ' staithe ' the old man and child lounge for hour , almost as if they revel in the odour of train - oil . 

 this be , perhaps , enough of a description of the town itself . I have say that the country for mile all around be moorland ; high above the level of the sea tower the purple crag , whose summit be crown with greensward that steal down the side of the scaur a little way in grassy vein . here and there a brook force its way from the height down to the sea , make its channel into a valley more or less broad in long process of time . and in the moorland hollow , as in these valley , tree and underwood grow and flourish ; so that , while on the bare swell of the high land you shiver at the waste desolation of the scenery , when you drop into these woode ' bottom ' you be charm with the nestling shelter which they give . but above and around these rare and fertile vale there be moor for many a mile , here and there bleak enough , with the red freestone crop out above the scanty herbage ; then , perhaps , there be a brown tract of peat and bog , uncertain footing for the pedestrian who try to make a short cut to his destination ; then on the high sandy soil there be the purple ling , or commonest specie of heather grow in beautiful wild luxuriance . tuft of fine elastic grass be occasionally to be find , on which the little black - face sheep browse ; but either the scanty food , or their goat - like agility , keep they in a lean condition that do not promise much for the butcher , nor yet be their wool of a quality fine enough to make they profitable in that way to their owner . in such district there be little population at the present day ; there be much less in the last century , before agriculture be sufficiently scientific to have a chance of contend with such natural disqualification as the moor present , and when there be no facility of railroad to bring sportsman from a distance to enjoy the shooting season , and make an annual demand for accommodation . 

 there be old stone hall in the valley ; there be bare farmhouse to be see on the moor at long distance apart , with small stack of coarse poor hay , and almost large stack of turf for winter fuel in their farmyard . the cattle in the pasture field belong to these farm look half starve ; but somehow there be an odd , intelligent expression in their face , as well as in those of the black - visage sheep , which be seldom see in the placidly stupid countenance of well - feed animal . all the fence be turf bank , with loose stone pile into wall on the top of these . 

 there be comparative fertility and luxuriance down below in the rare green dale . the narrow meadow stretch along the brookside seem as though the cow could really satisfy their hunger in the deep rich grass ; whereas on the high land the scanty herbage be hardly worth the fatigue of move about in search of it . even in these ' bottom ' the pip sea - wind , follow the current of the stream , stunt and cut low any tree ; but still there be rich thick underwood , tangle and tie together with bramble , and brier - rise ,   sic   and honeysuckle ; and if the farmer in these comparatively happy valley have have wife or daughter who care for gardening , many a flower would have grow on the western or southern side of the rough stone house . but at that time gardening be not a popular art in any part of England ; in the north it be not yet . nobleman and gentleman may have beautiful garden ; but farmer and day - labourer care little for they north of the Trent , which be all I can answer for . a few ' berry ' bush , a black currant tree or two   the leaf to be use in heighten the flavour of tea , the fruit as medicinal for cold and sore throat , a potato ground   and this be not so common at the close of the last century as it be now , a cabbage bed , a bush of sage , and balm , and thyme , and marjoram , with possibly a rose tree , and ' old man ' grow in the midst ; a little plot of small strong coarse onion , and perhaps some marigold , the petal of which flavour the salt - beef broth ; such plant make up a well - furnish garden to a farmhouse at the time and place to which my story belong . but for twenty mile inland there be no forget the sea , nor the sea - trade ; refuse shell - fish , seaweed , the offal of the melting - house , be the staple manure of the district ; great ghastly whale - jaw , bleach bare and white , be the arch over the gate - post to many a field or moorland stretch . out of every family of several son , however agricultural their position might be , one have go to sea , and the mother look wistfully seaward at the change of the keen piping moorland wind . the holiday ramble be to the coast ; no one care to go inland to see aught , unless indeed it might be to the great annual horse - fair hold where the dreary land break into habitation and cultivation . 

 somehow in this country sea thought follow the thinker far inland ; whereas in most other part of the island , at five mile from the ocean , he have all but forget the existence of such an element as salt water . the great Greenland trade of the coast town be the main and primary cause of this , no doubt . but there be also a dread and an irritation in every one 's mind , at the time of which I write , in connection with the neighbour sea . 

 since the termination of the american war , there have be nothing to call for any unusual energy in man the navy ; and the grant require by Government for this purpose diminish with every year of peace . in 1792 this grant touch its minimum for many year . in 1793 the proceeding of the French have set Europe on fire , and the English be rag with anti - gallican excitement , foment into action by every expedient of the Crown and its Ministers . we have our ship ; but where be our man ? the Admiralty have , however , a ready remedy at hand , with ample precedent for its use , and with common   if not statute   law to sanction its application . they issue ' press warrant , ' call upon the civil power throughout the country to support their officer in the discharge of their duty . the sea - coast be divide into district , under the charge of a captain in the navy , who again delegate sub - district to lieutenant ; and in this manner all homeward - bind vessel be watch and wait for , all port be under supervision ; and in a day , if need be , a large number of man could be add to the force of his Majesty 's navy . but if the Admiralty become urgent in their demand , they be also willing to be unscrupulous . Landsmen , if able - body , might soon be train into good sailor ; and once in the hold of the tender , which always await the success of the operation of the press - gang , it be difficult for such prisoner to bring evidence of the nature of their former occupation , especially when none have leisure to listen to such evidence , or be willing to believe it if they do listen , or would act upon it for the release of the captive if they have by possibility both listen and believe . man be kidnap , literally disappear , and nothing be ever hear of they again . the street of a busy town be not safe from such press - gang capture , as Lord Thurlow could have tell , after a certain walk he take about this time on Tower Hill , when he , the attorney - general of England , be impressed , when the Admiralty have its own peculiar way of get rid of tiresome besieger and petitioner . nor yet be lonely inland dweller more secure ; many a rustic go to a statute fair or ' mop , ' and never come home to tell of his hiring ; many a stout young farmer vanish from his place by the hearth of his father , and be no more hear of by mother or lover ; so great be the press for man to serve in the navy during the early year of the war with France , and after every great naval victory of that war . 

 the servant of the Admiralty lie in wait for all merchantman and trader ; there be many instance of vessel return home after long absence , and laden with rich cargo , be board within a day 's distance of land , and so many man press and carry off , that the ship , with her cargo , become unmanageable from the loss of her crew , drift out again into the wild wide ocean , and be sometimes find in the helpless guidance of one or two infirm or ignorant sailor ; sometimes such vessel be never hear of more . the man thus press be take from the near grasp of parent or wife , and be often deprive of the hard earning of year , which remain in the hand of the master of the merchantman in which they have serve , subject to all the chance of honesty or dishonesty , life or death . now all this tyranny   for I can use no other word   be marvellous to we ; we can not imagine how it be that a nation submit to it for so long , even under any warlike enthusiasm , any panic of invasion , any amount of loyal subservience to the govern power . when we read of the military be call in to assist the civil power in back up the press - gang , of party of soldier patrol the street , and sentry with screw bayonet place at every door while the press - gang enter and search each hole and corner of the dwelling ; when we hear of church be surround during divine service by troop , while the press - gang stand ready at the door to seize man as they come out from attend public worship , and take these instance as merely type of what be constantly go on in different form , we do not wonder at Lord Mayors , and other civic authority in large town , complain that a stop be put to business by the danger which the tradesman and their servant incur in leave their house and go into the street , infest by press - gang . 

 whether it be that live in close neighbourhood to the metropolis the centre of politic and news inspire the inhabitant of the southern county with a strong feeling of that kind of patriotism which consist in hat all other nation ; or whether it be that the chance of capture be so much great at all the southern port that the merchant sailor become inure to the danger ; or whether it be that serve in the navy , to those familiar with such town as Portsmouth and Plymouth , have an attraction to most man from the dash and brilliancy of the adventurous employment it be certain that the southerner take the oppression of press - warrant more submissively than the wild north - eastern people . for with they the chance of profit beyond their wage in the whaling or Greenland trade extend to the low description of sailor . he might rise by dare and save to be a ship - owner himself . number around he have do so ; and this very fact make the distinction between class and class less apparent ; and the common venture and danger , the universal interest feel in one pursuit , bind the inhabitant of that line of coast together with a strong tie , the severance of which by any violent extraneous measure , give rise to passionate anger and thirst for vengeance . a Yorkshireman once say to I , ' my county folk be all alike . their first thought be how to resist . why ! I myself , if I hear a man say it be a fine day , catch myself try to find out that it be no such thing . it be so in thought ; it be so in word ; it be so in deed . ' 

 so you may imagine the press - gang have no easy time of it on the Yorkshire coast . in other place they inspire fear , but here rage and hatred . the Lord Mayor of York be warn on 20th January , 1777 , by an anonymous letter , that ' if those man be not send from the city on or before the follow Tuesday , his lordship 's own dwelling , and the Mansion - house also , should be burn to the ground . ' 

 perhaps something of the ill - feeling that prevail on the subject be owe to the fact which I have notice in other place similarly situate . where the land possession of gentleman of ancient family but limited income surround a centre of any kind of profitable trade or manufacture , there be a sort of latent ill - will on the part of the squire to the tradesman , be he manufacturer , merchant , or ship - owner , in whose hand be hold a power of money - making , which no hereditary pride , or gentlemanly love of do nothing , prevent he from use . this ill - will , to be sure , be mostly of a negative kind ; its most common form of manifestation be in absence of speech or action , a sort of torpid and genteel ignore all unpleasant neighbour ; but really the whale - fishery of Monkshaven have become so impertinently and obtrusively prosperous of late year at the time of which I write , the Monkshaven ship - owner be grow so wealthy and consequential , that the squire , who live at home at ease in the old stone manor - house scatter up and down the surround moorland , feel that the check upon the monkshaven trade likely to be inflict by the press - gang , be wisely ordain by the high power   how high they place these power I will not venture to say , to prevent overhaste in get rich , which be a scriptural fault , and they also think that they be only do their duty in back up the Admiralty warrant by all the civil power at their disposal , whenever they be call upon , and whenever they could do so without take too much trouble in affair which do not after all much concern themselves . 

 there be just another motive in the mind of some provident parent of many daughter . the captain and lieutenant employ on this service be mostly agreeable bachelor , bring up to a genteel profession , at the least they be very pleasant visitor , when they have a day to spare ; who know what might come of it ? 

 indeed , these brave officer be not unpopular in Monkshaven itself , except at the time when they be bring into actual collision with the people . they have the frank manner of their profession ; they be know to have serve in those engagement , the very narrative of which at this day will warm the heart of a Quaker , and they themselves do not come prominently forward in the dirty work which , nevertheless , be permit and quietly sanction by they . so while few monkshaven people pass the low public - house over which the navy blue - flag stream , as a sign that it be the rendezvous of the press - gang , without spit towards it in sign of abhorrence , yet , perhaps , the very same person would give some rough token of respect to Lieutenant Atkinson if they meet he in High Street . touch their hat be an unknown gesture in those part , but they would move their head in a droll , familiar kind of way , neither a wag nor a nod , but mean all the same to imply friendly regard . the ship - owner , too , invite he to an occasional dinner or supper , all the time look forward to the chance of his turn out an active enemy , and not by any mean incline to give he ' the run of the house , ' however many unmarried daughter might grace their table . still as he could tell a rattle story , drink hard , and be seldom too busy to come at a short notice , he get on well than any one could have expect with the monkshaven folk . and the principal share of the odium of his business fall on his subordinate , who be one and all regard in the light of mean kidnapper and spy ' varmint , ' as the common people esteem they : and as such they be ready at the first provocation to hunt and to worry they , and little care the press - gang for this . whatever else they be , they be brave and daring . they have law to back they , therefore their business be lawful . they be serve their king and country . they be use all their faculty , and that be always pleasant . there be plenty of scope for the glory and triumph of outwit ; plenty of adventure in their life . it be a lawful and loyal employment , require sense , readiness , courage , and besides it call out that strange love of the chase inherent in every man . fourteen or fifteen mile at sea lie the Aurora , good man - of - war ; and to she be convey the living cargo of several tender , which be station at likely place along the sea - coast . one , the Lively Lady , might be see from the cliff above Monkshaven , not so far away , but hide by the angle of the high land from the constant sight of the townspeople ; and there be always the Randyvow - house   as the public - house with the navy blue - flag be call thereabout   for the crew of the Lively Lady to lounge about , and there to offer drink to unwary passer - by . at present this be all that the press - gang have do at Monkshaven . 




 CHAPTER II 

 home from GREENLAND 

 one hot day , early in October of the year 1796 , two girl set off from their country home to Monkshaven to sell their butter and egg , for they be both farmer ' daughter , though rather in different circumstance ; for Molly Corney be one of a large family of child , and have to rough it accordingly ; Sylvia Robson be an only child , and be much make of in more people 's estimation than Mary 's by her elderly parent . they have each purchase to make after their sale be effect , as sale of butter and egg be effect in those day by the market - woman sit on the step of the great old mutilate cross till a certain hour in the afternoon , after which , if all their good be not disposed of , they take they unwillingly to the shop and sell they at a low price . but good housewife do not despise come themselves to the Butter Cross , and , smell and depreciate the article they want , keep up a perpetual struggle of word , try , often in vain , to beat down price . a housekeeper of the last century would have think that she do not know her business , if she have not go through this preliminary process ; and the farmer ' wife and daughter treat it all as a matter of course , reply with a good deal of independent humour to the customer , who , once have discover where good butter and fresh egg be to be sell , come time after time to depreciate the article she always end in take . there be leisure for all this kind of work in those day . 

 molly have tie a knot on her pink - spot handkerchief for each of the various purchase she have to make ; dull but important article need for the week 's consumption at home ; if she forget any one of they she know she be sure of a good ' rating ' from her mother . the number of they make her pocket - handkerchief look like one of the nine - tail of a ' cat ; ' but not a single thing be for herself , nor , indeed , for any one individual of her numerous family . there be neither much thought nor much money to spend for any but collective want in the Corney family . 

 it be different with Sylvia . she be go to choose her first cloak , not to have an old one of her mother 's , that have go down through two sister , dye for the fourth time   and Molly would have be glad have even this chance be her , but to buy a bran - new duffle cloak all for herself , with not even an eld authority to curb she as to price , only molly to give her admire counsel , and as much sympathy as be consistent with a little patient envy of Sylvia 's happy circumstance . every now and then they wander off from the one grand subject of thought , but Sylvia , with unconscious art , soon bring the conversation round to the fresh consideration of the respective merit of gray and scarlet . these girl be walk bare - foot and carry their shoe and stocking in their hand during the first part of their way ; but as they be draw near Monkshaven they stop , and turn aside along a foot - path that lead from the main - road down to the bank of the Dee . there be great stone in the river about here , round which the water gather and eddy and form deep pool . molly sate down on the grassy bank to wash her foot ; but Sylvia , more active   or perhaps lighter - hearted with the notion of the cloak in the distance , place her basket on a gravelly bit of shore , and , give a long spring , seat herself on a stone almost in the middle of the stream . then she begin dip her little rosy toe in the cool rushing water and whisk they out with childish glee . 

 ' be quiet , wi ' the ' , Sylvia ? Thou'st splash I all ower , and my feyther'll noane be so keen o ' give I a new cloak as thine be , seemingly . ' 

 Sylvia be quiet , not to say penitent , in a moment . she draw up her foot instantly ; and , as if to take herself out of temptation , she turn away from Molly to that side of her stony seat on which the current run shallow , and break by pebble . but once disturb in her play , her thought revert to the great subject of the cloak . she be now as still as a minute before she have be full of frolic and gambol life . she have tuck herself up on the stone , as if it have be a cushion , and she a little sultana . 

 Molly be deliberately wash her foot and draw on her stocking , when she hear a sudden sigh , and her companion turn round so as to face she , and say , 

 ' I wish mother have not   speak up for t ' gray . ' 

 ' why , Sylvia , thou wert say as we top t'brow , as she do nought but bid thee think twice afore settle on scarlet . ' 

 ' ay ! but mother 's word be scarce , and weigh heavy . Feyther 's liker I , and we talk a deal o ' rubble ; but mother 's word be liker to hewn stone . she put a deal o ' meaning in them . and then , ' say Sylvia , as if she be put out by the suggestion , ' she bid I ask cousin Philip for his opinion . I hate a man as have getten an opinion on such - like thing . ' 

 ' well ! we shall niver get to Monkshaven this day , either for to sell our egg and stuff , or to buy thy cloak , if we be sittin ' here much long . T ' sun 's for slant low , so come along , lass , and let us be go . ' 

 ' but if I put on my stocking and shoon here , and jump back into yon wet gravel , I ' se not be fit to be see , ' say Sylvia , in a pathetic tone of bewilderment , that be funnily childlike . she stand up , her bare foot curve round the curve surface of the stone , her slight figure balance as if in act to spring . 

 ' Thou know thou'll have just to jump back barefoot , and wash thy foot afresh , without make all that ado ; thou shouldst ha ' do it at first , like I , and all other sensible folk . but thou'st getten no gumption . ' 

 Molly 's mouth be stop by Sylvia 's hand . she be already on the river bank by her friend 's side . 

 ' now dunnot lecture I ; I be none for a sermon hang on every peg o ' word . I be go to have a new cloak , lass , and I can not heed thee if thou dost lecture . Thou shall have all the gumption , and I will have my cloak . ' 

 it may be doubt whether molly think this an equal division . 

 each girl wear tightly - fit stocking , knit by her own hand , of the blue worst common in that country ; they have on neat high - heeled black leather shoe , come well over the instep , and fasten as well as ornament with bright steel buckle . they do not walk so lightly and freely now as they do before they be shod , but their step be still springy with the buoyancy of early youth ; for neither of they be twenty , indeed I believe Sylvia be not more than seventeen at this time . 

 they clamber up the steep grassy path , with bramble catch at their kilte petticoat , through the copse - wood , till they regain the high road ; and then they ' settle themselves , ' as they call it ; that be to say , they take off their black feel hat , and tie up their cluster hair afresh ; they shake off every speck of wayside dust ; straighten the little shawl   or large neck - kerchief , call they which you will   that be spread over their shoulder , pin below the throat , and confine at the waist by their apron - string ; and then put on their hat again , and pick up their basket , they prepare to walk decorously into the town of Monkshaven . 

 the next turn of the road show they the red peak roof of the closely pack house lie almost directly below the hill on which they be . the full autumn sun bring out the ruddy colour of the tile gable , and deepen the shadow in the narrow street . the narrow harbour at the mouth of the river be crowd with small vessel of all description , make an intricate forest of mast . beyond lie the sea , like a flat pavement of sapphire , scarcely a ripple vary its sunny surface , that stretch out league away till it blend with the softened azure of the sky . on this blue trackless water float score of white - sail fishing boat , apparently motionless , unless you measure their progress by some land - mark ; but still , and silent , and distant as they seem , the consciousness that there be man on board , each go forth into the great deep , add unspeakably to the interest feel in watch they . close to the bar of the river Dee a large vessel lie to . Sylvia , who have only recently come into the neighbourhood , look at this with the same quiet interest as she do at all the other ; but molly , as soon as her eye catch the build of it , cry out aloud 

 ' she be a whaler ! she be a whaler home from t ' Greenland sea ! t ' first this season ! God bless she ! ' and she turn round and shake both Sylvia 's hand in the fulness of her excitement . Sylvia 's colour rise , and her eye sparkle out of sympathy . 

 ' be ta sure ? ' she ask , breathless in her turn ; for though she do not know by the aspect of the different ship on what trade they be bind , yet she be well aware of the paramount interest attach to whale vessel . 

 ' three o'clock ! and it be not high water till five ! ' say Molly . ' if we be sharp we can sell our egg , and be down to the staithe before she come into port . be sharp , lass ! ' 

 and down the steep long hill they go at a pace that be almost a run . a run they dare not make it ; and as it be , the rate at which they walk would have cause destruction among egg less carefully packed . when the descent be end , there be yet the long narrow street before they , bend and swerve from the straight line , as it follow the course of the river . the girl feel as if they should never come to the market - place , which be situate at the crossing of Bridge Street and High Street . there the old stone cross be raise by the monk long ago ; now wear and mutilate , no one esteem it as a holy symbol , but only as the Butter Cross , where market - woman cluster on Wednesday , and whence the town crier make all his proclamation of household sale , thing lose or find , begin with ' oh ! yes , oh ! yes , oh ! yes ! ' and end with ' God bless the king and the lord of this manor , ' and a very brisk ' amen , ' before he go on his way and take off the livery - coat , the colour of which mark he as a servant of the Burnabys , the family who hold manorial right over Monkshaven . 

 of course the much frequent space surround the Butter Cross be the favourite centre for shop ; and on this day , a fine market day , just when good housewife begin to look over their winter store of blanket and flannel , and discover their need betime , these shop ought to have have plenty of customer . but they be empty and of even quieter aspect than their every - day will not . the three - legged creepie - stool that be hire out at a penny an hour to such market - woman as come too late to find room on the step be unoccupied ; knock over here and there , as if people have pass by in haste . 

 molly take in all at a glance , and interpret the sign , though she have no time to explain their meaning , and her consequent course of action , to Sylvia , but dart into a corner shop . 

 ' T ' whaler be come home ! there be one lie outside t ' bar ! ' 

 this be put in the form of an assertion ; but the tone be that of eager cross - questioning . 

 ' ay ! ' say a lame man , mend fishing - net behind a rough deal counter . ' she be come back airly , and she be bring good news o ' t ' other , as I have   heere say . time be I should ha ' be on th ' staithe throw up my cap wit ' t ' good on them ; but now it please t ' Lord to keep I at home , and set I to mind other folk ' gear . see thee , wench , there be a vast o ' folk ha ' leave their skep o ' thing wi ' I while they be away down to t ' quay side . leave I your egg and be off wi ' ye for t ' see t ' fun , for mebbe ye'll live to be palsy yet , and then ye'll be fret ower spilt milk , and that ye do not   tak ' all chance when ye be young . ay , well ! they be out o ' hearin ' o ' my morality ; I would well find a lamiter like mysen to preach to , for it be not iverybody have t ' luck t ' clargy have of say their say out whether folk like it or not . ' 

 he put the basket carefully away with much of such talk as this address to himself while he do so . then he sigh once or twice ; and then he take the well course and begin to sing over his tarry work . 

 Molly and Sylvia be far along the staithe by the time he get to this point of cheerfulness . they run on , regardless of stitch and pain in the side ; on along the river bank to where the concourse of people be gather . there be no great length of way between the Butter Cross and the harbour ; in five minute the breathless girl be close together in the good place they could get for seeing , on the outside of the crowd ; and in as short a time long they be press inward , by fresh arrival , into the very midst of the throng . all eye be direct to the ship , beat her anchor just outside the bar , not a quarter of a mile away . the custom - house officer be just go aboard of she to receive the captain 's report of his cargo , and make due examination . the man who have take he out in his boat be row back to the shore , and bring small fragment of news when they land a little distance from the crowd , which move as one man to hear what be to be tell . Sylvia take a hard grasp of the hand of the old and more experienced Molly , and listen open - mouthed to the answer she be extract from a gruff old sailor she happen to find near she . 

 ' what ship be she ? ' 

 ' T ' Resolution of Monkshaven ! ' say he , indignantly , as if any goose might have know that . 

 ' an ' a good resolution , and a bless ship she be be to I , ' pip out an old woman , close at Mary 's elbow . ' she be bring I home my ae ' lad for he shout to yon boatman to bid he tell I he be well . ' tell Peggy Christison , ' say he   my name be Margaret Christison   ' tell Peggy Christison as her son Hezekiah be come back safe and sound . ' the Lord 's name be praise ! an ' I a widow as never think to see my lad again ! ' 

 it seem as if everybody rely on every one else 's sympathy in that hour of great joy . 

 ' I ax pardon , but if you would gie I just a bit of elbow - room for a minute like , I would hold my babby up , so that he might see daddy 's ship , and happen , my master might see he . he be four month old last Tuesday se'nnight , and his feyther be never clapt eyne on he yet , and he wi ' a tooth through , an another just break , bless he ! ' 

 one or two of the well end of the monkshaven inhabitant stand a little before Molly and Sylvia ; and as they move in compliance with the young mother 's request , they overheard some of the information these ship - owner have receive from the boatman . 

 ' hayne say they will send the manifest of the cargo ashore in twenty minute , as soon as Fishburn have look over the cask . only eight whale , accord to what he say . ' 

 ' no one can tell , ' say the other , ' till the manifest come to hand . ' 

 ' I be afraid he be right . but he bring a good report of the Good Fortune . she be off St Abb 's Head , with something like fifteen whale to her share . ' 

 ' we shall see how much be true , when she come in . ' 

 ' that will be by the afternoon tide to - morrow . ' 

 ' that be my cousin 's ship , ' say Molly to Sylvia . ' he be specksioneer on board the Good Fortune . ' 

 an old man touch she as she speak 

 ' I humbly make my manner , missus , but I be stone blind ; my lad 's aboard yon vessel outside t ' bar ; and my old woman be bed - fast . will she be long , think ye , in make t ' harbour ? because , if so be as she be , I would just make my way back , and speak a word or two to my missus , who will be boil o'er into some mak o ' mischief now she know he be so near . may I be so bold as to ax if t ' Crooked Negro be cover yet ? ' 

 molly stand on tip - toe to try and see the black stone thus name ; but Sylvia , stoop and peep through the glimpse afford between the arm of the move people , see it first , and tell the blind old man it be still above water . 

 ' a watch pot , ' say he , ' ne'er boil , I reckon . it be ta'en a vast o ' watter t ' cover that stone to - day . anyhow , I will have time to go home and rate my missus for worritin ' hersen , as I will be bind she be do , for all as I bid she not , but to keep easy and content . ' 

 ' we would well be off too , ' say Molly , as an opening be make through the press to let out the grope old man . ' egg and butter be yet to sell , and tha ' cloak to be buy . ' 

 ' well , I suppose we have ! ' say Sylvia , rather regretfully ; for , though all the way into Monkshaven her head have be full of the purchase of this cloak , yet she be of that impressible nature that take the tone of feeling from those surround ; and though she know no one on board the Resolution , she be just as anxious for the moment to see she come into harbour as any one in the crowd who have a dear relation on board . so she turn reluctantly to follow the more prudent Molly along the quay back to the Butter Cross . 

 it be a pretty scene , though it be too familiar to the eye of all who then see it for they to notice its beauty . the sun be low enough in the west to turn the mist that fill the distant valley of the river into golden haze . above , on either bank of the Dee , there lie the moorland height swell one behind the other ; the nearer , russet brown with the tint of the fading bracken ; the more distant , gray and dim against the rich autumnal sky . the red and fluted tile of the gable house rise in crowded irregularity on one side of the river , while the new suburb be build in more orderly and less picturesque fashion on the opposite cliff . the river itself be swell and chafe with the incoming tide till its vex water rush over the very foot of the watch crowd on the staithe , as the great sea wave encroach more and more every minute . the quay - side be unsavourily ornament with glitter fish - scale , for the haul of fish be cleanse in the open air , and no sanitary arrangement exist for sweep away any of the relic of this operation . 

 the fresh salt breeze be bring up the lashing , leap tide from the blue sea beyond the bar . behind the return girl there rock the white - sail ship , as if she be all alive with eagerness for her anchor to be heave . 

 how impatient her crew of beat heart be for that moment , how those on land sicken at the suspense , may be imagine , when you remember that for six long summer month those sailor have be as if dead from all news of those they love ; shut up in terrible , dreary Arctic sea from the hungry sight of sweetheart and friend , wife and mother . no one know what might have happen . the crowd on shore grow silent and solemn before the dread of the possible news of death that might toll in upon their heart with this uprushing tide . the whaler go out into the Greenland sea full of strong , hopeful man ; but the whaler never return as they sail forth . on land there be death among two or three hundred man to be mourn over in every half - year 's space of time . whose bone have be leave to blacken on the gray and terrible iceberg ? who lie still until the sea should give up its dead ? who be those who should come back to Monkshaven never , no , never more ? 

 many a heart swell with passionate , unspoken fear , as the first whaler lie off the bar on her return voyage . 

 Molly and Sylvia have leave the crowd in this hushed suspense . but fifty yard along the staithe they pass five or six girl with flushed face and careless attire , who have mount a pile of timber , place there to season for ship - building , from which , as from the step of a ladder or staircase , they could command the harbour . they be wild and free in their gesture , and hold each other by the hand , and sway from side to side , stamp their foot in time , as they sing 

 Weel may the keel row , the keel row , the keel row , 
 Weel may the keel row that my laddie be in ! 
 ' what for be ye go off , now ? ' they call out to our two girl . ' she will be in in ten minute ! ' and without wait for the answer which never come , they resume their song . 

 old sailor stand about in little group , too proud to show their interest in the adventure they could no long share , but quite unable to keep up any semblance of talk on indifferent subject . 

 the town seem very quiet and desert as Molly and Sylvia enter the dark , irregular Bridge Street , and the market - place be as empty of people as before . but the skep and basket and three - legged stool be all clear away . 

 ' Market be over for to - day , ' say Molly Corney , in disappointed surprise . ' we mun make the good on't , and sell to t ' huxter , and a hard bargain they will be for drive . I doubt mother'll be vex . ' 

 she and Sylvia go to the corner shop to reclaim their basket . the man have his joke at they for their delay . 

 ' ay , ay ! lass as have sweetheart a - come home do not   care much what price they get for butter and egg ! I dare say , now , there be some un in yon ship that ' ud give as much as a shilling a pound for this butter if he only know who churn it ! ' this be to Sylvia , as he hand she back her property . 

 the fancy - free Sylvia redden , pout , toss back her head , and hardly deign a farewell word of thank or civility to the lame man ; she be at an age to be affront by any joke on such a subject . molly take the joke without disclaimer and without offence . she rather like the unfounded idea of she have a sweetheart , and be rather surprised to think how devoid of foundation the notion be . if she could have a new cloak as Sylvia be go to have , then , indeed , there might be a chance ! until some such good luck , it be as well to laugh and blush as if the surmise of she have a lover be not very far from the truth , and so she reply in something of the same strain as the lame net - maker to his joke about the butter . 

 ' he will need it all , and more too , to grease his tongue , if iver he reckon to win I for his wife ! ' 

 when they be out of the shop , Sylvia say , in a coax tone , 

 ' molly , who be it ? whose tongue will need grease ? just tell I , and I will never tell ! ' 

 she be so much in earnest that molly be perplexed . she do not quite like say that she have allude to no one in particular , only to a possible sweetheart , so she begin to think what young man have make the most civil speech to she in her life ; the list be not a long one to go over , for her father be not so well off as to make she seek after for her money , and her face be rather of the homely . but she suddenly remember her cousin , the specksioneer , who have give she two large shell , and take a kiss from her half - willing lip before he go to sea the last time . so she smile a little , and then say , 

 ' well ! I dunno . it be ill talk o ' these thing afore one have make up one 's mind . and perhaps if Charley Kinraid behave hissen , I might be bring to listen . ' 

 ' Charley Kinraid ! who be he ? ' 

 ' Yon specksioneer cousin o ' mine , as I be talk on . ' 

 ' and do yo ' think he care for yo ' ? ' ask Sylvia , in a low , tender tone , as if touch on a great mystery . 

 molly only say , ' be quiet wi ' yo ' , ' and Sylvia could not make out whether she cut the conversation so short because she be offend , or because they have come to the shop where they have to sell their butter and egg . 

 ' now , Sylvia , if thou'll leave I thy basket , I will make as good a bargain as iver I can on them ; and thou can be off to choose this grand new cloak as be to be , afore it get any darker . where be ta go to ? ' 

 ' mother say I would well go to Foster 's , ' answer Sylvia , with a shade of annoyance in her face . ' Feyther say just anywhere . ' 

 ' Foster 's be t ' good place ; thou canst try anywhere afterwards . I will be at Foster 's in five minute , for I reckon we mun hasten a bit now . it will be near five o'clock . ' 

 Sylvia hang her head and look very demure as she walk off by herself to Foster 's shop in the market - place . 




 CHAPTER III 

 buy a new cloak 

 Foster 's shop be the shop of Monkshaven . it be keep by two Quaker brother , who be now old man ; and their father have keep it before they ; probably his father before that . People remember it as an old - fashioned dwelling - house , with a sort of supplementary shop with unglazed window project from the low story . these opening have long be fill with pane of glass that at the present day would be account very small , but which seventy year ago be much admire for their size . I can well make you understand the appearance of the place by bid you think of the long opening in a butcher 's shop , and then to fill they up in your imagination with pane about eight inch by six , in a heavy wooden frame . there be one of these window on each side the door - place , which be keep partially close through the day by a low gate about a yard high . half the shop be appropriate to grocery ; the other half to drapery , and a little mercery . the good old brother give all their know customer a kindly welcome ; shake hand with many of they , and ask all after their family and domestic circumstance before proceed to business . they would not for the world have have any sign of festivity at Christmas , and scrupulously keep their shop open at that holy festival , ready themselves to serve soon than tax the conscience of any of their assistant , only nobody ever come . but on New Year 's day they have a great cake , and wine , ready in the parlour behind the shop , of which all who come in to buy anything be ask to partake . yet , though scrupulous in most thing , it do not go against the conscience of these good brother to purchase smuggled article . there be a back way from the river - side , up a covered entry , to the yard - door of the Fosters , and a peculiar kind of knock at this door always bring out either John or Jeremiah , or if not they , their shopman , Philip Hepburn ; and the same cake and wine that the excise officer 's wife might just have be taste , be bring out in the back parlour to treat the smuggler . there be a little locking of door , and drawing of the green silk curtain that be suppose to shut out the shop , but really all this be do very much for form 's sake . everybody in Monkshaven smuggle who could , and every one wear smuggle good who could , and great reliance be place on the excise officer 's neighbourly feeling . 

 the story go that John and Jeremiah Foster be so rich that they could buy up all the new town across the bridge . they have certainly begin to have a kind of primitive bank in connection with their shop , receive and take care of such money as people do not wish to retain in their house for fear of burglar . no one ask they for interest on the money thus deposit , nor do they give any ; but , on the other hand , if any of their customer , on whose character they could depend , want a little advance , the Fosters , after due inquiry make , and in some case due security give , be not unwilling to lend a moderate sum without charge a penny for the use of their money . all the article they sell be as good as they know how to choose , and for they they expect and obtain ready money . it be say that they only keep on the shop for their amusement . other aver that there be some plan of a marriage run in the brother ' head a marriage between William Coulson , Mr. Jeremiah 's wife 's nephew   Mr. Jeremiah be a widower , and Hester Rose , whose mother be some kind of distant relation , and who serve in the shop along with William Coulson and Philip Hepburn . again , this be deny by those who aver that Coulson be no blood relation , and that if the Fosters have intend to do anything considerable for hester , they would never have allow she and her mother to live in such a sparing way , ekee out their small income by have Coulson and Hepburn for lodger . no ; John and Jeremiah would leave all their money to some hospital or to some charitable institution . but , of course , there be a reply to this ; when be there not many side to an argument about a possibility concern which no fact be know ? part of the reply turn on this : the old gentleman have , probably , some deep plan in their head in permit their cousin to take Coulson and Hepburn as lodger , the one a kind of nephew , the other , though so young , the head man in the shop ; if either of they take a fancy to hester , how agreeably matter could be arrange ! 

 all this time hester be patiently wait to serve Sylvia , who be stand before she a little shy , a little perplexed and distract , by the sight of so many pretty thing . 

 hester be a tall young woman , sparely yet largely form , of a grave aspect , which make she look old than she really be . her thick brown hair be smoothly take off her broad forehead , and put in a very orderly fashion , under her linen cap ; her face be a little square , and her complexion sallow , though the texture of her skin be fine . her gray eye be very pleasant , because they look at you so honestly and kindly ; her mouth be slightly compress , as most have it who be in the habit of restrain their feeling ; but when she speak you do not perceive this , and her rare smile slowly break forth show her white even tooth , and when accompany , as it generally be , by a sudden uplifting of her soft eye , it make her countenance very win . she be dress in stuff of sober colour , both in accordance with her own taste , and in unasked compliance with the religious custom of the Fosters ; but hester herself be not a Friend . 

 Sylvia , stand opposite , not look at hester , but gaze at the ribbon in the shop window , as if hardly conscious that any one await the expression of her wish , be a great contrast ; ready to smile or to pout , or to show her feeling in any way , with a character as undeveloped as a child 's , affectionate , wilful , naughty , tiresome , charming , anything , in fact , at present that the chance of an hour call out . hester think her customer the prettiest creature ever see , in the moment she have for admiration before Sylvia turn round and , recall to herself , begin ,

 hester say nothing , but go to examine the shop - mark . 

 ' oh ! I do not mean that I want any , I only want some stuff for a cloak . thank you , miss , but I be very sorry some duffle , please . ' 

 hester silently replace the ribbon and go in search of the duffle . while she be go Sylvia be address by the very person she most wish to avoid , and whose absence she have rejoice over on first enter the shop , her cousin Philip Hepburn . 

 he be a serious - looking young man , tall , but with a slight stoop in his shoulder , bring on by his occupation . he have thick hair stand off from his forehead in a peculiar but not unpleasing manner ; a long face , with a slightly aquiline nose , dark eye , and a long upper lip , which give a disagreeable aspect to a face that might otherwise have be good - look .

 ' I be very well , and so be mother ; feyther 's get a touch of rheumatiz , and there be a young woman get what I want . ' 

 she turn a little away from he when she have end this sentence , as if it have comprise all she could possibly have to say to he . but he exclaim ,

 Sylvia take no notice of he , but pretend to be count over her money .

Sylvia devote her attention to the red cloth , push aside the gray . 

 Philip Hepburn be vex at his advice be slight ; and yet he urge it afresh .

 hester come in now .

 hester go on :

 there be a sound of child 's foot run up the street from the river - side , shout with excitement . at the noise , Sylvia forget her cloak and her little spirit of vexation , and run to the half - door of the shop . Philip follow because she go . hester look on with passive , kindly interest , as soon as she have complete her duty of measure . one of those girl whom Sylvia have see as she and Molly leave the crowd on the quay , come quickly up the street . her face , which be handsome enough as to feature , be whiten with excess of passionate emotion , her dress untidy and fly , her movement heavy and free . she belong to the low class of seaport inhabitant . as she come near , Sylvia see that the tear be stream down her cheek , quite unconsciously to herself . she recognize Sylvia 's face , full of interest as it be , and stop she clumsy run to speak to the pretty , sympathetic creature .

' when folk be glad I can not   help be glad too , and I just put out my hand , and she put out her . to think o ' yon ship come in at last ! and if yo'd be down see all t ' folk look and look their eye out , as if they fear they should die afore she come in and bring home the lad they love , yo'd ha ' shake hand wi ' that lass too , and no great harm do . I never set eyne upon she till half an hour ago on th ' staithe , and maybe I will niver see she again . '

 and now Molly Corney join they , hastily burst into the shop .

 no one speak , no one breathe , I have almost say no heart beat for listening . not long ; in an instant there rise the sharp simultaneous cry of many people in rage and despair . inarticulate at that distance , it be yet an intelligible curse , and the roll , and the roar , and the irregular tramp come near and nearer .

 by this time the vanguard of the crowd come press up Bridge Street , past the window of Foster 's shop . it consist of wild , half - amphibious boy , slowly move backwards , as they be compel by the pressure of the come multitude to go on , and yet anxious to defy and annoy the gang by insult , and curse half choke with their indignant passion , double their fist in the very face of the gang who come on with measured movement , arm to the tooth , their face show white with repressed and determined energy against the bronzed countenance of the half - dozen sailor , who be all they have think it wise to pick out of the whaler 's crew , this be the first time an Admiralty warrant have be use in Monkshaven for many year ; not since the close of the american war , in fact . one of the man be address to his townspeople , in a high pitch voice , an exhortation which few could hear , for , press around this nucleus of cruel wrong , be woman cry aloud , throw up their arm in imprecation , shower down abuse as hearty and rapid as if they have be a greek chorus . their wild , famished eye be strain on face they might not kiss , their cheek be flush to purple with anger or else livid with impotent craving for revenge . some of they look scarce human ; and yet an hour ago these lip , now tightly draw back so as to show the tooth with the unconscious action of an enrage wild animal , have be soft and gracious with the smile of hope ; eye , that be fiery and bloodshot now , have be love and bright ; heart , never to recover from the sense of injustice and cruelty , have be trustful and glad only one short hour ago . 

 there be man there , too , sullen and silent , brood on remedial revenge ; but not many , the great proportion of this class be away in the absent whaler . 

 the stormy multitude swell into the market - place and form a solid crowd there , while the press - gang steadily force their way on into High Street , and on to the rendezvous . a low , deep growl go up from the dense mass , as some have to wait for space to follow the other now and then go up , as a lion 's growl go up , into a shriek of rage . 

 a woman force her way up from the bridge . she live some little way in the country , and have be late in hearing of the return of the whaler after her six month ' absence ; and on rush down to the quay - side , she have be tell by a score of busy , sympathize voice , that her husband be kidnap for the service of the Government .

 she have be very busy about household work in the morning , and much agitated by all she have see and hear since come into Monkshaven ; and so it end in this .

'

I guess something of the sort be up from what I hear on t ' bridge as I come across fra ' brother Jeremiah 's . ' here he softly shut the door between the parlour and the shop . ' it beareth hard on th ' expectant woman and childer ; nor be it to be wonder at that they , be unconverted , rage together   poor creature !   like the very heathen . Philip , ' he say , come near to his ' head young man , ' ' keep Nicholas and Henry at work in the ware - room upstairs until this riot be over , for it would grieve I if they be mislead into violence . ' 

 Philip hesitate . 

 ' speak out , man ! always ease an uneasy heart , and never let it get hidebound . '

 but when he go to find the shop - boy with a gentle homily on his lip , those to whom it should have be address be absent . in consequence of the riotous state of thing , all the other shop in the market - place have put their shutter up ; and Nicholas and Henry , in the absence of their superior , have follow the example of their neighbour , and , as business be over , they have hardly wait to put the good away , but have hurry off to help their townsman in any struggle that might ensue . 

 there be no remedy for it , but Mr. John look rather discomfited . the state of the counter , and of the disarrange good , be such also as would have irritate any man as orderly but less sweet - temper . all he say on the subject be : ' the old Adam ! the old Adam ! ' but he shake his head long after he have finish speak .

 Philip and his master arrange the shop in the exact order the old man love . then he recollect the wish of his subordinate , and turn round and say 

 ' now go with thy cousin and her friend . hester be here , and old Hannah . I myself will take Hester home , if need be . but for the present I think she have good tarry here , as it be not   many step to her mother 's house , and we may need her help if any of those poor creature fall into suffer wi ' their violence . ' 

 with this , Mr. John knock at the door of the parlour , and wait for permission to enter . with old - fashioned courtesy he tell the two stranger how glad he be that his room have be of service to they ; that he would never have make so bold as to pass through it , if he have be aware how it be occupy . and then go to a corner cupboard , high up in the wall , he pull a key out of his pocket and unlock his little store of wine , and cake , and spirit ; and insist that they should eat and drink while wait for Philip , who be take some last measure for the security of the shop during the night . 

 Sylvia decline everything , with less courtesy than she ought to have show to the offer of the hospitable old man . molly take wine and cake , leave a good half of both , accord to the code of manner in that part of the country ; and also because Sylvia be continually urge she to make haste . for the latter dislike the idea of her cousin 's esteem it necessary to accompany they home , and want to escape from he by set off before he return . but any such plan be frustrate by Philip 's come back into the parlour , full of grave content , which brim over from his eye , with the parcel of Sylvia 's obnoxious red duffle under his arm ; anticipate so keenly the pleasure await he in the walk , that he be almost surprised by the gravity of his companion as they prepare for it . Sylvia be a little penitent for her rejection of Mr. John 's hospitality , now she find out how unavaile for its purpose such rejection have be , and try to make up by a modest sweetness of farewell , which quite win his heart , and make he praise she up to hester in a way to which she , observant of all , could not bring herself fully to respond . what business have the pretty little creature to reject kindly - mean hospitality in the pettish way she do , think hester . and , oh ! what business have she to be so ungrateful and to try and thwart Philip in his thoughtful wish of escort they through the street of the rough , riotous town ? what do it all mean ? 




 CHAPTER IV 

 PHILIP HEPBURN 

 the coast on that part of the island to which this story refer be border by rock and cliff . the inland country immediately adjacent to the coast be level , flat , and bleak ; it be only where the long stretch of dyke - enclose field terminate abruptly in a sheer descent , and the stranger see the ocean creep up the sand far below he , that he be aware on how great an elevation he have be . here and there , as I have say , a cleft in the level land   thus run out into the sea in steep promontory   occur what they would call a ' chine ' in the Isle of Wight ; but instead of the soft south wind steal up the woody ravine , as it do there , the eastern breeze come pip shrill and clear along these northern chasm , keep the tree that venture to grow on the side down to the mere height of scrubby brushwood . the descent to the shore through these ' bottom ' be in most case very abrupt , too much so for a cartway , or even a bridle - path ; but people can pass up and down without difficulty , by the help of a few rude step hew here and there out of the rock . 

 sixty or seventy year ago   not to speak of much late time   the farmer who own or hire the land which lie directly on the summit of these cliff be smuggler to the extent of their power , only partially check by the coast - guard distribute , at pretty nearly equal interspace of eight mile , all along the north - eastern seaboard . still sea - wrack be a good manure , and there be no law against carry it up in great osier basket for the purpose of tillage , and many a secret thing be lodge in hide crevice in the rock till the farmer send trusty people down to the shore for a good supply of sand and seaweed for his land . 

 one of the farm on the cliff have lately be take by Sylvia 's father . he be a man who have roam about a good deal be sailor , smuggl , horse - dealer , and farmer in turn ; a sort of fellow possess by a spirit of adventure and love of change , which do he and his own family more harm than anybody else . he be just the kind of man that all his neighbour find fault with , and all his neighbour like . late in life   for such an imprudent man as he , be one of a class who generally we d , trust to chance and luck for the provision for a family , farmer Robson marry a woman whose only want of practical wisdom consist in take he for a husband . she be Philip Hepburn 's aunt , and have have the charge of he until she marry from her widow brother 's house . he it be who have let she know when Haytersbank Farm have be to let ; esteem it a likely piece of land for his uncle to settle down upon , after a somewhat unprosperous career of horse - dealing . the farmhouse lie in the shelter of a very slight green hollow scarcely scoop out of the pasture field by which it be surround ; the short crisp turf come creep up to the very door and window , without any attempt at a yard or garden , or any nearer enclosure of the building than the stone dyke that form the boundary of the field itself . the building be long and low , in order to avoid the rough violence of the wind that sweep over that wild , bleak spot , both in winter and summer . it be well for the inhabitant of that house that coal be extremely cheap ; otherwise a southerner might have imagine that they could never have survive the cutting of the bitter gale that pip all round , and seem to seek out every crevice for admission into the house . 

 but the interior be warm enough when once you have mount the long bleak lane , full of round rough stone , enough to lame any horse unaccustomed to such road , and have cross the field by the little dry , hard footpath , which tack about so as to keep from directly face the prevail wind . Mrs. Robson be a Cumberland woman , and as such , be a clean housewife than the farmer ' wife of that north - eastern coast , and be often shock at their way , show it more by her look than by her word , for she be not a great talker . this fastidiousness in such matter make her own house extremely comfortable , but do not tend to render she popular among her neighbour . indeed , Bell Robson pique herself on her housekeeping generally , and once in - door in the gray , bare stone house , there be plenty of comfort to be have besides cleanliness and warmth . the great rack of clap - bread hung overhead , and Bell Robson 's preference of this kind of oat - cake over the leavened and partly sour kind use in Yorkshire be another source of her unpopularity . flitch of bacon and ' hand '   i.e. , shoulder of cure pork , the leg or ham be sell , as fetch a well price   abound ; and for any visitor who could stay , neither cream nor fine wheaten flour be want for ' turf cake ' and ' singing hinny , ' with which it be the delight of the northern housewife to regale the honour guest , as he sip their high - price tea , sweeten with dainty sugar . 

 this night farmer Robson be fidget in and out of his house - door , climb the little eminence in the field , and come down disappoint in a state of fretful impatience . his quiet , taciturn wife be a little put out by Sylvia 's non - appearance too ; but she show her anxiety by be short than usual in her reply to his perpetual wonder as to where the lass could have be tarrying , and by knit away with extra diligence . 

 ' I have   a vast o ' mind to go down to Monkshaven mysen , and see after t ' child . it be well on for seven . ' 

 ' no , Dannel , ' say his wife ; ' thou'd good not . Thy leg have be pain thee this week past , and thou'rt not up to such a walk . I will rouse Kester , and send he off , if thou think'st there be need on it . ' 

 ' A'll noan ha ' Kester rouse . who be to go afield betime after t ' sheep in t ' morn , if he be ca'e up to - neet ? he would miss t ' lass , and find a public - house , a reckon , ' say Daniel , querulously . 

 ' I be not afeard o ' Kester , ' reply Bell . ' he be a good one for know folk I ' th ' dark . but if thou'd rather , I will put on my hood and cloak and just go to th ' end o ' th ' lane , if thou'lt have an eye to th ' milk , and see as it do na ' boil o'er , for she canna stomach it if it be bishoppe e'er so little . ' 

 before Mrs. Robson , however , have put away her knitting , voice be hear at a good distance down the lane , but come nearer every moment , and once more Daniel climb the little brow to look and to listen . 

 ' it be a ' reet ! ' say he , hobble quickly down . ' niver fidget theesel ' wi ' gettin ' ready to go search for she . I will tak ' thee a bet it be Philip Hepburn 's voice , convoy her home , just as I say he would , an hour sin ' . ' 

 Bell do not answer , as she might have do , that this probability of Philip 's bring Sylvia home have be her own suggestion , set aside by her husband as utterly unlikely . another minute and the countenance of both parent imperceptibly and unconsciously relax into pleasure as Sylvia come in . 

 she look very rosy from the walk , and the October air , which begin to be frosty in the evening ; there be a little cloud over her face at first , but it be quickly disperse as she meet the love eye of home . Philip , who follow she , have an excited , but not altogether please look about he . he receive a hearty greeting from Daniel , and a quiet one from his aunt . 

 ' tak ' off thy pan o ' milk , missus , and set on t ' kettle . milk may do for wench , but Philip and I be for a drop o ' good holland and watter this cold night . I be a'most chill to t ' marrow wi ' look out for thee , lass , for t ' mother be in a peck o ' trouble about thy none coin home I ' t ' dayleet , and I would to keep hearken out on t ' browhead . ' 

 this be entirely untrue , and Bell know it to be so ; but her husband do not . he have persuade himself now , as he have do often before , that what he have in reality do for his own pleasure or satisfaction , he have do in order to gratify some one else . 

 ' the town be rough with a riot between the press - gang and the whale folk ; and I think I would well see Sylvia home . ' 

 ' ay , ay , lad ; always welcome , if it be only as an excuse for t ' liquor . but t ' whaler , say'st ta ? why , be t ' whaler in ? there be none I ' sight yesterday , when I be down on t ' shore . it be early day for them as yet . and t ' curse old press - gang 's agate again , do its devil 's work ! ' 

 his face change as he end his speech , and show a steady passion of old hatred . 

 ' ay , missus , yo ' may look . I wunnot pick and choose my word , noather for yo ' nor for nobody , when I speak o ' that daume gang . I be none ashamed o ' my word . they be true , and I be ready to prove them . where be my forefinger ? ay ! and as good a top - joint of a thumb as iver a man have ? I wish I would keep them I ' sperit , as they do thing at t ' ' potticary 's , just to show t ' lass what flesh and bone I make away wi ' to get free . I up wi ' a hatchet when I see as I be fast a - board a man - o'-war stand out for sea it be in t ' time o ' the war wi ' Amerikay , an ' I could na stomach the thought o ' be murder I ' my own language so I up wi ' a hatchet , and I say to Bill Watson , say I , " now , my lad , if thou'll do I a kindness , I will pay thee back , niver fear , and they will be glad enough to get shut on we , and send we to old England again . just come down with a will . " now , missus , why can not   ye sit still and listen to I , ' stead o ' pottering after pan and what not ? ' say he , speak crossly to his wife , who have hear the story score of time , and , it must be confess , be make some noise in prepare bread and milk for Sylvia 's supper . 

 Bell do not say a word in reply , but Sylvia tap his shoulder with a pretty little authoritative air . 

 ' it be for I , feyther . I be just keen - set for my supper . once let I get quickly set down to it , and Philip there to his glass o ' grog , and you will never have such listener in your life , and mother 's mind will be at ease too . ' 

 ' Eh ! thou be a wilfu ' wench , ' say the proud father , give she a great slap on her back . ' well ! set thee down to thy victual , and be quiet wi ' thee , for I want to finish my tale to Philip . but , perhaps , I have   tell it yo ' afore ? ' say he , turn round to question Hepburn . 

 Hepburn could not say that he have not hear it , for he pique himself on his truthfulness . but instead of frankly and directly own this , he try to frame a formal little speech , which would soothe Daniel 's mortified vanity ; and , of course , it have the directly opposite effect . Daniel resent be treat like a child , and yet turn his back on Philip with all the wilfulness of one . Sylvia do not care for her cousin , but hat the discomfort of have her father displease ; so she take up her tale of adventure , and tell her father and mother of her afternoon 's proceeding . Daniel pretend not to listen at first , and make ostentatious noise with his spoon and glass ; but by - and - by he get quite warm and excited about the doing of the press - gang , and scold both Philip and Sylvia for not have learn more particular as to what be the termination of the riot . 

 ' I have   be whale mysel ' , ' say he ; ' and I have   heerd tell as whaler wear knife , and I would ha ' gi'en t ' gang a taste o ' my whittle , if I have be cotche up just as I would set my foot a - shore . ' 

 ' I do not   know , ' say Philip ; ' we be at war wi ' the French , and we should not   like to be beat ; and yet if our number be not equal to theirs , we stand a strong chance of it . ' 

 ' not a bit on't   so be d d ! ' say Daniel Robson , bring down his fist with such violence on the round deal table , that the glass and earthenware shake again . ' Yo'd not strike a child or a woman , for sure ! yet it ' ud be like it , if we do na ' give the Frenchies some ' vantage if we take them wi ' equal number . it be not fair play , and that be one place where t ' shoe pinch . it be not fair play two way . it be not fair play to cotch up man as have no call for fightin ' at another man 's biddin ' , though they have   no objection to fight a bit on their own account and who be just land , all keen after bread i'stead o ' biscuit , and flesh - meat i'stead o ' junk , and bed i'stead o ' hammock .   I make naught o ' t ' sentiment side , for I be niver gi'en up to such carnal - mindedness and poesy .   it be noane fair to cotch them up and put them in a stifle hole , all line with metal for fear they should whittle their way out , and send them off to sea for year an ' year to come . and again it be no fair play to t ' French . four o ' they be rightly match wi ' one o ' we ; and if we go an ' fight them four to four it be like as if yo ' fall to beatin ' Sylvie there , or little Billy Croxton , as be not   breeched . and that be my mind . Missus , where 's t ' pipe ? ' 

 Philip do not smoke , so take his turn at talk , a chance he seldom have with Daniel , unless the latter have his pipe between his lip . so after Daniel have fill it , and use Sylvia 's little finger as a stopper to ram down the tobacco a habit of his to which she be so accustomed that she lay her hand on the table by he , as naturally as she would have fetch he his spittoon when he begin to smoke Philip arrange his argument , and begin 

 ' I be for fair play wi ' the French as much as any man , as long as we can be sure o ' beat they ; but , I say , make sure o ' that , and then give they ivery advantage . now I reckon Government be not sure as yet , for I ' the paper it say as half th ' ship I ' th ' Channel have not   get their proper complement o ' man ; and all as I say be , let Government judge a bit for we ; and if they say they be hamper for want o ' man , why we must make it up somehow . John and Jeremiah Foster pay in taxis , and Militiaman pay in person ; and if sailor can not pay in taxis , and will not pay in person , why they must be make to pay ; and that be what th ' press - gang be for , I reckon . for my part , when I read o ' the way those french chap be go on , I be thankful to be govern by King George and a British Constitution . ' 

 Daniel take his pipe out of his mouth at this . 

 ' and when do I say a word again King George and the Constitution ? I only ax them to govern I as I judge well , and that be what I call representation . when I give my vote to Measter Cholmley to go up to t ' Parliament House , I as good as say , ' now yo ' go up theer , sir , and tell them what I , Dannel Robson , think right , and what I , Dannel Robson , wish to have do . ' else I would be darn if I would ha ' gi'en my vote to he or any other man . and div yo ' think I want Seth Robson    as be my own brother 's son , and mate to a collier   to be cotche up by a press - gang , and ten to one his wage all unpaid ? Div yo ' think I would send up Measter Cholmley to speak up for that piece o ' work ? not i. ' he take up his pipe again , shake out the ash , puff it into a spark , and shut his eye , preparatory to listen . 

 ' but , ask pardon , law be make for the good of the nation , not for your good or mine . ' 

 Daniel could not stand this . he lay down his pipe , open his eye , star straight at Philip before speak , in order to enforce his word , and then say slowly 

 ' Nation here ! nation theere ! I be a man and yo're another , but nation 's nowheere . if Measter Cholmley talk to I I ' that fashion , he would look long for another vote frae I . I can make out King George , and Measter Pitt , and yo ' and I , but nation ! nation , go hang ! ' 

 Philip , who sometimes pursue an argument long than be politic for himself , especially when he feel sure of be on the conquering side , do not see that Daniel Robson be pass out of the indifference of conscious wisdom into that state of anger which ensue when a question become personal in some unspoken way . Robson have contest this subject once or twice before , and have the remembrance of former dispute to add to his present vehemence . so it be well for the harmony of the evening that Bell and Sylvia return from the kitchen to sit in the house - place . they have be to wash up the pan and basin use for supper ; Sylvia have privately show off her cloak , and get over her mother 's shake of the head at its colour with a coax kiss , at the end of which her mother have adjust her cap with a ' there ! there ! ha ' do wi ' thee , ' but have no more heart to show her disapprobation ; and now they come back to their usual occupation until it should please their visitor to go ; then they would rake the fire and be off to bed ; for neither Sylvia 's spinning nor Bell 's knitting be worth candle - light , and morning hour be precious in a dairy . 

 People speak of the way in which harp - playing set off a graceful figure ; spinning be almost as become an employment . a woman stand at the great wool - wheel , one arm extend , the other hold the thread , her head throw back to take in all the scope of her occupation ; or if it be the less spinning - wheel for flax and it be this that Sylvia move forwards to - night the pretty sound of the buzzing , whir motion , the attitude of the spinner , foot and hand alike engage in the business the bunch of gay coloured ribbon that tie the bundle of flax on the rock all make it into a picturesque piece of domestic business that may rival harp - play any day for the amount of softness and grace which it call out . 

 Sylvia 's cheek be rather flush by the warmth of the room after the frosty air . the blue ribbon with which she have think it necessary to tie back her hair before put on her hat to go to market have get rather loose , and allow she disarrange curl to stray in a manner which would have annoy she extremely , if she have be upstairs to look at herself in the glass ; but although they be not set in the exact fashion which Sylvia esteem as correct , they look very pretty and luxuriant . her little foot , place on the ' traddle ' , be still encase in its smartly buckle shoe not slightly to her discomfort , as she be unaccustomed to be shod in walk far ; only as Philip have accompany they home , neither she nor Molly have like to go barefoot . her round mottled arm and ruddy taper hand draw out the flax with nimble , agile motion , keep time to the movement of the wheel . all this Philip could see ; the great part of her face be lose to he as she half avert it , with a shy dislike to the way in which she know from past experience that cousin Philip always star at she . and avert it as she would she hear with silent petulance the harsh screech of Philip 's chair as he heavily drag it on the stone floor , sit on it all the while , and feel that he be move round so as to look at she as much as be in his power , without absolutely turn his back on either her father or mother . she get herself ready for the first opportunity of contradiction or opposition . 

 ' well , wench ! and have ta buy this grand new cloak ? ' 

 ' yes , feyther . it be a scarlet one . ' 

 ' ay , ay ! and what do mother say ? '
