 ' oh , mother 's content , ' say Sylvia , a little doubt in her heart , but determine to defy Philip at all hazard . 

 ' Mother will put up with it if it do na spot would be near fact , I be think , ' say Bell , quietly . 

 ' I want Sylvia to take the gray , ' say Philip . 

 ' and I choose the red ; it be so much gayer , and folk can see I the far off . Feyther like to see I at first turn o ' t ' lane , do not   yo ' , feyther ? and I will niver turn out when it be boun ' for to rain , so it shall niver get a spot near it , mammy . ' 

 ' I reckon it be to wear I ' bad weather , ' say Bell . ' leastway that be the pretext for coax feyther out o ' it . ' 

 she say it in a kindly tone , though the word become a prudent rather than a fond mother . but Sylvia understand she well than Daniel do as it appear . 

 ' Hou'd thy tongue , mother . she niver speak a pretext at all . ' 

 he do not rightly know what a ' pretext ' be : Bell be a touch well educate than her husband , but he do not acknowledge this , and make a particular point of differ from she whenever she use a word beyond his comprehension . 

 ' she be a good lass at time ; and if she like to wear a yellow - orange cloak she should have it . here be Philip here , as stand up for law and press - gang , I will set he to find we a law again please our lass ; and she our only one . Thou dostn't   think on that , mother ! 

 Bell do think of that often ; oftener than her husband , perhaps , for she remember every day , and many time a day , the little one that have be bear and have die while its father be away on some long voyage . but it be not her way to make reply . 

 Sylvia , who have more insight into her mother 's heart than Daniel , break in with a new subject . 

 ' oh ! as for Philip , he be be preach up law all t ' way home . I say naught , but let molly hold her own ; or else I could ha ' tell a tale about silk an ' lace an ' thing . ' 

 Philip 's face flush . not because of the smuggling ; every one do that , only it be consider polite to ignore it ; but he be annoy to perceive how quickly his little cousin have discover that his practice do not agree with his preaching , and vex , too , to see how delighted she be to bring out the fact . he have some little idea , too , that his uncle might make use of his practice as an argument against the preaching he have lately be indulge in , in opposition to Daniel ; but Daniel be too far go in his Hollands - and - water to do more than enunciate his own opinion , which he do with hesitate and labour distinctness in the following sentence : 

 ' what I think and say be this . law be make for to keep some folk fra ' harm other . press - gang and coast - guard harm I I ' my business , and keep I fra ' get what I want . Theerefore , what I think and say be this : Measter Cholmley should put down press - gang and coast - guard . if that theere be not   reason I ax yo ' to tell I what be ? an ' if Measter Cholmley do not   do what I ax he , he may go whistle for my vote , he may . ' 

 at this period in his conversation , Bell Robson interfere ; not in the least from any feeling of disgust or annoyance , or dread of what he might say or do if he go on drinking , but simply as a matter of health . Sylvia , too , be in no way annoyed ; not only with her father , but with every man whom she know , except her cousin Philip , be it a matter of course to drink till their idea become confused . so she simply put her wheel aside , as preparatory to go to bed , when her mother say , in a more decide tone than that which she have use on any other occasion but this , and similar one 

 ' come , measter , you have   have as much as be good for you . ' 

 ' let a ' be ! let a ' be , ' say he , clutch at the bottle of spirit , but perhaps rather more good - humour with what he have drunk than he be before ; he jerk a little more into his glass before his wife carry it off , and lock it up in the cupboard , put the key in her pocket , and then he say , wink at Philip 

 ' eh ! my man . niver gie a woman t ' whip hand o'er yo ' ! Yo ' see what it bring a man to ; but for a ' that I will vote for Cholmley , an ' d t ' press - gang ! ' 

 he have to shout out the last after Philip , for Hepburn , really anxious to please his aunt , and dislike drinking habit himself by constitution , be already at the door , and set out on his return home , think , it must be confess , far more of the character of Sylvia 's shake of the hand than of the parting word of either his uncle or aunt . 




 CHAPTER V 

 STORY of THE PRESS - gang 

 for a few day after the evening mention in the last chapter the weather be dull . not in quick , sudden shower do the rain come down , but in constant drizzle , blot out all colour from the surround landscape , and fill the air with fine gray mist , until people breathe more water than air . at such time the consciousness of the nearness of the vast unseen sea act as a dreary depression to the spirit ; but besides act on the nerve of the excitable , such weather affect the sensitive or ail in material way . Daniel Robson 's fit of rheumatism incapacitate he from stir abroad ; and to a man of his active habit , and somewhat inactive mind , this be a great hardship . he be not ill - temper naturally , but this state of confinement make he more ill - temper than he have ever be before in his life . he sit in the chimney - corner , abuse the weather and doubt the wisdom or desirableness of all his wife see fit to do in the usual daily household matter . the ' chimney - corner ' be really a corner at Haytersbank . there be two project wall on each side of the fire - place , run about six foot into the room , and a stout wooden settle be place against one of these , while opposite be the circular - back ' master 's chair , ' the seat of which be compose of a square piece of wood judiciously hollow out , and place with one corner to the front . here , in full view of all the operation go on over the fire , sit Daniel Robson for four live - long day , advise and direct his wife in all such minor matter as the boiling of potato , the making of porridge , all the work on which she specially pique herself , and on which she would have take advice no ! not from the most skilled housewife in all the three Ridings . but , somehow , she manage to keep her tongue quiet from tell he , as she would have do any woman , and any other man , to mind his own business , or she would pin a dish - clout to his tail . she even check Sylvia when the latter propose , as much for fun as for anything else , that his ignorant direction should be follow , and the consequence bring before his eye and his nose . 

 ' na , na ! ' say Bell , ' th ' feyther 's feyther , and we mun respect he . but it be dree work havin' a man I ' th ' house , nursing th ' fire , an ' such weather too , and not a soul come near we , not even to fall out wi ' he ; for thee and I must na ' do that , for th ' Bible 's sake , dear ; and a good stand - up wordy quarrel would do he a power of good ; stir his blood like . I wish Philip would turn up . ' 

 Bell sigh , for in these four day she have experience somewhat of Madame de Maintenon 's difficulty   and with few resource to meet it   of try to amuse a man who be not amusable . for Bell , good and sensible as she be , be not a woman of resource . Sylvia 's plan , undutiful as it be in her mother 's eye , would have do Daniel more good , even though it might have make he angry , than his wife 's quiet , careful monotony of action , which , however it might conduce to her husband 's comfort when he be absent , do not amuse he when present . 

 Sylvia scout the notion of cousin Philip come into their household in the character of an amusing or entertaining person , till she nearly make her mother angry at her ridicule of the good steady young fellow , to whom Bell look up as the pattern of all that early manhood should be . but the moment Sylvia see she have be give her mother pain , she leave off her wilful little joke , and kiss she , and tell she she would manage all famously , and run out of the back - kitchen , in which mother and daughter have be scrub the churn and all the wooden implement of butter - making . Bell look at the pretty figure of her little daughter , as , run past with her apron throw over her head , she darken the window beneath which her mother be do her work . she pause just for a moment , and then say , almost unawares to herself , ' bless thee , lass , ' before resume her scouring of what already look almost snow - white . 

 Sylvia scamper across the rough farmyard in the wetting , drizzle rain to the place where she expect to find Kester ; but he be not there , so she have to retrace her step to the cow - house , and , make her way up a rough kind of ladder - staircase fix straight against the wall , she surprise Kester as he sit in the wool - loft , look over the fleece reserve for the home - spinning , by pop her bright face , swathe round with her blue woollen apron , up through the trap - door , and thus , her head the only visible part , she address the farm - servant , who be almost like one of the family . 

 ' Kester , feyther 's just tiring hissel ' wi ' weariness an ' vexation , sit by t ' fireside wi ' his hand afore he , an ' nought to do . an ' mother and I can not   think on aught as will rouse he up to a bit of a laugh , or aught more cheerful than a scolding . now , Kester , thou mun just be off , and find Harry Donkin th ' tailor , and bring he here ; it be gettin ' on for Martinmas , an ' he will be come his round , and he may as well come here first as last , and feyther 's clothe want a deal o ' mend up , and Harry be always full of his news , and anyhow he will do for feyther to scold , an ' be a new person too , and that be somewhat for all on we . now go , like a good old Kester as yo ' be . ' 

 Kester look at she with loving , faithful admiration . he have set himself his day 's work in his master 's absence , and be very desirous of finish it , but , somehow , he never dream of resist Sylvia , so he only state the case . 

 ' T ' ' ool be a vast o ' muck in ' t , an ' a thowt as a'd fettle it , an ' do it up ; but a reckon a mun do yo'r biddin ' . ' 

 ' there be a good old kester , ' say she , smile , and nod her muffled head at he ; then she dip down out of his sight , then rise up again   he have never take his slow , mooney eye from the spot where she have disappear   to say ' now , Kester , be wary and deep thou mun tell Harry Donkin not to let on as we have   send for he , but just to come in as if he be on his round , and take we first ; and he mun ask feyther if there be any work for he to do ; and I will answer for ' t , he will have a welcome and a half . now , be deep and fause , mind thee ! ' 

 ' A'se deep an ' fause enow wi ' simple folk ; but what can a do I ' Donkin be as fause as I as happen he may be ? ' 

 ' Ga way wi ' thee ! I ' Donkin be Solomon , thou mun be t ' Queen o ' Sheba ; and i'se bind for to say she outwit he at last ! ' 

 Kester laugh so long at the idea of his be the Queen of Sheba , that Sylvia be back by her mother 's side before the cachinnation end . 

 that night , just as Sylvia be prepare to go to bed in her little closet of a room , she hear some shot rattle at her window . she open the little casement , and see Kester stand below . he recommence where he leave off , with a laugh 

 ' he , he , he ! A 's be t ' queen ! a'se ta'en Donkin on t ' reet side , an ' he will coom in to - morrow , just permiskus , an ' ax for work , like as if ' t be a favour ; t ' oud felley be a bit cross - grained at startin ' , for he be workin ' at farmer Crosskey 's up at t ' other side o ' t ' town , wheer they put a strike an ' a half of maut intil t ' beer , when most folk put nobbut a strike , an t ' make he ill to convince : but he will coom , niver fear ! ' 

 the honest fellow never say a word of the shilling he have pay out of his own pocket to forward Sylvia 's wish , and to persuade the tailor to leave the good beer . all his anxiety now be to know if he have be miss , and if it be likely that a scolding await he in the morning . 

 ' T ' oud measter do not   set up his back , 'cause a do not   coom in t ' supper ? ' 

 ' he question a bit as to what thou be about , but mother do not   know , an ' I hold my peace . mother carry thy supper in t ' loft for thee . ' 

 ' a'll gang after ' t , then , for a'm like a pair o ' bellows wi ' t ' wind out ; just two flat side wi ' nowt betwixt . ' 

 the next morning , Sylvia 's face be a little red than usual when Harry Donkin 's bow - leg be see circle down the path to the house door . 

 ' here be Donkin , for sure ! ' exclaim Bell , when she catch sight of he a minute after her daughter . ' well , I just call that lucky ! for he will be company for thee while Sylvia and I have to turn th ' cheese . ' 

 this be too original a remark for a wife to make in Daniel 's opinion , on this especial morning , when his rheumatism be twinge he more than usual , so he reply with severity 

 ' that be all t ' woman know about it . Wi ' they it be " coompany , coompany , coompany , " an ' they think a man 's no well than theirsel . A'd have yo ' to know a've   a vast o ' thought in myself ' , as I be noane willing to lay out for t ' benefit o ' every man . A've   niver get time for meditation sin ' a be marry ; leastway , sin ' a left t ' sea . Aboard ship , wi ' niver a woman wi'n league o ' hail , and upo ' t ' masthead , in special , a could . ' 

 ' then I would well tell Donkin as we have   no work for he , ' say Sylvia , instinctively manage her father by agree with he , instead of reasoning with or contradict he . 

 ' now , theere you go ! ' wrench himself round , for fear Sylvia should carry she meekly make threat into execution . ' Ugh ! ugh ! ' as his limb hurt he . ' come in , Harry , come in , and talk a bit o ' sense to I , for a've   be shut up wi ' woman these four day , and a'm a'most a nateral by this time . A'se bind for ' t , they will find yo ' some wark , if ' t 's nought but for to save their own finger . ' 

 so Harry take off his coat , and seat himself professional - wise on the hastily - clear dresser , so that he might have all the light afford by the long , low casement window . then he blow in his thimble , suck his finger , so that they might adhere tightly together , and look about for a subject for open conversation , while Sylvia and her mother might be hear open and shut drawer and box - lid before they could find the article that need repair , or that be require to mend each other . 

 ' woman 's well enough I ' their way , ' say Daniel , in a philosophize tone , ' but a man may have too much on them . now there be I , leg - fast these four day , and a'll make free to say to yo ' , a'd rather a deal ha ' be load dung I ' t ' wet weather ; an ' a reckon it be th ' be wi ' nought but woman as tire I so : they talk so foolish it get int ' t ' bone like . now thou know'st thou'rt not call much of a man oather , but bless yo ' , t ' ninth part 's summut to be thankful for , after nought but woman . an ' yet , yo ' see , they be for send yo ' away I ' their foolishness ! well ! missus , and who be to pay for t ' fettling of all they clothe ? ' as Bell come down with her arm full . she be go to answer her husband meekly and literally accord to she will not , but Sylvia , already detect the increase cheerfulness of his tone , call out from behind her mother 

 ' I be , feyther . I be go for to sell my new cloak as I buy Thursday , for the mending on your old coat and waistcoat . ' 

 ' Hearken till she , ' say Daniel , chuckle . ' she be a true wench . three day sin ' noane so full as she o ' t ' new cloak that now she be fain t ' sell . ' 

 ' ay , Harry . if feyther will not   pay yo ' for make all these old clothe as good as new , I will sell my new red cloak soon than yo ' shall go unpaid . ' 

 ' a reckon it be a bargain , ' say Harry , cast sharp , professional eye on the heap before he , and single out the good article as to texture for examination and comment . 

 ' they be all again these metal button , ' say he . ' silk weaver have be petition Ministers t ' make a law to favour silk button ; and I do hear tell as there be informer go about spyin ' after metal button , and as how they could haul yo ' before a justice for wear on them . ' 

 ' a be we d in them , and a'll wear them to my dyin ' day , or a'll wear noane at a ' . they be for make such a pack o ' law , they will be for meddle wi ' my fashion o ' sleep next , and tax I for ivery snore a give . they have   be after t ' winder , and after t ' vittle , and after t ' very saut to ' t ; it be dearer by hauf an ' more nor it be when a be a boy : they be a meddlesome set o ' folk , law - maker be , an ' a'll niver believe King George have ought t ' do wi ' ' t. but mark my word ; I be we d wi ' brass button , and brass button a'll wear to my death , an ' if they moither I about it , a'll wear brass button I ' my coffin ! ' 

 by this time Harry have arrange a certain course of action with Mrs Robson , conduct the consultation and agreement by sign . his thread be fly fast already , and the mother and daughter feel more free to pursue their own business than they have do for several day ; for it be a good sign that Daniel have take his pipe out of the square hollow in the fireside wall , where he usually keep it , and be prepare to diversify his remark with satisfying interlude of puffing . 

 ' why , look ye ; this very baccy have a run for ' t. it come ashore sew up neatly enough I ' a woman 's stay , as be wife to a fishing - smack down at t ' bay yonder . she be a lean thing as iver you see , when she go for t ' see her husband aboard t ' vessel ; but she coom back lustier by a deal , an ' wi ' many a thing on she , here and theere , beside baccy . an ' that be I ' t ' face o ' coast - guard and yon tender , an ' a ' . but she make as though she be tipsy , an ' so they do nought but curse she , an ' get out on her way . ' 

 ' speak of t ' tender , there be be a piece o ' wark I ' monkshaven this week wi ' t ' press - gang , ' say Harry . 

 ' ay ! ay ! our lass be tell about ' t ; but , Lord bless ye ! there be no gettin ' t ' right on a story out on a woman though a will say this for our Sylvie , she be as bright a lass as iver a man look at . ' 

 now the truth be , that Daniel have not like to demean himself , at the time when Sylvia come back so full of what she have see at Monkshaven , by evince any curiosity on the subject . he have then think that the next day he would find some business that should take he down to the town , when he could learn all that be to be learn , without flatter his womankind by ask question , as if anything they might say could interest he . he have a strong notion of be a kind of domestic Jupiter . 

 ' it be make a deal o ' wark I ' monkshaven . folk have get to think nought o ' t ' tender , she lie so still , an ' t ' leftenant pay such a good price for all he want for t ' ship . but o ' Thursday t ' resolution , first whaler back this season , come in port , and t ' press - gang show their tooth , and carry off four as good able - body seaman as iver I make trouser for ; and t ' place be all up like a nest o ' wasp , when yo've   set your foot in t ' midst . they be so mad , they be ready for t ' fight t ' very pavin ' stone . ' 

 ' a wish a'd be theere ! a just wish a had ! a've   a score for t ' reckon up wi ' t ' press - gang ! ' 

 and the old man lift up his right hand his hand on which the forefinger and thumb be maim and useless partly in denunciation , and partly as a witness of what he have endure to escape from the service , abhor because it be force . his face become a totally different countenance with the expression of settled and unrelenting indignation , which his word call out . 

 ' g'on , man , g'on , ' say Daniel , impatient with Donkin for the little delay occasion by the necessity of arrange his work more fully . 

 ' ay ! ay ! all in good time ; for a've   a long tale to tell yet ; an ' a mun have some ' un to iron I out my seam , and look I out my bit , for there be none here fit for my purpose . ' 

 ' dang thy bit ! here , Sylvie ! Sylvie ! come and be tailor 's man , and let t ' chap get settle sharp , for a'm fain t ' hear his story . ' 

 Sylvia take her direction , and place her iron in the fire , and run upstairs for the bundle which have be put aside by her careful mother for occasion like the present . it consist of small piece of various coloured cloth , cut out of old coat and waistcoat , and similar garment , when the whole have become too much worn for use , yet when part have be good enough to be treasure by a thrifty housewife . Daniel grow angry before Donkin have select his pattern and settle the work to his own mind . 

 ' well , ' say he at last ; ' a mought be a young man a - go a wooin ' , by t ' pain thou'st take for t ' match my oud clothe . I do not   care if they be patch wi ' scarlet , a tell thee ; so as thou'lt work away at thy tale wi ' thy tongue , same time as thou work at thy needle wi ' thy finger . ' 

 ' then , as a be say , all Monkshaven be like a nest o ' wasp , flyin ' hither and thither , and makin ' sich a buzzin ' and a talkin ' as niver be ; and each wi ' his sting out , ready for t ' vent his venom o ' rage and revenge . and woman cryin ' and sobbin ' I ' t ' street when , Lord help we ! o ' Saturday come a bad time than iver ! for all Friday there have be a kind o ' expectation an ' dismay about t ' Good Fortune , as t ' mariner have say be off St Abb 's Head o ' Thursday , when t ' Resolution come in ; and there be wife and maid wi ' husband an ' sweetheart aboard t ' Good Fortune ready to throw their eye out on their head wi ' gazin ' , gazin ' nor'ards over t'sea , as be all one haze o ' blankness wi ' t ' rain ; and when t ' afternoon tide come in , an ' niver a line on she to be see , folk be oncertain as t ' whether she be hold off for fear o ' t ' tender as be out o ' sight , too or what be her mak ' o ' go on . an ' t ' poor wet draggle woman folk come up t ' town , some slowly cryin ' , as if their heart be sick , an ' other just bent their head to t ' wind , and go straight to their home , nother look nor speak to ony one ; but bar their door , and stiffen theirsel up for a night o ' wait . Saturday morn yo'll mind Saturday morn , it be stormy and gusty , downreet dirty weather theere stand t ' folk again by daylight , a watch an ' a straining , and by that tide t ' Good Fortune come o'er t ' bar . but t ' exciseman have send back her news by t ' boat as take them there . they have a deal of oil , and a vast o ' blubber . but for all that her flag be droop I ' t ' rain , half mast high , for mourning and sorrow , an ' they have a dead man aboard a dead man as be live and strong last sunrise . an ' there be another as lie between life an ' death , and there be seven more as should ha ' be theere as be not , but be carry off by t ' gang . t ' frigate as we ' n a ' hear tell on , as lie off Hartlepool , get tiding fra ' t ' tender as capture t ' seaman o ' Thursday : and t ' Aurora , as they ca'e she , make off for t ' nor'ard ; and nine league off St Abb 's Head , t ' resolution think she be , she see'd t ' frigate , and know by her build she be a man - o'-war , and guess she be bind on king 's kidnapping . I see t ' wounded man mysen wi ' my own eye ; and he will live ! he will live ! niver a man die yet , wi ' such a strong purpose o ' vengeance in he . he could barely speak , for he be badly shoot , but his colour coome and go , as t ' master 's mate an ' t ' captain tell I and some other how t ' Aurora fire at them , and how t ' innocent whaler hoist her colour , but afore they be fairly run up , another shot coome close in t ' shroud , and then t ' Greenland ship be t ' windward , bear down on t ' frigate ; but as they know she be an oud fox , and bend on mischief , Kinraid   that be he who lie a - die , only he will noane die , a'se bind , the specksioneer , bade t ' man go down between deck , and fasten t ' hatch well , an ' he would stand guard , he an ' captain , and t ' oud master 's mate , be leave upo ' deck for t ' give a welcome just skin - deep to t ' boat 's crew fra ' t ' Aurora , as they could see come t'ward they o'er t ' watter , wi ' their reg'lar man - o'-war 's rowing ' 

 ' damn them ! ' say Daniel , in soliloquy , and under his breath . 

 Sylvia stand , poise her iron , and listen eagerly , afraid to give Donkin the hot iron for fear of interrupt the narrative , unwilling to put it into the fire again , because that action would perchance remind he of his work , which now the tailor have forget , so eager be he in tell his story . 

 ' well ! they coome on over t ' watter wi ' great bound , and up t ' side they coome like locust , all armed man ; an ' t ' captain say he see Kinraid hide away his whale knife under some tarpaulin ' , and he know he mean mischief , an ' he would no more ha ' stop he wi ' a word nor he would ha ' stop he fra ' kill a whale . and when t ' Aurora 's man be aboard , one on them run to t ' helm ; and at that t ' captain say , he feel as if his wife be kiss afore his face ; but say he , " I bethink I on t ' man as be shut up below hatch , an ' I remember t ' folk at Monkshaven as be look out for we even then ; an ' I say to mysel ' , I would speak fair as long as I could , more by token o ' the whaling - knife , as I could see glint bright under t ' black tarpaulin . " so he speak quite fair and civil , though he see'd they be near t ' Aurora , and t ' Aurora be near they . then t ' navy captain hail he thro ' t ' trumpet , wi ' a great rough blast , and , say he , " order your man to come on deck . " and t ' captain of t ' whaler say his man cry up from under t ' hatch as they would niver be gi'en up wi'out bloodshed , and he see Kinraid take out his pistol , and look well to t ' priming ; so he say to t ' navy captain , " we be protect Greenland - man , and you have no right t ' meddle wi ' we . " but t ' navy captain only bellow t ' more , " order your man t ' come on deck . if they will not   obey you , and you have lose the command of your vessel , I reckon you be in a state of mutiny , and you may come aboard t ' Aurora and such man as be willing t ' follow you , and I will fire int ' the rest . " Yo ' see , that be t ' depth o ' the man : he be for pretend and pretexte as t ' captain could na manage his own ship , and as he would help he . but our Greenland captain be noane so poor - spirit , and say he , " she be full of oil , and I ware you of consequence if you fire into she . anyhow , pirate , or no pirate "   for t ' word pirate stick in his gizzard , " I be a honest monkshaven man , an ' I come fra ' a land where there be great iceberg and many a deadly danger , but niver a press - gang , thank God ! and that be what you be , I reckon . " they be the word he tell I , but whether he speak them out so bold at t ' time , i'se not so sure ; they be in his mind for t ' speak , only maybe prudence get t ' well on he , for he say he pray I ' his heart to bring his cargo safe to t ' owner , come what might . well , t ' Aurora 's man aboard t ' Good Fortune cry out " might they fire down t ' hatch , and bring t ' man out that a way ? " and then t ' specksioneer , he speak , an ' he say he stand ower t ' hatch , and he have two good pistol , and summut besides , and he do not   care for his life , bein ' a bachelor , but all below be married man , yo ' see , and he will put an end to t ' first two chap as come near t ' hatch . an ' they say he pick two off as make for t ' come near , and then , just as he be stoop for t ' whale knife , an ' it be as big as a sickle ' 

 ' teach folk as do not   know a whale knife , ' cry Daniel . ' I be a Greenland - man mysel ' . ' 

 ' they shoot he through t ' side , and dizzy he , and kick he aside for dead ; and fire down t ' hatch , and kill one man , and disable two , and then t ' rest cry for quarter , for life be sweet , e'en aboard a king 's ship ; and t ' Aurora carry them off , wound man , an ' able man , an ' all : leave Kinraid for dead , as be not   dead , and Darley for dead , as be dead , an ' t ' captain and master 's mate as be too old for work ; and t ' captain , as love Kinraid like a brother , pour rum down his throat , and bandage he up , and have send for t ' first doctor in Monkshaven for to get t ' slug out ; for they say there be niver such a harpooner in a ' t ' Greenland sea ; an ' I can speak fra ' my own see he be a fine young fellow where he lie theere , all stark and wan for weakness and loss o ' blood . but Darley 's dead as a door - nail ; and there be to be such a burying of he as niver be see afore I ' monkshaven , come Sunday . and now gi ' us t ' iron , wench , and let us lose no more time a - talk . ' 

 ' it be noane loss o ' time , ' say Daniel , move himself heavily in his chair , to feel how helpless he be once more . ' if a be as young as once a be nay , lad , if a have na these sore rheumatic , now a reckon as t ' press - gang ' ud find out as t ' should not   do such thing for nothing . bless thee , man ! it be waur nor I ' my youth I ' th ' Ameriky war , and then ' t be bad enough . ' 

 ' and Kinraid ? ' say Sylvia , draw a long breath , after the effort of realize it all ; her cheek have flush up , and her eye have glitter during the progress of the tale . 

 ' oh ! he will do . he will not die . life 's stuff be in he yet . ' 

 ' he will be Molly Corney 's cousin , I reckon , ' say Sylvia , bethink she with a blush of Molly Corney 's implication that he be more than a cousin to she , and immediately long to go off and see Molly , and hear all the little detail which woman do not think it beneath they to give to woman . from that time Sylvia 's little heart be bent on this purpose . but it be not one to be openly avow even to herself . she only want sadly to see Molly , and she almost believe herself that it be to consult she about the fashion of her cloak ; which Donkin be to cut out , and which she be to make under his direction ; at any rate , this be the reason she give to her mother when the day 's work be do , and a fine gleam come out upon the pale and watery sky towards evening . 




 CHAPTER VI 

 the SAILOR 's FUNERAL 

 Moss Brow , the Corney 's house , be but a disorderly , comfortless place . you have to cross a dirty farmyard , all puddle and dungheap , on stepping - stone , to get to the door of the house - place . that great room itself be sure to have clothe hang to dry at the fire , whatever day of the week it be ; some one of the large irregular family have have what be call in the district a ' dab - wash ' of a few article , forget on the regular day . and sometimes these article lie in their dirty state in the untidy kitchen , out of which a room , half parlour , half bedroom , open on one side , and a dairy , the only clean place in the house , at the opposite . in face of you , as you enter the door , be the entrance to the working - kitchen , or scullery . still , in spite of disorder like this , there be a well - to - do aspect about the place ; the Corneys be rich in their way , in flock and herd as well as in child ; and to they neither dirt nor the perpetual bustle arise from ill - order work detract from comfort . they be all of an easy , good - temper nature ; Mrs. Corney and her daughter give every one a welcome at whatever time of the day they come , and would just as soon sit down for a gossip at ten o'clock in the morning , as at five in the evening , though at the former time the house - place be full of work of various kind which ought to be get out of hand and do with : while the latter hour be towards the end of the day , when farmer ' wife and daughter be usually ' clean ' be the word then , ' dress ' be that in vogue now . of course in such a household as this Sylvia be sure to be gladly receive . she be young , and pretty , and bright , and bring a fresh breeze of pleasant air about she as her appropriate atmosphere . and besides , Bell Robson hold her head so high that visit from her daughter be rather esteem as a favour , for it be not everywhere that Sylvia be allow to go . 

 ' sit yo ' down , sit yo ' down ! ' cry Dame Corney , dust a chair with her apron ; ' a reckon Molly will be in I ' no time . she be nobbut go int ' t ' orchard , to see if she can find wind - fall enough for t ' make a pie or two for t ' lad . they like nowt so weel for supper as apple - pie sweeten wi ' treacle , crust stout and leathery , as stand chew , and we hannot getten in our apple yet . ' 

 ' if molly be in t ' orchard , I will go find she , ' say Sylvia . 

 ' well ! yo ' lass will have your conk '   private talk , ' a know ; secret 'bout sweetheart and such like , ' say Mrs. Corney , with a knowing look , which make Sylvia hate she for the moment . ' A've   not forget as a be young mysen . tak ' care ; there be a pool o ' mucky watter just outside t ' back - door . ' 

 but Sylvia be half - way across the back - yard bad , if possible , than the front as to the condition in which it be keep and have pass through the little gate into the orchard . it be full of old gnarl apple - tree , their trunk cover with gray lichen , in which the cunning chaffinch build her nest in spring - time . the canker branch remain on the tree , and add to the knot interweave overhead , if they do not to the productiveness ; the grass grow in long tuft , and be wet and tangle under foot . there be a tolerable crop of rosy apple still hang on the gray old tree , and here and there they show ruddy in the green boss of untrimmed grass . why the fruit be not gather , as it be evidently ripe , would have puzzle any one not acquaint with the Corney family to say ; but to they it be always a maxim in practice , if not in precept , ' do nothing to - day that you can put off till to - morrow , ' and accordingly the apple drop from the tree at any little gust of wind , and lie rotting on the ground until the ' lad ' want a supply of pie for supper . 

 molly see Sylvia , and come quickly across the orchard to meet she , catch her foot in knot of grass as she hurry along . 

 ' well , lass ! ' say she , ' who would ha ' think o ' see yo ' such a day as it have be ? ' 

 ' but it be clear up now beautiful , ' say Sylvia , look up at the soft evening sky , to be see through the apple bough . it be of a tender , delicate gray , with the faint warmth of a promising sunset ting it with a pink atmosphere . ' rain be over and go , and I want to know how my cloak be to be make ; for Donkin 's work at our house , and I want to know all about the news , yo ' know . ' 

 ' what news ? ' ask Molly , for she have hear of the affair between the Good Fortune and the Aurora some day before ; and , to tell the truth , it have rather pass out of her head just at this moment . 

 ' hannot yo ' hear all about t ' press - gang and t ' whaler , and t ' great fight , and Kinraid , as be your cousin , act so brave and grand , and lie on his death - bed now ? ' 

 ' oh ! ' say Molly , enlighten as to Sylvia 's ' news , ' and half surprised at the vehemence with which the little creature speak ; ' yes ; a heerd that day ago . but Charley 's noane on his death - bed , he be a deal well ; an ' mother say as he be to be move up here next week for nursin ' and well air nor he get I ' t ' town yonder . ' 

 ' oh ! I be so glad , ' say Sylvia , with all her heart . ' I think he would maybe die , and I should niver see he . ' 

 ' a'll promise yo ' shall see he ; that be t ' say if a ' go on well , for he be getten an ugly hurt . Mother say as there be four blue mark on his side as'll last he his life , an ' t ' doctor fear bleed I ' his inside ; and then he will drop down dead when no one look for ' t. ' 

 ' but you say he be well , ' say Sylvia , blanch a little at this account . 

 ' ay , he be well , but life 's uncertain , special after gun - shot wound . ' 

 ' he act very fine , ' say Sylvia , meditate . 

 ' a allay know he would . many be the time a've   heerd he say " honour bright , " and now he be show how bright his be . ' 

 molly do not speak sentimentally , but with a kind of proprietorship in Kinraid 's honour , which confirm Sylvia in her previous idea of a mutual attachment between she and her cousin . consider this notion , she be a little surprised at Molly 's next speech . 

 ' An ' about yer cloak , be you for a hood or a cape ? a reckon that be the question . ' 

 ' oh , I do not   care ! tell I more about Kinraid . do yo ' really think he will get well ? ' 

 ' dear ! how t ' lass take on about he . A'll tell he what a deal of interest a young woman tak I ' he ! ' 

 from that time Sylvia never ask another question about he . in a somewhat dry and altered tone , she say , after a little pause 

 ' I think on a hood . what do you say to it ? ' 

 ' well ; hood be a bit old - fashioned , to my mind . if ' t be mine , I would have a cape cut I ' three point , one to tie on each shoulder , and one to dip down handsome behind . but let yo ' an ' I go to Monkshaven church o ' Sunday , and see Measter Fishburn 's daughter , as have their thing make I ' York , and notice a bit how they be make . we need not   do it I ' church , but just scan them o'er I ' t ' churchyard , and there will be no harm do . besides , there be to be this grand burryin ' o ' t ' man t ' press - gang shot , and ' t will be like kill two bird at once . ' 

 ' I should like to go , ' say Sylvia . ' I feel so sorry like for the poor sailor shoot down and kidnap just as they be come home , as we see'd them o ' Thursday last . I will ask mother if she will let I go . ' 

 ' ay , do . I know my mother will let I , if she do not   go hersen ; for it will be a sight to see and to speak on for many a long year , after what I have   heerd . and Miss Fishburns be sure to be theere , so I would just get Donkin to cut out cloak itsel ' , and keep back yer mind fra ' fixing o ' either cape or hood till Sunday 's turn'd . ' 

 ' will yo ' set I part o ' t ' way home ? ' say Sylvia , see the die daylight become more and more crimson through the blackening tree . 

 ' no ; I can not . a should like it well enough , but somehow , there be a deal o ' work to be do yet , for t ' hour slip through one 's finger so as there be no knowing . mind yo ' , then , o ' Sunday . A'll be at t ' stile one o'clock punctual ; and we will go slowly into t ' town , and look about we as we go , and see folk 's dress ; and go to t ' church , and say wer prayer , and come out and have a look at t ' funeral . ' 

 and with this programme of proceeding settle for the following Sunday , the girl whom neighbourhood and parity of age have force into some measure of friendship part for the time . 

 Sylvia hasten home , feel as if she have be absent long ; her mother stand on the little knoll at the side of the house watch for she , with her hand shade her eye from the low ray of the set sun : but as soon as she see her daughter in the distance , she return to her work , whatever that might be . she be not a woman of many word , or of much demonstration ; few observer would have guess how much she love her child ; but Sylvia , without any reasoning or observation , instinctively know that her mother 's heart be bind up in she . 

 her father and Donkin be go on much as when she have leave they ; talk and disputing , the one compel to be idle , the other stitch away as fast as he talk . they seem as if they have never miss Sylvia ; no more do her mother for that matter , for she be busy and absorb in her afternoon dairy - work to all appearance . but Sylvia have note the watch not three minute before , and many a time in she after life , when no one care much for her out - going and in - coming , the straight , upright figure of her mother , front the set sun , but search through its blind ray for a sight of her child , rise up like a sudden - see picture , the remembrance of which smite Sylvia to the heart with a sense of a lost blessing , not duly value while possess . 

 ' well , feyther , and how be a ' wi ' you ? ' ask Sylvia , go to the side of his chair , and lay her hand on his shoulder . 

 ' Eh ! harkee till this lass o ' mine . she think as because she be go galraverge , I maun ha ' miss she and be ail . why , lass , Donkin and I have have t ' most sensible talk a've   have this many a day . A've   gi'en he a vast o ' knowledge , and he be do I a power o ' good . please God , to - morrow a'll tak ' a start at walk , if t ' weather hold up . ' 

 ' ay ! ' say Donkin , with a touch of sarcasm in his voice ; ' feyther and I have settle many puzzle ; it be be a loss to Government as they hannot be here for profit by our wisdom . we have   do away wi ' taxis and press - gang , and many a plague , and beat t ' french I ' our own mind , that be to say . ' 

 ' it be a wonder t ' I as those Lunnon folk can not   see thing clear , ' say Daniel , all in good faith . 

 Sylvia do not quite understand the state of thing as regarded politic and taxis and politic and taxis be all one in her mind , it must be confess but she see that her innocent little scheme of give her father the change of society afford by Donkin 's coming have answer ; and in the gladness of her heart she go out and run round the corner of the house to find Kester , and obtain from he that sympathy in her success which she dare not ask from her mother . 

 ' Kester , Kester , lad ! ' say she , in a loud whisper ; but Kester be suppere the horse , and in the clamp of their foot on the round stable pavement , he do not hear she at first . she go a little far into the stable . ' Kester ! he be a vast well , he will go out to - morrow ; it be all Donkin 's do . I be beholden to thee for fetch he , and I will try and spare thee waistcoat front out o ' t ' stuff for my new red cloak . Thou'll like that , Kester , will not   ta ? ' 

 Kester take the notion in slowly , and weigh it . 

 ' na , lass , ' say he , deliberately , after a pause . ' a could na ' bear to see thee wi ' thy cloak scrimpit . a like t ' see a wench look bonny and smart , an ' a tak ' a kind o ' pride in thee , an should be a'most as much hurt I ' my mind to see thee I ' a pinched cloak as if old Moll 's tail here be dock too short . na , lass , a'se niver get a mirroring glass for t ' see mysen in , so what be waistcoat to I ? keep thy stuff to thysen , theere be a good wench ; but a'se main and glad about t ' measter . place be not   like itsen when he be shut up and cranky . ' 

 he take up a wisp of straw and begin rub down the old mare , and hiss over his work as if he wish to consider the conversation as end . and Sylvia , who have string herself up in a momentary fervour of gratitude to make the generous offer , be not sorry to have it refuse , and go back plan what kindness she could show to Kester without its involve so much sacrifice to herself . for give waistcoat front to he would deprive she of the pleasant power of select a fashionable pattern in Monkshaven churchyard next Sunday . 

 that wish - for day seem long a - come , as wish - for day most frequently do . her father get well by slow degree , and her mother be pleased by the tailor 's good piece of work ; show the neatly - place patch with as much pride as many matron take in new clothe now - a - day . and the weather clear up into a dim kind of autumnal fineness , into anything but an indian summer as far as regard gorgeousness of colour , for on that coast the mist and sea fog early spoil the brilliancy of the foliage . yet , perhaps , the more do the silvery gray and brown of the inland scenery conduce to the tranquillity of the time , the time of peace and rest before the fierce and stormy winter come on . it seem a time for gather up human force to encounter the come severity , as well as of store up the produce of harvest for the need of winter . old people turn out and sun themselves in that calm St. Martin 's summer , without fear of ' the heat o ' th ' sun , or the come winter 's rage , ' and we may read in their pensive , dreamy eye that they be wean themselves away from the earth , which probably many may never see dressed in her summer glory again . 

 many such old people set out betime , on the Sunday afternoon to which Sylvia have be so look forward , to scale the long flight of stone step wear by the foot of many generation which lead up to the parish church , place on a height above the town , on a great green area at the summit of the cliff , which be the angle where the river and the sea meet , and so overlook both the busy crowded little town , the port , the shipping , and the bar on the one hand , and the wide illimitable tranquil sea on the other type of life and eternity . it be a good situation for that church . Homeward - bind sailor catch sight of the tower of St Nicholas , the first land object of all . they who go forth upon the great deep might carry solemn thought with they of the word they have hear there ; not conscious thought , perhaps rather a distinct if dim conviction that buy and selling , eat and marrying , even life and death , be not all the reality in existence . nor be the word that come up to their remembrance word of sermon preach there , however impressive . the sailor mostly sleep through the sermon ; unless , indeed , there be incident such as be involve in what be call ' funeral discourse ' to be narrate . they do not recognize their daily fault or temptation under the grand alias befit their appearance from a preacher 's mouth . but they know the old , oft - repeat word pray for deliverance from the familiar danger of lightning and tempest ; from battle , murder , and sudden death ; and nearly every man be aware that he leave behind he some one who would watch for the prayer for the preservation of those who travel by land or by water , and think of he , as God - protect the more for the earnestness of the response then give . 

 there , too , lie the dead of many generation ; for St. Nicholas have be the parish church ever since Monkshaven be a town , and the large churchyard be rich in the dead . master , mariner , ship - owner , seaman : it seem strange how few other trade be represent in that great plain so full of upright gravestone . here and there be a memorial stone , place by some survivor of a large family , most of whom perish at sea : ' suppose to have perish in the Greenland sea , ' ' shipwreck in the Baltic , ' ' drown off the coast of Iceland . ' there be a strange sensation , as if the cold sea - wind must bring with they the dim phantom of those lose sailor , who have die far from their home , and from the hallowed ground where their father lay . 

 each flight of step up to this churchyard end in a small flat space , on which a wooden seat be place . on this particular Sunday , all these seat be fill by aged people , breathless with the unusual exertion of climbing . you could see the church stair , as it be call , from nearly every part of the town , and the figure of the numerous climber , diminish by distance , look like a busy ant - hill , long before the bell begin to ring for afternoon service . all who could manage it have put on a bit of black in token of mourning ; it might be very little ; an old ribbon , a rusty piece of crape ; but some sign of mourning be show by every one down to the little child in its mother 's arm , that innocently clutch the piece of rosemary to be throw into the grave ' for remembrance . ' Darley , the seaman shoot by the press - gang , nine league off St. Abb 's Head , be to be bury to - day , at the accustomed time for the funeral of the poor class , directly after evening service , and there be only the sick and their nurse - tender who do not come forth to show their feeling for the man whom they look upon as murder . the crowd of vessel in harbour bear their flag half - mast high ; and the crew be make their way through the High Street . the gentlefolk of Monkshaven , full of indignation at this interference with their ship , full of sympathy with the family who have lose their son and brother almost within sight of his home , come in unusual number no lack of pattern for Sylvia ; but her thought be far otherwise and more suitably occupy . the unwonted sternness and solemnity visible on the countenance of all whom she meet awed and affect she . she do not speak in reply to Molly 's remark on the dress or appearance of those who strike she . she feel as if these speech jar on she , and annoy she almost to irritation ; yet molly have come all the way to Monkshaven Church in her service , and deserve forbearance accordingly . the two mount the step alongside of many people ; few word be exchange , even at the breathing place , so often the little centre of gossip . look over the sea there be not a sail to be see ; it seem bared of life , as if to be in serious harmony with what be go on inland . 

 the church be of old Norman architecture ; low and massive outside : inside , of vast space , only a quarter of which be fill on ordinary Sundays . the wall be disfigure by numerous tablet of black and white marble intermix , and the usual ornamentation of that style of memorial as erect in the last century , of weep willow , urn , and droop figure , with here and there a ship in full sail , or an anchor , where the seafaring idea prevalent through the place have launch out into a little originality . there be no wood - work , the church have be strip of that , most probably when the neighbouring monastery have be destroy . there be large square pew , line with green baize , with the name of the family of the most flourishing ship - owner paint white on the door ; there be pew , not so large , and not line at all , for the farmer and shopkeeper of the parish ; and numerous heavy oaken bench which , by the united effort of several man , might be bring within earshot of the pulpit . these be be remove into the most convenient situation when molly and Sylvia enter the church , and after two or three whisper sentence they take their seat on one of these . 

 the vicar of Monkshaven be a kindly , peaceable old man , hat strife and troubled water above everything . he be a vehement Tory in theory , as become his cloth in those day . he have two bugbear to fear the French and the Dissenters . it be difficult to say of which he have the bad opinion and the most intense dread . perhaps he hat the dissenter most , because they come near in contact with he than the French ; besides , the French have the excuse of be papist , while the dissenter might have belong to the Church of England if they have not be utterly deprave . yet in practice Dr Wilson do not object to dine with Mr. Fishburn , who be a personal friend and follower of Wesley , but then , as the doctor would say , ' Wesley be an Oxford man , and that make he a gentleman ; and he be an ordain minister of the Church of England , so that grace can never depart from he . ' but I do not know what excuse he would have allege for send broth and vegetable to old Ralph Thompson , a rabid Independent , who have be give to abuse the Church and the vicar , from a dissenting pulpit , as long as ever he could mount the stair . however , that inconsistency between Dr Wilson 's theory and practice be not generally know in Monkshaven , so we have nothing to do with it . 

 Dr Wilson have have a very difficult part to play , and a still more difficult sermon to write , during this last week . the Darley who have be kill be the son of the vicar 's gardener , and Dr Wilson 's sympathy as a man have be all on the bereaved father 's side . but then he have receive , as the old magistrate in the neighbourhood , a letter from the captain of the Aurora , explanatory and exculpatory . Darley have be resist the order of an officer in his Majesty 's service . what would become of due subordination and loyalty , and the interest of the service , and the chance of beat those confound French , if such conduct as Darley 's be to be encourage ?   Poor Darley ! he be past all evil effect of human encouragement now ! 

 so the vicar mumble hastily over a sermon on the text , ' in the midst of life we be in death ' ; which might have do as well for a baby cut off in a convulsion - fit as for the strong man shoot down with all his eager blood hot within he , by man as hot - blooded as himself . but once when the old doctor 's eye catch the up - turn , strain gaze of the father Darley , seek with all his soul to find a grain of holy comfort in the chaff of word , his conscience smite he . have he nothing to say that should calm anger and revenge with spiritual power ? no breath of the comforter to soothe repine into resignation ? but again the discord between the law of man and the law of Christ stand before he ; and he give up the attempt to do more than he be do , as beyond his power . though the hearer go away as full of anger as they have enter the church , and some with a dull feeling of disappointment as to what they have get there , yet no one feel anything but kindly towards the old vicar . his simple , happy life lead amongst they for forty year , and open to all man in its daily course ; his sweet - temper , cordial way ; his practical kindness , make he belove by all ; and neither he nor they think much or care much for admiration of his talent . respect for his office be all the respect he think of ; and that be concede to he from old traditional and hereditary association . in look back to the last century , it appear curious to see how little our ancestor have the power of put two thing together , and perceive either the discord or harmony thus produce . be it because we be far off from those time , and have , consequently , a great range of vision ? will our descendant have a wonder about we , such as we have about the inconsistency of our forefather , or a surprise at our blindness that we do not perceive that , hold such and such opinion , our course of action must be so and so , or that the logical consequence of particular opinion must be conviction which at present we hold in abhorrence ? it seem puzzle to look back on man such as our vicar , who almost hold the doctrine that the King could do no wrong , yet be ever ready to talk of the glorious Revolution , and to abuse the Stuarts for have entertain the same doctrine , and try to put it in practice . but such discrepancy run through good man 's life in those day . it be well for we that we live at the present time , when everybody be logical and consistent . this little discussion must be take in place of Dr Wilson 's sermon , of which no one could remember more than the text half an hour after it be deliver . even the doctor himself have the recollection of the word he have utter sweep out of his mind , as , have doff his gown and don his surplice , he come out of the dusk of his vestry and go to the church - door , look into the broad light which come upon the plain of the church - yard on the cliff ; for the sun have not yet set , and the pale moon be slowly rise through the silvery mist that obscure the distant moor . there be a thick , dense crowd , all still and silent , look away from the church and the vicar , who await the bringing of the dead . they be watch the slow black line wind up the long step , rest their heavy burden here and there , stand in silent group at each landing - place ; now lose to sight as a piece of broken , overhang ground intervene , now emerge suddenly near ; and overhead the great church bell , with its mediaeval inscription , familiar to the vicar , if to no one else who hear it , I to the grave do summon all , keep on its heavy booming monotone , with which no other sound from land or sea , near or distant , intermingle , except the cackle of the goose on some far - away farm on the moor , as they be come home to roost ; and that one noise from so great a distance seem only to deepen the stillness . then there be a little movement in the crowd ; a little push from side to side , to make a path for the corpse and its bearer an aggregate of the fragment of room . 

 with bent head and spend strength , those who carry the coffin move on ; behind come the poor old gardener , a brown - black funeral cloak throw over his homely dress , and support his wife with step scarcely less feeble than her own . he have come to church that afternoon , with a promise to she that he would return to lead she to the funeral of her firstborn ; for he feel , in his sore perplexed heart , full of indignation and dumb anger , as if he must go and hear something which should exorcize the unwonted longing for revenge that disturb his grief , and make he conscious of that great blank of consolation which faithfulness produce . and for the time he be faithless . how come God to permit such cruel injustice of man ? permit it , he could not be good . then what be life , and what be death , but woe and despair ? the beautiful solemn word of the ritual have do he good , and restore much of his faith . though he could not understand why such sorrow have befall he any more than before , he have come back to something of his childlike trust ; he keep say to himself in a whisper , as he mount the weary step , ' it be the Lord 's doing ' ; and the repetition soothe he unspeakably . behind this old couple follow their child , grow man and woman , come from distant place or farmhouse service ; the servant at the vicarage , and many a neighbour , anxious to show their sympathy , and most of the sailor from the crew of the vessel in port , join in procession , and follow the dead body into the church . 

 there be too great a crowd immediately within the door for Sylvia and Molly to go in again , and they accordingly betook themselves to the place where the deep grave be wait , wide and hungry , to receive its dead . there , lean against the headstone all around , be many stand look over the broad and placid sea , and turn to the soft salt air which blow on their hot eye and rigid face ; for no one speak of all that number . they be think of the violent death of he over whom the solemn word be now be say in the gray old church , scarcely out of their hearing , have not the sound be break by the measured lapping of the tide far beneath . 

 suddenly every one look round towards the path from the churchyard step . two sailor be support a ghastly figure that , with feeble motion , be draw near the open grave . 

 ' it be t ' specksioneer as try to save he ! it be he as be leave for dead ! ' the people murmur round . 

 ' it be Charley Kinraid , as I be a sinner ! ' say Molly , start forward to greet her cousin . 

 but as he come on , she see that all his strength be need for the mere action of walk . the sailor , in their strong sympathy , have yield to his earn entreaty , and carry he up the step , in order that he might see the last of his messmate . they place he near the grave , rest against a stone ; and he be hardly there before the vicar come forth , and the great crowd pour out of the church , follow the body to the grave . 

 Sylvia be so much wrapt up in the solemnity of the occasion , that she have no thought to spare at the first moment for the pale and haggard figure opposite ; much less be she aware of her cousin Philip , who now single she out for the first time from among the crowd , press to her side , with an intention of companionship and protection . 

 as the service go on , ill - check sob rise from behind the two girl , who be among the foremost in the crowd , and by - and - by the cry and the wail become general . Sylvia 's tear rain down her face , and her distress become so evident that it attract the attention of many in that inner circle . among other who notice it , the specksioneer 's hollow eye be catch by the sight of the innocent bloom childlike face opposite to he , and he wonder if she be a relation ; yet , see that she bear no badge of mourning , he rather conclude that she must have be a sweetheart of the dead man . 

 and now all be over : the rattle of the gravel on the coffin ; the last long , linger look of friend and lover ; the rosemary sprig have be cast down by all who be fortunate enough to have bring they and oh ! how much Sylvia wish she have remember this last act of respect and slowly the outer rim of the crowd begin to slacken and disappear . 

 now Philip speak to Sylvia . 

 ' I never dream of see you here . I think my aunt always go to Kirk Moorside . ' 

 ' I come with Molly Corney , ' say Sylvia . ' Mother be stay at home with feyther . ' 

 ' how be his rheumatic ? ' ask Philip . 

 but at the same moment molly take hold of Sylvia 's hand , and say 

 ' a want t ' get round and speak to Charley . Mother will be main and glad to hear as he be getten out ; though , for sure , he look as though he would ha ' be well in 's bed . Come , Sylvia . ' 

 and Philip , fain to keep with Sylvia , have to follow the two girl close up to the specksioneer , who be prepare for his slow laborious walk back to his lodging . he stop on see his cousin . 

 ' well , Molly , ' say he , faintly , put out his hand , but his eye pass her face to look at Sylvia in the background , her tear - stain face full of shy admiration of the near approach to a hero she have ever see . 

 ' well , Charley , a niver be so take aback as when a saw yo ' theere , like a ghost , a - standin ' agin a gravestone . how white and wan yo ' do look ! ' 

 ' ay ! ' say he , wearily , ' wan and weak enough . ' 

 ' but I hope you be get well , sir , ' say Sylvia , in a low voice , long to speak to he , and yet wonder at her own temerity . 

 ' thank you , my lass . I be o'er th ' bad . ' 

 he sigh heavily . 

 Philip now speak . 

 ' we be do he no kindness a - keep he stand here I ' t ' night - fall , and he so tired . ' and he make as though he would turn away . Kinraid 's two sailor friend back up Philip 's word with such urgency , that , somehow , Sylvia think they have be to blame in speak to he , and blush excessively with the idea . 

 ' Yo'll come and be nurse at Moss Brow , Charley , ' say molly ; and Sylvia drop her little maidenly curtsey , and say , ' good - by ; ' and go away , wonder how molly could talk so freely to such a hero ; but then , to be sure , he be a cousin , and probably a sweetheart , and that would make a great deal of difference , of course . 

 meanwhile her own cousin keep close by her side . 




 CHAPTER VII 

 TETE - a - tete . the will 

 ' and now tell I all about th ' folk at home ? ' say Philip , evidently prepare to walk back with the girl . he generally come to Haytersbank every Sunday afternoon , so Sylvia know what she have to expect the moment she become aware of his neighbourhood in the churchyard . 

 ' my feyther be be sadly trouble with his rheumatic this week past ; but he be a vast well now , thank you kindly . ' then , address herself to Molly , she ask , ' have your cousin a doctor to look after he ? ' 

 ' ay , for sure ! ' say Molly , quickly ; for though she know nothing about the matter , she be determined to suppose that her cousin have everything become an invalid as well as a hero . ' he be well - to - do , and can afford everything as he need , ' continue she . ' his feyther 's leave he money , and he be a farmer out up in Northumberland , and he be reckon such a specksioneer as never , never be , and get what wage he ask for and a share on every whale he harpoon beside . ' 

 ' I reckon he will have to make himself scarce on this coast for awhile , at any rate , ' say Philip . 

 ' An ' what for should he ? ' ask Molly , who never like Philip at the good of time , and now , if he be go to disparage her cousin in any way , be ready to take up arm and do battle . 

 ' why , they do say as he fire the shot as have kill some o ' the men - o'-war 's man , and , of course , if he have , he will have to stand his trial if he be catch . ' 

 ' what lie people do say ! ' exclaim Molly . ' he niver kill nought but whale , a'll be bind ; or , if he do , it be all right and proper as he should , when they be for steal he an ' all t ' other , and do kill poor Darley as we come fra ' seeming bury . a suppose , now yo're such a Quaker that , if some one be to break through fra ' t ' other side o ' this dyke and offer for to murder Sylvia and I , yo'd look on wi ' yo'r hand hang by yo'r side . ' 

 ' but t ' press - gang have law on their side , and be do nought but what they would warrant for . ' 

 ' Th ' tender 's go away , as if she be ashamed o ' what she have do , ' say Sylvia , ' and t ' flag 's down fra ' o'er the Randyvowse . there will be no more press - ganging here awhile . ' 

 ' no ; feyther say , ' continue Molly , ' as they have   make t ' place too hot t ' hold them , come so strong afore people have getten use to their way o ' catchin ' up poor lad just come fra ' t ' Greenland sea . t ' folk ha ' their blood so up they would think no harm o ' fight them I ' t ' street ay , and o ' kill them , too , if they be for use fire - arm , as t ' Aurora 's man do . ' 

 ' woman be so fond o ' bloodshed , ' say Philip ; ' for t ' hear you talk , who would ha ' think you would just come fra ' crying ower the grave of a man who be kill by violence ? I should ha ' think you would see enough of what sorrow come o ' fighting . why , they lad o ' t ' Aurora as they say Kinraid shoot down have father and mother , maybe , a look out for they to come home . ' 

 ' I do not   think he could ha ' kill they , ' say Sylvia ; ' he look so gentle . ' 

 but Molly do not like this half - and - half view of the case . 

 ' a dare say he do kill them dead ; he be not one to do thing by half . and a think he serve them reet , that be what a do . ' 

 ' be na ' this Hester , as serf in Foster 's shop ? ' ask Sylvia , in a low voice , as a young woman come through a stile in the stone wall by the roadside , and suddenly appear before they . 

 ' yes , ' say Philip . ' why , Hester , where have you be ? ' he ask , as they draw near . 

 hester redden a little , and then reply , in her slow , quiet way 

 ' I have   be sit with Betsy Darley she that be bed - ridden . it be lonesome for she when the other be away at the burying . ' 

 and she make as though she would have pass ; but Sylvia , all her sympathy alive for the relation of the murder man , want to ask more question , and put her hand on Hester 's arm to detain she a moment . hester suddenly draw back a little , redden still more , and then reply fully and quietly to all Sylvia ask . 

 in the agricultural county , and among the class to which these four person belong , there be little analysis of motive or comparison of character and action , even at this present day of enlightenment . sixty or seventy year ago there be still less . I do not mean that amongst thoughtful and serious people there be not much reading of such book as Mason on Self - Knowledge and Law 's serious Call , or that there be not the experience of the Wesleyans , that be relate at class - meeting for the edification of the hearer . but , take as a general ride , it may be say that few know what manner of man they be , compare to the number now who be fully conscious of their virtue , quality , failing , and weakness , and who go about compare other with themselves not in a spirit of Pharisaism and arrogance , but with a vivid self - consciousness that more than anything else deprive character of freshness and originality . 

 to return to the party we leave stand on the high - raise footway that run alongside of the bridle - road to Haytersbank . Sylvia have leisure in her heart to think ' how good hester be for sit with the poor bed - ride sister of Darley ! ' without have a pang of self - depreciation in the comparison of her own conduct with that she be capable of so fully appreciate . she have go to church for the end of vanity , and remain to the funeral for curiosity and the pleasure of the excitement . in this way a modern young lady would have condemn herself , and therefore lose the simple , purify pleasure of admiration of another . 

 hester pass onwards , go down the hill towards the town . the other three walk slowly on . all be silent for a few moment , then Sylvia say 

 ' how good she be ! ' 

 and Philip reply with ready warmth , 

 ' yes , she be ; no one know how good but we , who live in the same house wi ' she . ' 

 ' her mother be an old Quakeress , bean't   she ? ' molly inquire . 

 ' Alice Rose be a Friend , if that be what you mean , ' say Philip . 

 ' well , well ! some folk 's so particular . be William Coulson a Quaker , by which a mean a friend ? ' 

 ' yes ; they be all on them right - down good folk . ' 

 ' deary I ! what a wonder yo ' can speak to such sinner as Sylvia and I , after keepin ' company with so much goodness , ' say Molly , who have not yet forgive Philip for doubt Kinraid 's power of kill man . ' be na ' it , Sylvia ? ' 

 but Sylvia be too highly strung for banter . if she have not be one of those who go to mock , but remain to pray , she have go to church with the thought of the cloak - that - be - to - be uppermost in her mind , and she have come down the long church stair with life and death suddenly become real to her mind , the endure sea and hill form a contrast background to the vanishing away of man . she be full of a solemn wonder as to the abide - place of the soul of the dead , and a childlike dread lest the number of the elect should be accomplish before she be include therein . how people could ever be merry again after they have be at a funeral , she could not imagine ; so she answer gravely , and slightly beside the question : 

 ' I wonder if I be a friend if I should be good ? ' 

 ' Gi ' I your red cloak , that be all , when yo ' turn Quaker ; they will none let thee wear scarlet , so it will be of no use t ' thee . ' 

 ' I think thou'rt good enough as thou art , ' say Philip , tenderly at least as tenderly as he durst , for he know by experience that it do not do to alarm her girlish coyness . either one speech or the other make Sylvia silent ; neither be accordant to her mood of mind ; so perhaps both contribute to her quietness . 

 ' folk say William Coulson look sweet on Hester Rose , ' say Molly , always up in monkshaven gossip . it be in the form of an assertion , but be say in the tone of a question , and as such Philip reply to it . 

 ' yes , I think he like she a good deal ; but he be so quiet , I never feel sure . John and Jeremiah would like the match , I have   a notion . ' 

 and now they come to the stile which have fill Philip 's eye for some minute past , though neither of the other have perceive they be so near it ; the stile which lead to Moss Brow from the road into the field that slop down to Haystersbank . here they would leave Molly , and now would begin the delicious tete - a - tete walk , which Philip always try to make as linger as possible . to - day he be anxious to show his sympathy with Sylvia , as far as he could read what be pass in her mind ; but how be he to guess the multitude of tangle thought in that unseen receptacle ? a resolution to be good , if she could , and always to be think on death , so that what seem to she now as simply impossible , might come true that she might ' dread the grave as little as her bed ' ; a wish that Philip be not come home with she ; a wonder if the specksioneer really have kill a man , an idea which make her shudder ; yet from the awful fascination about it , her imagination be compel to dwell on the tall , gaunt figure , and try to recall the wan countenance ; a hatred and desire of revenge on the press - gang , so vehement that it sadly militate against her intention of try to be good ; all these notion , and wonder , and fancy , be whirl about in Sylvia 's brain , and at one of their prompting she speak , 

 ' how many mile away be t ' Greenland sea ? I mean , how long do they take to reach ? ' 

 ' I do not   know ; ten day or a fortnight , or more , maybe . I will ask . ' 

 ' oh ! feyther will tell I all about it . he be be there many a time . ' 

 ' I say , Sylvie ! my aunt say I be to give you lesson this winter I ' write and cipher . I can begin to come up now , two evening , maybe , a week . t ' shop close early after November come in . ' 

 Sylvia do not like learn , and do not want he for her teacher ; so she answer in a dry little tone , 

 ' it will use a deal o ' candle - light ; mother will not like that . I can not   see to spell wi'out a candle close at my elbow . ' 

 ' niver mind about candle . I can bring up a candle wi ' me , for I should be burn one at Alice Rose 's . ' 

 so that excuse would not do . Sylvia beat her brain for another . 

 ' write cramp my hand so , I can not   do any sewing for a day after ; and feyther want his shirt very bad . ' 

 ' but , Sylvia , I will teach you geography , and ever such a vast o ' fine thing about t ' country , on t ' map . ' 

 ' be t ' Arctic sea down on t ' map ? ' she ask , in a tone of great interest . 

 ' yes ! Arctics , and tropic , and equator , and equinoctial line ; we will take them turn and turn about ; we will do write and cipher one night , and geography t ' other . ' 

 Philip speak with pleasure at the prospect , but Sylvia relax into indifference . 

 ' I be no scholard ; it be like throw away labour to teach I , I be such a dunce at my book . now there be Betsy Corney , third girl , she as be young than Molly , she would be a credit to you . there niver be such a lass for potter ower book . ' 

 if Philip have have his wit about he , he would have pretend to listen to this proposition of a change of pupil , and then possibly Sylvia might have repent make it . but he be too much mortified to be diplomatic . 

 ' my aunt ask I to teach you a bit , not any neighbour 's lass . ' 

 ' well ! if I mun be teach , I mun ; but I would rayther be whip and ha ' do with it , ' be Sylvia 's ungracious reply . 

 a moment afterwards , she repent of her little spirit of unkindness , and think that she should not like to die that night without make friend . sudden death be very present in her thought since the funeral . so she instinctively choose the good method of make friend again , and slip her hand into his , as he walk a little sullenly at her side . she be half afraid , however , when she find it firmly hold , and that she could not draw it away again without make what she call in her own mind a ' fuss . ' so , hand in hand , they slowly and silently come up to the door of Haytersbank Farm ; not unseen by Bell Robson , who sate in the window - seat , with her Bible open upon her knee . she have read her chapter aloud to herself , and now she could see no long , even if she have wish to read more ; but she gaze out into the darken air , and a dim look of contentment come like moonshine over her face when she see the cousin approach . 

 ' that be my prayer day and night , ' say she to herself . 

 but there be no unusual aspect of gladness on her face , as she light the candle to give they a more cheerful welcome . 

 ' Wheere 's feyther ? ' say Sylvia , look round the room for Daniel . 

 ' he be be to Kirk Moorside Church , for t ' see a bit o ' th ' world , as he can be it . and sin ' then he be go out to th ' cattle ; for Kester 's ta'en his turn of play hissel ' , now that father 's well . ' 

 ' I have   be talk to Sylvia , ' say Philip , his head still full of his pleasant plan , his hand still tingle from the touch of her , ' about turn schoolmaster , and come up here two night a week for t ' teach she a bit o ' writing and cipher . ' 

 ' and geography , ' put in Sylvia ; ' for , ' think she , ' if I be to learn they thing I do not   care a pin about , anyhow I will learn what I do care to know , if it will tell I about t ' Greenland sea , and how far they be off . ' 

 that same evening , a trio alike in many outward circumstance sate in a small neat room in a house opening out of a confine court on the hilly side of the High Street of Monkshaven a mother , her only child , and the young man who silently love that daughter , and be favour by Alice Rose , though not by hester . 

 when the latter return from her afternoon 's absence , she stand for a minute or two on the little flight of steep step , whiten to a snowy whiteness ; the aspect of the whole house partook of the same character of irreproachable cleanliness . it be wedge up into a space which necessitate all sort of odd projection and irregularity in order to obtain sufficient light for the interior ; and if ever the being situate in a dusky , confine corner might have be make an excuse for dirt , Alice Rose 's house have that apology . yet the small diamond pane of glass in the casement window be keep so bright and clear that a great sweet - scented - leave geranium grow and flourish , though it do not flower profusely . the leaf seem to fill the air with fragrance as soon as Hester summon up energy enough to open the door . perhaps that be because the young Quaker , William Coulson , be crush one between his finger and thumb , while wait to set down Alice 's next word . for the old woman , who look as if many year of life remain in she yet , be solemnly dictate she last will and testament . 

 it have be on her mind for many month ; for she have something to leave beyond the mere furniture of the house . something a few pound in the hand of John and Jeremiah Foster , her cousin : and it be they who have suggest the duty on which she be engage . she have ask William Coulson to write down her wish , and he have consent , though with some fear and trepidation ; for he have an idea that he be infringe on a lawyer 's prerogative , and that , for aught he know , he might be prosecute for make a will without a licence , just as a man might be punish for sell wine and spirit without go through the preliminary legal form that give permission for such a sale . but to his suggestion that Alice should employ a lawyer , she have reply 

 ' that would cost I five pound sterling ; and thee canst do it as well , if thee'll but attend to my word . ' 

 so he have buy , at her desire , a black - edge sheet of fine - wove paper , and a couple of good pen , on the previous Saturday ; and while wait for she to begin her dictation , and full serious thought himself , he have almost unconsciously make the grand flourish at the top of the paper which he have learn at school , and which be there call a spread - eagle . 

 ' what art thee do there ? ' ask Alice , suddenly alive to his proceeding . 

 without a word he show she his handiwork . 

 ' it be a vanity , ' say she , ' and ' t may make t ' will not stand . folk may think I be na in my right mind , if they see such fly - leg and cob - webs a - top . write , " this be my doing , William Coulson , and none of Alice Rose 's , she be in her sound mind . " ' 

 ' I do not   think it be need , ' say William . nevertheless he write down the word . 

 ' Hast thee put that I be in my sound mind and seven sens ? then make the sign of the Trinity , and write , " in the name of the Father , the Son , and the Holy Ghost . " ' 

 ' be that the right way o ' begin a will ? ' say Coulson , a little startled .
