 ' my father , and my father 's father , and my husband have it a - top of theirs , and I be noane go for to cease fra ' following after they , for they be godly man , though my husband be o ' t ' episcopal persuasion . ' 

 ' it be do , ' say William . 

 ' Hast thee date it ? ' ask Alice . 

 ' Nay . ' 

 ' then date it third day , ninth month . now , art ready ? ' 

 Coulson nod . 

 ' i , Alice Rose , do leave my furniture   that be , my bed and chest o ' drawer , for thy bed and thing be thine , and not mine , and settle , and saucepan , and dresser , and table , and kettle , and all the rest of my furniture , to my lawful and only daughter , Hester Rose . I think that be safe for she to have all , be ' t not , William ? ' 

 ' I think so , too , ' say he , write on all the time . 

 ' and thee shalt have t ' roller and paste - board , because thee be so fond o ' pudding and cake . it will serve thy wife after I be go , and I trust she will boil her paste long enough , for that be be t ' secret o ' mine , and thee'll noane be so easy t ' please . ' 

 ' I din't   reckon on marriage , ' say William . 

 ' Thee'll marry , ' say Alice . ' thee like to have thy victual hot and comfortable ; and there be noane many but a wife as'll look after that for t ' please thee . ' 

 ' I know who could please I , ' sigh forth William , ' but I can not   please she . ' 

 Alice look sharply at he from over her spectacle , which she have put on the well to think about the disposal of her property . 

 ' Thee art think on our hester , ' say she , plainly out . 

 he start a little , but look up at she and meet her eye . 

 ' hester care noane for I , ' say he , dejectedly . 

 ' bide a while , my lad , ' say Alice , kindly . ' young woman do not   always know their own mind . Thee and she would make a marriage after my own heart ; and the Lord have be very good to I hitherto , and I think he will bring it t ' pass . but do not   thee let on as thee care for her so much . I sometimes think she weary o ' thy look and thy way . show up thy manly heart , and make as though thee have much else to think on , and no leisure for to dawdle after she , and she will think a deal more on thee . and now mend thy pen for a fresh start . I give and bequeath do thee put " give and bequeath , " at th ' beginning ? ' 

 ' Nay , ' say William , look back . ' thee didst not tell I " give and bequeath ! " ' 

 ' then it will not   be legal , and my bit o ' furniture will be take to London , and put into chancery , and hester will have noane on it . ' 

 ' I can write it over , ' say William . 

 ' well , write it clear then , and put a line under it to show those be my special word . Hast thee do it ? then now start afresh . I give and bequeath my book o ' sermon , as be bind in good calfskin , and lie on the third shelf o ' corner cupboard at the right hand o ' t ' fire - place , to Philip Hepburn ; for I reckon he be as fond o ' reading sermon as thee art o ' light , well - boil paste , and I would be glad for each on ye to have somewhat ye like for to remember I by . be that down ? there ; now for my cousin John and Jeremiah . they be rich I ' world 's gear , but they will prize what I leave them if I could only onbethink I what they would like . Hearken ! be na ' that our hester 's step ? put it away , quick ! I be noane for grieve her wi ' tell she what I have   be about . we will take a turn at t ' will next First Day ; it will serve we for several Sabbaths to come , and maybe I can think on something as will suit cousin John and cousin Jeremiah afore then . ' 

 hester , as be mention , pause a minute or two before lift the latch of the door . when she enter there be no unusual sign of write about ; only Will Coulson look very red , and crush and smell at the geranium leaf . 

 hester come in briskly , with the little stock of enforce cheerfulness she have stop at the door to acquire . but it fade away along with the faint flush of colour in her cheek ; and the mother 's quick eye immediately note the wan heavy look of care . 

 ' I have keep t ' pot in t ' oven ; it will have a'most get a ' t ' goodness out of t ' tea by now , for it will be an hour since I make it . poor lass , thou look'st as if thou need a good cup o ' tea . it be dree work sit wi ' Betsy Darley , be it ? and how do she look on her affliction ? ' 

 ' she take it sore to heart , ' say Hester , take off her hat , and fold and smooth away her cloak , before put they in the great oak chest   or ' ark , ' as it be call , in which they be lay from Sunday to Sunday . 

 as she open the lid a sweet scent of dry lavender and rose - leaf come out . William step hastily forwards to hold up the heavy lid for she . she lift up her head , look at he full with her serene eye , and thank he for his little service . then she take a creepie - stool and sate down on the side of the fire - place , have she back to the window . 

 the hearth be of the same spotless whiteness as the step ; all that be black about the grate be polished to the utmost extent ; all that be of brass , like the handle of the oven , be burnish bright . her mother place the little black earthenware teapot , in which the tea have be stew , on the table , where cup and saucer be already set for four , and a large plate of bread and butter cut . then they sate round the table , bow their head , and keep silence for a minute or two . 

 when this grace be end , and they be about to begin , Alice say , as if without premeditation , but in reality with a keen shrinking of heart out of sympathy with her child 

 ' Philip would have be in to his tea by now , I reckon , if he have be come . ' 

 William look up suddenly at hester ; her mother carefully turn her head another way . but she answer quite quietly 

 ' he will be go to his aunt 's at Haytersbank . I meet he at t ' top o ' t ' Brow , with his cousin and Molly Corney . ' 

 ' he be a deal there , ' say William . 

 ' yes , ' say hester . ' it be likely ; he and his aunt come from Carlisle - way , and must need cle together in these strange part . ' 

 ' I see he at the burying of yon Darley , ' say William . 

 ' it be a vast o ' people go past th ' entry end , ' say Alice . ' it be a'most like election time ; I be just come back fra ' meeting when they be all go up th ' church step . I meet yon sailor as , they say , use violence and do murder ; he look like a ghost , though whether it be his bodily wound , or the sense of his sin stir within he , it be not for I to say . and by t ' time I be back here and settle to my Bible , t ' folk be return , and it be tramp , tramp , past th ' entry end for well nor a quarter of an hour . ' 

 ' they say Kinraid have getten slug and gun - shot in his side , ' say hester . 

 ' he be niver one Charley Kinraid , for sure , as I know at Newcastle , ' say William Coulson , rouse to sudden and energetic curiosity . 

 ' I do not   know , ' reply hester ; ' they call he just Kinraid ; and Betsy Darley say he be t ' most daring specksioneer of all that go off this coast to t ' Greenland sea . but he be be in Newcastle , for I mind I she say her poor brother meet with he there . ' 

 ' how didst thee come to know he ? ' inquire Alice . 

 ' I can not abide he if it be Charley , ' say William . ' he keep company with my poor sister as be dead for well nor two year , and then he leave off come to see she and go wi ' another girl , and it just break her heart . ' 

 ' he do not   look now as if he iver could play at that game again , ' say Alice ; ' he have have a warn fra ' the Lord . whether it be a call no one can tell . but to my eyne he look as if he have be call , and be go . ' 

 ' then he will meet my sister , ' say William , solemnly ; ' and I hope the Lord will make it clear to he , then , how he kill she , as sure as he shoot down yon sailor ; an ' if there be a gnash o ' tooth for murder I ' that other place , I reckon he will have his share on't . he be a bad man yon . ' 

 ' Betsy say he be such a friend to her brother as niver be ; and he be send her word and promise to go and see she , first place he go out to . 

 but William only shake his head , and repeat his last word , 

 ' he be a bad man , he be . ' 

 when Philip come home that Sunday night , he find only Alice up to receive he . the usual bedtime in the household be nine o'clock , and it be but ten minute past the hour ; but Alice look displeased and stern . 

 ' Thee art late , lad , ' say she , shortly . 

 ' I be sorry ; it be a long way from my uncle 's , and I think clock be different , ' say he , take out his watch to compare it with the round moon 's face that tell the time to Alice . 

 ' I know nought about thy uncle 's , but thee art late . take thy candle , and begone . ' 

 if Alice make any reply to Philip 's ' good - night , ' he do not hear it . 




 CHAPTER VIII 

 attraction and repulsion 

 a fortnight have pass over and winter be advance with rapid stride . in bleak northern farmstead there be much to be do before November weather should make the road too heavy for half - feed horse to pull cart through . there be the turf , par up on the distant moor , and leave out to dry , to be carry home and stack ; the brown fern be to be store up for winter bed for the cattle ; for straw be scarce and dear in those part ; even for thatch , heather   or rather ling   be use . then there be meat to salt while it could be have ; for , in default of turnip and mangold - wurzel , there be a great slaughtering of barren cow as soon as the summer herbage fail ; and good housewife store up their Christmas piece of beef in pickle before Martinmas be over . Corn be to be grind while yet it could be carry to the distant mill ; the great rack for oat - cake , that swing at the top of the kitchen , have to be fill . and last of all come the pig - killing , when the second frost set in . for up in the north there be an idea that the ice store in the first frost will melt , and the meat cure then taint ; the first frost be good for nothing but to be throw away , as they express it . 

 there come a breathing - time after this last event . the house have have its last autumn cleaning , and be neat and bright from top to bottom , from one end to another . the turf be lead ; the coal cart up from Monkshaven ; the wood store ; the corn ground ; the pig kill , and the ham and head and hand lie in salt . the butcher have be glad to take the good part of a pig of Dame Robson 's careful feeding ; but there be unusual plenty in the Haytersbank pantry ; and as Bell survey it one morning , she say to her husband 

 ' I wonder if yon poor sick chap at Moss Brow would fancy some o ' my sausage . they be something to crack on , for they be make fra ' an old Cumberland receipt , as be not know I ' yorkshire yet . ' 

 ' Thou 's allay so set upo ' Cumberland way ! ' say her husband , not displeased with the suggestion , however . ' still , when folk be sick they han their fancy , and maybe Kinraid will be glad o ' thy sausage . I ha ' know sick folk tak ' t ' eat snail . ' 

 this be not complimentary , perhaps . but Daniel go on to say that he do not mind if he step over with the sausage himself , when it be too late to do anything else . Sylvia long to offer to accompany her father ; but , somehow , she do not like to propose it . towards dusk she come to her mother to ask for the key of the great bureau that stand in the house - place as a state piece of furniture , although its use be to contain the family 's good wear apparel , and store of linen , such as might be suppose to be more need upstairs . 

 ' what for do yo ' want my key ? ' ask Bell . 

 ' only just to get out one of t ' damask napkin . ' 

 ' the good napkin , as my mother span ? ' 

 ' yes ! ' say Sylvia , her colour heightening . ' I think as how it would set off t ' sausage . ' 

 ' a good clean homespun cloth will serve they well , ' say Bell , wonder in her own mind what be come over the girl , to be think of set off sausage that be to be eat , not to be look at like a picture - book . she might have wonder still more , if she have see Sylvia steal round to the little flower border she have persuade Kester to make under the wall at the sunny side of the house , and gather the two or three Michaelmas daisy , and the one bud of the China rise , that , grow against the kitchen chimney , have escape the frost ; and then , when her mother be not look , softly open the cloth inside of the little basket that contain the sausage and a fresh egg or two , and lie her autumn blossom in one of the fold of the towel . 

 after Daniel , now pretty clear of his rheumatism , have have his afternoon meal   tea be a Sunday treat , he prepare to set out on his walk to Moss Brow ; but as he be take his stick he catch the look on Sylvia 's face ; and unconsciously interpret its dumb wistfulness . 

 ' Missus , ' say he , ' t ' wench have nought more t ' do , have she ? she may as well put on her cloak and step down wi ' I , and see Molly a bit ; she will be company like . ' 

 Bell consider . 

 ' there 's t ' yarn for thy stocking as be yet to spin ; but she can go , for I will do a bit at ' t mysel ' , and there be nought else agate . ' 

 ' put on thy thing in a jiffy , then , and let us be off , ' say Daniel . 

 and Sylvia do not need another word . down she come in a twinkling , dress in her new red cloak and hood , her face peep out of the fold of the latter , bright and blushing . 

 ' Thou should'st na ' ha ' put on thy new cloak for a night walk to Moss Brow , ' say Bell , shake her head . 

 ' shall I go take it off , and put on my shawl ? ' ask Sylvia , a little dolefully . 

 ' na , na , come along ! a'm noane go for t ' wait o ' woman 's chop and change . come along ; come , Lassie ! '   this last to his dog . 

 so Sylvia set off with a dancing heart and a dancing step , that have to be restrain to the sober gait her father choose . the sky above be bright and clear with the light of a thousand star , the grass be crisp under their foot with the come hoar frost ; and as they mount to the high ground they could see the dark sea stretch away far below they . the night be very still , though now and then crisp sound in the distant air sound very near in the silence . Sylvia carry the basket , and look like little Red Riding Hood . her father have nothing to say , and do not care to make himself agreeable ; but Sylvia enjoy her own thought , and any conversation would have be a disturbance to she . the long monotonous roll of the distant wave , as the tide bear they in , the multitudinous rush at last , and then the retreating rattle and trickle , as the baffle water fall back over the shingle that skirt the sand , and divide they from the cliff ; her father 's measure tread , and slow , even movement ; Lassie 's patter all lull Sylvia into a reverie , of which she could not have give herself any definite account . but at length they arrive at Moss Brow , and with a sudden sigh she quit the subject of her dreamy meditation , and follow her father into the great house - place . it have a more comfortable aspect by night than by day . the fire be always keep up to a wasteful size , and the dancing blaze and the partial light of candle leave much in shadow that be well ignore in such a disorderly family . but there be always a warm welcome to friend , however roughly give ; and after the word of this be speak , the next rise up equally naturally in the mind of Mrs Corney . 

 ' and what will ye tak ' ? eh ! but t ' measter will be fine and vex at your comin ' when he be away . he be off to Horncastle t ' sell some colt , and he will not be back till to - morrow 's neet . but here be Charley Kinraid as we have   getten to nurse up a bit , and ' t ' lad will be back fra ' Monkshaven in a crack o ' no time . ' 

 all this be address to Daniel , to whom she know that none but masculine company would be acceptable . amongst uneducated people whose range of subject and interest do not extend beyond their daily life it be natural that when the first blush and hurry of youth be over , there should be no great pleasure in the conversation of the other sex . man have plenty to say to man , which in their estimation   gain from tradition and experience   woman can not understand ; and farmer of a much later date than the one of which I be write , would have contemptuously consider it as a loss of time to talk to woman ; indeed , they be often more communicative to the sheep - dog that accompany they through all the day 's work , and frequently become a sort of dumb confidant . Farmer Robson 's Lassie now lie down at her master 's foot , place her nose between her paw , and watch with attentive eye the preparation go on for refreshment preparation which , to the disappointment of her canine heart , consist entirely of tumbler and sugar . 

 ' where 's t ' wench ? ' say Robson , after he have shake hand with Kinraid , and speak a few word to he and to Mrs. Corney . ' she be getten ' a basket wi ' sausage in them , as my missus have make , and she be a rare hand at sausage ; there be noane like she in a ' t ' three Ridings , I will be bind ! ' 

 for Daniel could praise his wife 's power in her absence , though he do not often express himself in an appreciative manner when she be by to hear . but Sylvia 's quick sense catch up the manner in which Mrs. Corney would apply the way in which her mother 's housewifery have be exalt , and step forwards out of the shadow , she say , 

 ' Mother think , maybe , you have not   kill a pig yet , and sausage be always a bit savoury for any one who be na ' well , and ' 

 she might have go on but that she catch Kinraid 's eye look at she with kindly admiration . she stop speak , and Mrs. Corney take up the word 

 ' as for sausage , I ha ' niver have a chance this year , else I stand again any one for t ' making of them . Yorkshire hams be a vast thought on , and I will niver let another county woman say as she can make well sausage nor I . but , as I be say , I would niver a chance ; for our pig , as I be sa fond on , and fed mysel ' , and as would ha ' be fourteen stone by now if he be an ounce , and as know I as well as any Christian , and a pig , as I may say , that I just idolize , go and take a fit a week after Michaelmas Day , and die , as if it have be to spite I ; and t ' next be na ' ready for killing , nor wunnot be this six week . so I be much beholden to your missus , and so be Charley , I be sure ; though he be ta'en a turn to betterin ' sin ' he come out here to be nurse . ' 

 ' I be a deal well , ' say Kinraid ; ' a'most ready for t ' press - gang to give chase to again . ' 

 ' but folk say they be go off this coast for one while , ' add Daniel . 

 ' they be go down towards Hull , as I have   be tell , ' say Kinraid . ' but they be a deep set , they will be here before we know where we be , some of these day . ' 

 ' see thee here ! ' say Daniel , exhibit his maimed hand ; ' a reckon a serve them out time o ' t ' Ameriky war . ' and he begin the story Sylvia know so well ; for her father never make a new acquaintance but what he tell he of his self - mutilation to escape the press - gang . it have be do , as he would himself have own , to spite himself as well as they ; for it have oblige he to leave a sea - life , to which , in comparison , all life spend on shore be bad than nothing for dulness . for Robson have never reach that rank aboard ship which make his be unable to run up the rigging , or to throw a harpoon , or to fire off a gun , of no great consequence ; so he have to be thankful that an opportune legacy enable he to turn farmer , a great degradation in his opinion . but his blood warm , as he tell the specksioneer , towards a sailor , and he press Kinraid to beguile the time when he be compel to be ashore , by come over to see he at Haytersbank , whenever he feel inclined . 

 Sylvia , appear to listen to Molly 's confidence , be hearken in reality to all this conversation between her father and the specksioneer ; and at this invitation she become especially attentive . 

 Kinraid reply , 

 ' I be much oblige to ye , I be sure ; maybe I can come and spend an ev'ning wi ' you ; but as soon as I be get round a bit , I must go see my own people as live at Cullercoats near Newcastle - upo'-Tyne . ' 

 ' well , well ! ' say Daniel , rise to take leave , with unusual prudence as to the amount of his drink . ' thou'lt see , thou'lt see ! I shall be main glad to see thee ; if thou'lt come . but I have   na ' lad to keep thee company , only one sprig of a wench . Sylvia , come here , an let us show thee to this young fellow ! ' 

 Sylvia come forwards , ruddy as any rose , and in a moment Kinraid recognize she as the pretty little girl he have see cry so bitterly over Darley 's grave . he rise up out of true sailor 's gallantry , as she shyly approach and stand by her father 's side , scarcely dare to lift her great soft eye , to have one fair gaze at his face . he have to support himself by one hand rest on the dresser , but she see he be look far well young , less haggard than he have seem to she before . his face be short and expressive ; his complexion have be weatherbeaten and bronze , though now he look so pale ; his eye and hair be dark , the former quick , deep - set , and penetrate ; the latter curly , and almost in ringlet . his tooth gleam white as he smile at she , a pleasant friendly smile of recognition ; but she only blush the deep , and hang her head . 

 ' I will come , sir , and be thankful . I daresay a turn'll do I good , if the weather hold up , an ' th ' frost keep on . ' 

 ' that be right , my lad , ' say Robson , shake he by the hand , and then Kinraid 's hand be hold out to Sylvia , and she could not avoid the same friendly action . 

 Molly Corney follow she to the door , and when they be fairly outside , she hold Sylvia back for an instant to say , 

 ' be na ' he a fine likely man ? I be so glad as yo've   see he , for he be to be off next week to Newcastle and that neighbourhood . ' 

 ' but he say he would come to we some night ? ' ask Sylvia , half in a fright . 

 ' ay , I will see as he do ; never fear . for I should like yo ' for to know he a bit . he be a rare talker . I will mind he o ' come to yo ' . ' 

 somehow , Sylvia feel as if this repeat promise of remind Kinraid of his promise to come and see her father take away part of the pleasure she have anticipate from his visit . yet what could be more natural than that Molly Corney should wish her friend to be acquaint with the man whom Sylvia believe to be all but Molly 's engaged lover ? 

 ponder these thought , the walk home be as silent as that go to Moss Brow have be . the only change seem to be that now they face the brilliant northern light flash up the sky , and that either this appearance or some of the whale narration of Kinraid have stir up Daniel Robson 's recollection of a sea ditty , which he keep sing to himself in a low , unmusical voice , the burden of which be , ' for I love the tossin ' say ! ' Bell meet they at the door . 

 ' well , and here ye be at home again ! and Philip have be , Sylvie , to give thee thy cipher lesson ; and he stay awhile , think thou'd be come back . ' 

 ' I be very sorry , ' say Sylvia , more out of deference to her mother 's tone of annoyance , than because she herself care either for her lesson or her cousin 's disappointment . 

 ' he will come again to - morrow night , he say . but thou must take care , and mind the night he say he will come , for it be a long way to come for nought . ' 

 Sylvia might have repeat she ' I be very sorry ' at this announcement of Philip 's intention ; but she restrain herself , inwardly and fervently hop that molly would not urge the fulfilment of the specksioneer 's promise for to - morrow night , for Philip 's be there would spoil all ; and besides , if she sate at the dresser at her lesson , and Kinraid at the table with her father , he might hear all , and find out what a dunce she be . 

 she need not have be afraid . with the next night Hepburn come ; and Kinraid do not . after a few word to her mother , Philip produce the candle he have promise , and some book and a quill or two . 

 ' what for hast thou bring candle ? ' ask Bell , in a half - affront tone . 

 Hepburn smile . 

 ' Sylvia think it would take a deal of candlelight , and be for make it into a reason not to learn . I should ha ' used t ' candle if I have stay at home , so I just bring they wi ' I . ' 

 ' then thou may'st just take they back again , ' say Bell , shortly , blow out that which he have light , and place one of her own on the dresser instead . 

 Sylvia catch her mother 's look of displeasure , and it make she docile for the evening , although she owe her cousin a grudge for her enforce good behaviour . 

 ' now , Sylvia , here be a copy - book wi ' t ' Tower o ' London on it , and we will fill it wi ' as pretty writing as any in t ' North Riding . ' 

 Sylvia sate quite still , unenlivene by this prospect . 

 ' here be a pen as will nearly write of itsel ' , ' continue Philip , still try to coax she out her sullenness of manner . 

 then he arrange she in the right position . 

 ' do not   lay your head down on your left arm , you will ne'er see to write straight . ' 

 the attitude be change , but not a word be speak . Philip begin to grow angry at such determined dumbness . 

 ' be you tired ? ' ask he , with a strange mixture of crossness and tenderness . 

 ' yes , very , ' be her reply . 

 ' but thou ought'st not to be tired , ' say Bell , who have not yet get over the offence to her hospitality ; who , moreover , like her nephew , and have , to boot , a great respect for the learning she have never acquire . 

 ' Mother ! ' say Sylvia , burst out , ' what be the use on my writing " Abednego , " " Abednego , " " Abednego , " all down a page ? if I could see t ' use on ' t , I would ha ' axed father to send I t ' school ; but I be none want to have learn . ' 

 ' it be a fine thing , tho ' , be learn . my mother and my grandmother have it : but th ' family come down I ' the world , and Philip 's mother and I , we have none of it ; but I ha ' set my heart on thy have it , child . ' 

 ' my finger be stiff , ' plead Sylvia , hold up her little hand and shake it . 

 ' let we take a turn at spelling , then , ' say Philip . 

 ' what be t ' use on't ? ' ask captious Sylvia . 

 ' why , it help one I ' read an ' writing . ' 

 ' and what do read and writing do for one ? ' 

 her mother give she another of the severe look that , quiet woman as she be , she could occasionally bestow upon the refractory , and Sylvia take her book and glance down the column Philip point out to she ; but , as she justly consider , one man might point out the task , but twenty could not make she learn it , if she do not choose ; and she sit herself down on the edge of the dresser , and idly gaze into the fire . but her mother come round to look for something in the drawer of the dresser , and as she pass her daughter she say in a low voice 

 ' Sylvie , be a good lass . I set a deal o ' store by learn , and father ' ud never send thee to school , as have stick by I sore . ' 

 if Philip , sit with his back to they , hear these word he be discreet enough not to show that he hear . and he have his reward ; for in a very short time , Sylvia stand before he with her book in her hand , prepare to say her spelling . at which he also stand up by instinct , and listen to her slow succeed letter ; help she out , when she look up at he with a sweet childlike perplexity in her face : for a dunce as to book - learn poor Sylvia be and be likely to remain ; and , in spite of his assumed office of schoolmaster , Philip Hepburn could almost have echo the word of the lover of Jess MacFarlane 

 I send my love a letter , 
 but , alas ! she canna read , 
 and I lo'e her a ' the well . 
 still he know his aunt 's strong wish on the subject , and it be very delightful to stand in the relation of teacher to so dear and pretty , if so wilful , a pupil . 

 perhaps it be not very flattering to notice Sylvia 's great joy when her lesson be over , sadly shorten as they be by Philip 's desire not to be too hard upon she . Sylvia dance round to her mother , bent her head back , and kiss her face , and then say defyingly to Philip , 

 ' if iver I write thee a letter it shall just be full of nothing but " Abednego ! Abednego ! Abednego ! " ' 

 but at this moment her father come in from a distant expedition on the moor with Kester to look after the sheep he have pasture there before the winter set fairly in . he be tired , and so be Lassie , and so , too , be Kester , who , lift his heavy leg one after the other , and smooth down his hair , follow his master into the house - place , and seat himself on a bench at the farth end of the dresser , patiently await the supper of porridge and milk which he share with his master . Sylvia , meanwhile , coax Lassie poor footsore dog to her side , and give she some food , which the creature be almost too tired to eat . Philip make as though he would be go , but Daniel motion to he to be quiet . 

 ' sit thee down , lad . as soon as I have   have my victual , I want t ' hear a bit o ' news . ' 

 Sylvia take her sewing and sit at the little round table by her mother , share the light of the scanty dip - candle . no one speak . every one be absorb in what they be do . what Philip be do be , gaze at Sylvia learn her face off by heart . 

 when every scrap of porridge be clear out of the mighty bowl , Kester yawn , and wish good - night , withdraw to his loft over the cow - house . then Philip pull out the weekly York paper , and begin to read the late account of the war then rag . this be give Daniel one of his great pleasure ; for though he could read pretty well , yet the double effort of reading and understand what he read be almost too much for he . he could read , or he could understand what be read aloud to he ; read be no pleasure , but listening be . 

 besides , he have a true John bullish interest in the war , without very well know what the English be fight for . but in those day , so long as they fight the French for any cause , or for no cause at all , every true patriot be satisfied . Sylvia and her mother do not care for any such far - extend interest ; a little bit of York news , the stealing of a few apple out of a scarborough garden that they know , be of far more interest to they than all the battle of Nelson and the North . 

 Philip read in a high - pitch and unnatural tone of voice , which deprive the word of their reality ; for even familiar expression can become unfamiliar and convey no idea , if the utterance be force or affect . Philip be somewhat of a pedant ; yet there be a simplicity in his pedantry not always to be meet with in those who be self - teach , and which might have interest any one who care to know with what labour and difficulty he have acquire the knowledge which now he prize so highly ; read out latin quotation as easily as if they be English , and take a pleasure in rolling polysyllable , until all at once look askance at Sylvia , he see that her head have fall back , her pretty rosy lip open , her eye fast shut ; in short , she be asleep . 

 ' ay , ' say Farmer Robson , ' and t ' reading have a'most send I off . mother would look angry now if I be to tell yo ' yo ' have a right to a kiss ; but when I be a young man I would ha ' kiss a pretty girl as I see asleep , afore yo'd say Jack Robson . ' 

 Philip tremble at these word , and look at his aunt . she give he no encouragement , stand up , and make as though she have never hear her husband 's speech , by extend her hand , and wish he ' good - night . ' at the noise of the chair move over the flag floor , Sylvia start up , confused and annoy at her father 's laughter . 

 ' ay , lass ; it be iver a good time t ' fall asleep when a young fellow be by . here be Philip here as thou'rt bind t ' give a pair o ' glove to . ' 

 Sylvia go like fire ; she turn to her mother to read her face . 

 ' it be only father 's joke , lass , ' say she . ' Philip know manner too well . ' 

 ' he would well , ' say Sylvia , flame round at he . ' if he have a touch I , I would niver ha ' speak to he no more . ' and she look even as it be as if she be far from forgive he . 

 ' hoot , lass ! wench be bring up sa mim , now - a - day ; I ' my time they would ha ' thought na ' such great harm of a kiss . ' 

 ' good - night , Philip , ' say Bell Robson , think the conversation unseemly . 

 ' good - night , aunt , good - night , Sylvie ! ' but Sylvia turn she back on he , and he could hardly say ' good - night ' to Daniel , who have cause such an unpleasant end to an evening that have at one time be go on so well . 




 CHAPTER IX 

 the specksioneer 

 a few day after , Farmer Robson leave Haytersbank betime on a longish day 's journey , to purchase a horse . Sylvia and her mother be busy with a hundred household thing , and the early winter 's evening close in upon they almost before they be aware . the consequence of darkness in the country even now be to gather the member of a family together into one room , and to make they settle to some sedentary employment ; and it be much more the case at the period of my story , when candle be far dear than they be at present , and when one be often make to suffice for a large family . 

 the mother and daughter hardly speak at all when they sit down at last . the cheerful click of the knitting - needle make a pleasant home - sound ; and in the occasional snatch of slumber that overcome her mother , Sylvia could hear the long - rush boom of the wave , down below the rock , for the Haytersbank gulley allow the sullen roar to come up so far inland . it might have be about eight o'clock though from the monotonous course of the evening it seem much later when Sylvia hear her father 's heavy step cranch down the pebbly path . more unusual , she hear his voice talk to some companion . 

 curious to see who it could be , with a lively instinctive advance towards any event which might break the monotony she have begin to find somewhat dull , she spring up to open the door . half a glance into the gray darkness outside make she suddenly timid , and she draw back behind the door as she open it wide to admit her father and Kinraid . 

 Daniel Robson come in bright and boisterous . he be pleased with his purchase , and have have some drink to celebrate his bargain . he have ride the new mare into Monkshaven , and leave she at the smithy there until morning , to have her foot look at , and to be new shod . on his way from the town he have meet Kinraid wander about in search of Haytersbank Farm itself , so he have just bring he along with he ; and here they be , ready for bread and cheese , and aught else the mistress would set before they . 

 to Sylvia the sudden change into brightness and bustle occasion by the entrance of her father and the specksioneer be like that which you may effect any winter 's night , when you come into a room where a great lump of coal lie hot and slumber on the fire ; just break it up with a judicious blow from the poker , and the room , late so dark , and dusk , and lone , be full of life , and light , and warmth . 

 she move about with pretty household briskness , attend to all her father 's want . Kinraid 's eye watch she as she go backwards and forwards , to and fro , into the pantry , the back - kitchen , out of light into shade , out of the shadow into the broad firelight where he could see and note her appearance . she wear the high - crown linen cap of that day , surmount her lovely mass of golden brown hair , rather than conceal they , and tie firm to her head by a broad blue ribbon . a long curl hang down on each side of her neck her throat rather , for her neck be conceal by a little spotted handkerchief carefully pin across at the waist of her brown stuff gown . 

 how well it be , think the young girl , that she have doff her bed - gown and linsey - woolsey petticoat , her working - dress , and make herself smart in her stuff gown , when she sate down to work with her mother . 

 by the time she could sit down again , her father and Kinraid have their glass fill , and be talk of the relative merit of various kind of spirit ; that lead on to tale of smuggling , and the different contrivance by which they or their friend have elude the preventive service ; the nightly relay of man to carry the good inland ; the keg of brandy find by certain farmer whose horse have go so far in the night , that they could do no work the next day ; the clever way in which certain woman manage to bring in prohibit good ; in fact , that when a woman do give her mind to smuggle , she be more full of resource , and trick , and impudence , and energy than any man . there be no question of the morality of the affair ; one of the great sign of the real progress we have make since those time seem to be that our daily concern of buying and sell , eat and drinking , whatsoever we do , be more test by the real practical standard of our religion than they be in the day of our grandfather . neither Sylvia nor her mother be in advance of their age . both listen with admiration to the ingenious device , and act as well as speak lie , that be talk about as fine and spirited thing . yet if Sylvia have attempt one tithe of this deceit in her every - day life , it would have half break her mother 's heart . but when the duty on salt be strictly and cruelly enforce , make it penal to pick up rough dirty lump contain small quantity that might be throw out with the ash of the brine - house on the high - road ; when the price of this necessary be so increase by the tax upon it as to make it an expensive , sometimes an unattainable , luxury to the work man , Government do more to demoralise the popular sense of rectitude and uprightness than heap of sermon could undo . and the same , though in small measure , be the consequence of many other taxis . it may seem curious to trace up the popular standard of truth to taxation ; but I do not think the idea would be so very far - fetched . 

 from smuggling adventure it be easy to pass on to story of what have happen to Robson , in his youth a sailor in the Greenland sea , and to Kinraid , now one of the good harpooner in any whaler that sail off the coast . 

 ' there be three thing to be afeare on , ' say Robson , authoritatively : ' there be t ' ice , that be bad ; there be dirty weather , that be bad ; and there be whale theirselve , as be t ' bad of all ; leastway , they be I ' my day ; t ' darn brute may ha ' larnt well manner sin ' . when I be young , they could niver be get to let theirsel be harpoon wi'out flounderin ' and makin ' play wi ' their tale and their fin , till t ' say be all in a foam , and t ' boat ' crew be all o'er wi ' spray , which I ' they latitude be a kind o ' shower - bath not need . ' 

 ' Th ' whale have not   mend their manner , as you call it , ' say Kinraid ; ' but th ' ice be not to be speak lightly on . I be once in th ' ship John of Hull , and we be in good green water , and be keen after whale ; and ne'er thought harm of a great gray iceberg as be on our lee - bow , a mile or so off ; it look as if it have be there from the day of Adam , and be likely to see th ' last man out , and it ne'er a bit big nor small in all they thousand and thousand o ' year . well , the fast - boat be out after a fish , and I be specksioneer in one ; and we be so keen after capture our whale , that none on we ever see that we be drift away from they right into deep shadow o ' th ' iceberg . but we be set upon our whale , and I harpoon it ; and as soon as it be dead we lash its fin together , and fasten its tail to our boat ; and then we take breath and look about we , and away from we a little space be th ' other boat , wi ' two other fish make play , and as likely as not to break loose , for I may say as I be th ' good harpooner on board the John , wi'out say great thing o ' mysel ' . so I say , " my lad , one o ' you stay I ' th ' boat by this fish , " the fin o ' which , as I say , I would reeve a rope through mysel ' , and which be as dead as Noah 's grandfather " and th ' rest on we shall go off and help th ' other boat wi ' their fish . " for , you see , we have another boat close by in order to sweep th ' fish .   I suppose they sweep fish I ' your time , master ? ' 

 ' ay , ay ! ' say Robson ; ' one boat lie still hold t ' end o ' t ' line ; t ' other make a circuit round t ' fish . ' 

 ' well ! luckily for we we have our second boat , for we all get into it , ne'er a man on we be leave I ' th ' fast - boat . and say I , " but who be to stay by t ' dead fish ? " and no man answer , for they be all as keen as I for to go and help our mate ; and we think as we could come back to our dead fish , as have a boat for a buoy , once we have help our mate . so off we row , every man Jack on we , out o ' the black shadow o ' th ' iceberg , as look as steady as th ' pole - star . well ! we have na ' be a dozen fathom away fra ' th ' boat as we have leave , when crash ! down wi ' a roar noise , and then a gulp of the deep water , and then a shower o ' blinding spray ; and when we have wipe our eye clear , and getten our heart down agen fra ' our mouth , there be never a boat nor a glitter belly o ' e'er a great whale to be see ; but th ' iceberg be there , still and grim , as if a hundred ton or more have fall off all in a mass , and crush down boat , and fish , and all , into th ' deep water , as go half through the earth in they latitude . Th ' coal - miner round about Newcastle way may come upon our good boat if they mine deep enough , else ne'er another man will see she . and I leave as good a clasp - knife in she as ever I clapt eye on . ' 

 ' but what a mercy no man stay in she , ' say Bell . 

 ' why , mistress , I reckon we a ' must die some way ; and I would as soon go down into the deep water as be choke up wi ' mould . ' 

 ' but it must be so cold , ' say Sylvia , shudder and give a little poke to the fire to warm her fancy . 

 ' cold ! ' say her father , ' what do ye stay - at - home know about cold , a should like to know ? if yo'd be where a be once , north latitude 81 , in such a frost as ye ha ' niver know , no , not I ' deep winter , and it be June I ' they sea , and a whale I ' sight , and a be off in a boat after she : an ' t ' ill - mannered brute , as soon as she be harpoon , up wi ' her big awkward tail , and strike t ' boat I ' her stern , and chuck I out into t ' watter . that be cold , a can tell the ' ! first , I smart all ower I , as if my skin be suddenly stript off I : and next , ivery bone I ' my body have getten t ' toothache , and there be a great roar I ' my ear , an ' a great dizziness I ' my eye ; an ' t ' boat 's crew keep throwin ' out their oar , an ' a kept clutchin ' at them , but a could na ' make out where they be , my eye dazzle so wi ' t ' cold , an ' I think I be bind for " kingdom come , " an ' a try to remember t ' Creed , as a might die a Christian . but all a could think on be , " what be your name , M or N ? " an ' just as a be give up both word and life , they heave I aboard . but , bless ye , they have but one oar ; for they have throw a ' t ' other after I ; so yo ' may reckon , it be some time afore we could reach t ' ship ; an ' a've   heerd tell , a be a precious sight to look on , for my clothe be just hard frozen to I , an ' my hair a'most as big a lump o ' ice as yon iceberg he be a - tell we on ; they rub I as missus theere be rub t ' ham yesterday , and gav ' me brandy ; an ' a've   niver getten t ' frost out o ' my bone for a ' their rubbin ' , and a deal o ' brandy as I ' ave ta'en sin ' . talk o ' cold ! it be little yo ' woman know o ' cold ! ' 

 ' but there be heat , too , I ' some place , ' say Kinraid . I be once a voyage I ' an American . they go for th ' most part south , to where you come round to t ' cold again ; and they will stay there for three year at a time , if need be , go into winter harbour I ' some o ' th ' Pacific Islands . well , we be I ' th ' southern seas , a - seeking for good whaling - ground ; and , close on our larboard beam , there be a great wall o ' ice , as much as sixty foot high . and say our captain as be a dare - devil , if ever a man be " there will be an opening in yon dark gray wall , and into that opening I will sail , if I coast along it till th ' day o ' judgment . " but , for all our sailing , we never seem to come near to th ' opening . the water be rock beneath we , and the sky be steady above we ; and th ' ice rise out o ' the water , and seem to reach up into the sky . we sail on , and we sail on , for more day nor I could count . our captain be a strange , wild man , but once he look a little pale when he come upo ' deck after his turn - in , and see the green - gray ice go straight up on our beam . many on we think as the ship be bewitch for th ' captain 's word ; and we get to speak low , and to say our prayer o ' night , and a kind o ' dull silence come into th ' very air ; our voice do na ' rightly seem our own . and we sail on , and we sail on . all at once , th ' man as be on watch give a cry : he see a break in the ice , as we would begin to think be everlaste ; and we all gather towards the bow , and the captain call to th ' man at the helm to keep she course , and cock his head , and begin to walk the quarter - deck jaunty again . and we come to a great cleft in th ' long weary rock of ice ; and the side o ' th ' cleft be not jagged , but go straight sharp down into th ' foaming water . but we take but one look at what lie inside , for our captain , with a loud cry to God , bid the helmsman steer nor'ards away fra ' th ' mouth o ' Hell . we all see wi ' our own eye , inside that fearsome wall o ' ice seventy mile long , as we could swear to inside that gray , cold ice , come leap flame , all red and yellow wi ' heat o ' some unearthly kind out o ' th ' very water o ' the sea ; make our eye dazzle wi ' their scarlet blaze , that shoot up as high , nay , high than th ' ice around , yet never so much as a shred on ' t be melt . they do say that some beside our captain see the black devil dart hither and thither , quick than the very flame themselves ; anyhow , he see they . and as he know it be his own daring as have lead he to have that peep at terror forbid to any on we afore our time , he just dwine away , and we have not   take but one whale afore our captain die , and first mate take th ' command . it be a prosperous voyage ; but , for all that , I will never sail those sea again , nor ever take wage aboard an American again . ' 

 ' eh , dear ! but it be awful t ' think o ' sit wi ' a man that have see th ' doorway into hell , ' say Bell , aghast . 

 Sylvia have drop her work , and sit gaze at Kinraid with fascinated wonder . 

 Daniel be just a little annoyed at the admiration which his own wife and daughter be bestow on the specksioneer 's wonderful story , and he say 

 ' ay , ay . if a'd be a talker , ye'd ha ' think a deal more on I nor ye've   iver do yet . A've   see such thing , and do such thing . ' 

 ' tell we , father ! ' say Sylvia , greedy and breathless . 

 ' some on them be past tell , ' he reply , ' an some be not to be have for t ' asking , see as how they might bring a man into trouble . but , as a said , if a have a fancy to reveal all as be on my mind a could make t ' hair on your head lift up your cap well , we will say an inch , at least . Thy mother , lass , have heerd one or two on them . Thou mind the story o ' my ride on a whale 's back , Bell ? that will maybe be within this young fellow 's comprehension o ' t ' danger ; thou 's heerd I tell it , hastn't   ta ? ' 

 ' yes , ' say Bell ; ' but it be a long time ago ; when we be court . ' 

 ' An ' that be afore this young lass be bear , as be a'most up to woman 's estate . but sin ' those day a ha ' be o'er busy to tell story to my wife , an ' as a'll warrant she be forget it ; an ' as Sylvia here niver heerd it , if yo'll fill your glass , Kinraid , yo ' shall ha ' t ' benefit o't . 

 ' a be a specksioneer mysel , though , after that , a rayther direct my talent int ' t ' smuggling branch o ' my profession ; but a be once a whale aboord t ' Ainwell of Whitby . an ' we be anchor off t ' coast o ' Greenland one season , an ' we would getten a cargo o ' seven whale ; but our captain he be a keen - eyed chap , an ' niver above do any man 's work ; an ' once seein ' a whale he throw himself int ' a boat an ' go off to it , makin ' signal to I , an ' another specksioneer as be off for diversion I ' another boat , for to come after he sharp . well , afore we come alongside , captain have harpoon t ' fish ; an ' say he , " now , Robson , all ready ! give into she again when she come to t ' top ; " an ' I stand up , right leg foremost , harpoon all ready , as soon as iver I cotche a sight o ' t ' whale , but niver a fin could a see . ' Twere no wonder , for she be right below t ' boat in which a be ; and when she want to rise , what do t ' great ugly brute do but come wi ' her head , as be like cast iron , up bang again t ' bottom o ' t ' boat . I be throw up in t ' air like a shuttlecock , I an ' my line an ' my harpoon up we go , an ' many a good piece o ' timber wi ' us , an ' many a good fellow too ; but a have t ' look after mysel ' , an a be up high I ' t ' air , afore I could say Jack Robinson , an ' a thowt a be safe for another dive int ' saut water ; but i'stead a come down plump on t ' back o ' t ' whale . ay ! yo ' may stare , master , but theere a be , an ' main an ' slippery it be , only a stick my harpoon intil she an ' steady mysel ' , an ' look abroad o'er t ' vast o ' wave , and get sea - sick in a manner , an ' put up a prayer as she may not   dive , and it be as good a prayer for wishin ' it might come true as iver t ' clargyman an ' t ' clerk too put up I ' monkshaven church . well , a reckon it be heerd , for all a be I ' they north latitude , for she keep steady , an ' a do my good for t ' keep steady ; an ' ' deed a be too steady , for a be fast wi ' t ' harpoon line , all knot and tangle about I . T ' captain , he sing out for I to cut it ; but it be easy singin ' out , and it be noane so easy fumblin ' for your knife I ' t ' pocket o ' your drawer , when yo've   t ' hold hard wi ' t ' other hand on t ' back of a whale , swimmin ' fourteen knot an hour . at last a think to mysel ' a can not   get free o ' t ' line , and t ' line be fast to t ' harpoon , and t ' harpoon be fast to t ' whale ; and t ' whale may go down fathom deep wheniver t ' maggot stir I ' her head ; an ' t ' watter 's cold , an noane good for drownin ' in ; a can not   get free o ' t ' line , and a connot get my knife out o ' my breech pocket though t ' captain should can ' it mutiny to disobey order , and t ' line 's fast to t ' harpoon let us see if t ' harpoon 's fast to t ' whale . so a tugged , and a lugged , and t ' whale do not   mistake it for ticklin ' , but she cock up her tail , and throw out shower o ' water as be ice or iver it touch I ; but a pull on at t ' shank , an ' a be only afeard as she would not   keep at t ' top wi ' it stick in she ; but at last t ' harpoon break , an ' just I ' time , for a reckon she be near as tired o ' I as a be on she , and down she go ; an ' a have hard work to make for t ' boat as be near enough to catch I ; for what wi ' t ' whale 's being but slippery an ' t ' watter be cold , an ' I hamper wi ' t ' line an ' t ' piece o ' harpoon , it be a chance , missus , as thou have stop an oud maid . ' 

 ' eh dear a ' I ! ' say Bell , ' how well I mind yo'r tell I that tale ! it be twenty - four year ago come October . I think I never could think enough on a man as have ride on a whale 's back ! ' 

 ' Yo ' may learn t ' way of winnin ' t ' woman , ' say Daniel , wink at the specksioneer . 

 and Kinraid immediately look at Sylvia . it be no premeditated action ; it come as naturally as waken in the morning when his sleep be end ; but Sylvia colour as red as any rise at his sudden glance , colour so deeply that he look away until he think she have recover her composure , and then he sit gaze at she again . but not for long , for Bell suddenly start up , do all but turn he out of the house . it be late , she say , and her master be tired , and they have a hard day before they next day ; and it be keep Ellen Corney up ; and they have have enough to drink , more than be good for they , she be sure , for they have both be take she in with their story , which she have be foolish enough to believe . no one see the real motive of all this almost inhospitable haste to dismiss her guest , how the sudden fear have take possession of she that he and Sylvia be ' fancy each other ' . Kinraid have say early in the evening that he have come to thank she for her kindness in send the sausage , as he be off to his own home near Newcastle in a day or two . but now he say , in reply to Daniel Robson , that he would step in another night before long and hear some more of the old man 's yarn . 

 Daniel have just have enough drink to make he very good - temper , or else his wife would not have dare to have act as she do ; and this maudlin amiability take the shape of hospitable urgency that Kinraid should come as often as he like to Haytersbank ; come and make it his home when he be in these part ; stay there altogether , and so on , till Bell fairly shut the outer door to , and lock it before the specksioneer have well get out of the shadow of their roof . 

 all night long Sylvia dreamed of burn volcano spring out of icy southern sea . but , as in the specksioneer 's tale the flame be people with demon , there be no human interest for she in the wondrous scene in which she be no actor , only a spectator . with daylight come waken and little homely every - day wonder . do Kinraid mean that he be go away really and entirely , or do he not ? be he Molly Corney 's sweetheart , or be he not ? when she have argue herself into certainty on one side , she suddenly wheel about , and be just of the opposite opinion . at length she settle that it could not be settle until she see Molly again ; so , by a strong gulp effort , she resolutely determine to think no more about he , only about the marvel he have tell . she might think a little about they when she sit at night , spin in silence by the household fire , or when she go out in the gloaming to call the cattle home to be milk , and saunter back behind the patient , slow - gaite creature ; and at time on future summer day , when , as in the past , she take her knitting out for the sake of the freshness of the faint sea - breeze , and drop down from ledge to ledge of the rock that face the blue ocean , establish herself in a perilous nook that have be her haunt ever since her parent have come to Haytersbank Farm . from thence she have often see the distant ship pass to and fro , with a certain sort of lazy pleasure in watch their swift tranquillity of motion , but no thought as to where they be bind to , or what strange place they would penetrate to before they turn again , homeward bind . 




 CHAPTER x 

 a refractory PUPIL 

 Sylvia be still full of the specksioneer and his story , when Hepburn come up to give she the next lesson . but the prospect of a little sensible commendation for write a whole page full of flourish ' abednego , ' have lose all the slight charm it have ever possess . she be much more inclined to try and elicit some sympathy in her interest in the peril and adventure of the northern sea , than to bend and control her mind to the right formation of letter . unwisely enough , she endeavour to repeat one of the narrative that she have hear from Kinraid ; and when she find that Hepburn   if , indeed , he do not look upon the whole as a silly invention   consider it only as an interruption to the real business in hand , to which he would try to listen as patiently as he could , in the hope of Sylvia 's apply herself diligently to her copy - book when she have clear her mind , she contract her pretty lip , as if to check they from make any further appeal for sympathy , and set about her writing - lesson in a very rebellious frame of mind , only restrain by her mother 's presence from spoken mutiny . 

 ' after all , ' say she , throw down her pen , and opening and shut she weary , cramped hand , ' I see no good in tire myself wi ' learning for t ' write letter when I'se never get one in a ' my life . what for should I write answer , when there be niver a one write to I ? and if I have one , I could not   read it ; it be bad enough wi ' a book o ' print as I have   niver see afore , for there be sure to be new - fangle word in ' t. I be sure I wish the man be farre who plague his brain wi ' strike out new word . why can not   folk just ha ' a set on them for good and a ' ? ' 

 ' why ! you will be after use two or three hundred yoursel ' every day as you live , Sylvie ; and yet I must use a great many as you never think on about t ' shop ; and t ' folk in t ' field want their set , let alone the high English that parson and lawyer speak . ' 

 ' well , it be weary work be read and writing . can not you learn I something else , if we mun do lesson ? ' 

 ' there 's sum and geography , ' say Hepburn , slowly and gravely . 

 ' Geography ! ' say Sylvia , brightening , and perhaps not pronounce the word quite correctly , ' I would like yo ' to learn I geography . there be a deal o ' place I want to hear all about . '

 CHAPTER XI 

 vision of the future

Bell 's opinion be , that it be creditable to a woman to go through life in the shadow of obscurity , never name except in connexion with good housewifery , husband , or child . too much talk about a girl , even in the way of praise , disturb Mrs. Robson 's opinion of she ; and when her neighbour tell she how her own daughter be admire , she would reply coldly , ' she be just well enough , ' and change the subject of conversation . but it be quite different with her husband . to his loose , less - restrained mind , it be agreeable to hear of , and still more to see , the attention which his daughter 's beauty receive . he feel it as reflect consequence on himself . he have never trouble his mind with speculation as to whether he himself be popular , still less whether he be respect . he be pretty welcome wherever he go , as a jovial good - natured man , who have do adventurous and illegal thing in his youth , which in some measure entitle he to speak out his opinion on life in general in the authoritative manner he generally use ; but , of the two , he prefer consort with young man , to take a sober stand of respectability with the elder of the place ; and he perceive , without reason upon it , that the gay dare spirit be more desirous of his company when Sylvia be by his side than at any other time . one or two of these would saunter up to Haytersbank on a Sunday afternoon , and lounge round his field with the old farmer . Bell keep herself from the nap which have be her weekly solace for year , in order to look after Sylvia , and on such occasion she always turn as cold a shoulder to the visitor as her sense of hospitality and of duty to her husband would permit . but if they do not enter the house , old Robson would always have Sylvia with he when he go the round of his land . Bell could see they from the upper window : the young man stand in the attitude of listener , while Daniel lay down the law on some point , enforce his word by pantomimic action with his thick stick ; and Sylvia , half turn away as if from some too admiring gaze , be possibly pick flower out of the hedge - bank . these Sunday afternoon stroll be the plague of Bell 's life that whole summer . then it take as much of artifice as be in the simple woman 's nature to keep Daniel from insist on have Sylvia 's company every time he go down to Monkshaven . and here , again , come a perplexity , the acknowledgement of which in distinct thought would have be an act of disloyalty , accord to Bell 's conscience . if Sylvia go with her father , he never drink to excess ; and that be a good gain to health at any rate   drinking be hardly a sin against moral in those day , and in that place ; so , occasionally , she be allow to accompany he to Monkshaven as a check upon his folly ; for he be too fond and proud of his daughter to disgrace she by any open excess . but one Sunday afternoon early in November , Philip come up before the time at which he usually pay his visit . he look grave and pale ; and his aunt begin ,

 his aunt 's face change immediately .
