' 

 ' and he take she there , do he ? ' say Bell , in severe meditation . ' I have never no opinion o ' th ' wench as will set theirselve to be hire for servant I ' th ' fair ; they be a bad lot , as can not find place for theirselve 'bout going and stannin ' to be star at by folk , and grinnin ' wi ' th ' plough - lad when no one 's look ; it be a bad look - out for t ' missus as take one o ' these wench for a servant ; and dost ta mean to say as my Sylvie go and demean hersel ' to dance and marlock wi ' a ' th ' fair - folk at th ' " Admiral 's head ? " ' 

 ' no , no , she do na ' dance ; she barely set foot I ' th ' room ; but it be her own pride as save she ; uncle would niver ha ' keep she from it , for he have fall in wi ' Hayley o ' Seaburn and one or two other , and they be have a glass I ' t ' bar , and Mrs. Lawson , t ' landlady , know how there be they who would come and dance among parish ' prentice if need be , just to get a word or a look wi ' Sylvie ! so she tempt she in , say that the room be all smartened and fine wi ' flag ; and there be they in the room as tell I that they never be so startled as when they see our Sylvie 's face peep in among all t ' flustered maid and man , rough and red wi ' weather and drink ; and Jem Macbean , he say she be just like a bit o ' apple - blossom among peony ; and some man , he do not   know who , go up and speak to she ; an ' either at that , or at some o ' t ' word she hear for they would get a good way on afore that time she go quite white and mad , as if fire be come out of her eye , and then she turn red and leave the room , for all t ' landlady try to laugh it off and keep she in . ' 

 ' I will be down to Monkshaven before I be a day old , and tell Margaret Lawson some on my mind as she will not forget in a hurry . ' 

 Bell move as though she would put on her cloak and hood there and then . 

 ' Nay , it be not in reason as a woman I ' that line o ' life should not   try to make her house agreeable , ' say Philip . 

 ' not wi ' my wench , ' say Bell , in a determined voice . 

 Philip 's information have make a deep impression on his aunt than he intend . he himself have be annoy more at the idea that Sylvia would be speak of as have be at a rough piece of rustic gaiety a yearly festival for the low class of Yorkshire servant , out - door as well as in - door than at the affair itself , for he have learn from his informant how instantaneous her appearance have be . he stand watch his aunt 's troubled face , and almost wish that he have not speak . at last she heave a deep sigh , and stir the fire , as if by this little household occupation to compose her mind , she say 

 ' it be a pity as wench be not   lad , or married folk . I could ha ' wish but it be the Lord 's will it would ha ' be summut to look to , if she have have a brother . my master be so full on his own thought , yo ' see , he be no mind leave for think on she , what wi ' th ' oat , and th ' wool , and th ' young colt , and his venture I ' th ' Lucky Mary . ' 

 she really believe her husband to have the serious and important occupation for his mind that she have be teach to consider befit the superior intellect of the masculine gender ; she would have tax herself severely , if , even in thought , she have blame he , and Philip respect her feeling too much to say that Sylvia 's father ought to look after she more closely if he make such a pretty creature so constantly his companion ; yet some such speech be only just pent within Philip 's closed lip . again his aunt speak 

 ' I use to think as she and yo ' might fancy one another , but thou'rt too old - fashioned like for she ; ye would na ' suit ; and it be as well , for now I can say to thee , that I would take it very kindly if thou would'st look after she a bit . ' 

 Philip 's countenance fall into gloom . he have to gulp down certain feeling before he could make answer with discretion . 

 ' how can I look after she , and I tie to the shop more and more every day ? ' 

 ' I could send she on a bit of an errand to Foster 's , and then , for sure , yo ' might keep an eye upon she when she be in th ' town ; and just walk a bit way with she when she be in th ' street , and keep t ' other fellow off her Ned Simpson , t ' butcher , in ' special , for folk do say he mean no good by any girl he go wi ' and I will ask father to leave she a bit more wi ' I . they be come down th ' brow , and Ned Simpson wi ' they . now , Philip , I look to thee to do a brother 's part by my wench , and warn off all as be not   fit . ' 

 the door open , and the coarse strong voice of Simpson make itself hear . he be a stout man , comely enough as to form and feature , but with a depth of colour in his face that betoken the come on of the habit of the sot . his Sunday hat be in his hand , and he smooth the long nap of it , as he say , with a mixture of shyness and familiarity 

 ' Sarvant , missus . Yo'r measter be fain that I should come in an ' have a drop ; no offence , I hope ? ' 

 Sylvia pass quickly through the house - place , and go upstairs without speak to her cousin Philip or to any one . he sit on , dislike the visitor , and almost dislike his hospitable uncle for have bring Simpson into the house , sympathize with his aunt in the spirit which prompt her curt answer , and in the interval of all these feeling wonder what ground she have for speak as if she have now give up all thought of Sylvia and he ever be marry , and in what way he be too ' old - fashioned . ' 

 Robson would gladly have persuade Philip to join he and Simpson in their drink , but Philip be in no sociable mood , and sate a little aloof , watch the staircase down which soon or later Sylvia must come ; for , as perhaps have be already say , the stair go up straight out of the kitchen . and at length his yearn watch be reward ; first , the little pointed toe come daintily in sight , then the trim ankle in the tight blue stocking , the wool of which be spin and the web of which be knit by her mother 's careful hand ; then the full brown stuff petticoat , the arm hold the petticoat back in decent fold , so as not to encumber the descend foot ; the slender neck and shoulder hide under the fold square of fresh white muslin ; the crown beauty of the soft innocent face radiant in colour , and with the light brown curl cluster around . she make her way quickly to Philip 's side ; how his heart beat at her approach ! and even more when she enter into a low - voice tete - a - tete . 

 ' be not   he go yet ? ' say she . ' I can not abide he ; I could ha ' pinched father when he ask he for t ' come in . ' 

 ' maybe , he will not stay long , ' say Philip , hardly understand the meaning of what he say , so sweet be it to have she make her whisper confidence to he . 

 but Simpson be not go to let she alone in the dark corner between the door and the window . he begin pay she some coarse country compliment too strong in their direct flattery for even her father 's taste , more especially as he see by his wife 's set lip and frown brow how much she disapprove of their visitor 's style of conversation . 

 ' come , measter , leave t ' lass alone ; she be set up enough a'ready , her mother make such a deal on she . Yo ' an ' me 's man for sensible talk at our time o ' life . an ' , as I be say , t ' horse be a weaver if iver one be , as any one could ha ' tell as have come within a mile on he . ' 

 and in this way the old farmer and the bluff butcher chat on about horse , while Philip and Sylvia sate together , he turn over all manner of hope and project for the future , in spite of his aunt 's opinion that he be too ' old - fashion ' for her dainty , bloom daughter . perhaps , too , Mrs. Robson see some reason for change her mind on this head as she watch Sylvia this night , for she accompany Philip to the door , when the time come for he to start homeward , and bid he ' good - night ' with unusual fervour , add 

 ' Thou'st be a deal o ' comfort to I , lad a'most as one as if thou wert a child o ' my own , as at time I could welly think thou art to be . anyways , I trust to thee to look after the lile lass , as have no brother to guide she among man and man 's very kittle for a woman to deal wi ; but if thou'lt have an eye on whom she consort wi ' , my mind will be easy . ' 

 Philip 's heart beat fast , but his voice be as calm as usual when he reply 

 ' I would just keep she a bit aloof from Monkshaven folk ; a lass be always the more thought on for be chary of herself ; and as for t ' rest , I will have an eye to the folk she go among , and if I see that they do not   befit she , I will just give she a warning , for she be not one to like such chap as yon Simpson there ; she can see what be become in a man to say to a lass , and what be not . ' 

 Philip set out on his two - mile walk home with a tumult of happiness in his heart . he be not often carry away by delusion of his own creating ; to - night he think he have good ground for believe that by patient self - restraint he might win Sylvia 's love . a year ago he have nearly earn her dislike by obtrude upon her look and word betoken his passionate love . he alarm her girlish coyness , as well as weary she with the wish he have then feel that she should take an interest in his pursuit . but , with unusual wisdom , he have perceive his mistake ; it be many month now since he have betray , by word or look , that she be anything more to he than a little cousin to be care for and protect when need be . the consequence be that she have become tame , just as a wild animal be tame ; he have remain tranquil and impassive , almost as if he do not perceive her shy advance towards friendliness . these advance be make by she after the lesson have cease . she be afraid lest he be displeased with her behaviour in reject his instruction , and be not easy till she be at peace with he ; and now , to all appearance , he and she be perfect friend , but nothing more . in his absence she would not allow her young companion to laugh at his grave sobriety of character , and somewhat prim demeanour ; she would even go against her conscience , and deny that she perceive any peculiarity . when she want it , she seek his advice on such small subject as come up in her daily life ; and she try not to show sign of weariness when he use more word and more difficult word than be necessary to convey his idea . but her ideal husband be different from Philip in every point , the two image never for an instant merge into one . to Philip she be the only woman in the world ; it be the one subject on which he dare not consider , for fear that both conscience and judgment should decide against he , and that he should be convince against his will that she be an unfit mate for he , that she never would be his , and that it be waste of time and life to keep she shrine in the dear sanctuary of his being , to the exclusion of all the serious and religious aim which , in any other case , he would have be the first to acknowledge as the object he ought to pursue . for he have be bring up among the Quakers , and share in their austere distrust of a self - seek spirit ; yet what else but self - seeking be his passionate prayer , ' give I Sylvia , or else , I die ? ' no other vision have ever cross his masculine fancy for a moment ; his be a rare and constant love that deserve a well fate than it meet with . at this time his hope be high , as I have say , not merely as to the growth of Sylvia 's feeling towards he , but as to the probability of his soon be in a position to place she in such comfort , as his wife , as she have never enjoy before . 

 for the brother Foster be think of retire from business , and relinquish the shop to their two shopman , Philip Hepburn and William Coulson . to be sure , it be only by look back for a few month , and notice chance expression and small indication , that this intention of theirs could be discover . but every step they take tend this way , and Philip know their usual practice of deliberation too well to feel in the least impatient for the quick progress of the end which he see steadily approach . the whole atmosphere of life among the friend at this date partook of this character of self - repression , and both Coulson and Hepburn share in it . Coulson be just as much aware of the prospect opening before he as Hepburn ; but they never speak together on the subject , although their mutual knowledge might be occasionally imply in their conversation on their future life . meanwhile the Fosters be impart more of the background of their business to their successor . for the present , at least , the brother mean to retain an interest in the shop , even after they have give up the active management ; and they sometimes think of set up a separate establishment as banker . the separation of the business , the introduction of their shopman to the distant manufacturer who furnish their good   in those day the system of ' traveller ' be not so widely organize as it be at present , all these step be in gradual progress ; and already Philip see himself in imagination in the dignified position of joint master of the principal shop in Monkshaven , with Sylvia instal as his wife , with certainly a silk gown , and possibly a gig at her disposal . in all Philip 's vision of future prosperity , it be Sylvia who be to be aggrandize by they ; his own life be to be spend as it be now , pretty much between the four shop wall . 




 CHAPTER XII 

 NEW YEAR 's FETE 

 all this enlargement of interest in the shop occupy Philip fully for some month after the period refer to in the precede chapter . remember his last conversation with his aunt , he might have be uneasy at his inability to perform his promise and look after his pretty cousin , but that about the middle of November Bell Robson have fall ill of a rheumatic fever , and that her daughter have be entirely absorb in nursing she . no thought of company or gaiety be in Sylvia 's mind as long as her mother 's illness last ; vehement in all her feeling , she discover in the dread of lose her mother how passionately she be attach to she . Hitherto she have suppose , as child so often do , that her parent would live for ever ; and now when it be a question of day , whether by that time the following week her mother might not be bury out of her sight for ever , she cling to every semblance of service to be render , or affection show , as if she hop to condense the love and care of year into the few day only that might remain . Mrs. Robson linger on , begin slowly to recover , and before Christmas be again sit by the fireside in the house - place , wan and pull down , muffle up with shawl and blanket , but still there once more , where not long before Sylvia have scarcely expect to see she again . Philip come up that evening and find Sylvia in wild spirit . she think that everything be do , now that her mother have once come downstairs again ; she laugh with glee ; she kiss her mother ; she shake hand with Philip , she almost submit to a speech of more than usual tenderness from he ; but , in the midst of his word , her mother 's pillow want arrange and she go to her chair , pay no more heed to his word than if they have be address to the cat , that lie on the invalid 's knee be purr out her welcome to the weak hand feebly stroke she back . Robson himself soon come in , look old and more subdue since Philip have see he last . he be very urgent that his wife should have some spirit and water ; but on her refusal , almost as if she loathe the thought of the smell , he content himself with share her tea , though he keep abuse the beverage as ' wash the heart out of a man , ' and attribute all the degeneracy of the world , grow up about he in his old age , to the drinking of such slop . at the same time , his little self - sacrifice put he in an unusually good temper ; and , mingle with his real gladness at have his wife once more on the way to recovery , bring back some of the old charm of tenderness combine with light - heartedness , which have win the sober Isabella Preston long ago . he sit by her side , hold her hand , and talk of old time to the young couple opposite ; of his adventure and escape , and how he have win his wife . she , faintly smile at the remembrance of those day , yet half - ashamed at have the little detail of her courtship reveal , from time to time keep say , 

 ' for shame wi ' thee , Dannel I never do , ' and faint denial of a similar kind . 

 ' niver believe she , Sylvie . she be a woman , and there be niver a woman but like to have a sweetheart , and can tell when a chap 's castin ' sheep' - eye at she ; ay , an ' afore he know what he be about hissen . she be a pretty one then , be my old ' ooman , an ' like they as think she so , though she do cock her head high , as bein ' a Preston , which be a family o ' standin ' and mean I ' those part aforetime . there be Philip there , I will warrant , be as proud o ' bein ' Preston by t ' mother 's side , for it run I ' t ' blood , lass . a can tell when a child of a Preston tak be to be proud o ' their kin , by t ' cut o ' their nose . now Philip 's and my missus 's have a turn beyond common I ' their nostril , as if they be sniffin ' at t ' rest of we world , an ' seein ' if we be good enough for them to consort wi ' . Thee an ' me , lass , be robson oat - cake folk , while they be pie - crust . Lord ! how Bell use to speak to I , as short as though a be not   a Christian , an ' a ' t ' time she love I as her very life , an ' well a know it , tho ' a'd to mak ' as tho ' a do not . Philip , when thou go courtin ' , come t ' I , and a'll give thee many a wrinkle . A've   show , too , as a know well how t ' choose a good wife by token an ' sign , hannot a , missus ? come t ' I , my lad , and show I t ' lass , an ' a'll just tak ' a squint at she , an ' tell yo ' if she will do or not ; an ' if she will do , a'll teach yo ' how to win she . ' 

 ' they say another o ' yon corney girl be go to be marry , ' say Mrs. Robson , in her faint deliberate tone . 

 ' by gosh , an ' it be well thou'st speak on them ; a be as clean forgettin ' it as iver could be . a meet Nanny Corney I ' monkshaven last neet , and she ax I for t ' let our Sylvia come o ' New Year 's Eve , an ' see Molly an ' her man , that ' n as be we d beyond Newcassel , they will be over at her feyther 's , for t ' New Year , an ' there be to be a merry - making . ' 

 Sylvia 's colour come , her eye brighten , she would have like to go ; but the thought of her mother come across she , and her feature fall . her mother 's eye catch the look and the change , and know what both mean as well as if Sylvia have speak out . 

 ' Thursday se'nnight , ' say she . ' I will be rare and strong by then , and Sylvie shall go play hersen ; she be be nurse - tend long enough . ' 

 ' you be but weakly yet , ' say Philip shortly ; he do not intend to say it , but the word seem to come out in spite of himself . 

 ' a say as our lass should come , God willin ' , if she only come and go , an ' thee go on sprightly , old ' ooman . an ' a'll turn nurse - tender mysen for t ' occasion , ' special if thou can stand t ' good honest smell o ' whisky by then . so , my lass , get up thy smart clothe , and cut t ' good on them out , as become a Preston . maybe , a'll fetch thee home , an ' maybe Philip will convoy thee , for Nanny Corney bade thee to t ' merry - making , as well . she say her measter would be seem ' thee about t ' wool afore then . ' 

 ' I do not   think as I can go , ' say Philip , secretly pleased to know that he have the opportunity in his power ; ' I be half bind to go Wi ' Hester Rose and her mother to t ' watch - night . ' 

 ' be hester a Methodee ? ' ask Sylvia in surprise . 

 ' no ! she be neither a Methodee , nor a Friend , nor a Church person ; but she be a turn for serious thing , choose wherever they be find . ' 

 ' well , then , ' say good - natured farmer Robson , only see the surface of thing , ' a'll make shift to fetch Sylvie back fra ' t ' merry - making , and thee an ' thy young woman can go to t ' prayer - makin ' ; it be every man to his taste , say I. ' 

 but in spite of his half - promise , nay against his natural inclination , Philip be lure to the Corneys ' by the thought of meet Sylvia , of watch she and exult in her superiority in pretty look and way to all the other girl likely to be assemble . besides   he tell his conscience   he be pledge to his aunt to watch over Sylvia like a brother . so in the interval before New Year 's Eve , he silently revel as much as any young girl in the anticipation of the happy coming time . 

 at this hour , all the actor in this story have play out their part and go to their rest , there be something touch in record the futile effort make by Philip to win from Sylvia the love he yearn for . but , at the time , any one who have watch he might have be amuse to see the grave , awkward , plain young man study pattern and colour for a new waistcoat , with his head a little on one side , after the meditative manner common to those who be choose a new article of dress . they might have smile could they have read in his imagination the frequent rehearsal of the come evening , when he and she should each be dress in their gala attire , to spend a few hour under a bright , festive aspect , among people whose company would oblige they to assume a new demeanour towards each other , not so familiar as their every - day manner , but allow more scope for the expression of rustic gallantry . Philip have so seldom be to anything of the kind , that , even have Sylvia not be go , he would have feel a kind of shy excitement at the prospect of anything so unusual . but , indeed , if Sylvia have not be go , it be very probable that Philip 's rigid conscience might have be arouse to the question whether such party do not savour too much of the world for he to form one in they . 

 as it be , however , the fact to he be simply these . he be go and she be go . the day before , he have hurry off to Haytersbank Farm with a small paper parcel in his pocket a ribbon with a little briar - rose pattern run upon it for Sylvia . it be the first thing he have ever venture to give she the first thing of the kind would , perhaps , be more accurate ; for when he have first begin to teach she any lesson , he have give she Mavor 's Spelling - book , but that he might have do , out of zeal for knowledge , to any dunce of a little girl of his acquaintance . this ribbon be quite a different kind of present ; he touch it tenderly , as if he be caress it , when he think of she wear it ; the briar - rise   sweetness and thorn   seem to be the very flower for she ; the soft , green ground on which the pink and brown pattern run , be just the colour to show off her complexion . and she would in a way belong to he : her cousin , her mentor , her chaperon , her lover ! while other only admire , he might hope to appropriate ; for of late they have be such happy friend ! her mother approve of he , her father like he . a few month , perhaps only a few week more of self - restraint , and then he might go and speak openly of his wish , and what he have to offer . for he have resolve , with the quiet force of his character , to wait until all be finally settle between he and his master , before he declare himself to either Sylvia or her parent . the interval be spend in patient , silent endeavour to recommend himself to she . 

 he have to give his ribbon to his aunt in charge for Sylvia , and that be a disappointment to his fancy , although he try to reason himself into think that it be well so . he have not time to wait for her return from some errand on which she have go , for he be daily more and more occupy with the affair of the shop . 

 Sylvia make many a promise to her mother , and more to herself , that she would not stay late at the party , but she might go as early as she like ; and before the December daylight have fade away , Sylvia present herself at the Corneys ' . she be to come early in order to help to set out the supper , which be arrange in the large old flagged parlour , which serve as good bed - room as well . it open out of the house - place , and be the sacred room of the house , as chamber of a similar description be still consider in retire farmhouse in the north of England . they be use on occasion like the one now describe for purpose of hospitality ; but in the state bed , overshadow so large a portion of the floor , the birth and , as far as may be , the death , of the household take place . at the Corneys ' , the united effort of some former generation of the family have produce patchwork curtain and coverlet ; and patchwork be patchwork in those day , before the early Yates and Peels have find out the secret of print the parsley - leaf . scrap of costly indian chintz and palempour be intermix with commoner black and red calico in minute hexagon ; and the variety of pattern serve for the useful purpose of promote conversation as well as the more obvious one of display the work - woman 's taste . Sylvia , for instance , begin at once to her old friend , Molly Brunton , who have accompany she into this chamber to take off her hat and cloak , with a remark on one of the chintz . stoop over the counterpane , with a face into which the flush would come whether or no , she say to Molly , 

 ' dear ! I never seed this one afore this for all t ' world like th ' eye in a peacock 's tail . ' 

 ' Thou be see it many a time and oft , lass . but be not   thou surprised to find Charley here ? we pick he up at Shields , quite by surprise like ; and when Brunton and I say as we be comin ' here , nought would serve he but comin ' with we , for t ' see t ' new year in . it be a pity as your mother 's ta'en this time for t ' fall ill and want yo ' back so early . ' 

 Sylvia have take off her hat and cloak by this time , and begin to help Molly and a young unmarried sister in lay out the substantial supper . 

 ' here , ' continue Mrs. Brunton ; ' stick a bit o ' holly I ' yon pig 's mouth , that be the way we do thing I ' newcassel ; but folk be so behindhand in Monkshaven . it be a fine thing to live in a large town , Sylvia ; an ' if yo're look out for a husband , I would advise yo ' to tak ' one as life in a town . I feel as if I be bury alive comin ' back here , such an out - o'-t'-way place after t ' side , wheere there be many a hundred cart and carriage go past in a day . I have   a great mind for t ' tak yo ' two lassie back wi ' I , and let yo ' see a bit o ' t ' world ; may - be , I may yet . 

 her sister Bessy look much pleased with this plan , but Sylvia be rather inclined to take offence at Molly 's patronizing way , and reply , 

 ' I be none so fond o ' noise and bustle ; why , yo'll not be able to hear yoursel speak wi ' all they cart and carriage . I would rayther bide at home ; let alone that mother can not   spare I . ' 

 it be , perhaps , a rather ungracious way of answer Molly Brunton 's speech , and so she feel it to be , although her invitation have be none of the most courteously word . she irritate Sylvia still far by repeat her last word , 

 ' " mother can not   spare I ; " why , mother will have to spare thee sometime , when t ' time for wedding come . ' 

 ' I be none go to be we d , ' say Sylvia ; ' and if I be , I would niver go far fra ' mother . ' 

 ' eh ! what a spoilt darling it be . how Brunton will laugh when I tell he about yo ' ; Brunton be a rare one for laughin ' . it be a great thing to have get such a merry man for a husband . why ! he have his joke for every one as come into t ' shop ; and he will ha ' something funny to say to everything this evenin ' . ' 

 Bessy see that Sylvia be annoy , and , with more delicacy than her sister , she try to turn the conversation . 

 ' that be a pretty ribbon in thy hair , Sylvia ; I would like to have one o ' t ' same pattern . Feyther like pickle walnut stick about t ' round o ' beef , Molly . ' 

 ' I know what I be about , ' reply Mrs. Brunton , with a toss of her married head . 

 Bessy resume her inquiry . 

 ' be there any more to be have wheere that come fra ' , Sylvia ? ' 

 ' I do not   know , ' reply Sylvia . ' it come fra ' Foster 's , and yo ' can ask . ' 

 ' what might it cost ? ' say Betsy , finger an end of it to test its quality . 

 ' I can not   tell , ' say Sylvia , ' it be a present . ' 

 ' niver mak ' ado about t ' price , ' say molly ; ' I will gi'e thee enough on ' t to tie up thy hair , just like Sylvia 's . only thou hastn't   such wealth o ' curl as she have ; it will niver look t ' same I ' thy straight lock . and who might it be as give it thee , Sylvia ? ' ask the unscrupulous , if good - natured Molly . 

 ' my cousin Philip , he as be shopman at Foster 's , ' say Sylvia , innocently . but it be far too good an opportunity for the exercise of Molly 's kind of wit for she to pass over . 

 ' oh , oh ! our cousin Philip , be it ? and he will not be live so far away from your mother ? I have   no need be a witch to put two and two together . he be a coming here to - night , be not   he , Bessy ? ' 

 ' I wish yo ' would not   talk so , Molly , ' say Sylvia ; ' I and Philip be good enough friend , but we niver think on each other in that way ; leastway , I do not . ' 

 ' sweet butter ! now that be my mother 's old - fashioned way ; as if folk must eat sweet butter now - a - day , because her mother do !   that way , ' continue Molly , in the manner that annoy Sylvia so much , repeat her word as if for the purpose of laugh at they . ' " that way ? " and pray what be t ' way yo're speak on ? I niver say nought about marrying , do I , that yo ' need look so red and shameface about yo'r cousin Philip ? but , as Brunton say , if t ' cap fit yo ' , put it on . I be glad he be comin ' to - night tho ' , for as I be do makin ' love and courtin ' , it be next good t ' watch other folk ; an ' yo'r face , Sylvia , have letten I into a secret , as I have some glimpse on afore I be we d. ' 

 Sylvia secretly determine not to speak a word more to Philip than she could help , and wonder how she could ever have like molly at all , much less have make a companion of she . the table be now lay out , and nothing remain but to criticize the arrangement a little . 

 Bessy be full of admiration . 

 ' Theere , Molly ! ' say she . ' Yo ' niver seed more vittle bring together I ' newcassel , I will be bind ; there will be above half a hundredweight o ' butcher 's meat , beside pie and custard . I have   eat no dinner these two day for think on ' t ; it be be a weary burden on my mind , but it be off now I see how well it look . I tell mother not to come near it till we would spread it all out , and now I will go fetch she . ' 

 Bessy run off into the house - place . 

 ' it be well enough in a country kind o ' way , ' say Molly , with the faint approbation of condescension . ' but if I have think on , I would ha ' bring them down a beast or two do I ' sponge - cake , wi ' currant for his eye to give t ' table an air . ' 

 the door be open , and Bessy come in smile and blush with proud pleasure . her mother follow she on tip - toe , smooth down her apron , and with her voice subdue to a whisper : 

 ' ay , my lass , it be fine ! but dunnot mak ' an ado about it , let them think it be just our common way . if any one say aught about how good t ' vittle be , tak ' it calm , and say we'n well I ' t ' house , it will mak ' them eat wi ' a well appetite , and think the more on we . Sylvie , I be much beholden t ' ye for comin ' so early , and helpin ' t ' lass , but yo ' mun come in t ' house - place now , t ' folk be gatherin ' , an ' yo'r cousin 's be ask after yo ' a'ready . ' 

 molly give she a nudge , which make Sylvia 's face go all aflame with angry embarrassment . she be conscious that the watching which Molly have threaten she with begin directly ; for Molly go up to her husband , and whisper something to he which set he off in a chuckle laugh , and Sylvia be aware that his eye follow she about with know look all the evening . she would hardly speak to Philip , and pretend not to see his outstretched hand , but pass on to the chimney - corner , and try to shelter herself behind the broad back of farmer Corney , who have no notion of relinquish his customary place for all the young people who ever come to the house , or for any old people either , for that matter . it be his household throne , and there he sit with no more idea of abdicate in favour of any comer than King George at St James 's . but he be glad to see his friend ; and have pay they the unwonted compliment of shaving on a week - day , and put on his Sunday coat . the united effort of wife and child have fail to persuade he to make any farth change in his attire ; to all their argument on this head he have reply , 

 ' they as do not   like t ' see I I ' my work - a - day wescut and breech may bide away . ' 

 it be the long sentence he say that day , but he repeat it several time over . he be glad enough to see all the young people , but they be not ' of his kidney , ' as he express it to himself , and he do not feel any call upon himself to entertain they . he leave that to his bustling wife , all smartness and smile , and to his daughter and son - in - law . his effort at hospitality consist in sit still , smoke his pipe ; when any one come , he take it out of his mouth for an instant , and nod his head in a cheerful friendly way , without a word of speech ; and then return to his smoking with the great relish for the moment 's intermission . he think to himself : 

 ' they be a set o ' young chap as think more on t ' lass than on baccy ; they will find out their mistake in time ; give them time , give them time . ' 

 and before eight o'clock , he go as quietly as a man of twelve stone can upstairs to bed , have make a previous arrangement with his wife that she should bring he up about two pound of spiced beef , and a hot tumbler of stiff grog . but at the beginning of the evening he form a good screen for Sylvia , who be rather a favourite with the old man , for twice he speak to she . 

 ' feyther smoke ? ' 

 ' yes , ' say Sylvia . 

 ' reach I t ' baccy - box , my lass . ' 

 and that be all the conversation that pass between she and her near neighbour for the first quarter of an hour after she come into company .

 ' Come , Measter Hepburn , here be Nancy Pratt wi'out ev'n a soul to speak t ' she , an ' yo ' mopin ' theere . she say she know yo ' by sight fra ' have deal at Foster 's these six year . see if yo ' can not   find summut t ' say t ' each other , for I mun go pour out tea . dixon , an ' walker , an ' Elliotts , an ' Smiths be come , ' say she , mark off the family on her finger , as she look round and call over their name ; ' an ' there be only Will Latham an ' his two sister , and Roger Harbottle , an ' Taylor t ' come ; an ' they will turn up afore tea 's end . ' 

 so she go off to her duty at the one table , which , place alongside of the dresser , be the only article of furniture leave in the middle of the room : all the seat be arrange as close to the four wall as could be manage . the candle of those day give but a faint light compare to the light of the immense fire , which it be a point of hospitality to keep at the high roaring , blaze pitch ; the young woman occupy the seat , with the exception of two or three of the elder one , who , in an eager desire to show their capability , insist on help Mrs. Corney in her duty , very much to her annoyance , as there be certain little contrivance for eke out cream , and adjust the strength of the cup of tea to the worldly position of the intend drinker , which she do not like every one to see . the young man , whom tea do not embolden , and who have as yet have no chance of strong liquor , cluster in rustic shyness round the door , not speak even to themselves , except now and then , when one , apparently the wag of the party , make some whisper remark , which set they all off laugh ; but in a minute they check themselves , and pass the back of their hand across their mouth to compose that unlucky feature , and then some would try to fix their eye on the rafter of the ceiling , in a manner which be decorous if rather abstract from the business in hand . Most of these be young farmer , with whom Philip have nothing in common , and from whom , in shy reserve , he have withdraw himself when he first come in . but now he wish himself among they soon than set to talk to Nancy Pratt , when he have nothing to say . and yet he might have have a companion less to his mind , for she be a decent young woman of a sober age , less inclined to giggle than many of the young one . but all the time that he be make commonplace remark to she he be wonder if he have offend Sylvia , and why she would not shake hand with he , and this pre - occupation of his thought do not make he an agreeable companion . Nancy Pratt , who have be engage for some year to a mate of a whaling - ship , perceive something of his state of mind , and take no offence at it ; on the contrary , she try to give he pleasure by admire Sylvia . 

 ' I have   often heerd tell on she , ' say she , ' but I niver think she be be so pretty , and so staid and quiet - like too . T ' most part o ' girl as have look like hers be always gape - gaze to catch other folk 's eye , and see what be think on them ; but she look just like a child , a bit flustered wi ' come into company , and gettin ' into as dark a corner and bidin ' as still as she can .

 ' your cupboard handle be so much I ' th ' way I hurt my elbow against it only this very afternoon . '

 more play with her apron - string , and head hang still low down , though the corner of her mouth would go up in a shy smile of pleasure . Philip watch it all as greedily as if it give he delight .

 but Sylvia steer her careful way to the cold chill of the dairy , and would not be satisfied till she have carry away all the unused provision into some fresher air than that heat by the fire and oven use for the long day 's cooking of pie and cake and much roast meat .

 before long one of the lad be seize with a fit of admiration for Toby the name of the old gentleman who contain liquor and go up to the tray for a close inspection . he be speedily follow by other amateur of curious earthenware ; and by - and - by Mr. Brunton   who have be charge by his mother - in - law with the due supplying of liquor by his father - in - law that every man should have his fill , and by his wife and her sister that no one should have too much , at any rate at the beginning of the evening ,   think fit to carry out Toby to be replenish ; and a fast spirit of enjoyment and mirth begin to reign in the room . 

 Kinraid be too well seasoned to care what amount of liquor he drink ; Philip have what be call a weak head , and dislike muddle himself with drink because of the immediate consequence of intense feeling of irritability , and the more distant one of a rack headache next day ; so both these two preserve very much the same demeanour they have hold at the beginning of the evening . 

 Sylvia be by all acknowledge and treat as the belle . when they play at blind - man's - buff go where she would , she be always catch ; she be call out repeatedly to do what be require in any game , as if all have a pleasure in see her light figure and deft way . she be sufficiently pleased with this to have get over her shyness with all except Charley . when other pay she their rustic compliment she toss her head , and make her little saucy repartee ; but when he say something low and flattering , it be too honey - sweet to her heart to be throw off thus . and , somehow , the more she yield to this fascination the more she avoid Philip . he do not speak flatteringly he do not pay compliment he watch she with discontent , long eye , and grow more inclined every moment , as he remember his anticipation of a happy evening , to cry out in his heart vanita vanitatum . 

 and now come cry the forfeit . molly Brunton kneel down , her face bury in her mother 's lap ; the latter take out the forfeit one by one , and as she hold they up , say the accustomed formula , 

 ' a fine thing and a very fine thing , what must he   or she   do who own this thing . ' 

 one or two have be tell to kneel to the prettiest , bow to the witty , and kiss those they love well ; other have have to bite an inch off the poker , or such play upon word . and now come Sylvia 's pretty new ribbon that Philip have give she   he almost long to snatch it out of Mrs. Corney 's hand and burn it before all their face , so annoyed be he with the whole affair . 

 ' a fine thing and a very fine thing a most particular fine thing choose how she come by it . what must she do as own this thing ? ' 

 ' she must blow out t ' candle and kiss t ' candlestick . ' 

 in one instant Kinraid have hold of the only candle within reach , all the other have be put up high on inaccessible shelf and other place .  Philip 's spirit rise , and he yearn to go to she and tell she how he approve of her conduct . alas , Philip ! ' go home ! not see t ' New Year in ! why , what should take them home these six hour ? be not   there a moon as clear as day ? and do such a time as this come often ? and be they to break up the party before the New Year come in ? and be there not supper , with a spice round of beef that have be in pickle pretty nigh sin ' Martinmas , and ham , and mince - pie , and what not ? and if they think any evil of her master 's go to bed , or that by that early retirement he mean to imply that he do not bid his friend welcome , why he would not stay up beyond eight o'clock for King George upon his throne , as he would tell they soon enough , if they would only step upstairs and ask he . well ; she know what it be to want a daughter when she be ail , so she would say nought more , but hasten supper . 

 and this idea now take possession of Mrs. Corney 's mind , for she would not willingly allow one of her guest to leave before they have do justice to her preparation ; and , cut her speech short , she hastily leave Sylvia and Philip together .

 by this time her cloak and hood be on , and she be slowly make her way down her side of the room follow by Charley , and often interrupt by indignant remonstrance against her departure , and the early breaking - up of the party . Philip stand , hat in hand , in the doorway between the kitchen and parlour , watch she so intently that he forget to be civil , and draw many a jest and gibe upon he for his absorption in his pretty cousin .

 ' yo're ready at last , be yo ' ? '

 ' no , ' say Sylvia , in affright at Philip 's fierce look and passionate tone . ' I tell he not . ' 

 but at that moment the heavy outer door be open by Daniel Robson himself bright , broad , and rosy , a jolly impersonation of Winter . his large drover 's coat be cover with snow - flake , and through the black frame of the doorway might be see a white waste world of sweep fell and field , with the dark air fill with the pure down - fall . Robson stamp his snow - laden foot and shake himself well , still stand on the mat , and let a cold frosty current of fresh air into the great warm kitchen . he laugh at they all before he speak . 

 ' it be a coud new year as I be lettin ' in though it be noan t ' new year yet . Yo'll a ' be snow up , as sure as my name s Dannel , if yo ' stop for twel ' o'clock . Yo'd well mak ' haste and go whoam . why , Charley , my lad ! how beest ta ? who would ha ' think o ' see thee I ' these part again ! Nay , missus , nay , t ' new year mun find its way int ' t ' house by itsel ' for I ; for a ha ' promise my oud woman to bring Sylvie whoam as quick as may - be ; she be lyin ' awake and frettin ' about t ' snow and what not . thank yo ' kindly , missus , but a'll tak ' nought to eat ; just a drop o ' something hot to keep out coud , and wish yo ' a ' the compliment o ' the season . Philip , my man , yo'll not be sorry to be spar t ' walk round by Haytersbank such a neet . my missus be I ' such a way about Sylvie that a thought a'd just step off mysel ' , and have a peep at yo ' a ' , and bring she some wrap . Yo'r sheep will be a ' fold , a reckon , Measter Pratt , for there will niver be a nibble o ' grass to be see this two month , accordin ' to my readin ' ; and a've   be at sea long enough , and on land long enough t ' know sign and wonder . it be good stuff that , any way , and worth comin ' for , ' after he have gulp down a tumblerful of half - and - half grog . " ' 

 Sylvia be all this while stand by her father quite ready for departure , and not a little relieved by his appearance as her convoy home . 

 ' I be ready to see Haytersbank to - night , master ! ' say Kinraid , with easy freedom a freedom which Philip envy , but could not have imitate , although he be deeply disappointed at the loss of his walk with Sylvia , when he have intend to exercise the power his aunt have delegate to he of remonstrance if her behaviour have be light or thoughtless , and of warn if he see cause to disapprove of any of her associate . 

 after the robson have leave , a blank fall upon both Charley and Philip . in a few minute , however , the former , accustom to prompt decision , resolve that she and no other should be his wife . accustomed to popularity among woman , and well verse in the incipient sign of their liking for he , he anticipate no difficulty in win she . satisfy with the past , and pleasantly hopeful about the future , he find it easy to turn his attention to the next prettiest girl in the room , and to make the whole gathering bright with his ready good temper and buoyant spirit . 

 Mrs. Corney have feel it her duty to press Philip to stay , now that , as she say , he have no one but himself to see home , and the new year so near come in . to any one else in the room she would have add the clinching argument , ' a shall take it very unkind if yo ' go now ' ; but somehow she could not say this , for in truth Philip 's look show that he would be but a wet blanket on the merriment of the party . so , with as much civility as could be muster up between they , he take leave . shut the door behind he , he go out into the dreary night , and begin his lonesome walk back to Monkshaven . the cold sleet almost blind he as the sea - wind drive it straight in his face ; it cut against he as it be blow with drift force . the roar of the wintry sea come bear on the breeze ; there be more light from the whitened ground than from the dark laden sky above . the field - path would have be a matter of perplexity , have it not be for the well - know gap in the dyke - side , which show the whitened land beyond , between the two dark stone wall . yet he go clear and straight along his way , have unconsciously leave all guidance to the animal instinct which co - exist with the human soul , and sometimes take strange charge of the human body , when all the nobler power of the individual be absorb in acute suffering . at length he be in the lane , toil up the hill , from which , by day , Monkshaven might be see . now all feature of the landscape before he be lose in the darkness of night , against which the white flake come close and near , thick and fast . on a sudden , the bell of Monkshaven church ring out a welcome to the new year , 1796 . from the direction of the wind , it seem as if the sound be fling with strength and power right into Philip 's face . he walk down the hill to its merry sound its merry sound , his heavy heart . as he enter the long High Street of Monkshaven he could see the watch light put out in parlour , chamber , or kitchen . the new year have come , and expectation be end . reality have begin . 

 he turn to the right , into the court where he lodge with Alice Rose . there be a light still burn there , and cheerful voice be hear . he open the door ; Alice , her daughter , and Coulson stand as if await he . hester 's wet cloak hang on a chair before the fire ; she have her hood on , for she and Coulson have be to the watch - night . 

 the solemn excitement of the service have leave its trace upon her countenance and in her mind . there be a spiritual light in her usually shadowed eye , and a slight flush on her pale cheek . merely personal and self - conscious feeling be merge in a love good - will to all her fellow - creature . under the influence of this large charity , she forget her habitual reserve , and come forward as Philip enter to meet he with her new year 's wish wish that she have previously interchange with the other two . 

 ' a happy new year to you , Philip , and may God have you in his keeping all the day thereof ! ' 

 he take her hand , and shake it warmly in reply . the flush on her cheek deepen as she withdraw it . Alice Rose say something curtly about the lateness of the hour and her be much tired ; and then she and her daughter go upstairs to the front chamber , and Philip and Coulson to that which they share at the back of the house . 




 CHAPTER XIII 

 perplexitie 

 Coulson and Philip be friendly , but not intimate . they never have have a dispute , they never be confidential with each other ; in truth , they be both reserved and silent man , and , probably , respect each other the more for be so self - contain . there be a private feeling in Coulson 's heart which would have make a less amiable fellow dislike Philip . but of this the latter be unconscious : they be not apt to exchange many word in the room which they occupy jointly . 

 Coulson ask Philip if he have enjoy himself at the Corneys ' , and Philip reply , 

 ' not much ; such party be noane to my liking . ' 

 ' and yet thou break off from t ' watch - night to go there . ' 

 no answer ; so Coulson go on , with a sense of the duty lay upon he , to improve the occasion the first that have present itself since the good old Methodist minister have give his congregation the solemn warning to watch over the opportunity of various kind which the come year would present . 

 ' Jonas Barclay tell we as the pleasure o ' this world be like apple o ' Sodom , pleasant to look at , but ash to taste . ' 

 Coulson wisely leave Philip to make the application for himself . if he do he make no sign , but throw himself on his bed with a heavy sigh .

 there have be a long pause of silence . Philip do not answer he , and he think he have fall asleep . but he be rouse from his first slumber by Hepburn 's soft movement about the room . Philip have think well of it , and , with some penitence in his heart for his gruffness to the unoffending Coulson , be try not to make any noise while he undress . 

 but he could not sleep . he keep see the Corneys ' kitchen and the scene that have take place in it , pass like a pageant before his closed eye . then he open they in angry weariness at the recur vision , and try to make out the outline of the room and the furniture in the darkness . the white ceiling slop into the whitewashed wall , and against they he could see the four rush - bottom chair , the look - glass hang on one side , the old carve oak - chest   his own property , with the initial of forgotten ancestor cut upon it , which hold his clothe ; the box that belong to Coulson , sleep soundly in the bed in the opposite corner of the room ; the casement window in the roof , through which the snowy ground on the steep hill - side could be plainly see ; and when he get so far as this in the catalogue of the room , he fall into a troubled feverish sleep , which last two or three hour ; and then he awake with a start , and a consciousness of uneasiness , though what about he could not remember at first . 

 when he recollect all that have happen the night before , it impress he much more favourably than it have do at the time . if not joy , hope have come in the morning ; and , at any rate , he could be up and be do , for the late wintry light be steal down the hill - side , and he know that , although Coulson lie motionless in his sleep , it be past their usual time of rise . still , as it be new year 's day , a time of some licence , Philip have mercy on his fellow - shopman , and do not waken he till just as he be leave the room .

Philip go about undo the shutter , and try to break up the rake coal , with as little noise as might be , for he have compassion on the tired sleeper . the kettle have not be fill , probably because Mrs. Rose have be unable to face the storm of the night before , in take it to the pump just at the entrance of the court . when Philip come back from fill it , he find Alice and Hester both in the kitchen , and try to make up for lost time by hasten over their work . hester look busy and notable with her gown pin up behind she , and her hair all tuck away under a clean linen cap ; but Alice be angry with herself for her late sleeping , and that and other cause make she speak crossly to Philip , as he come in with his snowy foot and well - fill kettle .

 during breakfast something be learn nearer home ; though not all that a man less unconscious and more vain than Philip might have discover . he only find out that Mrs. Rose be displeased with he for not have go to the watch - night with hester , accord to the plan make some week before . but he soothe his conscience by remember that he have make no promise ; he have merely speak of his wish to be present at the service , about which hester be speak ; and although at the time and for a good while afterwards , he have fully intend going , yet as there have be William Coulson to accompany she , his absence could not have be seriously notice . still he be make uncomfortable by Mrs. Rose 's change of manner ; once or twice he say to himself that she little know how miserable he have be during his ' gay evening , ' as she would persist in call it , or she would not talk at he with such persevere bitterness this morning . before he leave for the shop , he speak of his intention of go to see how his aunt be , and of pay she a new year 's day visit . 

 Hepburn and Coulson take it in turn week and week about to go first home to dinner ; the one who go first sate down with Mrs. Rose and her daughter , instead of have his portion put in the oven to keep warm for he . to - day it be Hepburn 's turn to be last . all morning the shop be full with customer , come rather to offer good wish than to buy , and with an unspoken remembrance of the cake and wine which the two hospitable brother Foster make a point of offering to all comer on new year 's day . it be busy work for all for hester on her side , where cap , ribbon , and woman 's gear be exclusively sell for the shopman and boy in the grocery and drapery department . Philip be try to do his business with his mind far away ; and the consequence be that his manner be not such as to recommend he to the customer , some of whom recollect it as very different , courteous and attentive , if grave and sedate . one buxom farmer 's wife notice the change to he . she have a little girl with she , of about five year old , that she have lift up on the counter , and who be watch Philip with anxious eye , occasionally whisper in her mother 's ear , and then hide her face against her cloak . 

 ' she be think a deal o ' come to see yo ' , and a dunnot think as yo ' mind she at all . my pretty , he be clean forget as how he say last new year 's day , he would gi ' thee a barley - sugar stick , if thou'd hem he a handkercher by this . ' 

 the child 's face be bury in the comfortable breadth of duffle at these word , while the little outstretched hand hold a small square of coarse linen .

Coulson be , however , the most resolute ; and when she return from the parlour the little parcel be in Philip 's hand .

 there be a lull of business for the next hour . John and Jeremiah be din like the rest of the world . even the eld errand - boy have vanish .  no ; he be resolve he would go on ; as long as there be life there be hope ; as long as Sylvia remain unpledged to any one else , there be a chance for he . he would remodel his behaviour to she . he could not be merry and light - hearted like other young man ; his nature be not cast in that mould ; and the early sorrow that have leave he a lonely orphan might have mature , but have not enliven , his character . he think with some bitterness on the power of easy talk about trifle which some of those he have meet with at the Corneys ' have exhibit . but then he feel stir within he a force of endure love which he believe to be unusual , and which seem as if it must compel all thing to his wish in the end . a year or so ago he have think much of his own cleverness and his painfully acquire learning , and he have imagine that these be the quality which be to gain Sylvia . but now , whether he have try they and have fail to win even her admiration , or whether some true instinct have tell he that a woman 's love may be gain in many way soon than by mere learning , he be only angry with himself for his past folly in make himself her school nay , her taskmaster . to - night , though , he would start off on a new tack . he would not even upbraid she for her conduct the night before ; he have show she his displeasure at the time ; but she should see how tender and forgiving he could be . he would lure she to he rather than find fault with she . there have perhaps be too much of that already .

'

 ' Shop here , shop there . the flesh and the devil be gettin ' hold on yo ' , and yo ' need more nor iver to seek t ' way o ' grace . new year 's day come and say , " watch and pray , " and yo ' say , " Nay , I will seek feast and market - place , and let time and season come and go without heedin ' into whose presence they be hasten I . " Time be , Philip , when thou'd niver ha ' letten a merry - make keep thee fra ' t ' watch - night , and t ' company o ' the godly . '

'

 Philip redden . he could not and would not give it up , and yet it be difficult to resist the pleading of the usually stern old woman .

I can do nought again Satan , but I can speak to they as can ; an ' we will see which pull hard , for it will be well for thee to be rive and rent I ' twain than to go body and soul to hell . '

' and once more , after lay his hand with as much of a caress as be in his nature on her , he leave the house . 

 probably Alice would have consider the first word that greet Philip on his entrance into the shop as an answer to her prayer , for they be such as put a stop to his plan of go to see Sylvia that evening ; and if Alice have form her inchoate thought into word , Sylvia would have appear as the near earthly representative of the spirit of temptation whom she dread for Philip .
