 molly Brunton begin to say something , and he gladly turn to look at she . she be ask he why he go away so early , for they have keep it up for four hour after he leave , and last of all , she add   turn to Kinraid , her cousin Charley have dance a hornpipe among the platter on the ground . 

 Philip hardly know what he say in reply , the mention of that pas seul lift such a weight off his heart . he could smile now , after his grave fashion , and would have shake hand again with Kinraid have it be require ; for it seem to he that no one , care ever so little in the way that he do for Sylvia , could have bear four mortal hour of a company where she have be , and be not ; least of all could have dance a hornpipe , either from gaiety of heart , or even out of complaisance . he feel as if the yearning after the absent one would have be a weight to his leg , as well as to his spirit ; and he imagine that all man be like himself . 




 CHAPTER XIV 

 PARTNERSHIP 

 as darkness close in , and the New Year 's throng become scarce , Philip 's hesitation about accompany Coulson fade away . he be more comfortable respect Sylvia , and his go to see she might be defer ; and , after all , he feel that the wish of his master ought to be attend to , and the honour of an invitation to the private house of Jeremiah not to be slight for anything short of a positive engagement . besides , the ambitious man of business exist strongly in Philip . it would never do to slight advance towards the second great earthly object in his life ; one also on which the first depend . 

 so when the shop be close , the two set out down Bridge Street to cross the river to the house of Jeremiah Foster . they stand a moment on the bridge to breathe the keen fresh sea air after their busy day . the water come down , swell full and dark , with rapid rush speed from the snow - feed spring high up on the moorland above . the close - pack house in the old town seem a cluster of white roof irregularly pile against the more unbroken white of the hill - side . light twinkle here and there in the town , and be sling from stern and bow of the ship in the harbour . the air be very still , settle in for a frost ; so still that all distant sound seem near : the rumble of a return cart in the High Street , the voice on board ship , the closing of shutter and barring of door in the new town to which they be bind . but the sharp air be fill , as it be , with saline particle in a freezing state ; little pungent crystal of sea salt burn lip and cheek with their cold keenness . it would not do to linger here in the very centre of the valley up which pass the current of atmosphere come straight with the rush tide from the icy northern sea . besides , there be the unusual honour of a supper with Jeremiah Foster await they . he have ask each of they separately to a meal before now ; but they have never go together , and they feel that there be something serious in the conjuncture . 

 they begin to climb the steep height lead to the freshly - build row of the new town of Monkshaven , feel as if they be rise into aristocratic region where no shop profane the street . Jeremiah Foster 's house be one of six , undistinguished in size , or shape , or colour ; but notice in the daytime by all passer - by for its spotless cleanliness of lintel and doorstep , window and window frame . the very brick seem as though they come in for the daily scrubbing which brighten handle , knocker , all down to the very scraper . 

 the two young man feel as shy of the interview with their master under such unusual relation of guest and host , as a girl do of her first party . each rather draw back from the decide step of knock at the door ; but with a rebuffing shake at his own folly , Philip be the one to give a loud single rap . as if they have be wait for , the door fly open , and a middle - aged servant stand behind , as spotless and neat as the house itself ; and smile a welcome to the familiar face . 

 ' let I dust yo ' a bit , William , ' say she , suit the action to the word . ' you have   be leanin ' again some whitewash , a'll be bind . ay , Philip , ' continue she , turn he round with motherly freedom , ' yo'll do if yo'll but gi ' your shoon a polishin ' wipe on yon other mat . this'n for takin ' t ' roughest mud off . Measter allay polish on that . ' 

 in the square parlour the same precise order be observe . every article of furniture be free from speck of dirt or particle of dust ; and everything be place either in a parallel line , or at exact right - angle with every other . even John and Jeremiah sit in symmetry on opposite side of the fire - place ; the very smile on their honest face seem draw to a line of exactitude . 

 such formality , however admirable , be not calculate to promote ease : it be not until after supper until a good quantity of Yorkshire pie have be swallow , and wash down , too , with the good and most generous wine in Jeremiah 's cellar that there be the least geniality among they , in spite of the friendly kindness of the host and his brother . the long silence , during which mute thank for the meal be give , have come to an end , Jeremiah call for pipe , and three of the party begin to smoke . 

 politic in those day be tickle subject to meddle with , even in the most private company . the nation be in a state of terror against France , and against any at home who might be suppose to sympathise with the enormity she have just be commit . the oppressive act against seditious meeting have be pass the year before ; and people be doubtful to what extremity of severity it might be construe . even the law authority forget to be impartial , but either their alarm or their interest make too many of they vehement partisan instead of calm arbiter , and thus destroy the popular confidence in what should have be consider the supreme tribunal of justice . yet for all this , there be some who dare to speak of reform of Parliament , as a preliminary step to fair representation of the people , and to a reduction of the heavy war - taxation that be imminent , if not already impose . but these pioneer of 1830 be generally obnoxious . the great body of the people glory in be Tories and hater of the French , with whom they be on tenter - hook to fight , almost unaware of the rise reputation of the young corsican warrior , whose name would be use ere a dozen year have pass to hush english baby with a terror such as that of Marlborough once have for the French . 

 at such a place as Monkshaven all these opinion be hold in excess . one or two might , for the mere sake of argument , dispute on certain point of history or government ; but they take care to be very sure of their listener before such argument touch on anything of the present day ; for it have be not unfrequently find that the public duty of prosecute opinion not your own overrode the private duty of respect confidence . Most of the monkshaven politician confine themselves , therefore , to such general question as these : ' could an Englishman lick more than four frenchman at a time ? ' ' what be the proper punishment for member of the Corresponding Society   correspondence with the french directory , hang and quarter , or burn ? ' ' would the forthcoming child of the Princess of Wales be a boy or a girl ? if a girl , would it be more loyal to call it Charlotte or Elizabeth ? ' 

 the Fosters be quite secure enough of their guest this evening to have speak freely on politic have they be so inclined . and they do begin on the outrage which have be lately offer to the king in cross St James 's Park to go and open the House of Lords ; but soon , so accustomed be their mind to caution and restraint , the talk drop down to the high price of provision . bread at 1 . 3d . the quartern loaf , accord to the London test . wheat at 120s . per quarter , as the home - bake northerner view the matter ; and then the conversation die away to an ominous silence . John look at Jeremiah , as if ask he to begin . Jeremiah be the host , and have be a married man . Jeremiah return the look with the same meaning in it . John , though a bachelor , be the eld brother . the great church bell , bring from the monkshaven monastery century ago , high up on the opposite hill - side , begin to ring nine o'clock ; it be get late . Jeremiah begin : 

 ' it seem a bad time for start any one on business , wi ' price and taxis and bread so dear ; but John and I be get into year , and we have   no child to follow we : yet we would fain draw out of some of our worldly affair . we would like to give up the shop , and stick to banking , to which there seemeth a plain path . but first there be the stock and goodwill of the shop to be dispose on . ' 

 a dead pause . this opening be not favourable to the hope of the two moneyless young man who have be hop to succeed their master by the more gradual process of partnership . but it be only the kind of speech that have be agree upon by the two brother with a view of impress on Hepburn and Coulson the great and unusual responsibility of the situation into which the Fosters wish they to enter . in some way the talk of many be much less simple and straightforward in those day than it be now . the study of effect show in the London diner - out of the last generation , who prepare their conversation beforehand , be not without its parallel in humbler sphere , and for different object than self - display . the brother Foster have all but rehearse the speech they be about to make this evening . they be aware of the youth of the party to whom they be go to make a most favourable proposal ; and they dread that if that proposal be too lightly make , it would be too lightly consider , and the duty involve in it too carelessly enter upon . so the role of one brother be to suggest , that of the other to repress . the young man , too , have their reserve . they foresaw , and have long foresee , what be come that evening . they be impatient to hear it in distinct word ; and yet they have to wait , as if unconscious , during all the long preamble . do age and youth never play the same part now ? to return . John Foster reply to his brother : 

 ' the stock and goodwill ! that would take much wealth . and there will be fixture to be consider . Philip , canst thee tell I the exact amount of stock in the shop at present ? ' 

 it have only just be take ; Philip have it at his finger ' end . ' one thousand nine hundred and forty - one pound , thirteen shilling and twopence . ' 

 Coulson look at he in a little dismay , and could not repress a sigh . the figure put into word and speak aloud seem to indicate so much large an amount of money than when quickly write down in numeral . but Philip read the countenance , nay , by some process of which he be not himself aware , he read the mind of the brother , and feel no dismay at what he see there . 

 ' and the fixture ? ' ask John Foster . 

 ' the appraiser value they at four hundred and thirty - five pound three and sixpence when father die . we have add to they since , but we will reckon they at that . how much do that make with the value of the stock ? ' 

 ' two thousand one hundred and seventy - six pound , sixteen shilling and eightpence , ' say Philip . 

 Coulson have do the sum quick , but be too much dishearten by the amount to speak . 

 ' and the goodwill ? ' ask the pitiless John . ' what dost thee set that at ? ' 

 ' I think , brother , that that would depend on who come forward with the purchase - money of the stock and fixture . to some folk we might make it sit easy , if they be know to we , and those as we wish well to . if Philip and William here , for instance , say they would like to purchase the business , I reckon thee and I would not ask them so much as we should ask Millers '   miller be an upstart petty rival shop at the end of the bridge in the New Town . 

 ' I wish Philip and William be to come after we , ' say John . ' but that be out of the question , ' he continue , know all the while that , far from be out of the question , it be the very question , and that it be as good as settle at this very time . 

 no one speak . then Jeremiah go on : 

 ' it be out of the question , I reckon ? ' 

 he look at the two young man . Coulson shake his head . Philip more bravely say , 

 ' I have fifty - three pound seven and fourpence in yo'r hand , Master John , and it be all I have I ' the world . ' 

 ' it be a pity , ' say John , and again they be silent . half - past nine strike . it be time to be begin to make an end . ' perhaps , brother , they have friend who could advance them the money . we might make it sit light to they , for the sake of their good service ? ' 

 Philip reply , 

 ' there be no one who can put forwards a penny for I : I have but few kin , and they have little to spare beyond what they need . ' 

 Coulson say 

 ' my father and mother have nine on we . ' 

 ' let alone , let alone ! ' say John , relent fast ; for he be weary of his part of cold , stern prudence . ' brother , I think we have enough of this world 's good to do what we like wi ' our own . ' 

 Jeremiah be a little scandalize at the rapid melting away of assumed character , and take a good pull at his pipe before he reply 

 ' Upwards of two thousand pound be a large sum to set on the well - being and well - doing of two lad , the elder of whom be not three - and - twenty . I fear we must look far a - field . ' 

 ' why , John , ' reply Jeremiah , ' it be but yesterday thee saidst thee would rather have Philip and William than any man o ' fifty that thee know . and now to bring up their youth again they . ' 

 ' well , well ! t ' half on it be thine , and thou shall do even as thou wilt . but I think as I must have security for my moiety , for it be a risk a great risk . have ye any security to offer ? any expectation ? any legacy , as other folk have a life - interest in at present ? ' 

 no ; neither of they have . so Jeremiah rejoin 

 ' then , I suppose , I mun do as thee dost , John , and take the security of character . and it be a great security too , lad , and t ' good o ' all , and one that I could not   ha ' do without ; no , not if yo'd pay I down five thousand for goodwill , and stock , and fixture . for John Foster and Son have be a shop I ' monkshaven this eighty year and more ; and I dunnot think there be a man live or dead , for that matter as can say Fosters wrong he of a penny , or give short measure to a child or a Cousin Betty . ' 

 they all four shake hand round with the same heartiness as if it have be a legal ceremony necessary to the completion of the partnership . the old man 's face be bright with smile ; the eye of the young one sparkle with hope . 

 ' but , after all , ' say Jeremiah , ' we have   not tell you particular . Yo're thank we for a pig in a poke ; but we have more forethought , and we put all down on a piece o ' paper . ' 

 he take down a fold piece of paper from the mantel - shelf , put on his horn spectacle , and begin to read aloud , occasionally peer over his glass to note the effect on the countenance of the young man . the only thing he be in the habit of read aloud be a chapter in the Bible daily to his housekeeper servant ; and , like many , he reserve a peculiar tone for that solemn occupation a tone which he unconsciously employ for the present enumeration of pound , shilling , and penny . 

 ' average return of the last three year , one hundred and twenty - seven pound , three shilling , and seven penny and one - sixth a week . profit thereupon thirty - four per cent . as near as may be . clear profit of the concern , after deduct all expense except rent for t ' house be our own one thousand two hundred and two pound a year . ' 

 this be far more than either Hepburn or Coulson have imagine it to be ; and a look of surprise , almost amount to dismay , creep over their face , in spite of their endeavour to keep simply motionless and attentive . 

 ' it be a deal of money , lad , and the Lord give you grace to guide it , ' say Jeremiah , put down his paper for a minute . 

 ' amen , ' say John , shake his head to give effect to his word . 

 ' now what we propose be this , ' continue Jeremiah , begin afresh to refer to his paper : ' we will call t ' value of stock and fixture two thousand one hundred and fifty . you may have John Holden , appraiser and auctioneer , in to set a price on they if yo ' will ; or yo ' may look over book and bill ; or , well still , do both , and so check one again t'other ; but for t ' sake o ' make the ground o ' the bargain , I state the sum as above ; and I reckon it so much capital leave in yo'r hand for the use o ' which yo're bind to pay we five per cent . quarterly that be one hundred and seven pound ten per annum at least for t ' first year ; and after it will be reduce by the gradual payment on our money , which must be at the rate of twenty per cent . , thus pay we our principal back in five year . and the rent , include all back yard , right of wharfage , warehouse , and premise , be reckon by we to be sixty - five pound per annum . so yo ' will have to pay we , John and Jeremiah Foster , brother , six hundred and twelve pound ten out of the profit of the first year , leave , at the present rate of profit , about five hundred and eighty - nine pound ten , for the share to be divide between yo ' . ' 

 the plan have , in all its detail , be carefully arrange by the two brother . they be afraid lest Hepburn and Coulson should be dazzle by the amount of profit , and have so arrange the slide - scale of payment as to reduce the first year 's income to what the eld man think a very moderate sum , but what to the young one appear an amount of wealth such as they , who have neither of they ever own much more than fifty pound , consider almost inexhaustible . it be certainly a remarkable instance of prosperity and desert meeting together so early in life . 

 for a moment or two the brother be disappoint at not hear any reply from either of they . then Philip stand up , for he feel as if anything he could say sit down would not be sufficiently expressive of gratitude , and William instantly follow his example . Hepburn begin in a formal manner , something the way in which he have read in the York newspaper that honourable member return thank when their health be give . 

 ' I can hardly express my feeling '   Coulson nudge he   ' his feeling , too of gratitude . oh , Master John ! Master Jeremiah , I think it might come I ' time ; nay , I have   think it might come afore long ; but I niver think as it would be so much , or make so easy . we have   get good kind friend we have , have we not , William ? and we will do our good , and I hope as we shall come up to their wish . ' 

 Philip 's voice quiver a little , as some remembrance pass across his mind ; at this unusual moment of expansion out it come . ' I wish mother could ha ' see this day . ' 

 ' she shall see a well day , my lad , when thy name and William 's be paint over t ' shop - door , and J. and J. Foster black out . ' 

 ' Nay , master , ' say William , ' that mun never be . I would a'most soon not come in for the business . anyhow , it must be ' late J. and J. Foster , ' and I be not sure as I can stomach that . ' 

 ' well , well , William , ' say John Foster , highly gratified , ' there be time enough to talk over that . there be one thing more to be say , be there not , brother Jeremiah ? we do not wish to have this talk over in Monkshaven until shortly before the time when yo ' must enter on the business . we have our own arrangement to make wi ' regard to the banking concern , and there will be lawyer 's work to do , after yo've   examine book and look over stock again together ; may - be we have   overstate it , or t ' fixture be not   worth so much as we say . anyhow yo ' must each on yo ' give we yo'r word for to keep fra ' name this night 's conversation to any one . meantime , Jeremiah and I will have to pay account , and take a kind of farewell of the merchant and manufacturer with whom foster have have dealing this seventy or eighty year ; and when and where it seem fitting to we we will take one of yo ' to introduce as our successor and friend . but all that be to come . but yo ' must each give we yo'r word not to name what have pass here to any one till further speech on the subject have pass between we . ' 

 Coulson immediately give the promise . Philip 's assent come lag . he have think of Sylvia live , almost as much as of the dead mother , whose last word have be a committal of her child to the Father of the friendless ; and now that a short delay be place between the sight of the cup and his enjoyment of it , there be an impatient chafing in the mind of the composed and self - restrain Philip ; and then repentance quick as lightning efface the feeling , and he pledge himself to the secrecy which be enjoin . some few more detail as to their mode of procedure of verify the Fosters ' statement , which to the young man seem a perfectly unnecessary piece of business of probable journey and introduction , and then farewell be bid , and Hepburn and Coulson be in the passage don their wrap , and rather to their indignation be assist therein by Martha , who be accustom to the office with her own master . suddenly they be recall into the parlour . 

 John Foster be fumble with the paper a little nervously : Jeremiah speak 

 ' we have not think it necessary to commend hester Rose to you ; if she have be a lad she would have have a third o ' the business along wi ' yo ' . be a woman , it be ill trouble she with a partnership ; well give she a fix salary till such time as she marry . ' 

 he look a little knowingly and curiously at the face of the young man he address . William Coulson seem sheepish and uncomfortable , but say nothing , leave it as usual to Philip to be spokesman . 

 ' if we have not   care for Hester for hersel ' , master , we should ha ' care for she as be forespeak by yo ' . Yo ' and Master John shall fix what we ought t ' pay she ; and I think I may make bold to say that , as our income rise , hers shall too eh , Coulson ? '   a sound of assent quite distinct enough ; ' for we both look on she as a sister and on Alice like a mother , as I tell she only this very day . ' 




 CHAPTER XV 

 a difficult question 

 Philip go to bed with that kind of humble penitent gratitude in his heart , which we sometimes feel after a sudden revulsion of feeling from despondency to hope . the night before it seem as if all event be so arrange as to thwart he in his dearest wish ; he feel now as if his discontent and repining , not twenty - four hour before , have be almost impious , so great be the change in his circumstance for the well . now all seem promise for the fulfilment of what he most desire . he be almost convinced that he be mistake in think that Kinraid have have anything more than a sailor 's admiration for a pretty girl with regard to Sylvia ; at any rate , he be go away to - morrow , in all probability not to return for another year   for Greenland ship leave for the northern sea as soon as there be a chance of the ice be break up , and ere then he himself might speak out openly , lay before her parent all his fortunate prospect , and before she all his deep passionate love . 

 so this night his prayer be more than the mere form that they have be the night before ; they be a vehement expression of gratitude to God for have , as it be , interfere on his behalf , to grant he the desire of his eye and the lust of his heart . he be like too many of we , he do not place his future life in the hand of God , and only ask for grace to do his will in whatever circumstance might arise ; but he yearn in that terrible way after a blessing which , when grant under such circumstance , too often turn out to be equivalent to a curse . and that spirit bring with it the material and earthly idea that all event that favour our wish be answer to our prayer ; and so they be in one sense , but they need prayer in a deep and high spirit to keep we from the temptation to evil which such event invariably bring with they . 

 Philip little know how Sylvia 's time have be pass that day . if he have , he would have lay down this night with even a heavy heart than he have do on the last . 

 Charley Kinraid accompany his cousin as far as the spot where the path to Haytersbank Farm diverge . then he stop his merry talk , and announce his intention of go to see farmer Robson . Bessy Corney look disappointed and a little sulky ; but her sister Molly Brunton laugh , and say , 

 ' tell truth , lad ! Dannel Robson would niver have a call fra ' thee if he have not   a pretty daughter . ' 

 ' indeed , but he would , ' reply Charley , rather annoyed ; ' when I have   say a thing , I do it . I promise last night to go see he ; besides , I like the old man . ' 

 ' well ! when shall we tell mother yo're comin ' whoam ? ' 

 ' toward eight o'clock may - be soon . ' 

 ' why it be bare five now ! bless t ' lad , do he think o ' stay theere a ' neet , and they up so late last night , and Mrs. Robson ail beside ? mother will not think it kind on yo ' either , will she , Bess ? ' 

 ' I dunno . Charley mun do as he like ; I daresay no one'll miss he if he do bide away till eight . ' 

 ' well , well ! I can not   tell what I shall do ; but yo'd good not stop linger here , for it be get on , and there will be a keen frost by t ' look o ' the star . ' 

 Haytersbank be close for the night as far as it ever be close ; there be no shutter to the window , nor do they care to draw the inside curtain , so few be the passer - by . the house door be fasten ; but the shippen door a little on in the same long low block of building stand open , and a dim light make an oblong upon the snowy ground outside . as Kinraid draw near he hear talk there , and a woman 's voice ; he throw a pass glance through the window into the fire - light house - place , and see Mrs. Robson asleep by the fireside in her easy - chair , he go on . 

 there be the intermittent sound of the sharp whistling of milk into the pail , and Kester , sit on a three - legged stool , cajole a capricious cow into let her fragrant burden flow . Sylvia stand near the farther window - ledge , on which a horn lantern be place , pretend to knit at a gray worsted stocking , but in reality laugh at Kester 's futile endeavour , and find quite enough to do with her eye , in keep herself untouched by the whisk tail , or the occasional kick . the frosty air be mellow by the warm and odorous breath of the cattle breath that hang about the place in faint misty cloud . there be only a dim light ; such as it be , it be not dearly define against the dark heavy shadow in which the old black rafter and manger and partition be envelop . 

 as Charley come to the door , Kester be say , ' quiet wi ' thee , wench ! theere now , she be a beauty , if she will stand still . there be niver sich a cow I ' t ' riding ; if she will only behave hersel ' . she be a bonny lass , she be ; let down her milk , theere be a pretty ! ' 

 ' why , Kester , ' laugh Sylvia , ' thou'rt ask she for her milk wi ' as many pretty speech as if thou wert woo a wife ! ' 

 ' hey , lass ! ' say Kester , turn a bit towards she , and shut one eye to cock the other the well upon she ; an operation which pucker up his already wrinkle face into a thousand new line and fold . ' an ' how do thee know how a man woo a wife , that thee talk so knowin ' about it ? that be tellin ' . some un 's be tryin ' it on thee . ' 

 ' there be niver a one be so impudent , ' say Sylvia , redden and toss her head a little ; ' I would like to see them try I ! ' 

 ' well , well ! ' say Kester , wilfully misunderstand her meaning , ' thou mun be patient , wench ; and if thou be a good lass , may - be thy turn will come and they will try it . ' 

 ' I wish thou'd talk of what thou be some knowledge on , Kester , i'stead of I ' that silly way , ' reply Sylvia . 

 ' then a mun talk no more 'bout woman , for they be past knowin ' , an ' druv e'en King Solomon silly . ' 

 at this moment Charley step in . Sylvia give a little start and drop her ball of worsted . Kester make as though absorb in his task of cajole Black Nell ; but his eye and ear be both vigilant . 

 ' I be go into the house , but I see yo'r mother asleep , and I do not   like to waken she , so I just come on here . be yo'r father to the fore ? ' 

 ' no , ' say Sylvia , hang down her head a little , wonder if he could have hear the way in which she and Kester have be talk , and think over her little foolish joke with anger against herself . ' Father be go to Winthrop about some pig as he be heerd on . he will not be back till seven o'clock or so . ' 

 it be but half - past five , and Sylvia in the irritation of the moment believe that she wish Kinraid would go . but she would have be extremely disappointed if he have . Kinraid himself seem to have no thought of the kind . he see with his quick eye , not unaccustomed to woman , that his come so unexpectedly have flutter Sylvia , and anxious to make she quite at her ease with he , and not unwilling to conciliate Kester , he address his next speech to he , with the same kind of air of interest in the old man 's pursuit that a young man of a different class sometimes put on when talk to the chaperone of a pretty girl in a ball - room . 

 ' that be a handsome beast yo've   just be milk , master . ' 

 ' ay ; but handsome be as handsome do . it be only yesterday as she aim her leg right at t ' pail wi ' t ' aftering in . she know it be aftering as well as any Christian , and t ' more t ' mischief t ' well she like it ; an ' if a have not   be too quick for she , it would have a ' go swash down I ' t ' litter . this'n be a far well cow I ' t ' long run , she be just a steady goer , ' as the milky down - pour come musical and even from the stall next to Black Nell 's . 

 Sylvia be knit away vigorously , think all the while that it be a great pity she have not put on a well gown , or even a cap with bright ribbon , and quite unconscious how very pretty she look stand against the faint light , her head a little bent down ; her hair catch bright golden touch , as it fall from under her little linen cap ; her pink bed - gown , confine by her apron - string , give a sort of easy grace to her figure ; her dark full linsey petticoat short above her trim ancle , look far more suitable to the place where she be stand than her long gown of the night before would have do . Kinraid be want to talk to she , and to make her talk , but be uncertain how to begin . in the meantime Kester go on with the subject last speak about . 

 ' Black Nell 's at her fourth calf now , so she ought to ha ' leave off her trick and turn sober - like . but bless yo ' , there be some cow as will be skittish till they be fat for t ' butcher . not but what a like milk she well nor a steady goer ; a man have allay summat to be watchin ' for ; and a'm kind o ' set up when a've   master she at last . t ' young missus theere , she be mighty fond o ' comin ' t ' see Black Nell at her tantrum . she would niver come near I if a ' cow be like this'n . ' 

 ' do you often come and see the cow milk ? ' ask Kinraid , 

 ' many a time , ' say Sylvia , smile a little . ' why , when we be throng , I help Kester ; but now we have   only Black Nell and Daisy give milk . Kester know as I can milk Black Nell quite easy , ' she continue , half vex that Kester have not name this accomplishment . 

 ' ay ! when she be in a good frame o ' mind , as she be sometimes . but t ' difficulty be to milk she at all time . ' 

 ' I wish I would come a bit soon . I should like t ' have see you milk Black Nell , ' address Sylvia . 

 ' Yo'd well come to - morrow e'en , and see what a hand she will mak ' on she , ' say Kester . 

 ' to - morrow night I shall be far on my road back to Shields . ' 

 ' to - morrow ! ' say Sylvia , suddenly look up at he , and then drop her eye , as she find he have be watch for the effect of his intelligence on she . 

 ' I mun be back at t ' whaler , where I be engage , ' continue he . ' she be fit up after a fresh fashion , and as I have   be one as want new way , I mun be on the spot for t ' look after she . maybe I shall take a run down here afore sail in March . I be sure I shall try . ' 

 there be a good deal mean and understand by these last few word . the tone in which they be speak give they a tender intensity not lose upon either of the hearer . Kester cock his eye once more , but with as little obtrusiveness as he could , and ponder the sailor 's look and way . he remember his come about the place the winter before , and how the old master have then appear to have take to he ; but at that time Sylvia have seem to Kester too little removed from a child to have either art or part in Kinraid 's visit ; now , however , the case be different . Kester in his sphere among his circle of acquaintance , narrow though it be have hear with much pride of Sylvia 's bear away the bell at church and at market , wherever girl of her age be congregate . he be a north countryman , so he give out no further sign of his feeling than his mistress and Sylvia 's mother have do on a like occasion . 

 ' T ' lass be weel enough , ' say he ; but he grin to himself , and look about , and listen to the hearsay of every lad , wonder who be handsome , and brave , and good enough to be Sylvia 's mate . now , of late , it have seem to the canny farm - servant pretty clear that Philip Hepburn be ' after she ' ; and to Philip , Kester have an instinctive objection , a kind of natural antipathy such as have exist in all age between the dweller in a town and those in the country , between agriculture and trade . so , while Kinraid and Sylvia keep up their half - tender , half - jesting conversation , Kester be make up his slow persistent mind as to the desirability of the young man then present as a husband for his darling , as much from his be other than Philip in every respect , as from the individual good quality he possess . Kester 's first opportunity of favour Kinraid 's suit consist in be as long as possible over his milking ; so never be cow that require such ' strip , ' or be expect to yield such ' aftering ' , as Black Nell and Daisy that night . but all thing must come to an end ; and at length Kester get up from his three - legged stool , on see what the other do not that the dip - candle in the lantern be come to an end and that in two or three minute more the shippen would be in darkness , and so his pail of milk be endanger . in an instant Sylvia have start out of her delicious dreamland , her droop eye be raise , and recover their power of observation ; her ruddy arm be free from the apron in which she have enfold they , as a protection from the gathering cold , and she have seize and adjust the wooden yoke across her shoulder , ready to bear the brim milk - pail to the dairy . 

 ' look yo ' at she ! ' exclaim Kester to Charley , as he adjust the fragrant pail on the yoke . ' she think she be missus a ready , and she be allay for carry in t ' milk since t ' rhumatiz cotche my shouther I ' t ' back end ; and when she say " Yea , " it be as much as my heed 's worth to say " Nay . " ' 

 and along the wall , round the corner , down the round slippery stone of the ramble farmyard , behind the building , do Sylvia trip , safe and well - poise , though the ground wear all one coating of white snow , and in many place be so slippery as to oblige Kinraid to linger near Kester , the lantern - bearer . Kester do not lose his opportunity , though the cold misty night air provoke his asthmatic cough when - ever he breathe , and often interrupt his word . 

 ' she be a good wench a good wench as iver be an come on a good stock , an ' that be summat , whether in a cow or a woman . A've   know she from a baby ; she be a reet down good un . ' 

 by this time they have reach the back - kitchen door , just as Sylvia have unladen herself , and be strike a light with flint and tinder . the house seem warm and invite after the pierce outer air , although the kitchen into which they enter contain only a raked and slumber fire at one end , over which , on a crook , hang the immense pan of potato cook for the evening meal of the pig . to this pan Kester immediately address himself , swing it round with ease , owe to the admirable simplicity of the old - fashioned machinery . Kinraid stand between Kester and the door into the dairy , through which Sylvia have vanish with the milk . he half wish to conciliate Kester by help he , but he seem also attract , by a force which annihilate his will , to follow she wherever she go . Kester read his mind . 

 ' let alone , let alone , ' say he ; ' pig ' vittle take noan such dainty carryin ' as milk . a may set it down an ' niver spill a drop ; she be noan fit for t ' serve swine , nor yo ' other , mester ; well help her t ' teem t ' milk . ' 

 so Kinraid follow the light his light into the icy chill of the dairy , where the bright polished tin can be quickly dim with the warm , sweet - smell milk , that Sylvia be empty out into the brown pan . in his haste to help she , Charley take up one of the pail . 

 ' eh ? that'n be to be strain . Yo ' have a ' the cow 's hair in . Mother 's very particular , and can not abide a hair . ' 

 so she go over to her awkward dairymaid , and before she but not before he be aware of the sweet proximity , she be adjust his happy awkward arm to the new office of hold a milk - strainer over the bowl , and pour the white liquid through it . 

 ' there ! ' say she , look up for a moment , and half blushing ; ' now yo'll know how to do it next time . ' 

 ' I wish next time be to come now , ' say Kinraid ; but she have return to her own pail , and seem not to hear he . he follow she to her side of the dairy . ' I have   but a short memory , can yo ' not show I again how t ' hold t ' strainer ? ' 

 ' no , ' say she , half laugh , but hold her strainer fast in spite of his insinuate effort to unlock her finger . ' but there be no need to tell I yo've   getten a short memory . ' 

 ' why ? what have I do ? how dun you know it ? ' 

 ' last night , ' she begin , and then she stop , and turn away her head , pretend to be busy in her dairy duty of rinse and such like . 

 ' well ! ' say he , half conjecture her meaning , and flatter by it , if his conjecture be right . ' last night what ? ' 

 ' oh , yo ' know ! ' say she , as if impatient at be both literally and metaphorically follow about , and drive into a corner . 

 ' no ; tell I , ' persist he . 

 ' well , ' say she , ' if yo ' will have it , I think yo ' show yo'd but a short memory when yo ' do not   know I again , and yo ' be five time at this house last winter , and that be not so long sin ' . but I suppose yo ' see a vast o ' thing on yo'r voyage by land or by sea , and then it be but natural yo ' should forget . ' she wish she could go on talk , but could not think of anything more to say just then ; for , in the middle of her sentence , the flattering interpretation he might put upon her word , on her know so exactly the number of time he have be to Haytersbank , flash upon she , and she want to lead the conversation a little far afield to make it a little less personal . this be not his wish , however . in a tone which thrill through she , even in her own despite , he say , 

 ' do yo ' think that can ever happen again , Sylvia ? ' 

 she be quite silent ; almost tremble . he repeat the question as if to force she to answer . drive to bay , she equivocate . 

 ' what happen again ? let I go , I dunno what yo're talk about , and I be a'most numb wi ' cold . ' 

 for the frosty air come sharp in through the open lattice window , and the ice be already form on the milk . Kinraid would have find a ready way of keep his cousin , or indeed most young woman , warm ; but he pause before he dare put his arm round Sylvia ; she have something so shy and wild in her look and manner ; and her very innocence of what her word , speak by another girl , might lead to , inspire he with respect , and keep he in check . so he content himself with say , 

 ' I will let yo ' go into t ' warm kitchen if yo'll tell I if yo ' think I can ever forget yo ' again . ' 

 she look up at he defiantly , and set her red lip firm . he enjoy her determination not to reply to this question ; it show she feel its significance . her pure eye look steadily into his ; nor be the expression in his such as to daunt she or make she afraid . they be like two child defy each other ; each determine to conquer . at last she unclose her lip , and nod her head as if in triumph , say , as she fold her arm once more in her check apron , 

 ' Yo'll have to go home sometime . ' 

 ' not for a couple of hour yet , ' say he ; ' and yo'll be freeze first ; so yo'd well say if I can ever forget yo ' again , without more ado . ' 

 perhaps the fresh voice break on the silence , perhaps the tone be less modulated than they have be before , but anyhow Bell Robson 's voice be hear call Sylvia through the second door , which open from the dairy to the house - place , in which her mother have be till this moment asleep . Sylvia dart off in obedience to the call ; glad to leave he , as at the moment Kinraid resentfully imagine . through the open door he hear the conversation between mother and daughter , almost unconscious of its meaning , so difficult do he find it to wrench his thought from the idea he have just be form with Sylvia 's bright lovely face right under his eye . 

 ' Sylvia ! ' say her mother , ' who be yonder ? ' Bell be sit up in the attitude of one startle out of slumber into intensity of listening ; her hand on each of the chair - arm , as if just go to rise . ' there be a fremd man I ' t ' house . I heerd his voice ! ' 

 ' it be only it be just Charley Kinraid ; he be a - talk to I I ' t ' dairy . ' 

 ' I ' t ' dairy , lass ! and how com'd he I ' t ' dairy ? ' 

 ' he com'd to see feyther . Feyther ask he last night , ' say Sylvia , conscious that he could overhear every word that be say , and a little suspecting that he be no great favourite with her mother . 

 ' Thy feyther 's out ; how com'd he I ' t ' dairy ? ' persevere Bell . 

 ' he com'd past this window , and see yo ' asleep , and do not   like for t ' waken yo ' ; so he com'd on to t ' shippen , and when I carry t ' milk in - ' 

 but now Kinraid come in , feel the awkwardness of his situation a little , yet with an expression so pleasant and manly in his open face , and in his exculpatory manner , that Sylvia lose his first word in a strange kind of pride of possession in he , about which she do not reason nor care to define the ground . but her mother rise from her chair somewhat formally , as if she do not intend to sit down again while he stay , yet be too weak to be keep in that standing attitude long . 

 ' I be afeared , sir , Sylvie have not   tell yo ' that my master 's out , and not like to be in till late . he will be main and sorry to have miss yo ' . ' 

 there be nothing for it after this but to go . his only comfort be that on Sylvia 's rosy face he could read unmistakable sign of regret and dismay . his sailor 's life , in bring he suddenly face to face with unexpected event , have give he something of that self - possession which we consider the attribute of a gentleman ; and with an apparent calmness which almost disappoint Sylvia , who construe it into a symptom of indifference as to whether he go or stay , he bid her mother good - night , and only say , in hold her hand a minute long than be absolutely necessary , 

 ' I be come back ere I sail ; and then , may - be , you will answer yon question . ' 

 he speak low , and her mother be rearrange herself in her chair , else Sylvia would have have to repeat the previous word . as it be , with soft thrilling idea ring through she , she could get her wheel , and sit down to her spinning by the fire ; wait for her mother to speak first , Sylvia dream her dream . 

 Bell Robson be partly aware of the state of thing , as far as it lie on the surface . she be not aware how deep down certain feeling have penetrate into the girl 's heart who sit on the other side of the fire , with a little sad air diffuse over her face and figure . Bell look upon Sylvia as still a child , to be warn off forbid thing by threat of danger . but the forbidden thing be already taste , and possible danger in its full acquisition only serve to make it more precious - sweet . 

 Bell sit upright in her chair , gaze into the fire . her milk - white linen mob - cap fringe round and soften her face , from which the usual apple - red be banish by illness , and the feature , from the same cause , render more prominent and stern . she have a clean buff kerchief round her neck , and stuff into the bosom of her Sunday woollen gown of dark blue , if she have be in working - trim she would have wear a bedgown like Sylvia 's . her sleeve be pin back at the elbow , and her brown arm and hard - work hand lie cross in unwonted idleness on her check apron . her knitting be by her side ; and if she have be go through any accustomed calculation or consideration she would have have it busily clink in her finger . but she have something quite beyond common to think about , and , perhaps , to speak about ; and for the minute she be not equal to knitting . 

 ' Sylvie , ' she begin at length , ' do I e'er tell thee on Nancy Hartley as I know when I be a child ? I be think a deal on she to - night ; may - be it be because I have   be dream on yon old time . she be a bonny lass as ever be see , I have   heerd folk say ; but that be afore I know she . when I know she she be crazy , poor wench ; wi ' her black hair a - stream down her back , and her eye , as be a'most as black , allay cry out for pity , though never a word she speak but " he once be here . " just that o'er and o'er again , whether she be cold or hot , full or hungry , " he once be here , " be all her speech . she have be farm - servant to my mother 's brother James Hepburn , thy great - uncle as be ; she be a poor , friendless wench , a parish ' prentice , but honest and gaum - like , till a lad , as nobody know , come o'er the hill one sheep - shear fra ' whitehaven ; he have summat to do wi ' th ' sea , though not rightly to be call a sailor : and he make a deal on Nancy Hartley , just to beguile the time like ; and he go away and ne'er send a thought after she more . it be the way as lad have ; and there be no hold them when they be fellow as nobody know neither where they come fro ' , nor what they have   be do a ' their life , till they come athwart some poor wench like Nancy Hartley . she be but a softy after all : for she leave off do her work in a proper manner . I have   heerd my aunt say as she find out as summat be wrong wi ' Nancy as soon as th ' milk turn bingy , for there ne'er have be such a clean lass about her milk - can afore that ; and from bad it grow to bad , and she would sit and do nothing but play wi ' her finger fro ' morn till night , and if they ask she what ail she , she just say , " he once be here ; " and if they bid she go about her work , it be a ' the same . and when they scold she , and pretty sharp too , she would stand up and put her hair from her eye , and look about she like a crazy thing search for her wit , and ne'er find they , for all she could think on be just , " he once be here . " it be a caution to I again think a man t ' mean what he say when he be a - talk to a young woman . ' 

 ' but what become on poor Nancy ? ' ask Sylvia . 

 ' what should become on she or on any lass as give hersel ' up to think on a man who care nought for she ? ' reply her mother , a little severely . ' she be craze , and my aunt could not   keep she on , could she ? she do keep she a long weary time , think as she would , may - be , come to hersel ' , and , anyhow , she be a motherless wench . but at length she have for t ' go where she come fro ' back to Keswick workhouse : and when last I heerd on she she be chain to th ' great kitchen dresser I ' t ' workhouse ; they would beat she till she be teach to be silent and quiet I ' th ' daytime , but at night , when she be leave alone , she would take up th ' oud cry , till it wring their heart , so they have many a time to come down and beat she again to get any peace . it be a caution to I , as I say afore , to keep fro ' thinking on man as think nought on I . ' 

 ' poor crazy Nancy ! ' sigh Sylvia . the mother wonder if she have take the ' caution ' to herself , or be only full of pity for the mad girl , dead long before . 




 CHAPTER XVI 

 the ENGAGEMENT 

 ' as the day lengthen so the cold strengthen . ' it be so that year ; the hard frost which begin on new year 's eve last on and on into late February , black and bitter , but welcome enough to the farmer , as it keep back the too early growth of autumn - sown wheat , and give they the opportunity of lead manure . but it do not suit invalid as well , and Bell Robson , though not get bad , do not make any progress towards amendment . Sylvia be keep very busy , notwithstanding that she have the assistance of a poor widow - woman in the neighbourhood on cleaning , or washing , or churn day . her life be quiet and monotonous , although hard - work ; and while her hand mechanically find and do their accustomed labour , the thought that rise in her head always centre on Charley Kinraid , his way , his word , his look , whether they all mean what she would fain believe they do , and whether , mean love at the time , such a feeling be likely to endure . her mother 's story of crazy Nancy have take hold of she ; but not as a ' caution , ' rather as a parallel case to her own . like Nancy , and borrow the poor girl 's own word , she would say softly to herself , ' he once be here ' ; but all along she believe in her heart he would come back again to she , though it touch she strangely to imagine the agony of forsaken love . 

 Philip know little of all this . he be very busy with fact and figure , doggedly fight through the necessary business , and only now and then allow himself the delicious relaxation of go to Haytersbank in an evening , to inquire after his aunt 's health , and to see Sylvia ; for the two Fosters be punctiliously anxious to make their shopman test all their statement ; insist on an examination of the stock , as if Hepburn and Coulson be stranger to the shop ; have the Monkshaven auctioneer in to appraise the fixture and necessary furniture ; go over the shop book for the last twenty year with their successor , an employment which take up evening after evening ; and not unfrequently take one of the young man on the long commercial journey which be tediously make in a gig . by degree both Hepburn and Coulson be introduce to distant manufacturer and wholesale dealer . they would have be willing to take the Fosters ' word for every statement the brother have make on new year 's day ; but this , it be evident , would not have satisfy their master , who be scrupulous in insist that whatever advantage there be should always fall on the side of the young man . 

 when Philip see Sylvia she be always quiet and gentle ; perhaps more silent than she have be a year ago , and she do not attend so briskly to what be pass around she . she be rather thin and pale ; but whatever change there be in she be always an improvement in Philip 's eye , so long as she speak graciously to he . he think she be suffer from long - continue anxiety about her mother , or that she have too much to do ; and either cause be enough to make he treat she with a grave regard and deference which have a repressed tenderness in it , of which she , otherwise occupy , be quite unaware . she like he well , too , than she have do a year or two before , because he do not show she any of the eager attention which tease she then , although its meaning be not fully understand . 

 thing be much in this state when the frost break , and milder weather succeed . this be the time so long look forward to by the invalid and her friend , as favour the doctor 's recommendation of change of air . her husband be to take she to spend a fortnight with a kindly neighbour , who live near the farm they have occupy , forty mile or so inland , before they come to Haytersbank . the widow - woman be to come and stay in the house , to keep Sylvia company , during her mother 's absence . Daniel , indeed , be to return home after convey his wife to her destination ; but there be so much to be do on the land at this time of the year , that Sylvia would have be alone all day have it not be for the arrangement just mention . 

 there be active stirring in Monkshaven harbour as well as on shore . the whaler be finish their fitting - out for the Greenland sea . it be a ' close ' season , that be to say , there would be difficulty in pass the barrier of ice which lie between the ship and the whaling - ground ; and yet these must be reach before June , or the year 's expedition would be of little avail . every blacksmith 's shop ring with the rhythmical clang of busy hammer , beat out old iron , such as horseshoe , nail or stub , into the great harpoon ; the quay be throng with busy and important sailor , rush hither and thither , conscious of the demand in which they be hold at this season of the year . it be war time , too . many captain unable to procure man in Monkshaven would have to complete their crew in the Shetlands . the shop in the town be equally busy ; store have to be purchase by the whaling - master , warm clothing of all sort to be provide . these be the large wholesale order ; but many a man , and woman , too , bring out their small hoard to purchase extra comfort , or precious keepsake for some beloved one . it be the time of the great half - yearly traffic of the place ; another impetus be give to business when the whaler return in the autumn , and the man be flush of money , and full of delight at once more see their home and their friend . 

 there be much to be do in Fosters ' shop , and later hour be keep than usual . some perplexity or other be occupy John and Jeremiah Foster ; their mind be not so much on the alert as usual , be engage on some weighty matter of which they have as yet speak to no one . but it thus happen that they do not give the prompt assistance they be accustom to render at such time ; and Coulson have be away on some of the new expedition devolving on he and Philip as future partner . one evening after the shop be closed , while they be examine the good , and compare the sale with the entry in the day - book , Coulson suddenly inquire 

 ' by the way , Hester , do thee know where the parcel of good bandana be go ? there be four left , as I be pretty sure , when I set off to Sandsend ; and to - day Mark Alderson come in , and would fain have have one , and I could find none nowhere . ' 

 ' I sell t ' last to - day , to yon sailor , the specksioneer , who fight the press - gang same time as poor Darley be kill . he take it , and three yard of yon pink ribbon wi ' t ' black and yellow cross on it , as Philip could never abide . Philip have get them I ' t ' book , if he will only look . ' 

 ' be he here again ? ' say Philip ; ' I do not   see he . what bring he here , where he be noan want ? ' 

 ' T ' shop be throng wi ' folk , ' say hester , ' and he know his own mind about the handkercher , and do not   tarry long . just as he be leave , his eye catch on t ' ribbon , and he come back for it . it be when yo ' be serve Mary Darby and there be a vast o ' folk about yo ' . ' 

 ' I wish I would see he , ' say Coulson . ' I would ha ' gi'en he a word and a look he would not ha ' forget in a hurry . ' 

 ' why , what be up ? ' say Philip , surprised at William 's unusual manner , and , at the same time , rather gratified to find a reflection of his own feeling about Kinraid . Coulson 's face be pale with anger , but for a moment or two he seem uncertain whether he would reply or not . 

 ' up ! ' say he at length . ' it be just this : he come after my sister for well nor two year ; and a well lass no , nor a pretty I ' my eye niver break bread . and then my master see another girl , that he like well ' William almost choke in his endeavour to keep down all appearance of violent anger , and then go on , ' and that he play t ' same game wi ' , as I have   heerd tell . ' 

 ' and how do thy sister take it ? ' ask Philip , eagerly . 

 ' she die in a six - month , ' say William ; ' she forgive he , but it be beyond I . I think it be he when I heerd of t ' work about Darley ; Kinraid and come fra ' Newcassel , where Annie live ' prentice and I make inquiry , and it be t ' same man . but I will say no more about he , for it stir t ' old Adam more nor I like , or be fitting . ' 

 out of respect to he , Philip ask no more question although there be many thing that he fain would have know . both Coulson and he go silently and grimly through the remainder of their day 's work . independent of any personal interest which either or both of they have or might have in Kinraid 's be a light o ' love , this fault of his be one with which the two grave , sedate young man have no sympathy . their heart be true and constant , whatever else might be their failing ; and it be no new thing to ' damn the fault we have no mind to . ' Philip wish that it be not so late , or that very evening he would have go to keep guard over Sylvia in her mother 's absence nay , perhaps he might have see reason to give she a warning of some kind . but , if he have do so , it would have be lock the stable - door after the steed be steal . Kinraid have turn his step towards Haytersbank Farm as soon as ever he have complete his purchase . he have only come that afternoon to Monkshaven , and for the sole purpose of see Sylvia once more before he go to fulfil his engagement as specksioneer in the Urania , a whaling - vessel that be to sail from North Shields on Thursday morning , and this be Monday . 

 Sylvia sit in the house - place , her back to the long low window , in order to have all the light the afternoon hour afford for her work . a basket of her father 's unmended stocking be on the little round table beside she , and one be on her left hand , which she suppose herself to be mend ; but from time to time she make long pause , and look in the fire ; and yet there be but little motion of flame or light in it out of which to conjure vision . it be ' redd up ' for the afternoon ; cover with a black mass of coal , over which the equally black kettle hang on the crook . in the back - kitchen Dolly Reid , Sylvia 's assistant during her mother 's absence , chant a lugubrious ditty , befit her condition as a widow , while she clean tin , and can , and milking - pail . perhaps these bustling sound prevent Sylvia from hear approach footstep come down the brow with swift advance ; at any rate , she start and suddenly stand up as some one enter the open door . it be strange she should be so much startle , for the person who enter have be in her thought all during those long pause . Charley Kinraid and the story of crazy Nancy have be the subject for her dream for many a day , and many a night . now he stand there , bright and handsome as ever , with just that much timidity in his face , that anxiety as to his welcome , which give his accost an add charm , could she but have perceive it . but she be so afraid of herself , so unwilling to show what she feel , and how much she have be think of he in his absence , that her reception seem cold and still . she do not come forward to meet he ; she go crimson to the very root of her hair ; but that , in the wan light , he could not see ; and she shake so that she feel as if she could hardly stand ; but the tremor be not visible to he . she wonder if he remember the kiss that have pass between they on new year 's eve the word that have be speak in the dairy on new year 's day ; the tone , the look , that have accompany those word . but all she say be 

 ' I do not   think to see yo ' . I think yo'd ha ' sail . ' 

 ' I tell yo ' I should come back , do not   I ? ' say he , still stand , with his hat in his hand , wait to be ask to sit down ; and she , in her bashfulness , forget to give the invitation , but , instead , pretend to be attentively mend the stocking she hold . neither could keep quiet and silent long . she feel his eye be upon she , watch every motion , and grow more and more confused in her expression and behaviour . he be a little take aback by the nature of his reception , and be not sure at first whether to take the great change in her manner , from what it have be when last he see she , as a favourable symptom or otherwise . by - and - by , luckily for he , in some turn of her arm to reach the scissor on the table , she catch the edge of her work - basket , and down it fall . she stoop to pick up the scatter stocking and ball of worsted , and so do he ; and when they rise up , he have fast hold of her hand , and her face be turn away , half ready to cry . 

 ' what ail yo ' at I ? ' say he , beseechingly . ' Yo ' might ha ' forget I ; and yet I think we make a bargain against forget each other . ' no answer . he go on : ' yo've   never be out o ' my thought , Sylvia Robson ; and I be come back to Monkshaven for nought but to see you once and again afore I go away to the northern sea . it be not two hour sin ' I land at Monkshaven , and I have   be near neither kith nor kin as yet ; and now I be here you will not   speak to I . ' 

 ' I do not   know what to say , ' say she , in a low , almost inaudible tone . then harden herself , and resolve to speak as if she do not understand his only half - express meaning , she lift up her head , and all but look at he while she wrench her hand out of his she say : ' Mother 's go to Middleham for a visit , and feyther be out I ' t ' plough - field wi ' Kester ; but he will be in afore long . ' 

 Charley do not speak for a minute or so . then he say 

 ' Yo're not so dull as to think I be come all this way for t ' see either your father or your mother . I have   a great respect for them both ; but I would hardly ha ' come all this way for to see them , and I bind to be back I ' shield , if I walk every step of the way , by Wednesday night . it be that yo ' will not   understand my meaning , Sylvia ; it be not that yo ' do not , or that yo ' can not . ' he make no effort to repossess himself of her hand . she be quite silent , but in spite of herself she draw long hard breath . ' I may go back to where I come from , ' he go on . ' I think to go to sea wi ' a blessed hope to cheer I up , and a knowledge o ' some one as love I as I would leave behind ; some one as love I half as much as I do she ; for th ' measure o ' my love toward she be so great and mighty , I would be content wi ' half as much from she , till I have teach she to love I more . but if she be a cold heart and can not care for a honest sailor , why , then , I would well go back at once . ' 

 he make for the door . he must have be pretty sure from some sign or other , or he would never have leave it to her womanly pride to give way , and for she to make the next advance . he have not take two step when she turn quickly towards he , and say something the echo of which , rather than the word themselves , reach he . 

 ' I do not   know yo ' care for I ; yo ' niver say so . ' in an instant he be back at her side , his arm round she in spite of her short struggle , and his eager passionate voice say , ' Yo ' never know I love you , Sylvia ? say it again , and look I ' my face while yo ' say it , if yo ' can . why , last winter I think yo'd be such a woman when yo'd come to be one as my een have never look upon , and this year , ever sin ' I see yo ' I ' the kitchen corner sit crouch behind my uncle , I as good as swore I would have yo ' for wife , or never we d at all . and it be not long ere yo ' know it , for all yo ' be so coy , and now yo ' have the face no , yo ' have not the face come , my darling , what be it ? ' for she be cry ; and on his turn her wet blushing face towards he the well to look at it , she suddenly hide it in his breast . he lull and soothe she in his arm , as if she have be a weep child and he her mother ; and then they sit down on the settle together , and when she be more composed they begin to talk . he ask she about her mother ; not sorry in his heart at Bell Robson 's absence . he have intend if necessary to acknowledge his wish and desire with regard to Sylvia to her parent ; but for various reason he be not sorry that circumstance have give he the chance of see she alone , and obtain her promise to marry he without be oblige to tell either her father or her mother at present . ' I ha ' spend my money pretty free , ' he say , ' and I have   ne'er a penny to the fore , and yo'r parent may look for something well for yo ' , my pretty : but when I come back fro ' this voyage I shall stand a chance of have a share I ' th ' Urania , and may - be I shall be mate as well as specksioneer ; and I can get a matter of from seventy to ninety pound a voyage , let alone th ' half - guineas for every whale I strike , and six shill a gallon on th ' oil ; and if I keep steady wi ' Forbes and Company , they will make I master I ' time , for I have   have good schooling , and can work a ship as well as any man ; an ' I leave yo ' wi ' yo'r parent , or take a cottage for yo ' nigh at hand ; but I would like to have something to the fore , and that I shall have , please God , when we come back I ' th ' autumn . I shall go to sea happy , now , think I have   yo'r word . Yo're not one to go back from it , I be sure , else it be a long time to leave such a pretty girl as yo ' , and ne'er a chance of a letter reach yo ' just to tell yo ' once again how I love yo ' , and to bid yo ' not forget yo'r true love . ' 

 ' there will be no need o ' that , ' murmur Sylvia . 

 she be too dizzy with happiness to have attend much to his detail of his worldly prospect , but at the sound of his tender word of love her eager heart be ready to listen . 

 ' I do not   know , ' say he , want to draw she out into more confession of her feeling . ' there be many a one ready to come after yo ' ; and yo'r mother be not o'er captivate wi ' I ; and there be yon tall fellow of a cousin as look black at I , for if I be not mista'en he be a notion of be sweet on yo ' hisself . ' 

 ' not he , ' say Sylvia , with some contempt in her tone . ' he be so full o ' business and t ' shop , and o ' makin ' money , and gettin ' wealth . ' 

 ' ay , ay ; but perhaps when he get a rich man he will come and ask my Sylvia to be his wife , and what will she say then ? ' 

 ' he will niver come ask such a foolish question , ' say she , a little impatiently ; ' he know what answer he would get if he do . ' 

 Kinraid say , almost as if to himself , ' Yo'r mother favour he though . ' but she , weary of a subject she care nothing about , and eager to identify herself with all his interest , ask he about his plan almost at the same time that he say these last word ; and they go on as lover do , intermix a great many tender expression with a very little conversation relate to fact . 

 dolly Reid come in , and go out softly , unheede by they . but Sylvia 's listen ear catch her father 's voice , as he and Kester return homeward from their day 's work in the plough - field ; and she start away , and flee upstairs in shy affright , leave Charley to explain his presence in the solitary kitchen to her father . 

 he come in , not see that any one be there at first ; for they have never think of light a candle . Kinraid step forward into the firelight ; his purpose of conceal what he have say to Sylvia quite melt away by the cordial welcome her father give he the instant that he recognize he . 

 ' bless thee , lad ! who would ha ' think o ' seein ' thee ? why , if iver a thought on thee at all , it be half way to Davis ' Straits . to be sure , t ' winter 's be a dree season , and thou'rt , may - be , I ' t ' reet on ' t to mak ' a late start . late start as iver I make be ninth o ' March , an ' we strike thirteen whale that year . ' 

 ' I have something to say to you , ' say Charley , in a hesitate voice , so different to his usual hearty way , that Daniel give he a keen look of attention before he begin to speak . and , perhaps , the eld man be not unprepared for the communication that follow . at any rate , it be not unwelcome . he like Kinraid , and have strong sympathy not merely with what he know of the young sailor 's character , but with the life he lead , and the business he follow . Robson listen to all he say with approve nod and wink , till Charley have tell he everything he have to say ; and then he turn and strike his broad horny palm into Kinraid 's as if conclude a bargain , while he express in word his hearty consent to their engagement . he wind up with a chuckle , as the thought strike he that this great piece of business , of dispose of their only child , have be conclude while his wife be away . 

 ' a'm noan so sure as t ' missus will like it , ' say he ; ' tho ' whativer she will ha ' to say again it , mischief only know . but she be noan keen on matterimony ; though a have make she as good a man as there be in a ' t ' Ridings . anyhow , a'm master , and that she know . but may - be , for t ' sake o ' peace an ' quietness tho ' she be niver a scold tongue , that a will say for her we'n good keep this matter to ourselves till thou come int ' port again . t ' lass upstairs will like nought well than t ' curl hersel ' round a secret , and purr o'er it , just as t ' oud cat do o'er her blind kitten . but thou'll be want to see t ' lass , a'll be bind . an oud man like I be not   as good company as a pretty lass . ' laugh a low rich laugh over his own wit , Daniel go to the bottom of the stair , and call , ' Sylvie , Sylvie ! come down , lass ! a 's reet ; come down ! ' 

 for a time there be no answer . then a door be unbolt , and Sylvia say , 

 ' I can not   come down again . I be noan comin ' down again to - night . ' 

 Daniel laugh the more at this , especially when he catch Charley 's look of disappointment . 

 ' Hearken how she be bolt her door . she will noane come near we this night . eh ! but she be a stiff little ' un ; she be be our only one , and we'n mostly let she have her own way . but we will have a pipe and a glass ; and that , to my thinking , be as good company as iver a woman in Yorkshire . ' 




 CHAPTER XVII 

 reject warning 

 the post arrive at Monkshaven three time in the week ; sometimes , indeed , there be not a dozen letter in the bag , which be bring thither by a man in a light mail - cart , who take the well part of a day to drive from York ; drop private bag here and there on the moor , at some squire 's lodge or roadside inn . of the number of letter that arrive in Monkshaven , the Fosters , shopkeeper and banker , have the large share . 

 the morning succeed the day on which Sylvia have engage herself to Kinraid , the Fosters seem unusually anxious to obtain their letter . several time Jeremiah come out of the parlour in which his brother John be sit in expectant silence , and , pass through the shop , look up and down the market - place in search of the old lame woman , who be charitably employ to deliver letter , and who must have be lamer than ever this morning , to judge from the lateness of she come . although none but the Fosters know the cause of their impatience for their letter , yet there be such tacit sympathy between they and those whom they employ , that Hepburn , Coulson , and hester be all much relieved when the old woman at length appear with her basket of letter . 

 one of these seem of especial consequence to the good brother . they each separately look at the direction , and then at one another ; and without a word they return with it unread into the parlour , shut the door , and draw the green silk curtain close , the well to read it in privacy . 

 both Coulson and Philip feel that something unusual be go on , and be , perhaps , as full of consideration as to the possible content of this London letter , as of attention to their more immediate business . but fortunately there be little do in the shop . Philip , indeed , be quite idle when John Foster open the parlour - door , and , half doubtfully , call he into the room . as the door of communication shut the three in , Coulson feel himself a little aggrieved . a minute ago Philip and he be on a level of ignorance , from which the former be evidently go to be raise . but he soon return to his usual state of acquiescence in thing as they be , which be partly constitutional , and partly the result of his Quaker training . 

 it be apparently by John Foster 's wish that Philip have be summon . Jeremiah , the less energetic and decide brother , be still discuss the propriety of the step when Philip enter . 

 ' no need for haste , John ; well not call the young man till we have far consider the matter . ' 

 but the young man be there in presence ; and John 's will carry the day . 

 it seem from his account to Philip   explanatory of what he , in advance of his brother 's slow judgment , think to be a necessary step , that the Fosters have for some time receive anonymous letter , warn they , with distinct meaning , though in ambiguous term , against a certain silk - manufacturer in Spitalfields , with whom they have have straightforward business dealing for many year ; but to whom they have latterly advanced money . the letter hint at the utter insolvency of this manufacturer . they have urge their correspondent to give they his name in confidence , and this morning 's letter have bring it ; but the name be totally unknown to they , though there seem no reason to doubt the reality of either it or the address , the latter of which be give in full . certain circumstance be mention regard the transaction between the Fosters and this manufacturer , which could be know only to those who be in the confidence of one or the other ; and to the Fosters the man be , as have be say , a perfect stranger . probably , they would have be unwilling to incur the risk they have do on this manufacturer Dickinson 's account , if it have not be that he belong to the same denomination as themselves , and be publicly distinguish for his excellent and philanthropic character ; but these letter be provocative of anxiety , especially since this morning 's post have bring out the writer 's full name , and various particular show his intimate knowledge of Dickinson 's affair . 

 after much perplexed consultation , John have hit upon the plan of send Hepburn to London to make secret inquiry respect the true character and commercial position of the man whose creditor , not a month ago , they have esteem it an honour to be .

 Philip have the satisfaction of feel himself employ on a mission which would call out his power , and yet not exceed they . in his own mind he forestall the instruction of his master , and be silently in advance of John Foster 's plan and arrangement , while he appear to listen to all that be say with quiet business - like attention .

 hester say nothing .
