 CHAPTER xviii 

 EDDY in LOVE 's CURRENT 

 the next morning shine bright and clear , if ever a March morning do . the beguiling month be come in like a lamb , with whatever storm it might go rag out . it be long since Philip have taste the freshness of the early air on the shore , or in the country , as his employment at the shop detain he in Monkshaven till the evening . and as he turn down the quay   or staithe   on the north side of the river , towards the shore , and meet the fresh sea - breeze blow right in his face , it be impossible not to feel bright and elastic . with his knapsack sling over his shoulder , he be prepare for a good stretch towards Hartlepool , whence a coach would take he to Newcastle before night . for seven or eight mile the level sand be as short and far more agreeable a road than the up and down land - way . Philip walk on pretty briskly , unconsciously enjoy the sunny landscape before he ; the crisp curling wave rush almost up to his foot , on his right hand , and then swish back over the fine small pebble into the great swell sea . to his left be the cliff rise one behind another , have deep gully here and there between , with long green slope upward from the land , and then sudden fall of brown and red soil or rock deepening to a yet great richness of colour at their base towards the blue ocean before he . the loud , monotonous murmur of the advance and recede water lull he into dreaminess ; the sunny look of everything ting his day - dream with hope . so he trudge merrily over the first mile or so ; not an obstacle to his measured pace on the hard , level pavement ; not a creature to be see since he have leave the little gathering of bare - legged urchin dabble in the sea - pool near Monkshaven . the care of land be shut out by the glorious barrier of rock before he . there be some great mass that have be detach by the action of the weather , and lie half embed in the sand , draperie over by the heavy pendent olive - green seaweed . the wave be near at this point ; the advance sea come up with a mighty distant length of roar ; here and there the smooth swell be lash by the fret against unseen rock into white breaker ; but otherwise the wave come up from the German Ocean upon that English shore with a long steady roll that might have take its first impetus far away , in the haunt of the sea - serpent on the coast of ' norroway over the foam . ' the air be soft as May ; right overhead the sky be blue , but it deaden into gray near the sea line . flock of seagull hover about the edge of the wave , slowly rise and turn their white under - plumage to glimmer in the sunlight as Philip approach . the whole scene be so peaceful , so soothing , that it dispel the care and fear   too well found in fact   which have weigh down on his heart during the dark hour of the past night . 

 there be Haytersbank gully open down its green entrance among the warm brown basis of the cliff . below , in the sheltered brushwood , among the last year 's wither leaf , some primrose might be find . he half think of gather Sylvia a posy of they , and rush up to the farm to make a little farewell peace - offering . but on look at his watch , he put all thought of such an action out of his head ; it be above an hour later than he have suppose , and he must make all haste on to Hartlepool . just as he be approach this gully , a man come dash down , and run out some way upon the sand with the very force of his descent ; then he turn to the left and take the direction of Hartlepool a hundred yard or so in advance of Philip . he never stay to look round he , but go swiftly and steadily on his way . by the peculiar lurch in his walk by everything Philip know it be the specksioneer , Kinraid . 

 now the road up Haytersbank gully lead to the farm , and nowhere else . still any one wish to descend to the shore might do so by first go up to the Robsons ' house , and skirt the wall till they come to the little slender path down to the shore . but by the farm , by the very house - door they must of necessity pass . Philip slacken his pace , keep under the shadow of the rock . by - and - by Kinraid , walk on the sunlight open sand , turn round and look long and earnestly towards Haytersbank gully . Hepburn pause when he pause , but as intently as he look at some object above , so intently do Hepburn look at he . no need to ascertain by sight towards whom his look , his thought be direct . he take off his hat and wave it , touch one part of it as if with particular meaning . when he turn away at last , Hepburn heave a heavy sigh , and creep yet more into the cold dank shadow of the cliff . each step be now a heavy task , his sad heart tired and weary . after a while he climb up a few foot , so as to mingle his form yet more completely with the stone and rock around . stumble over the uneven and often jagged point , slip on the sea - weed , plunge into little pool of water leave by the ebb tide in some natural basin , he yet keep his eye fix as if in fascination on Kinraid , and make his way almost alongside of he . but the last hour have pinch Hepburn 's feature into something of the wan haggardness they would wear when he should first be lie still for ever . 

 and now the two man be draw near a creek , about eight mile from Monkshaven . the creek be form by a beck   or small stream   that come flow down from the moor , and take its way to the sea between the widen rock . the melting of the snow and run of the flooded water - spring above make this beck in the early spring - time both deep and wide . Hepburn know that here they both must take a path lead inland to a narrow foot - bridge about a quarter of a mile up the stream ; indeed from this point , owe to the jutting out of the rock , the land path be the short ; and this way lie by the water - side at an angle right below the cliff to which Hepburn 's step be lead he . he know that on this long level field path he might easily be see by any one follow ; nay , if he follow any one at a short distance , for it be full of turning ; and he resolve , late as he be , to sit down for a while till Kinraid be far enough in advance for he to escape be see . he come up to the last rock behind which he could be conceal ; seven or eight foot above the stream he stand , and look cautiously for the specksioneer . up by the rush stream he look , then right below .

 CHAPTER XIX 

 AN IMPORTANT MISSION

 CHAPTER XX 

 LOVED and lose

Hepburn do not care to think would not realize what this drawback , which need not have be mysterious in his case , be .

Philip take upon himself to decide that , with such a man as the specksioneer , absence be equivalent to faithless forgetfulness . he think that he have just ground for this decision in the account he have hear of Kinraid 's behaviour to Annie Coulson ; to the other nameless young girl , her successor in his fickle heart ; in the ribald talk of the sailor in the Newcastle public - house . it would be well for Sylvia if she could forget as quickly ; and , to promote this oblivion , the name of her lover should never be bring up , either in praise or blame . and Philip would be patient and enduring ; all the time watch over she , and labour to win her reluctant love . 

 there she be ! he see she as he stand at the top of the little hill - path lead down to the robson ' door . she be out of door , in the garden , which , at some distance from the house , slop up the bank on the opposite side of the gully ; much too far off to be speak to not too far off to be gaze at by eye that caress she every movement . how well Philip know that garden ; place long ago by some tenant of the farm on a southern slope ; wall in with rough moorland stone ; plant with berry - bush for use , and southernwood and sweet - briar for sweetness of smell . when the Robsons have first come to Haytersbank , and Sylvia be scarcely more than a pretty child , how well he remember help she with the arrangement of this garden ; lay out his few spare penny in hen - and - chicken daisy at one time , in flower - seed at another ; again in a rose - tree in a pot . he know how his unaccustomed hand have labour with the spade at form a little primitive bridge over the beck in the hollow before winter stream should make it too deep for fording ; how he have cut down branch of the mountain - ash and cover they over , yet deck with their scarlet berry , with sod of green turf , beyond which the brilliancy creep out ; but now it be month and year since he have be in that garden , which have lose its charm for Sylvia , as she find the bleak sea - wind come up and blight all endeavour at cultivate more than the most useful thing pot - herb , marigold , potato , onion , and such - like . why do she tarry there now , stand quite motionless up by the high bit of wall , look over the sea , with her hand shade her eye ? quite motionless ; as if she be a stone statue . he begin to wish she would move would look at he but any way that she would move , and not stand gaze thus over that great dreary sea . 

 he go down the path with an impatient step , and enter the house - place . there sit his aunt spinning , and apparently as well as ever . he could hear his uncle talk to Kester in the neighbouring shippen ; all be well in the household . why be Sylvia stand in the garden in that strange quiet way ? 

 ' why , lad ! thou'rt a sight for sair een ! ' say his aunt , as she stand up to welcome he back . ' an ' when didst ta come , eh ? but thy uncle will be glad to see thee , and to hear thee talk about yon pleugh ; he be think a deal o ' thy letter . I will go call he in . ' 

 ' not yet , ' say Philip , stop she in her progress towards the door . ' he be busy talk to Kester . I be in no haste to be go . I can stay a couple of hour . sit down , and tell I how you be yoursel ' and how iverything be . and I have   a deal to tell you . ' 

 ' to be sure to be sure . to think thou be be in Lunnon sin ' I see thee ! well to be sure ! there be a vast o ' come and go I ' this world . Thou'll mind yon specksioneer lad , he as be cousin to t ' Corneys Charley Kinraid ? ' 

 mind he ! as if he could forget he . 

 ' well ! he be dead and go . ' 

 ' dead ! who tell you ? I do not   understand , ' say Philip , in strange bewilderment . could Kinraid have try to escape after all , and be wound , kill in the attempt ? if not , how should they know he be dead ? miss he might be , though how this should be know be strange , as he be suppose to be sail to the Greenland sea . but dead ! what do they mean ? at Philip 's bad moment of hatred he have hardly dare to wish he dead . 

 ' Dunnot yo ' mention it afore our Sylvie ; we niver speak on he to she , for she take it a deal to heart , though I be thinkin ' it be a good thing for she ; for he would got a hold of she he have on Bessy Corney , too , as her mother tell I ; not that I iver let on to they as Sylvia fret after he , so keep a calm sough , my lad . it be a girl 's fancy just a kind o ' calf - love ; let it go by ; and it be well for she he be dead , though it be hard to say so on a drowned man . ' 

 ' drown ! ' say Philip . ' how do yo ' know ? ' half hop that the poor drench swollen body might have be find , and thus all question and dilemma solve . Kinraid might have struggle overboard with rope or handcuff on , and so have be drown . 

 ' eh , lad ! there be no misdoubtin ' it . he be think a deal on by t ' captain o ' t ' Urania ; and when he niver come back on t ' day when she ought for to have sail , he send to Kinraid 's people at Cullercoats , and they send to Brunton 's i ' Newcassel , and they know he have be here . T ' captain put off sailing for two or three day , that he might ha ' that much law ; but when he hear as Kinraid be not at Corneys ' , but have leave them a'most on to a week , he go off to they Northern seas wi ' t ' next good specksioneer he could find . for there be no use speak ill on t ' dead ; an ' though I could not   abear his come for iver about t ' house , he be a rare good specksioneer , as I have   be tell . ' 

 ' but how do you know he be drown ? ' say Philip , feel guiltily disappointed at his aunt 's story . 

 ' why , lad ! I be a'most ashamed to tell thee , I be sore put out mysel ' ; but Sylvia be so break - hearted like I could not   cast it up to she as I should ha ' like : th ' silly lass have go and gi'en he a bit o ' ribbon , as many a one knowed , for it have be a vast notice and admire that evenin ' at th ' Corneys ' new year 's eve I think it be and t ' poor vain peacock have tie it on his hat , so that when t ' tide hist ! there be Sylvie come in at t ' back - door ; never let on , ' and in a forced make - up voice she inquire aloud , for hitherto she have be speak almost in a whisper , 

 ' and didst ta see King George an ' Queen Charlotte ? ' 

 Philip could not answer do not hear . his soul have go out to meet Sylvia , who enter with quiet slowness quite unlike her former self . her face be wan and white ; her gray eye seem large , and full of dumb tearless sorrow ; she come up to Philip , as if his be there touch she with no surprise , and give he a gentle greeting as if he be a familiar indifferent person whom she have see but yesterday . Philip , who have recollect the quarrel they have have , and about Kinraid too , the very last time they have meet , have expect some trace of this remembrance to linger in her look and speech to he . but there be no such sign ; her great sorrow have wipe away all anger , almost all memory . her mother look at her anxiously , and then say in the same manner of force cheerfulness which she have use before , 

 ' here 's Philip , lass , a ' full o ' Lunnon ; call thy father in , an we will hear a ' about t ' new - fangle pleugh . it will be rare an ' nice a ' sit together again . ' 

 Sylvia , silent and docile , go out to the shippen to obey her mother 's wish . Bell Robson leant forward towards Philip , misinterpret the expression on his face , which be guilt as much as sympathy , and check the possible repentance which might have urge he on at that moment to tell all he know , by say , ' Lad ! it be a ' for t ' good . he be noane good enough for she ; and I misdoubt I he be only playin ' wi ' she as he have do by other . let she a - be , let she a - be ; she will come round to be thankful . ' 

 Robson bustle in with loud welcome ; all the loud and more talkative because he , like his wife , assume a cheerful manner before Sylvia . yet he , unlike his wife , have many a secret regret over Kinraid 's fate . at first , while merely the fact of his disappearance be know , Daniel Robson have hit on the truth , and have stick to his opinion that the cursed press - gang be at the bottom of it . he have back his word by many an oath , and all the more because he have not a single reason to give that apply to the present occasion . no one on the lonely coast have remark any sign of the presence of the man - of - war , or the tender that accompany they , for the purpose of impressment on the king 's ship . at Shields , and at the mouth of the Tyne , where they lay in greedy wait , the owner of the Urania have cause strict search to be make for their skilled and protect specksioneer , but with no success . all this positive evidence in contradiction to Daniel Robson 's opinion only make he cle to it the more ; until the day when the hat be find on the shore with Kinraid 's name write out large and fair in the inside , and the tell - tale bit of ribbon knot in the band . then Daniel , by a sudden revulsion , give up every hope ; it never enter his mind that it could have fall off by any accident . no ! now Kinraid be dead and drown , and it be a bad job , and the soon it could be forget the well for all party ; and it be well no one know how far it have go with Sylvia , especially now since Bessy Corney be cry her eye out as if he have be engage to she . so Daniel say nothing to his wife about the mischief that have go on in her absence , and never speak to Sylvia about the affair ; only he be more than usually tender to she in his rough way , and thought , morning , noon , and night , on what he could do to give her pleasure , and drive away all recollection of her ill - star love . 

 to - night he would have she sit by he while Philip tell his story , or heavily answer question put to he . Sylvia sit on a stool by her father 's knee , hold one of his hand in both of her ; and presently she lay down her head upon they , and Philip see her sad eye look into the flicker fire - light with long unwinking stare , show that her thought be far distant . he could hardly go on with his tale of what he have see , and what do , he be so full of pity for she . yet , for all his pity , he have now resolve never to soothe she with the knowledge of what he know , nor to deliver the message send by her false lover . he feel like a mother withhold something injurious from the foolish wish of her plaining child . 

 but he go away without breathe a word of his good fortune in business . the telling of such kind of good fortune seem out of place this night , when the thought of death and the loss of friend seem to brood over the household , and cast its shadow there , obscure for the time all worldly thing . 

 and so the great piece of news come out in the ordinary course of gossip , tell by some monkshaven friend to Robson the next market day . for month Philip have be look forward to the sensation which the intelligence would produce in the farm household , as a preliminary to lay his good fortune at Sylvia 's foot . and they hear of it , and he away , and all chance of his make use of it in the manner he have intend vanish for the present . 

 Daniel be always curious after other people 's affair , and now be more than ever bent on collect scrap of news which might possibly interest Sylvia , and rouse she out of the state of indifference as to everything into which she have fall . perhaps he think that he have not act altogether wisely in allow she to engage herself to Kinraid , for he be a man apt to judge by result ; and moreover he have have so much reason to repent of the encouragement which he have give to the lover whose untimely end have so deeply affect his only child , that he be more unwilling than ever that his wife should know of the length to which the affair have go during her absence . he even urge secrecy upon Sylvia as a personal favour ; unwilling to encounter the silent blame which he openly affect to despise . 

 ' we will noane fret thy mother by lettin ' on how oft he come and go . she will , may - be , be thinkin ' he be for speakin ' to thee , my poor lass ; an ' it would put she out a deal , for she be a woman of a stern mind towards matteremony . and she will be noane so strong till summer - weather come , and I would be loath to give her aught to worrit hersel ' about . so thee and I will keep our own counsel . ' 

 ' I wish mother have be here , then she would ha ' know all , without my tell she . ' 

 ' cheer up , lass ; it be well as it be . Thou'll get o'er it soon for havin' no one to let on to . a myself be noane go to speak on't   again . ' 

 no more he do ; but there be a strange tenderness in his tone when he speak to she ; a half - pathetic way of seek after she , if by any chance she be absent for a minute from the place where he expect to find she ; a consideration for she , about this time , in his way of bring back trifling present , or small piece of news that he think might interest she , which sink deep into her heart . 

 ' and what dun yo ' think a ' t ' folk be talkin ' on I ' monkshaven ? ' ask he , almost before he have take off his coat , on the day when he have hear of Philip 's promotion in the world . ' why , missus , thy nephew , Philip Hepburn , have get his name up I ' gold letter four inch long o'er Fosters ' door ! he and Coulson have set up shop together , and Fosters be go out ! ' 

 ' that 's t ' secret of his journey t ' Lunnon , ' say Bell , more gratified than she choose to show . 

 ' four inch long if they be theere at all ! I heerd on it at t ' Bay Horse first ; but I think yo'd niver be satisfied 'bout I seed it wi ' my own eye . they do say as Gregory Jones , t ' plumber , get it do I ' York , for that nought else would satisfy old Jeremiah . it will be a matter o ' some hundred a year I ' Philip 's pocket . ' 

 ' there will be Fosters I ' th ' background , as one may say , to take t ' big share on t ' profit , ' say Bell . 

 ' ay , ay , that be but as it should be , for I reckon they will ha ' to find t ' brass the first , my lass ! ' say he , turn to Sylvia . ' A'm fain to tak ' thee in to t ' town next market - day , just for thee t ' see ' t. A'll buy thee a bonny ribbon for thy hair out o ' t ' cousin 's own shop . ' 

 some thought of another ribbon which have once tie up her hair , and afterwards be cut in twain , must have cross Sylvia 's mind , for she answer , as if she shrink from her father 's word , 

 ' I can not go , I be noane wantin ' a ribbon ; I be much oblige , father , a ' t ' same . ' 

 her mother read her heart clearly , and suffer with she , but never speak a word of sympathy . but she go on rather more quickly than she would otherwise have do to question her husband as to all he know about this great rise of Philip 's . once or twice Sylvia join in with languid curiosity ; but presently she become tired and go to bed . for a few moment after she leave , her parent sate silent . then Daniel , in a tone as if he be justify his daughter , and comfort himself as well as his wife , observe that it be almost on for nine ; the evening be light so long now . Bell say nothing in reply , but gather up her wool , and begin to arrange the thing for night . 

 by - and - by Daniel break the silence by say , 

 ' a thowt at one time as Philip have a fancy for our Sylvie . '

' Woman 's allay so full o ' sweetheart and matteremony . a only say as a'd thowt once as Philip have a fancy for our lass , and a think so still ; and he will be worth his two hunder a year afore long . but a niver say nought about sweetheart . ' 




 CHAPTER XXI 

 a reject suitor 

 there be many domestic arrangement to be make in connection with the new commercial one which affect Hepburn and Coulson . 

 the Fosters , with something of the busybodiness which be apt to mingle itself with kindly patronage , have plan in their own mind that the Rose household should be remove altogether to the house belong to the shop ; and that Alice , with the assistance of the capable servant , who , at present , manage all John 's domestic affair , should continue as mistress of the house , with Philip and Coulson for her lodger . 

 but arrangement without her consent do not suit Alice at any time , and she have very good reason for decline to accede to this . she be not go to be uproot at her time of life , she say , nor would she consent to enter upon a future which might be so uncertain . why , Hepburn and Coulson be both young man , she say , and they be as likely to marry as not ; and then the bride would be sure to wish to live in the good old - fashioned house at the back of the shop . 

 it be in vain she be tell by every one concern , that , in case of such an event , the first married partner should take a house of his own , leave she in undisputed possession . she reply , with apparent truth , that both might wish to marry , and surely the wife of one ought to take possession of the house belong to the business ; that she be not go to trust herself to the fancy of young man , who be always , the good of they , go and do the very thing that be most foolish in the way of marriage ; of which state , in fact , she speak with something of acrimonious contempt and dislike , as if young people always get mismatch , yet have not the sense to let old and wise people choose for they . 

 ' Thou'll not have be understand why Alice Rose speak as she do this morning , ' say Jeremiah Foster to Philip , on the afternoon succeed the final discussion of this plan . ' she be a - thinking of her youth , I reckon , when she be a well - favour young woman , and our John be full of the thought of marry she . as he could not have she , he have live a bachelor all his day . but if I be not a vast mistaken , all that he have will go to she and to hester , for all that hester be the child of another man . Thee and Coulson should have a try for Hester , Philip . I have tell Coulson this day of hester 's chance . I tell he first because he be my wife 's nephew ; but I tell thee now , Philip . it would be a good thing for the shop if one of ye be marry . ' 

 Philip redden . often as the idea of marriage have come into his mind , this be the first time it have be gravely suggest to he by another . but he reply quietly enough . 

 ' I do not   think hester Rose have any thought of matrimony . ' 

 ' to be sure not ; it be for thee , or for William Coulson , to make she think . she , may - be , remember enough of her mother 's life with her father to make she slow to think on such thing . but it be in she to think on matrimony ; it be in all of we . ' 

 ' Alice 's husband be dead before I know she , ' say Philip , rather evade the main subject . 

 ' it be a mercy when he be take . a mercy to they who be leave , I mean . Alice be a bonny young woman , with a smile for everybody , when he we d she a smile for every one except our John , who never could do enough to try and win one from she . but , no ! she would have none of he , but set her heart on Jack Rose , a sailor in a whale - ship . and so they be marry at last , though all her own folk be against it . and he be a profligate sinner , and go after other woman , and drank , and beat she . she turn as stiff and as grey as thou seest she now within a year of hester 's birth . I believe they would have perish for want and cold many a time if it have not be for John . if she ever guess where the money come from , it must have hurt her pride above a bit , for she be always a proud woman . but mother 's love be strong than pride . ' 

 Philip fall to thinking ; a generation ago something of the same kind have be go on as that which he be now live through , quick with hope and fear . a girl belove by two nay , those two so identical in occupation as he and Kinraid be Rose identical even in character with what he know of the specksioneer ; a girl choose the wrong lover , and suffering and sour all her life in consequence of her youth 's mistake ; be that to be Sylvia 's lot ? or , rather , be she not save from it by the event of the impressment , and by the course of silence he himself have resolve upon ? then he go on to wonder if the life of one generation be but a repetition of the life of those who have go before , with no variation but from the internal cause that some have great capacity for suffer than other . would those very circumstance which make the interest of his life now , return , in due cycle , when he be dead and Sylvia be forget ? 

 perplexed thought of this and a similar kind keep return into Philip 's mind whenever he have leisure to give himself up to consideration of anything but the immediate throng of business . and every time he dwell on this complication and succession of similar event , he emerge from his reverie more and more satisfied with the course he have take in withholding from Sylvia all knowledge of her lover 's fate . 

 it be settle at length that Philip be to remove to the house belong to the shop , Coulson remain with Alice and her daughter . but in the course of the summer the latter tell his partner that he have offer marriage to hester on the previous day , and be refuse . it be an awkward affair altogether , as he live in their house , and be in daily companionship with Hester , who , however , seem to preserve her gentle calmness , with only a tinge more of reserve in her manner to Coulson . 

 ' I wish yo ' could find out what she have again ' I , Philip , ' say Coulson , about a fortnight after he have make the proposal . the poor young man think that Hester 's composure of manner towards he since the event argue that he be not distasteful to she ; and as he be now on very happy term with Philip , he come constantly to he , as if the latter could interpret the meaning of all the little occurrence between he and his beloved . ' I be o ' right age , not two month betwixt we ; and there be few in Monkshaven as would think on her wi ' well prospect than I ; and she know my folk ; we be kind o ' cousin , in fact ; and I would be like a son to her mother ; and there be noane I ' monkshaven as can speak again ' my character . there be nought between yo ' and she , be there , Philip ? ' 

 ' I ha ' tell thee many a time that she and I be like brother and sister . she be no more thought on I nor I have for she . so be content wi't , for I'se not tell thee again . ' 

 ' do not   be vex , Philip ; if thou know what it be to be in love , thou'd be always fancy thing , just as I be . ' 

 ' I might be , ' say Philip ; ' but I dunnut think I should be always talk about my fancy . ' 

 ' I wunnot talk any more after this once , if thou'll just find out fra ' thysel ' , as it be , what it be she have again ' I . I would go to chapel for iver with she , if that be what she want . just ask she , Philip . ' 

 ' it be an awkward thing for I to be melle wi ' , ' say Hepburn , reluctantly . 

 ' but thou say thee and she be like brother and sister ; and a brother would ask a sister , and niver think twice about it . ' 

 ' well , well , ' reply Philip , ' I will see what I can do ; but , lad , I dunnot think she will have thee . she do not   fancy thee , and fancy be three part o ' love , if reason be t ' other fourth . ' 

 but somehow Philip could not begin on the subject with hester . he do not know why , except that , as he say , ' it be so awkward . ' but he really like Coulson so much as to be anxious to do what the latter wish , although he be almost convinced that it would be of no use . so he watch his opportunity , and find Alice alone and at leisure one Sunday evening . 

 she be sit by the window , read her Bible , when he go in . she give he a curt welcome , hearty enough for she , for she be always chary in her expression of pleasure or satisfaction . but she take off her horn spectacle and place they in the book to keep her place ; and then turn more fully round on her chair , so as to face he , she say , 

 ' well , lad ! and how do it go on ? though it be not a day for t ' ask about worldly thing . but I niver see thee now but on Sabbath day , and rarely then . still we munnot speak o ' such thing on t ' Lord 's day . so thee mun just say how t ' shop be do , and then we will leave such vain talk . ' 

 ' T ' shop be do main an ' well , thank ye , mother . but Coulson could tell yo ' o ' that any day . ' 

 ' I would a deal rayther hear fra ' thee , Philip . Coulson do not   know how t ' manage his own business , let alone half the business as it take John and Jeremiah 's head ay , and task them , too to manage . I have   no patience with Coulson . ' 

 ' why ? he be a decent young fellow as ever there be in Monkshaven . ' 

 ' he may be . he be noane cut his wisdom - teeth yet . but , for that matter , there be other folk as far fra ' sense as he be . ' 

 ' ay , and far . Coulson may not   be so bright at all time as he might be , but he be a steady - goer , and I would back he again ' any chap o ' his age I ' monkshaven . ' 

 ' I know who I would soon back in many a thing , Philip ! ' she say it with so much meaning that he could not fail to understand that he himself be mean , and he reply , ingenuously enough , 

 ' if yo ' mean I , mother , I will noane deny that in a thing or two I may be more knowledgeable than Coulson . I have   have a deal o ' time on my hand I ' my youth , and I would good schooling as long as father live . ' 

 ' Lad ! it be not school , nor knowledge , nor book - learning as carry a man through t ' world . it be mother - wit . and it be noane schooling , nor knowledge , nor book - learning as take a young woman . it be summat as can not be put into word . ' 

 ' that be just what I tell Coulson ! ' say Philip , quickly . ' he be sore put about because Hester have gi'en he the bucket , and come to I about it . ' 

 ' and what do thou say ? ' ask Alice , her deep eye gleam at he as if to read his face as well as his word . Philip , think he could now do what Coulson have beg of he in the neat manner , go on , 

 ' I tell he I would help he all as I could - ' 

 ' Thou do , do thou ? well , well , there be nought sa queer as folk , that a will say , ' muttered Alice , between her tooth . 

 ' but that fancy have three part to do wi ' love , ' continue Philip , ' and it would be hard , may - be , to get a reason for she not fancy he . yet I wish she would think twice about it ; he so set upon have she , I think he will do himself a mischief wi ' fretting , if it go on as it be . ' 

 ' it will noane go on as it be , ' say Alice , with gloomy oracularness . 

 ' how not ? ' ask Philip . then , receive no answer , he go on , ' he love she true , and he be within a month or two on her age , and his character will bear handle on a ' side ; and his share on t ' shop will be worth hundred a year afore long . ' 

 another pause . Alice be try to bring down her pride to say something , which she could not with all her effort . 

 ' maybe yo'll speak a word for he , mother , ' say Philip , annoy at her silence . 

 ' I will do no such thing . marriage be well make wi'out melling . how do I know but what she like some one well ? ' 

 ' our Hester 's not th ' lass to think on a young man unless he be be a - wooing on she . and yo ' know , mother , as well as I do and Coulson do too she be niver give any one a chance to woo she ; live half her time here , and t ' other half in t ' shop , and niver speak to no one by t ' way . ' 

 ' I wish thou would not   come here trouble I on a Sabbath day wi ' thy vanity and thy worldly talk . I would liefer by far be I ' that world wheere there be neither marry nor give in marriage , for it be all a moithere mess here . ' she turn to the closed Bible lie on the dresser , and open it with a bang . while she be adjust her spectacle on her nose , with hand tremble with passion , she hear Philip say , 

 ' I ask yo'r pardon , I be sure . I could not   well come any other day . ' 

 ' it be a ' t ' same I care not . but thou might as well tell truth . I will be bind thou be be at Haytersbank Farm some day this week ? ' 

 Philip redden ; in fact , he have forget how he have get to consider his frequent visit to the farm as a regular piece of occupation . he keep silence . 

 Alice look at he with a sharp intelligence that read his silence through . 

 ' I think so . next time thou think to thyself , ' I be more knowledgeable than Coulson , ' just remember Alice Rose 's word , and they be these : If Coulson 's too thick - sighted to see through a board , thou'rt too blind to see through a window . as for comin ' and speakin ' up for Coulson , why he will be married to some one else afore t ' year 's out , for all he think he be so set upon hester now . go thy way , and leave I to my scripture , and come no more on Sabbath day wi ' thy vain babbling . ' 

 so Philip return from his mission rather crestfallen , but quite as far as ever from ' see through a glass window . ' 

 before the year be out , Alice 's prophecy be fulfil . Coulson , who find the position of a reject lover in the same house with the girl who have refuse he , too uncomfortable to be endure , as soon as he be convince that his object be decidedly out of his reach , turn his attention to some one else . he do not love his new sweetheart as he have do hester : there be more of reason and less of fancy in his attachment . but it end successfully ; and before the first snow fall , Philip be good man at his partner 's wedding . 




 CHAPTER XXII 

 deepen SHADOWS 

 but before Coulson be marry , many small event happen small event to all but Philip . to he they be as the sun and moon . the day when he go up to Haytersbank and Sylvia speak to he , the day when he go up and she have apparently no heart to speak to any one , but leave the room as soon as he come , or never enter it at all , although she must have know that he be there these be his alternation from happiness to sorrow . 

 from her parent he always have a welcome . oppress by their daughter 's depression of spirit , they hail the coming of any visitor as a change for she as well as for themselves . the former intimacy with the Corneys be in abeyance for all party , owe to Bessy Corney 's out - speak grief for the loss of her cousin , as if she have have reason to look upon he as her lover , whereas Sylvia 's parent feel this as a slur upon their daughter 's cause of grief . but although at this time the member of the two family cease to seek after each other 's society , nothing be say . the thread of friendship might be join afresh at any time , only just now it be break ; and Philip be glad of it . before go to Haytersbank he seek each time for some little present with which to make his come welcome . and now he wish even more than ever that Sylvia have care for learn ; if she have he could have take she many a pretty ballad , or story - book , such as be then in vogue . he do try she with the translation of the sorrow of Werther , so popular at the time that it have a place in all pedlar ' basket , with Law 's serious Call , the Pilgrim 's Progress , Klopstock 's Messiah , and Paradise Lost . but she could not read it for herself ; and after turn the leaf languidly over , and smile a little at the picture of Charlotte cut bread and butter in a left - handed manner , she put it aside on the shelf by the Complete Farrier ; and there Philip see it , upside down and untouched , the next time he come to the farm . 

 many a time during that summer do he turn to the few verse in Genesis in which Jacob 's twice seven year ' service for Rachel be relate , and try and take fresh heart from the reward which come to the patriarch 's constancy at last . after try book , nosegay , small present of pretty article of dress , such as suit the notion of those day , and find they all receive with the same languid gratitude , he set himself to endeavour to please she in some other way . it be time that he should change his tactic ; for the girl be become weary of the necessity for thank he , every time he come , for some little favour or other . she wish he would let she alone and not watch she continually with such sad eye . her father and mother hail her first sign of impatient petulance towards he as a return to the old state of thing before Kinraid have come to disturb the tenour of their life ; for even Daniel have turn against the specksioneer , irritate by the Corneys ' loud moan over the loss of the man to whom their daughter say that she be attach . if Daniel wish for he to be alive again , it be mainly that the Corneys might be convince that his last visit to the neighbourhood of Monkshaven be for the sake of the pale and silent Sylvia , and not for that of Bessy , who complain of Kinraid 's untimely death rather as if by it she have be cheat of a husband than for any overwhelming personal love towards the deceased . 

 ' if he be after she he be a big black scoundrel , that be what he be ; and a wish he be alive again to be hang . but a dunnot believe it ; they Corney lass be allay a - talkin ' an ' a - thinking on sweetheart , and niver a man cross t ' threshold but they try he on as a husband . an ' their mother be no well : Kinraid have speak civil to Bessy as become a lad to a lass , and she make an ado over he as if they have be to church together not a week sin ' . ' 

 ' I dunnot uphold t ' Corneys ; but Molly Corney as be Molly Brunton now use to speak on this dead man to our Sylvie as if he be her sweetheart in old day . now there be no smoke without fire , and I be think it be likely enough he be one of they fellow as be always after some lass or another , and , as often as not , two or three at a time . now look at Philip , what a different one he be ! he be niver think on a woman but our Sylvie , I will be bind . I wish he wern't   so old - fashioned and faint - hearted . ' 

 ' ay ! and t ' shop 's do a vast o ' business , I have   hear say . he be a deal well company , too , ' n or he use to be . he have a way o ' preach wi ' he as a could not   abide ; but now he tak be his glass , an ' hold his tongue , leavin ' room for wise man to say their say . ' 

 such be a conjugal colloquy about this time . Philip be gain ground with Daniel , and that be something towards win Sylvia 's heart ; for she be unaware of her father 's change of feeling towards Kinraid , and take all his tenderness towards herself as if they be mark of his regard for her lost lover and his sympathy in her loss , instead of which he be rather feel as if it might be a good thing after all that the fickle - hearted sailor be dead and drown . in fact , Daniel be very like a child in all the part of his character . he be strongly affect by whatever be present , and apt to forget the absent . he act on impulse , and too often have reason to be sorry for it ; but he hat his sorrow too much to let it teach he wisdom for the future . with all his many fault , however , he have something in he which make he be dearly love , both by the daughter whom he indulge , and the wife who be in fact superior to he , but whom he imagine that he rule with a wise and absolute sway . 

 love to Sylvia give Philip tact . he seem to find out that to please the woman of the household he must pay all possible attention to the man ; and though he care little in comparison for Daniel , yet this autumn he be continually think of how he could please he . when he have say or do anything to gratify or amuse her father , Sylvia smile and be kind . whatever he do be right with his aunt ; but even she be unusually glad when her husband be pleased . still his progress be slow towards his object ; and often he sigh himself to sleep with the word , ' seven year , and maybe seven year more ' . then in his dream he see Kinraid again , sometimes struggle , sometimes sail towards land , the only one on board a swift advance ship , alone on deck , stern and avenging ; till Philip awake in remorseful terror . 

 such and similar dream return with the great frequency when , in the November of that year , the coast between Hartlepool and Monkshaven be overshadow by the presence of guard - ship , drive south from their station at North Shields by the resolution which the sailor of that port have enter into to resist the press - gang , and the energy with which they have begin to carry out their determination . for on a certain Tuesday evening yet remember by old inhabitant of North Shields , the sailor in the merchant service meet together and overpower the press - gang , dismiss they from the town with the high contempt , and with their jacket reverse . a numerous mob go with they to Chirton Bar ; give they three cheer at parting , but vow to tear they limb from limb should they seek to re - enter North Shields . but a few day afterwards some fresh cause of irritation arise , and five hundred sailor , arm with such sword and pistol as they could collect , parade through the town in the most riotous manner , and at last attempt to seize the tender Eleanor , on some pretext of the ill - treatment of the impressed man aboard . this endeavour fail , however , owe to the energetic conduct of the officer in command . next day this body of sailor set off for Newcastle ; but learn , before they reach the town , that there be a strong military and civil force prepare to receive they there , they disperse for the time ; but not before the good citizen have receive a great fright , the drum of the North Yorkshire militia beat to arm , and the terrified people rush out into the street to learn the reason of the alarm , and some of they see the militia , under the command of the Earl of Fauconberg , march from the guard - house adjoining New Gate to the house of rendezvous for impressed seaman in the Broad Chase . 

 but a few week after , the impressment service take their revenge for the insult they have be subject to in North Shields . in the dead of night a cordon be form round that town by a regiment station at Tynemouth barrack ; the press - gang belong to armed vessel lie off Shields harbour be let loose ; no one within the circle could escape , and upwards of two hundred and fifty man , sailor , mechanic , labourer of every description , be force on board the armed ship . with that prize they set sail , and wisely leave the place , where deep passionate vengeance be swear against they . not all the dread of an invasion by the French could reconcile the people of these coast to the necessity of impressment . fear and confusion prevail after this to within many mile of the sea - shore . a Yorkshire gentleman of rank say that his labourer disperse like a covey of bird , because a press - gang be report to have establish itself so far inland as Tadcaster ; and they only return to work on the assurance from the steward of his master 's protection , but even then beg leave to sleep on straw in the stable or outhouse belong to their landlord , not dare to sleep at their own home . no fish be catch , for the fisherman dare not venture out to sea ; the market be desert , as the press - gang might come down on any gathering of man ; price be raise , and many be impoverish ; many other ruin . for in the great struggle in which England be then involve , the navy be esteem her safeguard ; and man must be have at any price of money , or suffering , or of injustice . Landsmen be kidnap and take to London ; there , in too many instance , to be discharge without redress and penniless , because they be discover to be useless for the purpose for which they have be take . 

 Autumn bring back the whaling - ship . but the period of their return be full of gloomy anxiety , instead of its be the annual time of rejoice and feasting ; of gladdened household , where brave steady husband or son return ; of unlimited and reckless expenditure , and boisterous joviality among those who think that they have earn unbounded licence on shore by their six month of compelled abstinence . in other year this have be the time for new and handsome winter clothing ; for cheerful if humble hospitality ; for the shopkeeper to display their gayest and good ; for the public - house to be crowd ; for the street to be full of blue jacket , roll along with merry word and open heart . in other year the boiling - house have be full of active worker , the staithe crowd with barrel , the ship - carpenter ' yard throng with seaman and captain ; now a few man , tempt by high wage , go stealthily by back lane to their work , cluster together , with sinister look , glance round corner , and fearful of every approach footstep , as if they be go on some unlawful business , instead of true honest work . Most of they keep their whaling - knife about they ready for bloody defence if they be attack . the shop be almost deserted ; there be no unnecessary expenditure by the man ; they dare not venture out to buy lavish present for the wife or sweetheart or little child . the public - house keep scout on the look - out ; while fierce man drank and swear deep oath of vengeance in the bar man who do not maunder in their cup , nor grow foolishly merry , but in whom liquor call forth all the desperate , bad passion of human nature . 

 indeed , all along the coast of Yorkshire , it seem as if a blight hang over the land and the people . man dodged about their daily business with hatred and suspicion in their eye , and many a curse go over the sea to the three fatal ship lie motionless at anchor three mile off monkshaven . when first Philip have hear in his shop that these three man - of - war might be see lie fall and still on the gray horizon , his heart sink , and he scarcely dare to ask their name . for if one should be the Alcestis ; if Kinraid should send word to Sylvia ; if he should say he be live , and loving , and faithful ; if it should come to pass that the fact of the undelivered message send by her lover through Philip should reach Sylvia 's ear : what would be the position of the latter , not merely in her love that , of course , would be hopeless but in her esteem ? all sophistry vanish ; the fear of detection awaken Philip to a sense of guilt ; and , besides , he find out , that , in spite of all idle talk and careless slander , he could not help believe that Kinraid be in terrible earnest when he utter those passionate word , and entreat that they might be bear to Sylvia . some instinct tell Philip that if the specksioneer have only flirt with too many , yet that for Sylvia Robson his love be true and vehement . then Philip try to convince himself that , from all that be say of his previous character , Kinraid be not capable of an endure constant attachment ; and with such poor opiate to his conscience as he could obtain from this notion Philip be oblige to remain content , until , a day or two after the first intelligence of the presence of those three ship , he learn , with some trouble and pain , that their name be the Megoera , the Bellerophon , and the Hanover . 

 then he begin to perceive how unlikely it be that the Alcestis should have be linger on this shore all these many month . she be , doubtless , go far away by this time ; she have , probably , join the fleet on the war station . who could tell what have become of she and her crew ? she might have be in battle before now , and if so - 

 so his previous fancy shrank to nothing , rebuke for their improbability , and with they vanish his self - reproach . yet there be time when the popular attention seem totally absorb by the dread of the press - gang ; when no other subject be talk about hardly , in fact , think about . at such flow of panic , Philip have his own private fear lest a flash of light should come upon Sylvia , and she should suddenly see that Kinraid 's absence might be account for in another way besides death . but when he reason , this seem unlikely . no man - of - war have be see off the coast , or , if see , have never be speak about , at the time of Kinraid 's disappearance . if he have vanish this winter time , every one would have be convince that the press - gang have seize upon he . Philip have never hear any one breathe the dreaded name of the Alcestis . besides , he go on to think , at the farm they be out of hearing of this one great weary subject of talk . but it be not so , as he become convinced one evening . his aunt catch he a little aside while Sylvia be in the dairy , and her husband talk in the shippen with Kester . 

 ' for good 's sake , Philip , dunnot thee bring we talk about t ' press - gang . it be a thing as have get hold on my measter , till thou'd think he possess . he be speak perpetual on it I ' such a way , that thou'd think he be itch to kill them a ' afore he taste bread again . he really tremble wi ' rage and passion ; an ' a ' night it be just as bad . he start up I ' his sleep , swear and cursing at them , till I be sometimes afeard he will mak ' an end o ' I by mistake . and what mun he do last night but open out on Charley Kinraid , and tell Sylvie he think m'appen t ' gang have get hold on he . it might make her cry a ' her saut tear o'er again . ' 

 Philip speak , by no wish of his own , but as if compel to speak . 

 ' An ' who know but what it be true ? ' 

 the instant these word have come out of his lip he could have bite his tongue off . and yet afterwards it be a sort of balm to his conscience that he have so speak . 

 ' what nonsense , Philip ! ' say his aunt ; ' why , these fearsome ship be far out o ' sight when he go away , good go wi ' he , and Sylvie just get o'er her trouble so nicely , and even my master go on for to say if they would getten hold on he , he be not a chap to stay wi ' them ; he would gi'en proof on his hatred to them , time on . he either ha ' make off an ' then sure enough we should ha ' heerd on he somehow they Corneys be full on he still and they have   a deal to wi ' his folk beyond Newcassel or , as my master say , he be just t ' chap to hang or drown hissel , soon nor do aught against his will . ' 

 ' what do Sylvie say ? ' ask Philip , in a hoarse low voice . 

 ' say ? why , a ' she could say be to burst out cry , and after a bit , she just repeat her feyther 's word , and say anyhow he be dead , for he would niver live to go to sea wi ' a press - gang . she know he too well for that . Thou see she think a deal on he for a spirited chap , as can do what he will . I belie ' I she first begin to think on he time o ' t ' fight aboard th ' Good Fortune , when Darley be kill , and he would seem tame - like to she if he could not   conquer press - gang , and man - o'-war . she be soon think on he drown , as she be ne'er to see he again . ' 

 ' it be good so , ' say Philip , and then , to calm his unusually excited aunt , he promise to avoid the subject of the press - gang as much as possible . 

 but it be a promise very difficult of performance , for Daniel Robson be , as his wife say , like one possess . he could hardly think of anything else , though he himself be occasionally weary of the same constantly recur idea , and would fain have banish it from his mind . he be too old a man to be likely to be take by they ; he have no son to become their victim ; but the terror of they , which he have brave and defy in his youth , seem to come back and take possession of he in his age ; and with the terror come impatient hatred . since his wife 's illness the previous winter he have be a more sober man until now . he be never exactly drunk , for he have a strong , well - season head ; but the craving to hear the last news of the action of the press - gang draw he into Monkshaven nearly every day at this dead agricultural season of the year ; and a public - house be generally the focus from which gossip radiate ; and probably the amount of drink thus consume weaken Robson 's power over his mind , and cause the concentration of thought on one subject . this may be a physiological explanation of what afterwards be speak of as a supernatural kind of possession , lead he to his doom . 




 CHAPTER XXIII 

 retaliation 

 the public - house that have be choose by the leader of the press - gang in Monkshaven at this time , for their rendezvous   or ' randyvowse ' , as it be generally pronounce , be an inn of poor repute , with a yard at the back which open on to the staithe or quay near to the open sea . a strong high stone wall bound this grass - grow mouldy yard on two side ; the house , and some unused out - building , form the other two . the choice of the place be good enough , both as to situation , which be sufficiently isolate , and yet near to the widening river ; and as to the character of the landlord , John Hobbs be a fail man , one who seem as if doom to be unfortunate in all his undertaking , and the consequence of all this be that he be envious of the more prosperous , and willing to do anything that might bring he in a little present success in life . his household consist of his wife , her niece , who act as servant , and an out - of - door man , a brother of Ned Simpson , the well - do butcher , who at one time have have a fancy for Sylvia . but the one brother be prosperous , the other have go on sink in life , like he who be now his master . neither Hobbs nor his man Simpson be absolutely bad man ; if thing have go well with they they might each have be as scrupulous and conscientious as their neighbour , and even now , suppose the gain in money to be equal , they would soon have do good than evil ; but a very small sum be enough to turn the balance . and in a great degree than in most case be the famous maxim of rochefoucault true with they ; for in the misfortune of their friend they seem to see some justification of their own . it be blind fate deal out event , not that the event themselves be the inevitable consequence of folly or misconduct . to such man as these the large sum offer by the lieutenant of the press - gang for the accommodation of the Mariners ' arm be simply and immediately irresistible . the good room in the dilapidated house be put at the service of the command officer of the impress service , and all other arrangement make at his desire , irrespective of all the former unprofitable source of custom and of business . if the relative both of Hobbs and of Simpson have not be so well known and so prosperous in the town , they themselves would have receive more mark of popular ill opinion than they do during the winter the event of which be now be record . as it be , people speak to they when they appear at kirk or at market , but hold no conversation with they ; no , not although they each appear well dressed than they have either of they do for year past , and although their whole manner show a change , inasmuch as they have be formerly snarl and misanthropic , and be now civil almost to deprecation . 

 every one who be capable of understand the state of feeling in Monkshaven at this time must have be aware that at any moment an explosion might take place ; and probably there be those who have judgment enough to be surprised that it do not take place soon than it do . for until February there be only occasional cry and growl of rage , as the press - gang make their capture first here , then there ; often , apparently , tranquil for day , then hear of at some distance along the coast , then carry off a seaman from the very heart of the town . they seem afraid of provoke any general hostility , such as that which have drive they from Shields , and would have conciliate the inhabitant if they could ; the officer on the service and on board the three man - of - war come often into the town , spend largely , talk to all with cheery friendliness , and make themselves very popular in such society as they could obtain access to at the house of the neighbouring magistrate or at the rectory . but this , however agreeable , do not forward the object the impress service have in view ; and , accordingly , a more decide step be take at a time when , although there be no apparent evidence as to the fact , the town be full of the Greenland mariner come quietly in to renew their yearly engagement , which , when do , would legally entitle they to protection from impressment . one night it be on a Saturday , February 23rd , when there be a bitter black frost , with a north - east wind sweep through the street , and man and woman be close shut in their house all be startle in their household content and warmth by the sound of the fire - bell busily swing , and peal out for help . the fire - bell be keep in the market - house where High Street and Bridge Street meet : every one know what it mean . some dwelling , or maybe a boiling - house be on fire , and neighbourly assistance be summon with all speed , in a town where no water be lay on , nor fire - engine keep in readiness . man snatch up their hat , and rush out , wife follow , some with the readiest wrap they could lay hand on , with which to clothe the over - hasty husband , other from that mixture of dread and curiosity which draw people to the scene of any disaster . those of the market people who be make the good of their way homeward , have wait in the town till the early darkness conceal their path , turn back at the sound of the ever - clang fire - bell , ring out fast and fast as if the danger become every instant more pressing . 

 as man run against or alongside of each other , their breathless question be ever , ' where be it ? ' and no one could tell ; so they press onwards into the market - place , sure of obtain the information desire there , where the fire - bell keep call out with its furious metal tongue . 

 the dull oil - lamp in the adjoining street only make darkness visible in the throng market - place , where the buzz of many man 's unanswered question be rise louder and louder . a strange feeling of dread creep over those near to the closed market - house . above they in the air the bell be still clang ; but before they be a door fast shut and lock ; no one to speak and tell they why they be summon where they ought to be . they be at the heart of the mystery , and it be a silent blank ! their unformed dread take shape at the cry from the outside of the crowd , from where man be still come down the eastern side of Bridge Street . ' the gang ! the gang ! ' shriek out some one . ' the gang be upon we ! help ! help ! ' then the fire - bell have be a decoy ; a sort of seethe the kid in its mother 's milk , lead man into a snare through their kindliest feeling . some dull sense of this add to utter dismay , and make they struggle and strain to get to all the outlet save that in which a fight be now go on ; the swish of heavy whip , the thud of bludgeon , the groan , the growl of wounded or infuriate man , come with terrible distinctness through the darkness to the quickened ear of fear . 

 a breathless group rush up the blackness of a narrow entry to stand still awhile , and recover strength for fresh running . for a time nothing but heavy pant and gasp be hear amongst they . no one know his neighbour , and their good feeling , so lately abuse and prey upon , make they full of suspicion . the first who speak be recognize by his voice . 

 ' be it thee , Daniel Robson ? ' ask his neighbour , in a low tone . 

 ' ay ! who else should it be ? ' 

 ' a dunno . ' 

 ' if a be to be any one else , I would like to be a chap of nobbut eight stun . A'm welly do for ! ' 

 ' it be as bloody a shame as iver I heerd on . who be to go t ' t ' next fire , a'd like to know ! ' 

 ' a tell yo ' what , lad , ' say Daniel , recover his breath , but speak in gasp . ' we be a pack o ' coward to let them carry off yon chap as easy as they do , a'm reckon ! ' 

 ' a think so , indeed , ' say another voice . 

 Daniel go on 

 ' we be two hunder , if we be a man ; an ' t ' gang have niver number above twelve . ' 

 ' but they be arm . a seen t ' glitter on their cutlass , ' speak out a fresh voice . 

 ' what then ! ' reply he who have late come , and who stand at the mouth of the entry . ' a have my whalin ' knife wi ' I I ' my pea - jacket as my missus throw at I , and a'd ha ' rip them up as soon as winkin ' , if a could ha ' think what be good to do wi ' that d d bell makin ' such a din reet above we . a man can but die onest , and we be ready to go int ' t ' fire for t ' save folk ' life , and yet we would none on we t ' wit to see as we might ha ' save yon poor chap as screech out for help . ' 

 ' they will ha ' getten them to t ' randyvowse by now , ' say some one . 

 ' they can not tak ' them aboard till morning ; t ' tide will not   serve , ' say the last speaker but one . 

 Daniel Robson speak out the thought that be surge up into the brain of every one there . 

 ' there be a chance for we a ' . how many be we ? ' by dint of touch each other the number be count . seven . ' Seven . but if we seven turn out and rouse t ' town , there will be many a score ready to gang t ' Mariners ' arm , and it will be easy work reskyin ' they chap as be press . Us seven , each man jack on we , go and seek up his friend , and get he as well as he can to t ' church step ; then , mebbe , there will be some theere as'll not be so soft as we be , lettin ' they poor chap be carry off from under our nose , just becase our ear be busy listenin ' to yon confound bell , whose clip - clappin ' tongue a'll tear out afore this week be out . ' 

 before Daniel have finish speak , those near to the entrance mutter their assent to his project , and have steal off , keep to the dark side of the street and lane , which they thread in different direction ; most of they go straight as sleuth - hound to the haunt of the wild and most desperate portion of the seafare population of Monkshaven . for , in the breast of many , revenge for the misery and alarm of the past winter take a deep and more ferocious form than Daniel have think of when he make his proposal of a rescue . to he it be an adventure like many he have be engage in in his young day ; indeed , the liquor he have drunk have give he a fictitious youth for the time ; and it be more in the light of a rough frolic of which he be to be the leader , that he limp along    always lame from old attack of rheumatism , chuckle to himself at the apparent stillness of the town , which give no warning to the press - gang at the rendezvous of anything in the wind . Daniel , too , have his friend to summon ; old hand like himself , but ' deep un ' , also , like himself , as he imagine . 

 it be nine o'clock when all who be summon meet at the church step ; and by nine o'clock , Monkshaven , in those day , be more quiet and asleep than many a town at present be at midnight . the church and churchyard above they be flood with silver light , for the moon be high in the heaven : the irregular step be here and there in pure white clearness , here and there in black shadow . but more than half way up to the top , man cluster like bee ; all press so as to be near enough to question those who stand near to the planning of the attack . here and there , a woman , with wild gesture and shrill voice , that no entreaty would hush down to the whisper pitch of the man , push her way through the crowd this one implore immediate action , that adjure those around she to smite and spare not those who have carry off her ' man ' , the father , the breadwinner . low down in the darken silent town be many whose heart go with the angry and excited crowd , and who would bless they and caress they for that night 's deed . Daniel soon find himself a laggard in planning , compare to some of those around he . but when , with the rush sound of many step and but few word , they have arrive at the blank , dark , shut - up Mariners ' arm , they pause in surprise at the uninhabited look of the whole house : it be Daniel once more who take the lead . 

 ' speak them fair , ' say he ; ' try good word first . Hobbs will mebbe let them out quiet , if we can catch a word wi ' he . a say , Hobbs , ' say he , raise his voice , ' be a ' shut up for t ' neet ; for a'd be glad of a glass . A'm Dannel Robson , thou know . ' 

 not one word in reply , any more than from the tomb ; but his speech have be hear nevertheless . the crowd behind he begin to jeer and to threaten ; there be no long any keeping down their voice , their rage , their terrible oath . if door and window have not of late be strengthen with bar of iron in anticipation of some such occasion , they would have be break in with the onset of the fierce and now yell crowd who rush against they with the force of a battering - ram , to recoil in baffled rage from the vain assault . no sign , no sound from within , in that breathless pause . 

 ' come away round here ! a've   find a way to t ' back o ' behint , where belike it be not so well fence , ' say Daniel , who have make way for young and more powerful man to conduct the assault , and have employ his time meanwhile in examine the back premise . the man rush after he , almost knock he down , as he make his way into the lane into which the door of the outbuilding belong to the inn open . Daniel have already break the fastening of that which open into a damp , mouldy - smell shippen , in one corner of which a poor lean cow shift herself on her leg , in an uneasy , restless manner , as her sleep - place be invade by as many man as could cram themselves into the dark hold . Daniel , at the end farth from the door , be almost smother before he could break down the rotten wooden shutter , that , when open , display the weedy yard of the old inn , the full clear light define the outline of each blade of grass by the delicate black shadow behind . 

 this hole , use to give air and light to what have once be a stable , in the day when horse traveller be in the habit of come to the Mariners ' arm , be large enough to admit the passage of a man ; and Daniel , in virtue of its discovery , be the first to get through . but he be large and heavy than he have be ; his lameness make he less agile , and the impatient crowd behind he give he a help push that send he down on the round stone with which the yard be pave , and for the time disable he so much that he could only just crawl out of the way of leap foot and heavy nail boot , which come through the opening till the yard be fill with man , who now set up a fierce , derisive shout , which , to their delight , be answer from within . no more silence , no more dead opposition : a live struggle , a glowing , rag fight ; and Daniel think he should be oblige to sit there still , lean against the wall , inactive , while the strife and the action be go on in which he have once be foremost . 

 he see the stone tear up ; he see they use with good effect on the unguarde back - door ; he cry out in useless warning as he see the upper window open , and aim take among the crowd ; but just then the door give way , and there be an involuntary forward motion in the throng , so that no one be so disabled by the shot as to prevent his force his way in with the rest . and now the sound come veil by the wall as of some rag ravening beast growl over his prey ; the noise come and go once utterly cease ; and Daniel raise himself with difficulty to ascertain the cause , when again the roar come clear and fresh , and man pour into the yard again , shout and rejoice over the rescue victim of the press - gang . Daniel hobble up , and shout , and rejoice , and shake hand with the rest , hardly care to understand that the lieutenant and his gang have quit the house by a front window , and that all have pour out in search of they ; the great part , however , return to liberate the prisoner , and then glut their vengeance on the house and its content . 

 from all the window , upper and low , furniture be now be throw into the yard . the smash of glass , the heavy crash of wood , the cry , the laughter , the oath , all excite Daniel to the utmost ; and , forget his bruise , he press forwards to lend a help hand . the wild , rough success of his scheme almost turn his head . he hurrae at every flagrant piece of destruction ; he shake hand with every one around he , and , at last , when the destroyer inside pause to take breath , he cry out , 

 ' if a be as young as one a be , a'd have t ' randyvowse down , and mak ' a bonfire on it . we would ring t ' fire - bell then t ' some purpose . ' 

 no soon say than do . their excitement be ready to take the slight hint of mischief ; old chair , broken table , odd drawer , smash chest , be rapidly and skilfully heap into a pyramid , and one , who at the first broaching of the idea have go for live coal the speedy to light up the fire , come now through the crowd with a large shovelful of red - hot cinder . the rioter stop to take breath and look on like child at the uncertain flickering blaze , which spring high one moment , and drop down the next only to creep along the base of the heap of wreck , and make secure of its future work . then the lurid blaze dart up wild , high , and irrepressible ; and the man around give a cry of fierce exultation , and in rough mirth begin to try and push each other in . in one of the pause of the rushing , roar noise of the flame , the moan low and groan of the poor alarmed cow fasten up in the shippen catch Daniel 's ear , and he understand her groan as well as if they have be word . he limp out of the yard through the now desert house , where man be busy at the mad work of destruction , and find his way back to the lane into which the shippen open . the cow be dance about at the roar , and dazzle , and heat of the fire ; but Daniel know how to soothe she , and in a few minute he have a rope round her neck , and lead she gently out from the scene of her alarm . he be still in the lane when Simpson , the man - of - all - work at the Mariners ' arm , creep out of some hiding - place in the deserted outbuilding , and stand suddenly face to face with Robson . 

 the man be white with fear and rage . 

 ' here , tak ' thy beast , and lead she wheere she will noane hear yon cry and shout . she be fairly moithered wi ' heat an ' noise . ' 

 ' they be brennin ' ivery rag I have I ' t ' world , ' gasp out Simpson : ' I niver have much , and now I be a beggar . ' 

 ' well ! thou should not   ha ' turn again ' thine own town - folk , and harbour t ' gang . sarve thee reet . A'd noane be here leadin ' beast if a be as young as a were ; a'd be in t ' thick on it . ' 

 ' it be thee set ' m on a heerd thee a see'd thee a helping on them t ' break in ; they would niver ha ' thought on attackin ' t ' house , and settin ' fire to yon thing , if thou have not   speak on it . ' Simpson be now fairly cry . but Daniel do not realize what the loss of all the small property he have in the world be to the poor fellow   rapscallion though he be , break down , unprosperous ne'er - do - weel !   in his pride at the good work he believe he have set on foot . 

 ' ay , ' say he ; ' it be a great thing for folk to have a chap for t ' lead them wi ' a head on his shouther . a misdoubt I if there be a felly theere as would ha ' think o ' routle out yon wasp ' nest ; it tak be a deal o ' mother - wit to be up to thing . but t ' gang'll niver harbour theere again , one while . a only wish we would cotche them . an ' a should like t ' ha ' gi'en Hobbs a bit o ' my mind . ' 

 ' he be have his sauce , ' say Simpson , dolefully . ' he and I be ruin . ' 

 ' tut , tut , thou 's get thy brother , he be rich enough . and Hobbs will do a deal well ; he be have his lesson now , and he will stick to his own side time to come . here , tak ' thy beast an ' look after she , for my bone be achin ' . an ' mak ' thysel ' scarce , for some o ' they felly have getten their blood up , an ' wunnot be for treat thee o'er well if they fall in wi ' thee . ' 

 ' Hobbs ought to be serve out ; it be he as make t ' bargain wi ' lieutenant ; and he be off safe wi ' his wife and his money bag , and a'm leave a beggar this neet I ' monkshaven street . my brother and I have have word , and he will do nought for I but curse I . a have three crown - piece , and a good pair o ' breech , and a shirt , and a dare say well nor two pair o ' stocking . a wish t ' gang , and thee , and Hobbs and they mad folk up yonder , be a ' down I ' hell , a do . ' 

 ' Coom , lad , ' say Daniel , noway offend at his companion 's wish on his behalf . ' a'm noane flush mysel ' , but here be half - a - crown and tuppence ; it be a ' a've   getten wi ' me , but it will keep thee and t ' beast I ' food and shelter to - neet , and get thee a glass o ' comfort , too . a have think o ' takin ' one mysel ' , but a shannot ha ' a penny left , so a'll just toddle whoam to my missus . ' 

 Daniel be not in the habit of feel any emotion at action not directly affect himself ; or else he might have despise the poor wretch who immediately clutch at the money , and overwhelm that man with slobbery thank whom he have not a minute before be curse . but all Simpson 's strong passion have be long ago use up ; now he only faintly like and dislike , where once he love and hat ; his only vehement feeling be for himself ; that care for , other man might wither or flourish as well suit they . 

 many of the door which have be close shut when the crowd go down the High Street , be partially open as Daniel slowly return ; and light stream from they on the otherwise dark road . the news of the successful attempt at rescue have reach those who have sate in mourning and in desolation an hour or two ago , and several of these press forwards as from their watch corner they recognize Daniel 's approach ; they press forward into the street to shake he by the hand , to thank he   for his name have be bruit abroad as one of those who have plan the affair , and at several place he be urge to have a dram urgency that he be loath for many reason to refuse , but his increase uneasiness and pain make he for once abstinent , and only anxious to get home and rest . but he could not help be both touch and flatter at the way in which those who form his ' world ' look upon he as a hero ; and be not insensible to the word of blessing which a wife , whose husband have be impress and rescue this night , pour down upon he as he pass . 

 ' theere , theere , dunnot crack thy throat wi ' blessin ' . Thy man would ha ' do as much for I , though mebbe he might not   ha ' show so much gumption and capability ; but they 's gift , and not to be proud on . ' 

 when Daniel reach the top of the hill on the road home , he turn to look round ; but he be lame and bruise , he have go along slowly , the fire have pretty nearly die out , only a red hue in the air about the house at the end of the long High Street , and a hot lurid mist against the hill - side beyond where the Mariners ' arm have stand , be still leave as sign and token of the deed of violence . 

 Daniel look and chuckle . ' that come o ' ringin ' t ' fire - bell , ' say he to himself ; ' it be shame for it to be tellin ' a lie , poor oud story - teller . ' 




 CHAPTER XXIV 

 BRIEF REJOICING 

 Daniel 's unusually late absence from home disturb Bell and Sylvia not a little . he be generally at home between eight and nine on market day . they expect to see he the bad for liquor at such time ; but this do not shock they ; he be no bad than most of his neighbour , indeed well than several , who go off once or twice a year , or even oftener , on drink bout of two or three day ' duration , return pale , sodden , and somewhat shame - face , when all their money be go ; and , after the conjugal reception be well over , settle down into hard - work and decently sober man until the temptation again get power over they . but , on market day , every man drink more than usual ; every bargain or agreement be ratify by drink ; they come from great or less distance , either afoot or on horseback , and the ' good accommodation for man and beast '   as the old inn - sign express it   always include a considerable amount of liquor to be drunk by the man . 

 Daniel 's way of announce his intention of drink more than ordinary be always the same . he would say at the last moment , ' Missus , I have   a mind to get fuddle to - neet , ' and be off , disregard her look of remonstrance , and little heed the injunction she would call after he to beware of such and such companion , or to attend to his footstep on his road home . 

 but this night he have give no such warning . Bell and Sylvia put the candle on the low window - seat at the usual hour to guide he through the field it be a habit keep up even on moonlight night like this and sate on each side of the fire , at first scarcely care to listen , so secure be they of his return .
