Bell doze , and Sylvia sate gaze at the fire with abstracted eye , think of the past year and of the anniversary which be approach of the day when she have last see the lover whom she believe to be dead , lie somewhere fathom deep beneath the surface of that sunny sea on which she look day by day without ever see his upturned face through the depth , with whatsoever heart - sick longing for just one more sight she yearn and inwardly cry . if she could set her eye on his bright , handsome face , that face which be fade from her memory , overtaske in the too frequent effort to recall it ; if she could but see he once again , come over the water beneath which he lie with supernatural motion , await she at the stile , with the evening sun shin ruddy into his bonny eye , even though , after that one instant of vivid and visible life , he fade into mist ; if she could but see he now , sit in the faintly flicker fire - light in the old , happy , careless way , on a corner of the dresser , his leg dangle , his busy finger play with some of her woman 's work ; she wring her hand tight together as she implore some , any power , to let she see he just once again just once for one minute of passionate delight . never again would she forget that dear face , if but once more she might set her eye upon it . 

 her mother 's head fall with a sudden jerk , and she rouse herself up ; and Sylvia put by her thought of the dead , and her craving after his presence , into that receptacle of her heart where all such be keep closed and sacred from the light of common day . 

 ' Feyther 's late , ' say Bell . 

 ' it be go eight , ' reply Sylvia . 

 ' but our clock be well nor an hour forrard , ' answer Bell . 

 ' ay , but t ' wind bring monkshaven bell clear to - night . I heerd t ' eight o'clock bell ring not five minute ago . ' 

 it be the fire - bell , but she have not distinguish the sound . 

 there be another long silence ; both wide awake this time . 

 ' he will have his rheumatic again , ' say Bell . 

 ' it be cold for sartin , ' say Sylvia . ' March weather come afore its time . but I will make he a treacle - posset , it be a famous thing for keep off hoast . ' 

 the treacle - posset be entertainment enough for both while it be be make . but once place in a little basin in the oven , there be again time for wonder and anxiety . 

 ' he say nought about have a bout , do he , mother ? ' ask Sylvia at length . 

 ' no , ' say Bell , her face a little contracting . after a while she add , ' there be many a one as have husband that go off drink without iver say a word to their wife . my master be none o ' that mak ' . ' 

 ' mother , ' break in Sylvia again , ' I will just go and get t ' lantern out of t ' shippen , and go up t ' brow , and mebbe to t ' ash - field end . ' 

 ' do , lass , ' say her mother . ' I will get my wrap and go with thee . ' 

 ' thou shall do niver such a thing , ' say Sylvia . ' Thou 's too frail to go out I ' t ' night air such a night as this . ' 

 ' then call Kester up . ' 

 ' not i. I be noane afraid o ' t ' dark . ' 

 ' but of what thou mayst meet I ' t ' dark , lass ? ' 

 Sylvia shiver all over at the sudden thought , suggest by this speech of her mother 's , that the idea that have flash into her own mind of go to look for her father might be an answer to the invocation to the Powers which she have make not long ago , that she might indeed meet her dead lover at the ash - field stile ; but though she shiver as this superstitious fancy come into her head , her heart beat firm and regular ; not from darkness nor from the spirit of the dead be she go to shrink ; her great sorrow have take away all her girlish nervous fear . 

 she go ; and she come back . neither man nor spirit have she see ; the wind be blow on the height enough to sweep all creature before it ; but no one be come . 

 so they sate down again to keep watch . at length his step be hear close to the door ; and it startle they even in their state of expectation . 

 ' why , feyther ! ' cry Sylvia as he enter ; while his wife stand up tremble , but not say a word . 

 ' a'm a'most do up , ' say he , sit heavily down on the chair nearest the door . 

 ' poor old feyther ! ' say Sylvia , stoop to take off his heavy clogged shoe ; while Bell take the posset out of the oven . 

 ' what be this ? posset ? what creature woman be for slop , ' say he ; but he drink it all the same , while Sylvia fasten the door , and bring the flare candle from the window - seat . the fresh arrangement of light display his face blacken with smoke , and his clothe disarrange and tear . 

 ' who be be melle wi ' thee ? ' ask Bell . 

 ' no one have melle wi ' I ; but a've   be mellin ' wi ' t ' gang at last . ' 

 ' thee : they niver be for press thee ! ' exclaim both the woman at once . 

 ' no ! they know well . they'n getten their belly - full as it be . next time they try it on , a reckon they will ax if Daniel Robson be wi'in hearin ' . A've   lead a resky this neet , and save nine or ten honest chap as be press , and carry off to t ' randyvowse . I and some other do it . and Hobbs ' thing and t ' lieutenant 's be a ' burn ; and by this time a reckon t ' randyvowse be pretty nigh four wall , ready for a parish - pound . ' 

 ' Thou'rt niver for say thou burn it down wi ' t ' gang in it , for sure ? ' ask Bell . 

 ' na , na , not this time . T ' ' gang flee up t ' hill like coney ; and Hobbs and his folk carry off a bag o ' money ; but t ' oud tumbledown place be just a heap o ' brick and mortar ; an ' t ' furniture be smoulderin ' int ' ash ; and , good of a ' , t ' man be free , and will niver be cotche wi ' a fire - bell again . ' 

 and so he go on to tell of the ruse by which they have be entice into the market - place ; interrupt from time to time by their eager question , and interrupt himself every now and then with exclamation of weariness and pain , which make he at last say , 

 ' now a'm willing to tell yo ' a ' about it to - morrow , for it be not ivery day a man can do such great thing ; but to - neet a mun go to bed , even if King George be wantin ' for to know how a manage it a ' . ' 

 he go wearily upstairs , and wife and daughter both strove their good to ease his ache limb , and make he comfortable . the warming - pan , only use on state occasion , be take down and unpapere for his service ; and as he get between the warm sheet , he thank Sylvia and her mother in a sleepy voice , add , 

 ' it be a vast o ' comfort to think on yon poor lad as be sleepin ' I ' their own home this neet , ' and then slumber fall upon he , and he be hardly rouse by Bell 's softly kiss his weather - beat cheek , and say low , 

 ' God bless thee , my man ! Thou be allay for they that be down and put upon . ' 

 he murmur some monosyllabic reply , unheard by his wife , who steal away to undress herself noiselessly , and lay herself down on her side of the bed as gently as her stiffen limb would permit . 

 they be late in rise the next morning . Kester be long since up and at his work among the cattle before he see the house - door open to admit the fresh chill morning air ; and even then Sylvia brush softly , and go about almost on tip - toe . when the porridge be ready , Kester be call in to his breakfast , which he take sit at the dresser with the family . a large wooden platter stand in the middle ; and each have a bowl of the same material fill with milk . the way be for every one to dip his pewter spoon into the central dish , and convey as much or as little as he like at a time of the hot porridge into his pure fresh milk . but to - day Bell tell Kester to help himself all at once , and to take his bowl up to the master 's room and keep he company . for Daniel be in bed , rest from his weariness , and bemoan his painful bruise whenever he think of they . but his mind be still so much occupy with the affair of the previous night , that Bell judge rightly that a new listener would give ease to his body as well as to his mind , and her proposal of Kester 's carry up his breakfast have be receive by Daniel with satisfaction . 

 so Kester go up slowly , carry his over - full basin tenderly , and seat himself on the step lead down into the bed - room   for level have not be calculate when the old house be build   face his master , who , half sit up in the blue check bed , not unwillingly begin his relation again ; to which Kester listen so attentively , that his spoon be often arrest in its progress from the basin to his mouth , open ready to receive it , while he gaze with unwinke eye at Daniel narrate his exploit . 

 but after Daniel have fight his battle o'er again to every auditor within his reach , he find the seclusion of his chamber rather oppressive , without even the usual week - day ' noise below ; so after dinner , though far from well , he come down and wander about the stable and the field near to the house , consult with Kester as to crop and manure for the most part ; but every now and then break out into an episodical chuckle over some part of last night 's proceeding . Kester enjoy the day even more than his master , for he have no bruise to remind he that , although a hero , he be also flesh and blood . 

 when they return to the house they find Philip there , for it be already dusk . it be Kester 's usual Sunday plan to withdraw to bed at as early an hour as he could manage to sleep , often in winter before six ; but now he be too full of interest in what Philip might have to tell of Monkshaven news to forego his Sabbath privilege of spend the evening sit on the chair at the end of the dresser behind the door . 

 Philip be as close to Sylvia as he could possibly get without give her offence , when they come in . her manner be listless and civil ; she have lose all that active feeling towards he which make he positively distasteful , and have call out her girlish irritation and impertinence . she now be rather glad to see he than otherwise . he bring some change into the heavy monotony of her life monotony so peaceful until she have be stir by passion out of that content with the small daily event which have now become burdensome recurrence . insensibly to herself she be become dependent on his timid devotion , his constant attention ; and he , lover - like , once so attract , in spite of his judgment , by her liveliness and piquancy , now dot on her languor , and think her silence more sweet than word . 

 he have only just arrive when master and man come in . he have be to afternoon chapel ; none of they have think of go to the distant church ; worship with they be only an occasional duty , and this day their mind have be too full of the event of the night before . Daniel sate himself heavily down in his accustomed chair , the three - cornered arm - chair in the fireside corner , which no one think of anybody else ever occupy on any occasion whatever . in a minute or two he interrupt Philip 's word of greeting and inquiry by break out into the story of the rescue of last night . but to the mute surprise of Sylvia , the only one who notice it , Philip 's face , instead of express admiration and pleasant wonder , lengthen into dismay ; once or twice he begin to interrupt , but stop himself as if he would consider his word again . Kester be never tired of hear his master talk ; by long live together they understand every fold of each other 's mind , and small expression have much significance to they . Bell , too , sate thankful that her husband should have do such deed . only Sylvia be make uneasy by Philip 's face and manner . when Daniel have end there be a great silence , instead of the question and compliment he look to receive . he become testy , and turn to Bell , say , 

 ' my nephew look as though he be a - thinking more on t ' little profit he have make on his pin an ' bobs , than as if he be heed how honest man be save from be hale out to yon tender , an ' carry out o ' sight o ' wife and little ' uns for iver . wife an ' little ' uns may go t ' workhouse or clem for aught he care . 

 Philip go very red , and then more sallow than usual . he have not be think of Charley Kinraid , but of quite another thing , while Daniel have tell his story ; but this last speech of the old man 's bring up the remembrance that be always quick , do what he would to smother or strangle it . he do not speak for a moment or two , then he say , 

 ' to - day have not be like Sabbath in Monkshaven . t ' rioter , as folk call them , have be about all night . they want to give battle to t ' man - o'-war 's man ; and it be take up by th ' well end , and they have   send to my Lord Malton for t ' militia ; and they be come into t ' town , and they be hunt for a justice for t ' read th ' act ; folk do say there will be niver a shop open to - morrow . ' 

 this be rather a more serious account of the progress of the affair than any one have calculate upon . they look grave upon it awhile , then Daniel take heart and say , 

 ' a think we would do a'most enough last neet ; but man be not to be stop wi ' a straw when their blood be up ; still it be hard line to call out t ' sojer , even if they be but militia . so what we seven hatch in a dark entry have ta'en a lord to put a stop to ' t ! ' continue he , chuckle a little , but more faintly this time . 

 Philip go on , still graver than before , boldly continue to say what he know would be discordant to the family he love so well . 

 ' I should ha ' tell yo ' all about it ; I think on it just as a bit o ' news ; I would niver think on such a thing as uncle there have be in it , and I be main sorry to hear on it , I be . ' 

 ' why ? ' say Sylvia , breathlessly . 

 ' it be niver a thing to be sorry on . I be proud and glad , ' say Bell . 

 ' let - a - be , let - a - be , ' say Daniel , in much dudgeon . ' a be a fool to tell he o ' such - like doing , they be noane I ' his line ; we will talk on yard measure now . 

 Philip take no notice of this poor attempt at sarcasm : he seem as if lose in thought , then he say , 

 ' I be vex to plague yo ' , but I would well say all I have   get I ' my mind . there be a vast o ' folk at our chapel speak about it last night 's doing and this morning 's work and how they as set it afoot be assure o ' be clapt int ' prison and try for it ; and when I heere uncle say as he be one , it like run through I ; for they say as t ' justice will be all on t ' Government side , and mad for vengeance . ' 

 for an instant there be dead silence . the woman look at each other with blank eye , as if they be as yet unable to take in the new idea that the conduct which have seem to they a subject for such just pride could be regard by any one as deserve of punishment or retribution . Daniel speak before they have recover from their amazement . 

 ' A'm noane sorry for what a do , an ' a'd do it again to - neet , if need be . so theere be for thee . Thou may tell t ' justice fra ' I that a reckon a do righter nor they , as letten poor felly be carry off I ' t ' very midst o ' t ' town they be call justice for . ' 

 perhaps Philip have well have hold his tongue ; but he believe in the danger , which he be anxious to impress upon his uncle , in order that , know what be to be apprehend , the latter might take some pain to avert it . 

 he go on . 

 ' but they be make a coil about the Randyvowse be all destroy ! ' 

 Daniel have take down his pipe from the shelf in the chimney corner , and be stuff tobacco into the bowl . he go on pretend to do this a little while after it be fill ; for , to tell the truth , he be begin to feel uncomfortable at the new view of his conduct present to he . still he be not go to let this appear , so lift up his head with an indifferent air he light the pipe , blow into it , take it out and examine it as something be wrong about it , and until that be put to right he be unable to attend to anything else ; all the while the faithful three who hang upon his well - being , gaze , breathless , at his proceeding , and anxious for his reply . 

 ' randyvowse ! ' say he at length , ' it be a good job it be brenne down , for such a harbour for vermin a never seed : t ' rat run across t ' yard by hunder an ' thousand ; an ' it be no man 's property as a've   heerd tell , but belong to Chancery , up I ' Lunnon ; so wheere 's t ' harm do , my fine felly ? ' 

 Philip be silent . he do not care to brave any furth his uncle 's angry frown and contract eye . if he have only know of Daniel Robson 's part in the riot before he have leave the town , he would have take care to have have well authority for the reality of the danger which he have hear speak about , and in which he could not help believe . as it be , he could only keep quiet until he have ascertain what be the legal peril overhang the rioter , and how far his uncle have be recognize . 

 Daniel go on puff angrily . Kester sigh audibly , and then be sorry he have do so , and begin to whistle . Bell , full of her new fear , yet desirous to bring all present into some kind of harmony , say , 

 ' it will ha ' be a loss to John Hobbs all his thing burn , or trample on . Mebbe he desarve it all , but one be a kind o ' tender feeling to one 's table and chair , special if one 's have t ' bee - waxing on them . ' 

 ' a wish he have be burn on t ' top on them , a do , ' growl out Daniel , shake the ash out of his pipe . 

 ' do not   speak so ill o ' thysel ' , ' say his wife . ' Thou'd ha ' be t ' first t ' pluck he down if he have screech out . ' 

 ' an ' a'll warrant if they come about wi ' a paper ask for feyther 's name to make up for what Hobbs have lose by t ' fire , feyther will be for give he summut , ' say Sylvia . 

 ' Thou know nought about it , ' say Daniel . ' hold thy tongue next time till thou 's ax to speak , my wench . ' 

 his sharp irritated way of speak be so new to Sylvia , that the tear spring to her eye , and her lip quiver . Philip see it all , and yearn over she . he plunge headlong into some other subject to try and divert attention from she ; but Daniel be too ill at ease to talk much , and Bell be oblige to try and keep up the semblance of conversation , with an occasional word or two from Kester , who seem instinctively to fall into her way of thinking , and to endeavour to keep the dark thought in the background . 

 Sylvia steal off to bed ; more concerned at her father 's angry way of speak than at the idea of his be amenable to law for what he have do ; the one be a sharp present evil , the other something distant and unlikely . yet a dim terror of this latter evil hang over she , and once upstairs she throw herself on her bed and sob . Philip hear she where he sate near the bottom of the short steep staircase , and at every sob the cord of love round his heart seem tighten , and he feel as if he must there and then do something to console she . 

 but , instead , he sit on talk of nothing , a conversation in which Daniel join with somewhat of surliness , while Bell , grave and anxious , keep wistfully look from one to the other , desirous of glean some further information on the subject , which have begin to trouble her mind . she hop some chance would give she the opportunity of privately question Philip , but it seem to be equally her husband 's wish to thwart any such intention of her . he remain in the house - place , till after Philip have leave , although he be evidently so much fatigue as to give some very distinct , though unintentional , hint to his visitor to be go . 

 at length the house - door be lock on Philip , and then Daniel prepare to go to bed . Kester have leave for his loft above the shippen more than an hour before . Bell have still to rake the fire , and then she would follow her husband upstairs . 

 as she be scrap up the ash , she hear , intermix with the noise she be make , the sound of some one rap gently at the window . in she then frame of mind she start a little ; but on look round , she see Kester 's face press against the glass , and , reassure , she softly open the door . there he stand in the dusk outer air , distinct against the gray darkness beyond , and in his hand something which she presently perceive be a pitchfork . 

 ' Missus ! ' whisper he , ' a've   watch t ' maister t ' bed ; an ' now a'd be greatly beholden to yo ' if yo'd let I just lay I down I ' t ' house - place . A'd warrant niver a constable I ' a ' Monkshaven should get sight o ' t ' maister , an ' I below t ' keep ward . ' 

 Bell shiver a little . 

 ' Nay , Kester , ' she say , pat her hand kindly on his shoulder ; ' there be nought for t ' fear . Thy master be not one for t ' hurt nobody ; and I dunnot think they can harm he for set yon poor chap free , as t ' gang catch I ' their wicked trap . ' 

 Kester stand still ; then he shake his head slowly . 

 ' it be t ' work at t ' randyvowse as a'm afeare on . some folk think such a deal o ' a bonfire . then a may lay I down afore t ' fire , missus ? ' say he , beseechingly . 

 ' Nay , Kester ' she begin ; but suddenly change , she say , ' God bless thee , my man ; come in and lie thee down on t ' settle , and I will cover thee up wi ' my cloak as hang behind t ' door . we be not many on we that love he , an ' we will be all on we under one roof , an ' niver a stone wall or a lock betwixt we . ' 

 so Kester take up his rest in the house - place that night , and none know of it besides Bell . 




 CHAPTER XXV 

 come trouble 

 the morning bring more peace if it do not entirely dissipate fear . Daniel seem to have get over his irritability , and be unusually kind and tender to wife and daughter , especially strive by silent little deed to make up for the sharp word he have say the night before to the latter . 

 as if by common consent , all allusion to the Saturday night 's proceeding be avoid . they speak of the day 's work before they ; of the crop to be sow ; of the cattle ; of the market ; but each one be conscious of a wish to know more distinctly what be the chance of the danger that , to judge from Philip 's word , hang over they , fall upon they and cut they off from all these place for the come day . 

 Bell long to send Kester down into Monkshaven as a sort of spy to see how the land lay ; but she dare not manifest her anxiety to her husband , and could not see Kester alone . she wish that she have tell he to go to the town , when she have have he to herself in the house - place the night before ; now it seem as though Daniel be resolve not to part from he , and as though both have forget that any peril have be anticipate . Sylvia and her mother , in like manner , clung together , not speak of their fear , yet each know that it be ever present in the other 's mind . 

 so thing go on till twelve o'clock dinner - time . if at any time that morning they have have the courage to speak together on the thought which be engross all their mind , it be possible that some mean might have be find to avert the calamity that be come towards they with swift foot . but among the uneducated the partially educate nay , even the weakly educate the feeling exist which prompt the futile experiment of the well - know ostrich . they imagine that , by close their own eye to apprehend evil , they avert it . the expression of fear be suppose to accelerate the coming of its cause . yet , on the other hand , they shrink from acknowledge the long continuance of any blessing , in the idea that when unusual happiness be speak about , it disappear . so , although perpetual complaint of past or present grievance and sorrow be most common among this class , they shrink from embody apprehension for the future in word , as if it then take shape and draw near . 

 they all four sate down to dinner , but not one of they be incline to eat . the food be scarcely touch on their plate , yet they be try to make talk among themselves as usual ; they seem as though they dare not let themselves be silent , when Sylvia , sit opposite to the window , see Philip at the top of the brow , run rapidly towards the farm . she have be so full of the anticipation of some kind of misfortune all the morning that she feel now as if this be the very precursive circumstance she have be expect ; she stand up , turn quite white , and , point with her finger , say , 

 ' there he be ! ' 

 every one at table stand up too . an instant afterwards , Philip , breathless , be in the room . 

 he gasp out , ' they be come ! the warrant be out . you must go . I hop you be go . ' 

 ' God help we ! ' say Bell , and sate suddenly down , as if she have receive a blow that make her collapse into helplessness ; but she get up again directly . 

 Sylvia fly for her father 's hat . he really seem the most unmoved of the party . 

 ' a'm noane afeare , ' say he . ' A'd do it o'er again , a would ; an ' a'll tell them so . it be a fine time o ' day when man be to be trap and carry off , an ' they as lay trap to set them free be to be put I ' t ' lock - up for it . ' 

 ' but there be rioting , beside the rescue ; t ' house be burn , ' continue eager , breathless Philip . 

 ' an ' a'm noane go t ' say a'm sorry for that , neyther ; tho ' , mebbe , a would not   do it again . ' 

 Sylvia have his hat on his head by this time ; and Bell , wan and stiff , tremble all over , have his over - coat , and his leather purse with the few coin she could muster , ready for he to put on . 

 he look at these preparation , at his wife and daughter , and his colour change from its ruddy brown . 

 ' A'd face lock - up , an ' a fair spell o ' jail , but for these , ' say he , hesitate . 

 ' oh ! ' say Philip , ' for God 's sake , lose no time , but be off . ' 

 ' where mun he go ? ' ask Bell , as if Philip must decide all . 

 ' anywhere , anywhere , out of this house say Haverstone . this evening , I will go and meet he there and plan far ; only be off now . ' Philip be so keenly eager , he hardly take note at the time of Sylvia 's one vivid look of unspoken thank , yet he remember it afterwards . 

 ' a'll dang them dead , ' say Kester , rush to the door , for he see what the other do not that all chance of escape be over ; the constable be already at the top of the little field - path not twenty yard off . 

 ' hide he , hide he , ' cry Bell , wring her hand in terror ; for she , indeed they all , know that flight would now be impossible . Daniel be heavy , rheumatic , and , moreover , have be pretty severely bruise on that unlucky night . 

 Philip , without another word , push Daniel before he upstairs , feel that his own presence at Haytersbank Farm at that hour of the day would be a betrayal . they have just time to shut themselves up in the large bed - room , before they hear a scuffle and the constable ' entry down - stair . 

 ' they be in , ' say Philip , as Daniel squeeze himself under the bed ; and then they hold quite still , Philip as much conceal by the scanty , blue - check curtain as he could manage to be . they hear a confusion of voice below , a hasty moving of chair , a banging of door , a further parley , and then a woman 's scream , shrill and pitiful ; then step on the stair . 

 ' that screech spoil all , ' sigh Philip . 

 in one instant the door be open , and each of the hider be conscious of the presence of the constable , although at first the latter stand motionless , survey the apparently empty room with disappointment . then in another moment they have rush at Philip 's leg , expose as these be . they draw he out with violence , and then let he go . 

 ' Measter Hepburn ! ' say one in amaze . but immediately they put two and two together ; for in so small a place as Monkshaven every one 's relationship and connexion , and even liking , be know ; and the motive of Philip 's come out to Haytersbank be perfectly clear to these man . 

 ' T ' other will not be far off , ' say the other constable . ' his plate be down - stair , full o ' victual ; a seed Measter Hepburn a - walk briskly before I as a left monkshaven . ' 

 ' here he be , here he be , ' call out the other man , drag Daniel out by his leg , ' we have   getten he . ' 

 Daniel kick violently , and come out from his hiding - place in a less ignominious way than by be pull out by his heel . 

 he shake himself , and then turn , face his captor . 

 ' a wish a'd niver hidden mysel ' ; it be his doing , ' jerk his thumb toward Philip : ' a'm ready to stand by what a've   do . Yo've   getten a warrant a'll be bind , for they justice be grand at writin ' when t ' fight be over . ' 

 he be try to carry it off with bravado , but Philip see that he have receive a shock , from his sudden look of wither colour and shrunken feature . 

 ' do not   handcuff he , ' say Philip , put money into the constable 's hand . ' you will be able to guard he well enough without they thing . ' 

 Daniel turn round sharp at this whisper . 

 ' let - a - be , let - a - be , my lad , ' he say . ' it will be summut to think on I ' t ' lock - up how two able - bodied felly be so afeared on t ' chap as reskye they honest sailor o ' Saturday neet , as they mun put he I ' gyve , and he sixty - two come Martinmas , and sore lay up wi ' t ' rheumatic . ' 

 but it be difficult to keep up this tone of bravado when he be lead a prisoner through his own house - place , and see his poor wife quiver and shake all over with her effort to keep back all sign of emotion until he be go ; and Sylvia stand by her mother , her arm round Bell 's waist and stroke the poor shrunken finger which work so perpetually and nervously in futile unconscious restlessness . Kester be in a corner of the room , sullenly stand . 

 Bell quake from head to foot as her husband come down - stair a prisoner . she open her lip several time with an uneasy motion , as if she would fain say something , but know not what . Sylvia 's passionate swollen lip and her beautiful defiant eye give her face quite a new aspect ; she look a helpless fury . 

 ' a may kiss my missus , a reckon , ' say Daniel , come to a standstill as he pass near she . 

 ' oh , Dannel , Dannel ! ' cry she , open her arm wide to receive he . ' Dannel , Dannel , my man ! ' and she shake with her crying , lay her head on his shoulder , as if he be all her stay and comfort . 

 ' come , missus ! come , missus ! ' say he , ' there could not   be more ado if a'd be guilty of murder , an ' yet a say again , as a said afore , a'm noane ashamed o ' my doing . here , Sylvie , lass , tak ' thy mother off I , for a can not do it mysel ' , it like set I off . ' his voice be quaver as he say this . but he cheer up a little and say , ' now , good - by , oud wench '   kiss she , ' and keep a good heart , and let I see thee lookin ' lusty and strong when a come back . good - by , my lass ; look well after mother , and ask Philip for guidance if it be need . ' 

 he be take out of his home , and then arise the shrill cry of the woman ; but in a minute or two they be check by the return of one of the constable , who , cap in hand at the sight of so much grief , say , 

 ' he want a word wi ' his daughter . ' 

 the party have come to a halt about ten yard from the house . Sylvia , hastily wipe her tear on her apron , run out and throw her arm round her father , as if to burst out afresh on his neck . 

 ' Nay , nay , my wench , it be thee as mun be a comfort to mother : nay , nay , or thou'll niver hear what a've   get to say . Sylvie , my lass , a'm main and sorry a be so short wi ' thee last neet ; a ax thy pardon , lass , a be cross to thee , and send thee to thy bed wi ' a sore heart . Thou munnot think on it again , but forgie I , now a'm leavin ' thee . ' 

 ' oh , feyther ! feyther ! ' be all Sylvia could say ; and at last they have to make as though they would have use force to separate she from their prisoner . Philip take her hand , and softly lead she back to her weeping mother . 

 for some time nothing be to be hear in the little farmhouse kitchen but the sobbing and wail of the woman . Philip stand by silent , thinking , as well as he could , for his keen sympathy with their grief , what have well be do next . Kester , after some growl at Sylvia for have hold back the uplifted arm which he think might have save Daniel by a well - consider blow on his captor as they enter the house , go back into his shippen his cell for meditation and consolation , where he might hope to soothe himself before go out to his afternoon 's work ; labour which his master have plan for he that very morning , with a strange foresight , as Kester think , for the job be one which would take he two or three day without need any further direction than those he have receive , and by the end of that time he think that his master would be at liberty again . so he so they all think in their ignorance and inexperience . 

 although Daniel himself be unreasoning , hasty , impulsive in a word , often think and act very foolishly yet , somehow , either from some quality in his character , or from the loyalty of nature in those with whom he have to deal in his every - day life , he have make his place and position clear as the arbiter and law - giver of his household . on his decision , as that of husband , father , master , perhaps superior nature wait . so now that he be go and have leave they in such strange new circumstance so suddenly , it seem as though neither Bell nor Sylvia know exactly what to do when their grief be spend , so much have every household action and plan be regulate by the thought of he . meanwhile Philip have slowly be arrive at the conclusion that he be more want at Monkshaven to look after Daniel 's interest , to learn what be the legal probability in consequence of the old man 's arrest , and to arrange for his family accordingly , than stand still and silent in the Haytersbank kitchen , too full of fellow - feeling and heavy foreboding to comfort , awkwardly unsympathetic in appearance from the very aching of his heart . 

 so when his aunt , with instinctive sense of regularity and propriety , begin to put away the scarcely taste dinner , and Sylvia , blind with crying , and convulsively sob , be yet try to help her mother , Philip take his hat , and brush it round and round with the sleeve of his coat , say , 

 ' I think I will just go back , and see how matter stand . ' he have a more distinct plan in his head than these word imply , but it depend on so many contingency of which he be ignorant that he say only these few word ; and with a silent resolution to see they again that day , but a dread of be compel to express his fear , so far beyond theirs , he go off without say anything more . then Sylvia lift up her voice with a great cry . somehow she have expect he to do something what , she do not know ; but he be go , and they be leave without stay or help . 

 ' hush thee , hush thee , ' say her mother , tremble all over herself ; ' it be for the good . the Lord know . ' 

 ' but I niver think he would leave we , ' moan Sylvia , half in her mother 's arm , and think of Philip . her mother take the word as apply to Daniel . 

 ' and he would niver ha ' leave we , my wench , if he could ha ' stay . ' 

 ' oh , mother , mother , it be Philip as have leave we , and he could ha ' stay . ' 

 ' he will come back , or mebbe send , I will be bind . leastway he will be go to see feyther , and he will need comfort most on all , in a fremd place in Bridewell and niver a morsel of victual or a piece o ' money . ' and now she sate down , and weep the dry hot tear that come with such difficulty to the eye of the aged . and so first one grieve , and then the other , and each drain her own heart of every possible hope by way of comfort , alternately try to cheer and console the February afternoon pass away ; the continuous rain close in the daylight even early than usual , and add to the dreariness , with the natural accompaniment of wail wind , come with long sweep over the moor , and make the sobbing at the window that always sound like the gasp of some one in great agony . meanwhile Philip have hasten back to Monkshaven . he have no umbrella , he have to face the drive rain for the great part of the way ; but he be thankful to the weather , for it keep man indoor , and he want to meet no one , but to have time to think and mature his plan . the town itself be , so to speak , in mourning . the rescue of the sailor be a distinctly popular movement ; the subsequent violence   which have , indeed , go much far than have be describe , after Daniel leave it   be , in general , consider as only a kind of due punishment inflict in wild justice on the press - gang and their abettor . the feeling of the monkshaven people be , therefore , in decide opposition to the vigorous step take by the county magistrate , who , in consequence of an appeal from the naval officer in charge of the impressment service , have call out the militia   from a distant and inland county   station within a few mile , and have thus summarily quench the riot that be continue on the Sunday morning after a somewhat languid fashion ; the great part of the destruction of property have be accomplish during the previous night . still there be little doubt but that the violence would have be renew as evening draw on , and the more desperate part of the population and the enrage sailor have have the Sabbath leisure to brood over their wrong , and to encourage each other in a passionate attempt at redress , or revenge . so the authority be quite justified in the decide step they have take , both in their own estimation then , and now , in ours , look back on the affair in cold blood . but at the time feeling run strongly against they ; and all mean of express itself in action be prevent , man brood sullenly in their own house . Philip , as the representative of the family , the head of which be now suffer for his deed in the popular cause , would have meet with more sympathy , ay , and more respect than he imagine , as he go along the street , glance from side to side , fearful of meet some who would shy he as the relation of one who have be ignominiously take to Bridewell a few hour before . but in spite of this wincing of Philip 's from observation and remark , he never dream of act otherwise than as become a brave true friend . and this he do , and would have do , from a natural faithfulness and constancy of disposition , without any special regard for Sylvia . 

 he know his service be need in the shop ; business which he have leave at a moment 's warning await he , unfinishe ; but at this time he could not bear the torture of give explanation , and allege reason to the languid intelligence and slow sympathy of Coulson . 

 he go to the office of Mr. Donkin , the old established and most respected attorney in Monkshaven he who have be employ to draw up the law paper and deed of partnership consequent on Hepburn and Coulson succeed to the shop of John and Jeremiah Foster , Brothers . 

 Mr. Donkin know Philip from this circumstance . but , indeed , nearly every one in Monkshaven know each other ; if not enough to speak to , at least enough to be acquaint with the personal appearance and reputation of most of those whom they meet in the street . it so happen that Mr. Donkin have a favourable opinion of Philip ; and perhaps for this reason the latter have a short time to wait before he obtain an interview with the head of the house , than many of the client who come for that purpose from town or country for many mile round . 

 Philip be usher in . Mr. Donkin sate with his spectacle push up on his forehead , ready to watch his countenance and listen to his word . 

 ' good afternoon , Mr. Hepburn ! ' 

 ' good afternoon , sir . ' Philip hesitate how to begin . Mr. Donkin become impatient , and tap with the finger of his left hand on his desk . Philip 's sensitive nerve feel and rightly interpret the action . 

 ' please , sir , I be come to speak to you about Daniel Robson , of Haytersbank Farm . ' 

 ' Daniel Robson ? ' say Mr. Donkin , after a short pause , to try and compel Philip into speed in his story . 

 ' yes , sir . he be be take up on account of this affair , sir , about the press - gang on Saturday night . ' 

 ' to be sure ! I think I know the name . ' and Mr. Donkin 's face become graver , and the expression more concentrated . look up suddenly at Philip , he say , ' you be aware that I be the clerk to the magistrate ? ' 

 ' no , sir , ' in a tone that indicate the unexpressed ' what then ? ' 

 ' well , but I be . and so of course , if you want my service or advice in favour of a prisoner whom they have commit , or be go to commit , you can not   have they , that be all . ' 

 ' I be very sorry very ! ' say Philip ; and then he be again silent for a period ; long enough to make the busy attorney impatient . 

 ' well , Mr. Hepburn , have you anything else to say to I ? ' 

 ' yes , sir . I have   a deal to ask of you ; for you see I do not   rightly understand what to do ; and yet I be all as Daniel 's wife and daughter have to look to ; and I have   their grief heavy on my heart . you could not tell I what be to be do with Daniel , could you , sir ? ' 

 ' he will be bring up before the magistrate to - morrow morning for final examination , along with the other , you know , before he be send to York Castle to take his trial at the spring assize . ' 

 ' to York Castle , sir ? ' 

 Mr. Donkin nod , as if word be too precious to waste . 

 ' and when will he go ? ' ask poor Philip , in dismay . 

 ' to - morrow : most probably as soon as the examination be over . the evidence be clear as to his be present , aid and abetting , indict on the 4th section of 1 George I. , statute 1 , chapter 5 . I be afraid it be a bad look - out . be he a friend of your , Mr. Hepburn ? ' 

 ' only an uncle , sir , ' say Philip , his heart get full ; more from Mr. Donkin 's manner than from his word . ' but what can they do to he , sir ? ' 

 ' do ? ' Mr. Donkin half smile at the ignorance display . ' why , hang he , to be sure ; if the judge be in a hang mood . he be be either a principal in the offence , or a principal in the second degree , and , as such , liable to the full punishment . I draw up the warrant myself this morning , though I leave the exact name to be fill up by my clerk . ' 

 ' oh , sir ! can you do nothing for I ? ' ask Philip , with sharp beseeching in his voice . he have never imagine that it be a capital offence ; and the thought of his aunt 's and Sylvia 's ignorance of the possible fate await he whom they so much love , be like a stab to his heart . 

 ' no , my good fellow . I be sorry ; but , you see , it be my duty to do all I can to bring criminal to justice . ' 

 ' my uncle think he be do such a fine deed . ' 

 ' demolish and pull down , destroy and burn dwelling - house and outhouse , ' say Mr. Donkin . ' he must have some peculiar notion . ' 

 ' the people be so mad with the press - gang , and Daniel have be at sea hisself ; and take it so to heart when he hear of mariner and seafare folk be carry off , and just cheat into do what be kind and helpful leastway , what would have be kind and helpful , if there have be a fire . I be against violence and riot myself , sir , I be sure ; but I can not help think as Daniel have a deal to justify he on Saturday night , sir . ' 

 ' well ; you must try and get a good lawyer to bring out all that side of the question . there be a good deal to be say on it ; but it be my duty to get up all the evidence to prove that he and other be present on the night in question ; so , as you will perceive , I can give you no help in defend he . ' 

 ' but who can , sir ? I come to you as a friend who , I think , would see I through it . and I do not   know any other lawyer ; leastway , to speak to . ' 

 Mr. Donkin be really more concerned for the misguided rioter than he be aware ; and he be aware of more interest than he care to express . so he soften his tone a little , and try to give the good advice in his power . 

 ' you would well go to Edward Dawson on the other side of the river ; he that be article clerk with I two year ago , you know . he be a clever fellow , and have not too much practice ; he will do the good he can for you . he will have to be at the court - house , tell he , to - morrow morning at ten , when the justice meet . he will watch the case for you ; and then he will give you his opinion , and tell you what to do . you can not   do well than follow his advice . I must do all I can to collect evidence for a conviction , you know . ' 

 Philip stand up , look at his hat , and then come forward and lay down six and eightpence on the desk in a blushing , awkward way . 

 ' Pooh ! pooh ! ' say Mr. Donkin , push the money away . ' do not   be a fool ; you will need it all before the trial be over . I have   do nothing , man . it would be a pretty thing for I to be feed by both party . ' 

 Philip take up the money , and leave the room . in an instant he come back again , glance furtively at Mr. Donkin 's face , and then , once more have recourse to brush his hat , he say , in a low voice 

 ' you will not be hard upon he , sir , I hope ? ' 

 ' I must do my duty , ' reply Mr. Donkin , a little sternly , ' without any question of hardness . ' 

 Philip , discomfited , leave the room ; an instant of thought and Mr Donkin have jump up , and hasten to the door he open it and call after Philip . 

 ' Hepburn Hepburn I say , he will be take to York as soon as may be to - morrow morning ; if any one want to see he before then , they would well look sharp about it . ' 

 Philip go quickly along the street towards Mr. Dawson 's , ponder upon the meaning of all that he have hear , and what he have well do . he have make his plan pretty clearly out by the time he arrive at Mr. Dawson 's smart door in one of the new street on the other side of the river . a clerk as smart as the door answer Philip 's hesitating knock , and reply to his inquiry as to whether Mr. Dawson be at home , in the negative , add , after a moment 's pause 

 ' he will be at home in less than an hour ; he be only go to make Mrs Dawson 's will Mrs. Dawson , of Collyton she be not expect to get well . ' 

 probably the clerk of an older - establish attorney would not have give so many particular as to the nature of his master 's employment ; but , as it happen it be of no consequence , the unnecessary information make no impression on Philip 's mind ; he think the matter over and then say 

 ' I will be back in an hour , then . it be go a quarter to four ; I will be back before five , tell Mr. Dawson . ' 

 he turn on his heel and go back to the High Street as fast as he could , with a far more prompt and decide step than before . he hasten through the street , empty by the bad weather , to the principal inn of the town , the George the sign of which be fasten to a piece of wood stretch across the narrow street ; and go up to the bar with some timidity   for the inn be frequent by the gentry of Monkshaven and the neighbourhood , and be consider as a touch above such customer as Philip , he ask if he could have a tax - cart make ready in a quarter of an hour , and send up to the door of his shop . 

 ' to be sure he could ; how far be it to go ? ' 

 Philip hesitate before he reply 

 ' up the Knotting Lane , to the stile lead down to Haytersbank Farm ; they will have to wait there for some as be come . ' 

 ' they must not wait long such an evening as this ; stand in such rain and wind as there will be up there , be enough to kill a horse . ' 

 ' they shall not   wait long , ' say Philip , decisively : ' in a quarter of an hour , mind . ' 

 he now go back to the shop , beat against the storm , which be increase as the tide come in and the night hour approach . 

 Coulson have no word for he , but he look reproachfully at his partner for his long , unexplained absence . hester be put away the ribbon and handkerchief , and bright - colour thing which have be use to deck the window ; for no more customer be likely to come this night through the bluster weather to a shop dimly light by two tallow candle and an inefficient oil - lamp . Philip come up to she , and stand look at she with unseee eye ; but the strange consciousness of his fix stare make she uncomfortable , and call the faint flush to her pale cheek , and at length compel she , as it be , to speak , and break the spell of the silence . so , curiously enough , all three speak at once . hester ask   without look at Philip  

 ' Yo're sadly wet , I be fear ? ' 

 Coulson say 

 ' Thou might have a bit o ' news to tell one after be on the gad all afternoon . ' 

 Philip whisper to hester 

 ' Wilt come into t ' parlour ? I want a word wi ' thee by oursel 's . ' 

 hester quietly finish roll up the ribbon she have in her hand when he speak , and then follow he into the room behind the shop before speak of . 

 Philip set down on the table the candle which he have bring out of the shop , and turn round to hester , take her trembling hand into both of his , and grip it nervously , say 

 ' oh ! hester , thou must help I thou will , will not thou ? ' 

 hester gulp down something that seem to rise in her throat and choke she , before she answer . 

 ' anything , thou know , Philip . ' 

 ' yes , yes , I know . Thou see the matter be this : Daniel Robson he who marry my aunt be take up for yon riot on Saturday night at t ' Mariners ' arm ' 

 ' they speak on it this afternoon ; they say the warrant be out , ' say Hester , fill up the sentence as Philip hesitate , lose for an instant in his own thought . 

 ' ay ! the warrant be out , and he be in t ' lock - up , and will be carry to York Castle to - morrow morn ; and I be afeared it will go bad with he ; and they at Haytersbank be not prepare , and they must see he again before he go . now , Hester , will thou go in a tax - cart as will be here in less than ten minute from t ' George , and bring they back here , and they must stay all night for to be ready to see he to - morrow before he go ? it be dree weather for they , but they will not mind that . ' 

 he have use word as if he be make a request to hester ; but he do not seem to await her answer , so sure be he that she would go . she notice this , and notice also that the rain be speak of in reference to they , not to she . a cold shadow pass over her heart , though it be nothing more than she already know that Sylvia be the one centre of his thought and his love . 

 ' I will go put on my thing at once , ' say she , gently . 

 Philip press her hand tenderly , a glow of gratitude overspread he . 

 ' Thou be a real good one , God bless thee ! ' say he . ' Thou must take care of thyself , too , ' continue he ; ' there be wrap and plenty I ' th ' house , and if there be not , there be those I ' the shop as will be none the bad for once wear at such a time as this ; and wrap thee well up , and take shawl and cloak for they , and mind as they put them on . Thou'll have to get out at a stile , I will tell t ' driver where ; and thou must get over t ' stile and follow t ' path down two field , and th ' house be right before ye , and bid them make haste and lock up th ' house , for they mun stay all night here . Kester will look after thing . ' 

 all this time hester be hastily put on her hat and cloak , which she have fetch from the closet where they usually hang through the day ; now she stand listen , as it be , for final direction . 

 ' but suppose they will not come , ' say she ; ' they dunnot know I , and may not   believe my word . ' 

 ' they must , ' say he , impatiently . ' they do not   know what await them , ' he continue . ' I will tell thee , because thou will not let out , and it seem as if I mun tell some one it be such a shock he be to be try for 's life . they know not it be so serious ; and , Hester , ' say he , go on in his search after sympathy , ' she be like as if she be bind up in her father . ' 

 his lip quiver as he look wistfully into Hester 's face at these word . no need to tell she who be she . no need to put into word the fact , tell plainer than word could have speak it , that his heart be bind up in Sylvia . 

 hester 's face , instead of respond to his look , contract a little , and , for the life of she , she could not have help say , 

 ' why do not   yo ' go yourself , Philip ? ' 

 ' I can not , I can not , ' say he , impatiently . ' I would give the world to go , for I might be able to comfort she ; but there be lawyer to see , and iver so much to do , and they have   niver a man friend but I to do it all . you will tell she , ' say Philip , insinuatingly , as if a fresh thought have strike he , ' as how I would ha ' come . I would fain ha ' come for them , myself , but I could not , because of th ' lawyer , mind yo ' say because of th ' lawyer . I would be loath for she to think I be mind any business of my own at this time ; and , whatever yo ' do , speak hopeful , and , for t ' life of yo ' , do not   speak of th ' hanging , it be likely it be a mistake o ' Donkin 's ; and anyhow there be t ' cart anyhow I should perhaps not ha ' tell thee , but it be a comfort to make a clean breast to a friend at time . God bless thee , Hester . I do not   know what I should ha ' do without thee , ' say he , as he wrap she well up in the cart , and place the bundle of cloak and thing by her side . 

 along the street , in the jolting cart , as long as hester could see the misty light stream out of the shop door , so long be Philip standing bareheade in the rain look after she . but she know that it be not her own poor self that attract his linger gaze . it be the thought of the person she be bind to . 




 CHAPTER XXVI 

 a dreary VIGIL 

 through the dark rain , against the cold wind , shake over the rough stone , go hester in the little tax - cart . her heart keep rise against her fate ; the hot tear come unbidden to her eye . but rebellious heart be soothe , and hot tear be send back to their source before the time come for she alight . 

 the driver turn his horse in the narrow lane , and shout after she an injunction to make haste as , with her head bent low , she struggle down to the path to Haytersbank Farm . she see the light in the window from the top of the brow , and involuntarily she slacken her pace . she have never see Bell Robson , and would Sylvia recollect she ? if she do not how awkward it would be to give the explanation of who she be , and what her errand be , and why she be send . nevertheless , it must be do ; so on she go , and stand within the little porch , she knock faintly at the door ; but in the bluster of the element the sound be lose . again she knock , and now the murmur of woman 's voice inside be hush , and some one come quickly to the door , and open it sharply . 

 it be Sylvia . although her face be completely in shadow , of course hester know she well ; but she , if indeed she would have recognize Hester less disguised , do not know in the least who the woman , muffle up in a great cloak , with her hat tie down with a silk handkerchief , stand in the porch at this time of night , could be . nor , indeed , be she in a mood to care or to inquire . she say hastily , in a voice render hoarse and arid with grief : 

 ' go away . this be no house for stranger to come to . we have   enough on our own to think on ; ' and she hastily shut the door in Hester 's face , before the latter could put together the right word in which to explain her errand . hester stand outside in the dark , wet porch discomfit , and wonder how next to obtain a hearing through the shut and bolt door . not long do she stand , however ; some one be again at the door , talk in a voice of distress and remonstrance , and slowly unbar the bolt . a tall , thin figure of an elderly woman be see against the warm fire - light inside as soon as the door be open ; a hand be put out , like that which take the dove into the ark , and Hester be draw into the warmth and the light , while Bell 's voice go on speak to Sylvia before address the dripping stranger 

 ' it be not a night to turn a dog fra ' t ' door ; it be ill let our grief harden our heart . but oh ! missus   to Hester , yo ' mun forgive we , for a great sorrow have fall upon we this day , an ' we be like beside ourselves wi ' cry an ' plaining . ' 

 Bell sate down , and throw her apron over her poor worn face , as if decently to shield the sign of her misery from a stranger 's gaze . Sylvia , all tear - swollen , and look askance and almost fiercely at the stranger who have make good her intrusion , be draw , as it be , to her mother 's side , and , kneel down by she , put her arm round her waist , and almost lie across her lap , still gaze at hester with cold , distrustful eye , the expression of which repel and daunt that poor , unwilling messenger , and make she silent for a minute or so after her entrance . Bell suddenly put down her apron . 

 ' yo're cold and drench , ' say she . ' come near to t ' fire and warm yo'rsel ' ; yo ' mun pardon we if we dunnot think on everything at onest . ' 

 ' yo're very kind , very kind indeed , ' say hester , touch by the poor woman 's evident effort to forget her own grief in the duty of hospitality , and love Bell from that moment . 

 ' I be hester Rose , ' she continue , half address Sylvia , who she think might remember the name , ' and Philip Hepburn have send I in a tax - cart to t ' stile yonder , to fetch both on yo ' back to Monkshaven . ' Sylvia raise her head and look intently at hester . Bell clasp her hand tight together and leant forwards . 

 ' it be my master as want we ? ' say she , in an eager , question tone . 

 ' it be for to see yo'r master , ' say hester . ' Philip say he will be send to York to - morrow , and yo'll be fain to see he before he go ; and if yo'll come down to Monkshaven to - night , yo'll be on t ' spot again ' the time come when t ' justice will let ye . ' 

 Bell be up and about , make for the place where she keep she out - go thing , almost before Hester have begin to speak . she hardly understand about her husband 's be send to York , in the possession of the idea that she might go and see he . she do not understand or care how , in this wild night , she be to get to monkshaven ; all she think of be , that she might go and see her husband . but Sylvia take in more point than her mother , and , almost suspiciously , begin to question hester . 

 ' why be they send he to York ? what make Philip leave we ? why do not   he come hissel ' ? ' 

 ' he could not   come hissel ' , he bid I say ; because he be bind to be at the lawyer 's at five , about yo'r father 's business . I think yo ' might ha ' know he would ha ' come for any business of his own ; and , about York , it be Philip as tell I , and I never ask why . I never think on yo'r ask I so many question . I think yo'd be ready to fly on any chance o ' see your father . ' hester speak out the sad reproach that run from her heart to her lip . to distrust Philip ! to linger when she might hasten ! 

 ' oh ! ' say Sylvia , break out into a wild cry , that carry with it more conviction of agony than much weeping could have do . ' I may be rude and hard , and I may ask strange question , as if I care for t ' answer yo ' may gi ' I ; an ' , in my heart o ' heart , I care for nought but to have father back wi ' us , as love he so dear . I can hardly tell what I say , much less why I say it . mother be so patient , it put I past mysel ' , for I could fight wi ' t ' very wall , I be so mad wi ' grieving . sure , they will let he come back wi ' us to - morrow , when they hear from his own sel ' why he do it ? ' 

 she look eagerly at hester for an answer to this last question , which she have put in a soft , entreat tone , as if with Hester herself the decision rest . hester shake her head . Sylvia come up to she and take her hand , almost fondle they . 

 ' Yo ' dunnot think they will be hard wi ' he when they hear all about it , do yo ' ? why , York Castle 's t ' place they send a ' t ' thief and robber to , not honest man like feyther . ' 

 hester put her hand on Sylvia 's shoulder with a soft , caress gesture . 

 ' Philip will know , ' she say , use Philip 's name as a kind of spell it would have be so to she . ' come away to Philip , ' say she again , urge Sylvia , by her look and manner , to prepare for the little journey . Sylvia move away for this purpose , say to herself , 

 ' it be go to see feyther : he will tell I all . ' 

 Poor Mrs. Robson be collect a few clothe for her husband with an eager , tremble hand , so tremble that article after article fall to the floor , and it be hester who pick they up ; and at last , after many vain attempt by the grief - shake woman , it be hester who tie the bundle , and arrange the cloak , and fasten down the hood ; Sylvia stand by , not unobservant , though apparently absorb in her own thought . 

 at length , all be arrange , and the key give over to Kester . as they pass out into the storm , Sylvia say to Hester , 

 ' Thou be a real good wench . Thou 's fitter to be about mother than I . I be but a cross - patch at well , an ' now it be like as if I be no good to nobody . ' 

 Sylvia begin to cry , but hester have no time to attend to she , even have she the inclination : all her care be need to help the hasty , totter step of the wife who be feebly speed up the wet and slippery brow to her husband . all Bell think of be that ' he ' be at the end of her toil . she hardly understand when she be to see he ; her weary heart and brain have only receive one idea that each step she be now take be lead she to he . tired and exhaust with her quick walk up hill , battle all the way with wind and rain , she could hardly have hold up another minute when they reach the tax - cart in the lane , and Hester have almost to lift she on to the front seat by the driver . she cover and wrap up the poor old woman , and afterwards place herself in the straw at the back of the cart , pack up close by the shivering , weep Sylvia . neither of they speak a word at first ; but hester 's tender conscience smite she for her silence before they have reach Monkshaven . she want to say some kind word to Sylvia , and yet know not how to begin . somehow , without know why , or reasoning upon it , she hit upon Philip 's message as the good comfort in her power to give . she have deliver it before , but it have be apparently little heed . 

 ' Philip bade I say it be business as keep he from fetchin ' yo ' hissel ' business wi ' the lawyer , about about yo'r father . ' 

 ' what do they say ? ' say Sylvia , suddenly , lift her bow head , as though she would read her companion 's face in the dim light . 

 ' I dunnot know , ' say Hester , sadly . they be now jolt over the paved street , and not a word could be speak . they be now at Philip 's door , which be open to receive they even before they arrive , as if some one have be watch and listen . the old servant , Phoebe , the fixture in the house , who have belong to it and to the shop for the last twenty year , come out , hold a candle and shelter it in her hand from the weather , while Philip help the tottering step of Mrs. Robson as she descend behind . as Hester have get in last , so she have now to be the first to move . just as she be move , Sylvia 's cold little hand be lay on her arm . 

 ' I be main and thankful to yo ' . I ask yo'r pardon for speak cross , but , indeed , my heart 's a'most break wi ' fear about feyther . ' 

 the voice be so plaintive , so full of tear , that hester could not but yearn towards the speaker . she bent over and kiss she cheek , and then clamber unaided down by the wheel on the dark side of the cart . Wistfully she long for one word of thank or recognition from Philip , in whose service she have perform this hard task ; but he be otherwise occupy , and on cast a further glance back as she turn the corner of the street , she see Philip lift Sylvia carefully down in his arm from her footing on the top of the wheel , and then they all go into the light and the warmth , the door be shut , the lightened cart drive briskly away , and Hester , in rain , and cold , and darkness , go homeward with her tired sad heart . 

 Philip have do all he could , since his return from lawyer Dawson 's , to make his house bright and warm for the reception of his beloved . he have a strong apprehension of the probable fate of poor Daniel Robson ; he have a warm sympathy with the miserable distress of the wife and daughter ; but still at the back of his mind his spirit dance as if this be to they a festal occasion . he have even take unconscious pleasure in Phoebe 's suspicious look and tone , as he have hurry and superintend she in her operation . a fire blaze cheerily in the parlour , almost dazzle to the traveller bring in from the darkness and the rain ; candle burn two candle , much to Phoebe 's discontent . poor Bell Robson have to sit down almost as soon as she enter the room , so wear out be she with fatigue and excitement ; yet she grudge every moment which separate she , as she think , from her husband . 

 ' I be ready now , ' say she , stand up , and rather repulse Sylvia 's care ; ' I be ready now , ' say she , look eagerly at Philip , as if for he to lead the way . 

 ' it be not to - night , ' reply he , almost apologetically . ' you can not   see he to - night ; it be to - morrow morning before he go to York ; it be well for yo ' to be down here in town ready ; and beside I do not   know when I send for ye that he be lock up for the night . ' 

 ' well - a - day , well - a - day , ' say Bell , rock herself backwards and forwards , and try to soothe herself with these word . suddenly she say , 

 ' but I have   bring his comforter wi ' I his red woollen comforter as he be allay sleep in this twelvemonth past ; he will get his rheumatiz again ; oh , Philip , can not I get it to he ? ' 

 ' I will send it by Phoebe , ' say Philip , who be busy make tea , hospitable and awkward . 

 ' can not I take it mysel ' ? ' repeat Bell . ' I could make surer nor anybody else ; they would maybe not mind yon woman Phoebe d'ye call she ? ' 

 ' nay , mother , ' say Sylvia , ' thou be not fit to go . ' 

 ' shall I go ? ' ask Philip , hop she would say ' no ' , and be content with Phoebe , and leave he where he be . 

 ' oh , Philip , would yo ' ? ' say Sylvia , turn round . 

 ' ay , ' say Bell , ' if thou would take it they would be mind yo ' . ' 

 so there be nothing for it but for he to go , in the first flush of his delightful rite of hospitality . 

 ' it be not far , ' say he , console himself rather than they . ' I will be back in ten minute , the tea be maskit , and Phoebe will take yo'r wet thing and dry them by t ' kitchen fire ; and here be the stair , ' open a door in the corner of the room , from which the stair immediately ascend . ' there be two room at the top ; that to t ' left be all make ready , t ' other be mine , ' say he , redden a little as he speak . Bell be busy undo her bundle with tremble finger . 

 ' here , ' say she ; ' and oh , lad , here be a bit o ' peppermint cake ; he be main and fond on it , and I catch sight on it by good luck just t ' last minute . ' 

 Philip be go , and the excitement of Bell and Sylvia flag once more , and sink into wonder despondency . Sylvia , however , rouse herself enough to take off her mother 's wet clothe , and she take they timidly into the kitchen and arrange they before Phoebe 's fire . 

 Phoebe open her lip once or twice to speak in remonstrance , and then , with an effort , gulp her word down ; for her sympathy , like that of all the rest of the monkshaven world , be in favour of Daniel Robson ; and his daughter might place her dripping cloak this night wherever she would , for Phoebe . 

 Sylvia find her mother still sit on the chair next the door , where she have first place herself on enter the room . 

 ' I will gi'e you some tea , mother , ' say she , strike with the shrunken look of Bell 's face . 

 ' no , no ' say her mother . ' it be not manner for t ' help oursel 's . ' 

 ' I be sure Philip would ha ' wish yo ' for to take it , ' say Sylvia , pour out a cup . 

 just then he return , and something in his look , some dumb expression of delight at her occupation , make her blush and hesitate for an instant ; but then she go on , and make a cup of tea ready , say something a little incoherent all the time about her mother 's need of it . after tea Bell Robson 's weariness become so extreme , that Philip and Sylvia urge she to go to bed . she resist a little , partly out of ' manner , ' and partly because she keep fancy , poor woman , that somehow or other her husband might send for she . but about seven o'clock Sylvia persuade she to come upstairs . Sylvia , too , bade Philip good - night , and his look follow the last wave of her dress as she disappear up the stair ; then lean his chin on his hand , he gaze at vacancy and think deeply for how long he know not , so intent be his mind on the chance of futurity . 

 he be arouse by Sylvia 's come down - stair into the sitting - room again . he start up . 

 ' Mother be so shivery , ' say she . ' may I go in there , ' indicate the kitchen , ' and make she a drop of gruel ? ' 

 ' phoebe shall make it , not you , ' say Philip , eagerly prevent she , by go to the kitchen door and give his order . when he turn round again , Sylvia be stand over the fire , lean her head against the stone mantel - piece for the comparative coolness . she do not speak at first , or take any notice of he . he watch she furtively , and see that she be cry , the tear run down her cheek , and she too much absorb in her thought to wipe they away with her apron . 

 while he be turn over in his mind what he could well say to comfort her   his heart , like her , be almost too full for word , she suddenly look he full in the face , say , 

 ' Philip ! will not   they soon let he go ? what can they do to he ? ' her open lip tremble while await his answer , the tear come up and fill her eye . it be just the question he have most dread ; it lead to the terror that possess his own mind , but which he have hop to keep out of her . he hesitate . ' speak , lad ! ' say she , impatiently , with a little passionate gesture . ' I can see thou know ! ' 

 he have only make it bad by consideration ; he rush blindfold at a reply . 

 ' he be ta'en up for felony . ' 

 ' felony , ' say she . ' there thou're out ; he be in for let yon man out ; thou may call it rioting if thou be a mind to set folk again ' he , but it be too bad to cast such hard word at he as yon felony , ' she repeat , in a half - offend tone . 

 ' it be what the lawyer call it , ' say Philip , sadly ; ' it be no word o ' mine . ' 

 ' lawyer be allay for make the bad o ' thing , ' say she , a little pacified , ' but folk should not   allay believe they . ' 

 ' it be lawyer as have to judge I ' t ' long run . ' 

 ' can not the justice , Mr. Harter and they as be no lawyer , give he a sentence to - morrow , wi'out send he to York ? ' 

 ' no ! ' say Philip , shake his head . he go to the kitchen door and ask if the gruel be not ready , so anxious be he to stop the conversation at this point ; but Phoebe , who hold her young master in but little respect , scold he for a stupid man , who think , like all his sex , that gruel be to be make in a minute , whatever the fire be , and bid he come and make it for himself if he be in such a hurry . 

 he have to return discomfited to Sylvia , who meanwhile have arrange her thought ready to return to the charge . 

 ' and say he be send to York , and say he be try theere , what be t ' bad they can do again ' he ? ' ask she , keep down her agitation to look at Philip the more sharply . her eye never slacken their penetrate gaze at his countenance , until he reply , with the utmost unwillingness , and most apparent confusion , 

 ' they may send he to Botany Bay . ' 

 he know that he hold back a bad contingency , and he be mortally afraid that she would perceive this reserve . but what he do say be so much beyond her utmost apprehension , which have only reach to various term of imprisonment , that she do not imagine the dark shadow lurk behind . what he have say be too much for she . her eye dilate , her lip blanch , her pale cheek grow yet pale . after a minute 's look into his face , as if fascinate by some horror , she stumble backwards into the chair in the chimney comer , and cover her face with her hand , moan out some inarticulate word . 

 Philip be on his knee by she , dumb from excess of sympathy , kiss her dress , all unfelt by she ; he murmur half - word , he begin passionate sentence that die away upon his lip ; and she she think of nothing but her father , and be possess and rapt out of herself by the dread of lose he to that fearful country which be almost like the grave to she , so all but impassable be the gulf . but Philip know that it be possible that the separation impend might be that of the dark , mysterious grave that the gulf between the father and child might indeed be that which no living , breathing , warm human creature can ever cross . 

 ' Sylvie , Sylvie ! ' say he , and all their conversation have to be carry on in low tone and whisper , for fear of the listen ear above , ' do not , do not , thou'rt rend my heart . oh , Sylvie , hearken . there be not a thing I will not do ; there be not a penny I have   get , th ' last drop of blood that be in I , I will give up my life for his . ' 

 ' life , ' say she , put down her hand , and look at he as if her look could pierce his soul ; ' who talk o ' touch his life ? thou're go crazy , Philip , I think ; ' but she do not think so , although she would fain have believe it . in her keen agony she read his thought as though they be an open page ; she sate there , upright and stony , the conviction creep over her face like the grey shadow of death . no more tear , no more trembling , almost no more breathing . he could not bear to see she , and yet she hold his eye , and he fear to make the effort necessary to move or to turn away , lest the shun motion should carry conviction to her heart . alas ! conviction of the probable danger to her father 's life be already there : it be that that be calm she down , tighten her muscle , brace her nerve . in that hour she lose all her early youth . 

 ' then he may be hung , ' say she , low and solemnly , after a long pause . Philip turn away his face , and do not utter a word . again deep silence , break only by some homely sound in the kitchen . ' mother must not know on it , ' say Sylvia , in the same tone in which she have speak before . 

 ' it be t ' bad as can happen to he , ' say Philip . ' more likely he will be transport : maybe he will be bring in innocent after all . ' 

 ' no , ' say Sylvia , heavily , as one without hope as if she be read some dreadful doom in the tablet of the awful future . ' they will hang he . oh , feyther ! feyther ! ' she choke out , almost stuff her apron into her mouth to deaden the sound , and catch at Philip 's hand , and wring it with convulsive force , till the pain that he love be nearly more than he could bear . no word of his could touch such agony ; but irrepressibly , and as he would have do it to a wound child , he bend over she , and kiss she with a tender , tremble kiss . she do not repulse it , probably she do not even perceive it . 

 at that moment Phoebe come in with the gruel . Philip see she , and know , in an instant , what the old woman 's conclusion must need be ; but Sylvia have to be shake by the now stand Philip , before she could be bring back to the least consciousness of the present time . she lift up her white face to understand his word , then she rise up like one who slowly come to the use of her limb . 

 ' I suppose I mun go , ' she say ; ' but I would soon face the dead . if she ask I , Philip , what mun I say ? ' 

 ' she will not ask yo ' , ' say he , ' if yo ' go about as common . she be never ask yo ' all this time , an ' if she do , put she on to I . I will keep it from she as long as I can ; I will manage well nor I have   do wi ' thee , Sylvie , ' say he , with a sad , faint smile , look with fond penitence at her altered countenance . 

 ' Thou must not   blame thysel ' , ' say Sylvia , see his regret . ' I bring it on I mysel ' ; I think I would ha ' t ' truth , whativer come on it , and now I be not strong enough to stand it , God help I ! ' she continue , piteously . 

 ' oh , Sylvie , let I help yo ' ! I can not do what God can , I be not mean that , but I can do next to he of any man . I have love yo ' for year an ' year , in a way it be terrible to think on , if my love can do nought now to comfort yo ' in your sore distress . ' 

 ' Cousin Philip , ' she reply , in the same measure tone in which she have always speak since she have learn the extent of her father 's danger , and the slow stillness of her word be in harmony with the stony look of her face , ' thou be a comfort to I , I could not   bide my life without thee ; but I can not take in the thought o ' love , it seem beside I quite ; I can think on nought but they that be quick and they that be dead . ' 




 CHAPTER XXVII 

 GLOOMY day 

 Philip have money in the Fosters ' bank , not so much as it might have be if he have not have to pay for the furniture in his house . much of this furniture be old , and have belong to the brother Foster , and they have let Philip have it at a very reasonable rate ; but still the purchase of it have diminish the amount of his saving . but on the sum which he possess he draw largely he draw all nay , he overdraw his account somewhat , to his former master ' dismay , although the kindness of their heart overrule the hard argument of their head . 

 all be want to defend Daniel Robson at the approach York assize . his wife have hand over to Philip all the money or money 's worth she could lay her hand upon . Daniel himself be not one to be much beforehand with the world ; but to Bell 's thrifty imagination the round golden guineas , tie up in the old stocking - foot against rent - day , seem a mint of money on which Philip might draw infinitely . as yet she do not comprehend the extent of her husband 's danger . Sylvia go about like one in a dream , keep back the hot tear that might interfere with the course of life she have prescribe for herself in that terrible hour when she first learn all . every penny of money either she or her mother could save go to Philip . Kester 's hoard , too , be place in Hepburn 's hand at Sylvia 's earnest entreaty ; for Kester have no great opinion of Philip 's judgment , and would rather have take his money straight himself to Mr. Dawson , and beg he to use it for his master 's behoof . 

 indeed , if anything , the noiseless breach between Kester and Philip have widen of late . it be seed - time , and Philip , in his great anxiety for every possible interest that might affect Sylvia , and also as some distraction from his extreme anxiety about her father , have take to study agriculture of an evening in some old book which he have borrow the Farmer 's Complete Guide , and such like ; and from time to time he come down upon the practical dog Kester with direction gather from the theory in his book . of course the two fall out , but without many word . Kester persevere in his old way , make light of Philip and his book in manner and action , till at length Philip withdraw from the contest . ' many a man may lead a horse to water , but there be few can make he drink , ' and Philip certainly be not one of those few .
