Kester , indeed , look upon he with jealous eye on many account . he have favour Charley Kinraid as a lover of Sylvia 's ; and though he have no idea of the truth though he believe in the drowning of the specksioneer as much as any one yet the year which have elapse since Kinraid 's suppose death be but a very short while to the middle - aged man , who forget how slowly time pass with the young ; and he could often have scold Sylvia , if the poor girl have be a whit less heavy at heart than she be , for let Philip come so much about she come , though it be on her father 's business . for the darkness of their common dread draw they together , occasionally to the comparative exclusion of Bell and Kester , which the latter perceive and resent . Kester even allow himself to go so far as to wonder what Philip could want with all the money , which to he seem unaccountable ; and once or twice the ugly thought cross his mind , that shop conduct by young man be often not so profitable as when guide by old head , and that some of the coin pour into Philip 's keeping might have another destination than the defence of his master . Poor Philip ! and he be spend all his own , and more than all his own money , and no one ever know it , as he have bind down his friendly banker to secrecy . 

 once only Kester venture to speak to Sylvia on the subject of Philip . she have follow her cousin to the field just in front of their house , just outside the porch , to ask he some question she dare not put in her mother 's presence   Bell , indeed , in her anxiety , usually absorb all the question when Philip come   and stand , after Philip have bid her good - by , hardly think about he at all , but look unconsciously after he as he ascend the brow ; and at the top he have turn to take a last glance at the place his love inhabit , and , see she , he have wave his hat in gratified farewell . she , meanwhile , be rouse from far other thought than of he , and of his now acknowledge love , by the motion against the sky , and be turn back into the house when she hear Kester 's low hoarse call , and see he stand at the shippen door . 

 ' come hither , wench , ' say he , indignantly ; ' be this a time for courtin ' ? ' 

 ' court ? ' say she , draw up her head , and look back at he with proud defiance . 

 ' ay , courtin ' ! what other mak ' o ' thing is't when thou 's gazin ' after yon meddlesome chap , as if thou'd send thy eye after he , and he make marlock back at thee ? it be what we ca'ed courtin ' I ' my young day anyhow . and it be noane a time for a wench to go courtin ' when her feyther 's i ' prison , ' say he , with a consciousness as he utter these last word that he be cruel and unjust and go too far , yet carry on to say they by his hot jealousy against Philip . 

 Sylvia continue look at he without speak : she be too much offend for expression . 

 ' Thou may glower an ' thou may look , lass , ' say he , ' but a'd think well on thee . it be like last week thy last sweetheart be drown ; but thou be not one to waste time I ' rememberin ' they as be go if , indeed , thou iver care a button for yon Kinraid if it be not   a make - believe . ' 

 her lip be contract and draw up , show her small glitter tooth , which be scarcely apart as she breathe out 

 ' Thou think so , do thou , that I have   forgetten he ? thou'd well have a care o ' thy tongue . ' 

 then , as if fearful that her self - command might give way , she turn into the house ; and go through the kitchen like a blind person , she go up to she now unused chamber , and throw herself , face downward , flat on her bed , almost smother herself . 

 ever since Daniel 's committal , the decay that have imperceptibly begin in his wife 's bodily and mental strength during her illness of the previous winter , have be make quick progress . she lose her reticence of speech , and often talk to herself . she have not so much forethought as of old ; slight difference , it be true , but which , with some other of the same description , give foundation for the homely expression which some now apply to Bell , ' she will never be t ' same woman again . 

 this afternoon she have cry herself to sleep in her chair after Philip 's departure . she have not hear Sylvia 's sweeping passage through the kitchen ; but half an hour afterwards she be startle up by Kester 's abrupt entry . 

 ' where be Sylvie ? ' ask he . 

 ' I do not   know , ' say Bell , look scared , and as if she be ready to cry . ' it be no news about he ? ' say she , stand up , and support herself on the stick she be now accustomed to use . 

 ' bless yo ' , no , dunnot be afeare , missus ; it be only as a speak hasty to t ' wench , an ' a want t ' tell she as a'm sorry , ' say Kester , advance into the kitchen , and look round for Sylvia . 

 ' Sylvie , Sylvie ! ' shout he ; ' she mun be I ' t ' house . ' 

 Sylvia come slowly down the stair , and stand before he . her face be pale , her mouth set and determine ; the light of her eye veil in gloom . Kester shrink from her look , and even more from her silence . 

 ' A'm come to ax pardon , ' say he , after a little pause . 

 she be still silent . 

 ' A'm noane above ax pardon , though a'm fifty and more , and thee be but a silly wench , as a've   nurse I ' my arm . a'll say before thy mother as a ought niver to ha ' use they word , and as how a'm sorry for ' t. ' 

 ' I do not   understand it all , ' say Bell , in a hurried and perplexed tone . ' what have Kester be say , my lass ? ' she add , turn to Sylvia . 

 Sylvia go a step or two nearer to her mother , and take hold of her hand as if to quieten she ; then face once more round , she say deliberately to Kester , 

 ' if thou be not   Kester , I would niver forgive thee . Niver , ' she add , with bitterness , as the word he have use recur to her mind . ' it be in I to hate thee now , for say what thou do ; but thou're dear old Kester after all , and I can not   help mysel ' , I mun need forgive thee , ' and she go towards he . he take her little head between his horny hand and kiss it . she look up with tear in her eye , say softly , 

 ' niver say thing like they again . Niver speak on ' 

 ' a'll bite my tongue off first , ' he interrupt . 

 he keep his word . 

 in all Philip 's coming and going to and from Haytersbank Farm at this time , he never speak again of his love . in look , word , manner , he be like a thoughtful , tender brother ; nothing more . he could be nothing more in the presence of the great dread which loom large upon he after every conversation with the lawyer . 

 for Mr. Donkin have be right in his prognostication . government take up the attack on the Rendezvous with a high and heavy hand . it be necessary to assert authority which have be of late too often brave . an example must be make , to strike dismay into those who oppose and defy the press - gang ; and all the minor authority who hold their power from Government be in a similar manner severe and relentless in the execution of their duty . so the attorney , who go over to see the prisoner in York Castle , tell Philip . he add that Daniel still retain his pride in his achievement , and could not be bring to understand the dangerous position in which he be place ; that when press and question as to circumstance that might possibly be use in his defence , he always wander off to account of previous outrage commit by the press - gang , or to passionate abuse of the trick by which man have be lure from their home on the night in question to assist in put out an imaginary fire , and then seize and carry off . some of this very natural indignation might possibly have some effect on the jury ; and this seem the only ground of hope , and be indeed a slight one , as the judge be likely to warn the jury against allow their natural sympathy in such a case to divert their mind from the real question . 

 such be the substance of what Philip hear , and hear repeatedly , during his many visit to Mr. Dawson . and now the time of trial draw near ; for the York assize open on March the twelfth ; not much above three week since the offence be commit which take Daniel from his home and place he in peril of death . 

 Philip be glad that , the extremity of his danger never have be hint to Bell , and travel some forty mile be a most unusual exertion at that time to person of her class , the idea of go to see her husband at York have never suggest itself to Bell 's mind . her increase feebleness make this seem a step only to be take in case of the fatal extreme necessity ; such be the conclusion that both Sylvia and he have come to ; and it be the knowledge of this that make Sylvia strangle her own daily longing to see her father . not but that her hope be strong than her fear . Philip never tell she the cause for despondency ; she be young , and she , like her father , could not understand how fearful sometimes be the necessity for prompt and severe punishment of rebellion against authority . 

 Philip be to be in York during the time of the assize ; and it be understand , almost without word , that if the terrible bad occur , the wife and daughter be to come to York as soon as might be . for this end Philip silently make all the necessary arrangement before leave monkshaven . the sympathy of all man be with he ; it be too large an occasion for Coulson to be anything but magnanimous . he urge Philip to take all the time requisite ; to leave all business care to he . and as Philip go about pale and sad , there be another cheek that grow paler still , another eye that fill with quiet tear as his heaviness of heart become more and more apparent . the day for open the assize come on . Philip be in York Minster , watch the solemn antique procession in which the high authority in the county accompany the judge to the House of the Lord , to be there admonish as to the nature of their duty . as Philip listen to the sermon with a strained and beat heart , his hope rise high than his fear for the first time , and that evening he write his first letter to Sylvia . 


 ' DEAR SYLVIA , 

 ' it will be long first than I think for . Mr. Dawson say Tuesday in next week . but keep up your heart . I have be hear the sermon to - day which be preach to the judge ; and the clergyman say so much in it about mercy and forgiveness , I think they can not fail to be lenient this assize . I have see uncle , who look but thin , but be in good heart : only he will keep say he would do it over again if he have the chance , which neither Mr. Dawson nor I think be wise in he , in especial as the gaoler be by and hear every word as be say . he be very fain of hear all about home ; and want you to rear Daisy 's calf , as he think she will prove a good one . he bid I give his good love to you and my aunt , and his kind duty to Kester . 

 ' Sylvia , will you try and forget how I use to scold you about your writing and spelling , and just write I two or three line . I think I would rather have they badly spelt than not , because then I shall be sure they be your . and never mind about capital ; I be a fool to say such a deal about they , for a man do just as well without they . a letter from you would do a vast to keep I patient all these day till Tuesday . direct 

 ' Mr. Philip Hepburn , 

 ' care of Mr. Fraser , Draper , 
 ' Micklegate , York . 
 ' my affectionate duty to my aunt . 
 ' your respectful cousin and servant , 
 ' PHILIP HEPBURN . 
 ' P.S. the sermon be grand . the text be Zechariah vii . 9 , " execute true judgment and show mercy . " God grant it may have put mercy into the judge 's heart as be to try my uncle . ' 


 heavily the day pass over . on Sunday Bell and Sylvia go to church , with a strange , half - superstitious feeling , as if they could propitiate the most high to order the event in their favour by pay he the compliment of attend to duty in their time of sorrow which they have too often neglect in their prosperous day . 

 but he ' who knoweth our frame , and remembereth that we be dust , ' take pity upon his child , and send some of his blessed peace into their heart , else they could scarce have endure the agony of suspense of those next hour . for as they come slowly and wearily home from church , Sylvia could no long bear her secret , but tell her mother of the peril in which Daniel stand . cold as the March wind blow , they have not feel it , and have sate down on a hedge bank for Bell to rest . and then Sylvia speak , tremble and sick for fear , yet utterly unable to keep silence any long . Bell heave up her hand , and let they fall down on her knee before she reply . 

 ' the Lord be above we , ' say she , solemnly . ' he have send a fear o ' this into my heart afore now . I niver breathe it to thee , my lass ' 

 ' and I niver speak on it to thee , mother , because ' 

 Sylvia choke with crying , and lay her head on her mother 's lap , feel that she be no long the strong one , and the protector , but the protect . Bell go on , stroke her head , 

 ' the Lord be like a tender nurse as wean a child to look on and to like what it lothe once . he have send I dream as have prepare I for this , if so be it come to pass . 

 ' Philip be hopeful , ' say Sylvia , raise her head and look through her tear at her mother . 

 ' ay , he be . and I can not tell , but I think it be not for nought as the Lord have ta'en away all fear o ' death out o ' my heart . I think he mean as Daniel and I be to go hand - in - hand through the valley like as we walk up to our wedding in Crosthwaite Church . I could never guide th ' house without Daniel , and I should be fear he would take a deal more nor be good for he without I . ' 

 ' but I , mother , thou 's forget I , ' moan out Sylvia . ' oh , mother , mother , think on I ! ' 

 ' Nay , my lass , I be noane forget yo ' . I have a sore heart a ' last winter a - thinking on thee , when that chap Kinraid be hang about thee . I will noane speak ill on the dead , but I be uneasylike . but sin ' Philip and thee seem to ha ' make it up ' 

 Sylvia shiver , and open her mouth to speak , but do not say a word . 

 ' and sin ' the Lord have be comfort I , and talk to I many a time when thou 's think I be asleep , thing have seem to redd theirselve up , and if Daniel go , I be ready to follow . I could niver stand live to hear folk say he have be hang ; it seem so unnatural and shameful . ' 

 ' but , mother , he will not ! he shall not   be hang ! ' say Sylvia , spring to her foot . ' Philip say he will not . ' 

 Bell shake her head . they walk on , Sylvia both dishearten and almost irritated at her mother 's despondency . but before they go to bed at night Bell say thing which seem as though the morning 's feeling have be but temporary , and as if she be refer every decision to the period of her husband 's return . ' when father come home , ' seem a sort of burden at the beginning or end of every sentence , and this reliance on his certain come back to they be almost as great a trial to Sylvia as the absence of all hope have be in the morning . but that instinct tell she that her mother be become incapable of argument , she would have ask she why her view be so essentially change in so few hour . this inability of reason in poor Bell make Sylvia feel very desolate . 

 Monday pass over how , neither of they know , for neither speak of what be fill the thought of both . before it be light on Tuesday morning , Bell be astir . 

 ' it be very early , mother , ' say weary , sleepy Sylvia , dread return consciousness . 

 ' ay , lass ! ' say Bell , in a brisk , cheerful tone ; ' but he will , maybe , be home to - night , and i'se bind to have all thing ready for he . ' 

 ' anyhow , ' say Sylvia , sit up in bed , ' he could not   come home to - night . ' 

 ' Tut , lass ! thou do not   know how quick a man come home to wife and child . I will be a ' ready at any rate . ' 

 she hurry about in a way which Sylvia wonder to see ; till at length she fancy that perhaps her mother do so to drive away thought . every place be clean ; there be scarce time allow for breakfast ; till at last , long before mid - day , all the work be do , and the two sit down to their spinning - wheel . Sylvia 's spirit sink lower and low at each speech of her mother 's , from whose mind all fear seem to have disappear , leave only a strange restless kind of excitement . 

 ' it be time for t ' potato , ' say Bell , after her wool have snap many a time from her uneven tread . 

 ' Mother , ' say Sylvia , ' it be but just go ten ! ' 

 ' put them on , ' say Bell , without attend to the full meaning of her daughter 's word . ' it will , maybe , hasten t ' day on if we get dinner do betime . ' 

 ' but Kester be in t ' Far Acre field , and he will not be home till noon . ' 

 this seem to settle matter for a while ; but then Bell push her wheel away , and begin search for her hood and cloak . Sylvia find they for she , and then ask sadly 

 ' what do ta want them for , mother ? ' 

 ' I will go up t ' brow and through t ' field , and just have a look down t ' lane . ' 

 ' I will go wi ' thee , ' say Sylvia , feel all the time the uselessness of any looking for intelligence from York so early in the day . very patiently do she wait by her mother 's side during the long half - hour which Bell spend in gaze down the road for those who never come . 

 when they get home Sylvia put the potato on to boil ; but when dinner be ready and the three be seat at the dresser , Bell push her plate away from she , say it be so long after dinner time that she be past eat . Kester would have say something about its be only half - past twelve , but Sylvia give he a look beseech silence , and he go on with his dinner without a word , only brush away the tear from his eye with the back of his hand from time to time . 

 ' A'll noane go far fra ' home t ' rest o ' t ' day , ' say he , in a whisper to Sylvia , as he go out . 

 ' will this day niver come to an end ? ' cry Bell , plaintively . 

 ' oh , mother ! it will come to an end some time , never fear . I have   heerd say " be the day weary or be the day long , at length it ringeth to even - song . " ' 

 ' to even - song to even - song , ' repeat Bell . ' D'ye think now that even - song mean death , Sylvie ? ' 

 ' I can not tell I can not bear it . mother , ' say Sylvia , in despair , ' I will make some clap - bread : that be a heavy job , and will while away t ' afternoon . ' 

 ' ay , do ! ' reply the mother . ' he will like it fresh he will like it fresh . ' 

 Murmuring and talk to herself , she fall into a doze , from which Sylvia be careful not to disturb she . 

 the day be now get long , although as cold as ever ; and at Haytersbank Farm the light linger , as there be no near horizon to bring on early darkness . Sylvia have all ready for her mother 's tea against she waken ; but she sleep on and on , the peaceful sleep of a child , and Sylvia do not care to waken she . just after the sun have set , she see Kester outside the window make sign to she to come out . she steal out on tip - toe by the back - kitchen , the door of which be stand open . she almost run against Philip , who do not perceive she , as he be await she come the other way round the corner of the house , and who turn upon she a face whose import she read in an instant . ' Philip ! ' be all she say , and then she faint at his foot , come down with a heavy bang on the round pave stone of the yard . 

 ' Kester ! Kester ! ' he cry , for she look like one dead , and with all his strength the weary man could not lift she and carry she into the house . 

 with Kester 's help she be bear into the back - kitchen , and Kester rush to the pump for some cold water to throw over she . 

 while Philip , kneeling at her head , be partly support she in his arm , and heedless of any sight or sound , the shadow of some one fall upon he . he look up and see his aunt ; the old dignified , sensible expression on her face , exactly like her former self , compose , strong , and calm . 

 ' my lass , ' say she , sit down by Philip , and gently take she out of his arm into her own . ' lass , bear up ! we mun bear up , and be agait on our way to he , he will be need we now . bear up , my lass ! the Lord will give we strength . we mun go to he ; ay , time 's precious ; thou mun cry thy cry at after ! ' 

 Sylvia open her dim eye , and hear her mother 's voice ; the idea come slowly into her mind , and slowly she rise up , stand still , like one who have be stun , to regain her strength ; and then , take hold of her mother 's arm , she say , in a soft , strange voice 

 ' let us go . I be ready . ' 




 CHAPTER XXVIII 

 the ordeal 

 it be the afternoon of an April day in that same year , and the sky be blue above , with little sailing white cloud catch the pleasant sunlight . the earth in that northern country have scarcely yet put on her robe of green . the few tree grow near brooks run down from the moor and the high ground . the air be full of pleasant sound prophesy of the come summer . the rush , and murmur , and tinkle of the hidden watercourse ; the song of the lark poise high up in the sunny air ; the bleat of the lamb call to their mother everything inanimate be full of hope and gladness . 

 for the first time for a mournful month the front door of Haytersbank Farm be open ; the warm spring air might enter , and displace the sad dark gloom , if it could . there be a newly - light fire in the unused grate ; and Kester be in the kitchen , with his clog off his foot , so as not to dirty the spotless floor , stir here and there , and try in his awkward way to make thing look home - like and cheerful . he have bring in some wild daffodil which he have be to seek in the dawn , and he place they in a jug on the dresser . dolly Reid , the woman who have come to help Sylvia during her mother 's illness a year ago , be attend to something in the back - kitchen , make a noise among the milk - can , and sing a ballad to herself as she work ; yet every now and then she check herself in her singing , as if a sudden recollection come upon she that this be neither the time nor the place for song . once or twice she take up the funeral psalm which be sing by the bearer of the body in that country 

 our God , our help in age past . 

 but it be of no use : the pleasant April weather out of door , and perhaps the natural spring in the body , dispose her nature to cheerfulness , and insensibly she return to her old ditty . 

 Kester be turn over many thing in his rude honest mind as he stand there , give his finishing touch every now and then to the aspect of the house - place , in preparation for the return of the widow and daughter of his old master . 

 it be a month and more since they have leave home ; more than a fortnight since Kester , with three halfpence in his pocket , have set out after his day 's work to go to York to walk all night long , and to wish Daniel Robson his last farewell . 

 Daniel have try to keep up and have bring out one or two familiar , thread - bare , well - wear joke , such as he have make Kester chuckle over many a time and oft , when the two have be together afield or in the shippen at the home which he should never more see . but no ' Old Grouse in the gunroom ' could make Kester smile , or do anything except groan in but a heart - break sort of fashion , and presently the talk have become more suitable to the occasion , Daniel be up to the last the more composed of the two ; for Kester , when turn out of the condemn cell , fairly break down into the heavy sobbing he have never think to sob again on earth . he have leave Bell and Sylvia in their lodging at York , under Philip 's care ; he dare not go to see they ; he could not trust himself ; he have send they his duty , and bade Philip tell Sylvia that the game - hen have bring out fifteen chicken at a hatch . 

 yet although Kester send this message through Philip although he see and recognize all that Philip be do in their behalf , in the behalf of Daniel Robson , the condemn felon , his honoured master he like Hepburn not a whit well than he have do before all this sorrow have come upon they . 

 Philip have , perhaps , show a want of tact in his conduct to Kester . Acute with passionate keenness in one direction , he have a sort of dull straightforwardness in all other . for instance , he have return Kester the money which the latter have so gladly advanced towards the expense incur in defend Daniel . now the money which Philip give he back be part of an advance which Foster Brothers have make on Philip 's own account . Philip have think that it be hard on Kester to lose his saving in a hopeless cause , and have make a point of repay the old man ; but Kester would far rather have feel that the earning of the sweat of his brow have go in the attempt to save his master 's life than have have twice ten time as many golden guineas . 

 moreover , it seem to take his action in lend his hoard out of the sphere of love , and make it but a leaden common loan , when it be Philip who bring he the sum , not Sylvia , into whose hand he have give it . 

 with these feeling Kester feel his heart shut up as he see the long - watch - for two come down the little path with a third person ; with Philip hold up the fail step of poor Bell Robson , as , load with her heavy mourning , and feeble from the illness which have detain she in York ever since the day of her husband 's execution , she come falter back to her desolate home . Sylvia be also occupy in attend to her mother ; one or twice , when they pause a little , she and Philip speak , in the familiar way in which there be no coyness nor reserve . Kester catch up his clog , and go quickly out through the back - kitchen into the farm - yard , not stay to greet they , as he have mean to do ; and yet it be dull - sighted of he not to have perceive that whatever might be the relation between Philip and Sylvia , he be sure to have accompany they home ; for , alas ! he be the only male protector of their blood remain in the world . poor Kester , who would fain have take that office upon himself , choose to esteem himself cast off , and go heavily about the farmyard , know that he ought to go in and bid such poor welcome as he have to offer , yet feel too much to like to show himself before Philip . 

 it be long , too , before any one have leisure to come and seek he . Bell 's mind have flash up for a time , till the fatal day , only to be reduce by her subsequent illness into complete and hopeless childishness . it be all Philip and Sylvia could do to manage she in the first excitement of return home ; her restless inquiry for he who would never more be present in the familiar scene , her feverish weariness and uneasiness , all require tender soothing and most patient endurance of her refusal to be satisfied with what they say or do . 

 at length she take some food , and , refresh by it , and warm by the fire , she sink asleep in her chair . then Philip would fain have speak with Sylvia before the hour come at which he must return to Monkshaven , but she elude he , and go in search of Kester , whose presence she have miss . 

 she have guess some of the cause which keep he from greet they on their first return . but it be not as if she have shape these cause into the definite form of word . it be astonishing to look back and find how differently constitute be the mind of most people fifty or sixty year ago ; they feel , they understand , without go through reasoning or analytic process , and if this be the case among the more educated people , of course it be still more so in the class to which Sylvia belong . she know by some sort of intuition that if Philip accompany they home   as , indeed , under the circumstance , be so natural as to be almost unavoidable , the old servant and friend of the family would absent himself ; and so she slip away at the first possible moment to go in search of he . there he be in the farm - yard , lean over the gate that open into the home - field , apparently watch the poultry that scratch and peck at the new - spring grass with the utmost relish . a little far off be the ewe with their new - drop lamb , beyond that the great old thorn - tree with its round fresh cluster of bud , again beyond that there be a glimpse of the vast sunny rippling sea ; but Sylvia know well that Kester be look at none of these thing . she go up to he and touch his arm . he start from his reverie , and turn round upon she with his dim eye full of unshed tear . when he see her black dress , her deep mourning , he have hard work to keep from break out , but by dint of a good brush of his eye with the back of his hand , and a moment 's pause , he could look at she again with tolerable calmness . 

 ' why , Kester : why didst niver come to speak to we ? ' say Sylvia , find it necessary to be cheerful if she could . 

 ' a dun know ; niver ax I . a say , they'n gi'en Dick Simpson '   whose evidence have be all material against poor Daniel Robson at the trial   ' a ' t ' rotten egg and fou ' thing they could o ' Saturday , they do , ' continue he , in a tone of satisfaction ; ' ay , and they niver stop t ' see whether t ' egg be rotten or fresh when their blood be up nor whether stone be hard or soft , ' he add , in a low tone , and chuckle a little . 

 Sylvia be silent . he look at she now , chuckle still . her face be white , her lip tighten , her eye a - flame . she draw a long breath . 

 ' I wish I would be theere ! I wish I could do he an ill turn , ' sigh she , with some kind of expression on her face that make Kester quail a little . 

 ' Nay , lass ! he will get it fra ' other . niver fret thysel ' about sich rubbish . A'n do ill to speak on he . ' 

 ' no ! thou have not . then as be friend o ' father 's I will love for iver and iver ; they as help for t ' hang he '   she shudder from head to foot a sharp irrepressible shudder !   ' I will niver forgive niver ! ' 

 ' Niver be a long word , ' say Kester , musingly . ' a could horsewhip he , or cast stone at he , or duck he mysel ' ; but , lass ! niver be a long word ! ' 

 ' well ! niver heed if it be it be I as say it , and I be turn savage late day . come in , Kester , and see poor mother . ' 

 ' a can not , ' say he , turn his wrinkle pucker face away , that she might not see the twitching of emotion on it . ' there be kine to be fetch up , and what not , and he be theere , be not   he , Sylvie ? ' face round upon she with inquisitiveness . under his peer eye she redden a little . 

 ' yes , if it be Philip thou mean ; he be be all we have   have to look to sin ' . ' again the shudder . 

 ' well , now he will be seein ' after his shop , a reckon ? ' 

 Sylvia be call to the old mare nibble tuft of early - spring grass here and there , and half unconsciously coax the creature to come up to the gate to be stroke . but she hear Kester 's word well enough , and so he see , although she make this excuse not to reply . but Kester be not to be put off . 

 ' folk be talkin ' about thee and he ; thou'll ha ' to mind lest thee and he get yo'r name couple together . ' 

 ' it be right down cruel on folk , then , ' say she , crimson from some emotion . ' as if any man as be a man would not   do all he could for two lone woman at such a time and he a cousin , too ! tell I who say so , ' continue she , fire round at Kester , ' and I will niver forgive them that be all . ' 

 ' hoot ! ' say Kester , a little conscious that he himself be the principal representative of that name of multitude folk . ' here be a pretty lass ; she be ' get " a'll niver forgi'e " at her tongue 's end wi ' a vengeance . ' 

 Sylvia be a little confused . 

 ' oh , Kester , man , ' say she , ' my heart be sore again ' every one , for feyther 's sake . ' 

 and at length the natural relief of plentiful tear come ; and Kester , with instinctive wisdom , let she weep undisturbed ; indeed , he cry not a little himself . they be interrupt by Philip 's voice from the back - door . 

 ' Sylvie , your mother 's awake , and want you ! ' 

 ' come , Kester , come , ' and take hold of he she draw he with she into the house . 

 Bell rise as they come in , hold by the arm of the chair . at first she receive Kester as though he have be a stranger . 

 ' I be glad to see yo ' , sir ; t ' master 's out , but he will be in afore long . it will be about t ' lamb yo're come , mebbe ? ' 

 ' mother ! ' say Sylvia , ' dunnot yo ' see ? it be Kester , Kester , wi ' his Sunday clothe on . ' 

 ' Kester ! ay , sure it be ; my eye have getten so sore and dim of late ; just as if I have be greet . I be sure , lad , I be glad to see thee ! it be a long time I have   be away , but it be not pleasure - seeking as take I , it be business o ' some mak ' tell he , Sylvie , what it be , for my head 's clean gone . I only know I would not   ha ' leave home if I could ha ' help it ; for I think I should ha ' keep my health well if I have bid at home wi ' my master . I wonder as he be not come in for t ' bid I welcome ? be he far afield , think ye , Kester ? ' 

 Kester look at Sylvia , mutely implore she to help he out in the dilemma of answering , but she be do all she could to help cry . Philip come to the rescue . 

 ' Aunt , ' say he , ' the clock have stop ; can you tell I where t ' find t ' key , and I will wind it up . ' 

 ' t ' key , ' say she , hurriedly , ' t ' key , it be behind th ' big Bible on yon shelf . but I would rayther thou would not   touch it , lad ; it be t ' master 's work , and he distrust folk meddle wi ' it . ' 

 Day after day there be this constant reference to her dead husband . in one sense it be a blessing ; all the circumstance attendant on his sad and untimely end be sweep out of her mind along with the recollection of the fact itself . she refer to he as absent , and have always some plausible way of account for it , which satisfy her own mind ; and , accordingly they fall into the habit of humour she , and speak of he as go to Monkshaven , or afield , or weary out , and take a nap upstairs , as her fancy lead she to believe for the moment . but this forgetfulness , though happy for herself , be terrible for her child . it be a constant renewing of Sylvia 's grief , while her mother could give she no sympathy , no help , or strength in any circumstance that arise out of this grief . she be drive more and more upon Philip ; his advice and his affection become daily more necessary to she . 

 Kester see what would be the end of all this more clearly than Sylvia do herself ; and , impotent to hinder what he fear and dislike , he grow more and more surly every day . yet he try to labour hard and well for the interest of the family , as if they be bind up in his good management of the cattle and land . he be out and about by the early dawn , work all day long with might and main . he buy himself a pair of new spectacle , which might , he fancy , enable he to read the Farmer 's Complete Guide , his dead master 's vade - mecum . but he have never learn more than his capital letter , and have forget many of they ; so the spectacle do he but little good . then he would take the book to Sylvia , and ask she to read to he the instruction he need ; instruction , be it note , that he would formerly have despise as mere book - learning : but his present sense of responsibility have make he humble . 

 Sylvia would find the place with all deliberation : and put her finger under the line to keep the exact place of the word she be read , she would strive in good earnest to read out the direction give ; but when every fourth word have to be spell , it be rather hopeless work , especially as all these word be unintelligible to the open - mouthed listener , however intent he might be . he have generally to fall back on his own experience ; and , guide by that , thing be not do badly in his estimation , when , one day , Sylvia say to he , as they be in the hay - field , heap up the hay into cock with Dolly Reid 's assistance 

 ' Kester I do not   tell thee there be a letter from Measter Hall , Lord Malton 's steward , that come last night and that Philip read I . ' 

 she stop for a moment . 

 ' ay , lass ! Philip read it thee , and whatten might it say ? ' 

 ' only that he have an offer for Haytersbank Farm , and would set mother free to go as soon as t ' crop be off t ' ground . ' 

 she sigh a little as she say this . 

 " ' only ! " sayst ta ? Whatten business have he for to go an ' offer to let t ' farm afore iver he be tell as yo ' wish to leave it ? ' observe Kester , in high dudgeon . 

 ' oh ! ' reply Sylvia , throw down her rake , as if weary of life . ' what could we do wi ' t ' farm and land ? if it be all dairy I might ha ' do , but wi ' so much on it arable . ' 

 ' and if ' tis arable be not I allay to t ' fore ? ' 

 ' oh , man , dunnot find fault wi ' I ! I be just fain to lie down and die , if it be not for mother . ' 

 ' ay ! thy mother will be sore unsettled if thou be for quit Haytersbank , ' say merciless Kester . 

 ' I can not help it ; I can not help it ! what can I do ? it would take two pair o ' man 's hand to keep t ' land up as Measter Hall like it ; and beside ' 

 ' beside what ? ' say Kester , look up at she with his sudden odd look , one eye shut , the other open : there she stand , her two hand clasp tight together , her eye fill with tear , her face pale and sad . ' beside what ? ' he ask again , sharply . 

 ' T ' answer 's send to Measter Hall Philip write it last night ; so there be no use planning and fret , it be do for t ' good , and mun be do . ' she stoop and pick up her rake , and begin toss the hay with energy , the tear stream down her cheek unheeded . it be Kester 's turn to throw down his rake . she take no notice , he do not feel sure that she have observe his action . he begin to walk towards the field - gate ; this movement do catch her eye , for in a minute her hand be on his arm , and she be stoop forward to look into his face . it be work and twitch with emotion . ' Kester ! oh , man ! speak out , but dunnot leave I a this - ns . what could I ha ' do ? mother be go dateless wi ' sorrow , and I be but a young lass , I ' year I mean ; for I be old enough wi ' weeping . ' 

 ' I would ha ' put up for t ' farm mysel ' , soon than have thee turn out , ' say Kester , in a low voice ; then work himself up into a passion , as a new suspicion cross his mind , he add , ' An ' what for do not   yo ' tell I on t ' letter ? Yo ' be in a mighty hurry to settle it a ' , and get rid on t ' oud place . '

 wedding RAIMENT

Philip see her reluctance , and , by intuition rather than reason , he know that what she would not do for gaiety or pleasure she would consent to , if by so do she could render any service to another . so he go on . 

 ' besides , Sylvie and I have plan to go for our wedding jaunt to Robin Hood 's Bay . I ha ' be to engage a shandry this very morn , before t ' shop be open ; and there be no one to leave wi ' my aunt . th ' poor old body be sore crush with sorrow ; and be , as one may say , childish at time ; she be to come down here , that we may find she when we come back at night ; and there be niver a one she will come with so willing and so happy as with thee , hester . Sylvie and I have both say so . ' 

 hester look up in his face with her grave honest eye .

Sylvie , Sylvie , take I home , and away from this cruel place ! ' 

 hester could not but be touch with the young girl 's manner to her mother as tender , as protect as if their relation to each other have be reverse , and she be lulling and tenderly soothe a wayward , frightened child . she have neither eye nor ear for any one till her mother be sit in tremble peace , hold her daughter 's hand tight in both of her , as if afraid of lose sight of she : then Sylvia turn to hester , and , with the sweet grace which be a natural gift to some happy people , thank she ; in common word enough she thank she , but in that nameless manner , and with that strange , rare charm which make Hester feel as if she have never be thank in all her life before ; and from that time forth she understand , if she do not always yield to , the unconscious fascination which Sylvia could exercise over other at time . 

 do it enter into Philip 's heart to perceive that he have wed his long - seek bride in mourning raiment , and that the first sound which greet they as they approach their home be those of weeping and wail ? 




 CHAPTER XXX 

 HAPPY day 

 and now Philip seem as prosperous as his heart could desire . the business flourish , and money beyond his moderate want come in . as for himself he require very little ; but he have always look forward to place his idol in a befit shrine ; and mean for this be now furnish to he . the dress , the comfort , the position he have desire for Sylvia be all her . she do not need to do a stroke of household work if she prefer to ' sit in her parlour and sew up a seam ' . indeed Phoebe resent any interference in the domestic labour , which she have perform so long , that she look upon the kitchen as a private empire of her own . ' Mrs Hepburn '   as Sylvia be now term   have a good dark silk gown - piece in her drawer , as well as the poor dove - colour , against the day when she choose to leave off mourning ; and stuff for either gray or scarlet cloak be her at her bidding . 

 what she care for far more be the comfort with which it be in her power to surround her mother . in this Philip vie with she ; for besides his old love , and new pity for his aunt Bell , he never forget how she have welcome he to Haytersbank , and favour his love to Sylvia , in the yearn day when he little hop he should ever win his cousin to be his wife . but even if he have not have these grateful and affectionate feeling towards the poor woman , he would have do much for she if only to gain the sweet , rare smile which his wife never bestow upon he so freely as when she see he attend to ' mother , ' for so both of they now call Bell . for her creature comfort , her silk gown , and her humble luxury , Sylvia do not care ; Philip be almost annoyed at the indifference she often manifest to all his effort to surround she with such thing . it be even a hardship to she to leave off her country dress , her uncovered hair , her linsey petticoat , and loose bed - gown , and to don a stiff and stately gown for her morning dress . sit in the dark parlour at the back of the shop , and do ' white work , ' be much more wearying to she than run out into the field to bring up the cow , or spin wool , or make up butter . she sometimes think to herself that it be a strange kind of life where there be no out - door animal to look after ; the ' ox and the ass ' have hitherto come into all her idea of humanity ; and her care and gentleness have make the dumb creature round her father 's home into mute friend with love eye , look at she as if wistful to speak in word the grateful regard that she could read without the poor expression of language . 

 she miss the free open air , the great dome of sky above the field ; she rebel against the necessity of ' dress '   as she call it   to go out , although she acknowledge that it be a necessity where the first step beyond the threshold must be into a populous street .

'

 after all , and though he do not acknowledge it even to himself , the long - desire happiness be not so delicious and perfect as he have anticipate . many have feel the same in their first year of married life ; but the faithful , patient nature that still work on , strive to gain love , and capable itself of steady love all the while , be a gift not give to all .

 have end his oration , much to his own satisfaction , Kester toss off his glass of wine , smack his lip , wipe his mouth with the back of his hand , pocket his cake , and make off .

 CHAPTER XXXI 

 evil OMENS 

 the first step in Philip 's declension happen in this way . Sylvia have make rapid progress in her recovery ; but now she seem at a stationary point of weakness ; wakeful night succeed to languid day . occasionally she catch a little sleep in the afternoon , but she usually awake startled and feverish .

 Philip 's wild tone catch the nurse 's ear , and she enter full of the dignified indignation of wisdom . 

 ' be yo ' for kill yo'r wife , measter ? ' she ask . ' she be noane so strong as she can bear flytin ' and scoldin ' , nor will she be for many a week to come . go down wi ' ye , and leave she I ' peace if yo're a man as can be call a man ! ' 

 her anger be rise as she catch sight of Sylvia 's avert face . it be flush crimson , her eye full of intense emotion of some kind , her lip compress ; but an involuntary twitching overmaster her resolute stillness from time to time . Philip , who do not see the avert face , nor understand the real danger in which he be place his wife , feel as though he must have one word , one responsive touch of the hand which lie passive in his , which be not even draw away from the kiss with which he cover it , any more than if it have be an impassive stone . the nurse have fairly to take he by the shoulder , and turn he out of the room . 

 in half an hour the doctor have to be summon . of course , the nurse give he her version of the event of the afternoon , with much animus against Philip ; and the doctor think it his duty to have some very serious conversation with he . 

 ' I do assure you , Mr. Hepburn , that , in the state your wife have be in for some day , it be little less than madness on your part to speak to she about anything that could give rise to strong emotion . ' 

 ' it be madness , sir ! ' reply Philip , in a low , miserable tone of voice . the doctor 's heart be touch , in spite of the nurse 's accusation against the scold husband . yet the danger be now too serious for he to mince matter . 

 ' I must tell you that I can not answer for her life , unless the great precaution be take on your part , and unless the measure I shall use have the effect I wish for in the next twenty - four hour . she be on the verge of a brain fever . any allusion to the subject which have be the final cause of the state in which she now be must be most cautiously avoid , even to a chance word which may bring it to her memory . ' 

 and so on ; but Philip seem to hear only this : then he might not express contrition , or sue for pardon , he must go on unforgiven through all this stress of anxiety ; and even if she recover the doctor warn he of the undesirableness of recur to what have pass ! 

 heavy miserable time of endurance and waiting have to be pass through by all during the course of their life ; and Philip have have his share of such season , when the heart , and the will , and the speech , and the limb , must be bind down with strong resolution to patience . 

 for many day , nay , for week , he be forbid to see Sylvia , as the very sound of his footstep bring on a recurrence of the fever and convulsive movement . yet she seem , from question she feebly ask the nurse , to have forget all that have happen on the day of her attack from the time when she drop off to sleep . but how much she remember of after occurrence no one could ascertain . she be quiet enough when , at length , Philip be allow to see she . but he be half jealous of his child , when he watch how she could smile at it , while she never change a muscle of her face at all he could do or say . 

 and of a piece with this extreme quietude and reserve be her behaviour to he when at length she have fully recover , and be able to go about the house again . Philip think many a time of the word she have use long before before their marriage . ominous word they be . 

 ' it be not in I to forgive ; I sometimes think it be not in I to forget . ' 

 Philip be tender even to humility in his conduct towards she . but nothing stir she from her fortress of reserve . and he know she be so different ; he know how loving , nay , passionate , be her nature vehement , demonstrative oh ! how could he stir she once more into expression , even if the first show or speech she make be of anger ? then he try be angry with she himself ; he be sometimes unjust to she consciously and of a purpose , in order to provoke she into defend herself , and appeal against his unkindness . he only seem to drive her love away still more . 

 if any one have know all that be pass in that household , while yet the story of it be not end , nor , indeed , come to its crisis , their heart would have be sorry for the man who linger long at the door of the room in which his wife sate cooing and talk to her baby , and sometimes laugh back to it , or who be soothe the querulousness of fail age with every possible patience of love ; sorry for the poor listener who be hunger for the profusion of tenderness thus scatter on the senseless air , yet only by stealth catch the echo of what ought to have be his . 

 it be so difficult to complain , too ; impossible , in fact . everything that a wife could do from duty she do ; but the love seem to have flee , and , in such case , no reproach or complaint can avail to bring it back . so reason outsider , and be convince of the result before the experiment be make . but Philip could not reason , or could not yield to reason ; and so he complain and reproach . she do not much answer he ; but he think that her eye express the old word , 

 ' it be not in I to forgive ; I sometimes think it be not in I to forget . ' 

 however , it be an old story , an ascertained fact , that , even in the most tender and stable masculine nature , at the supreme season of their life , there be room for other thought and passion than such as be connect with love . even with the most domestic and affectionate man , their emotion seem to be keep in a cell distinct and away from their actual life . Philip have other thought and other occupation than those connect with his wife during all this time . 

 an uncle of his mother 's , a Cumberland ' statesman ' , of whose existence he be barely conscious , die about this time , leave to his unknown great - nephew four or five hundred pound , which put he at once in a different position with regard to his business . henceforward his ambition be rouse , such humble ambition as befit a shop - keeper in a country town sixty or seventy year ago . to be respect by the man around he have always be an object with he , and be , perhaps , become more so than ever now , as a sort of refuge from his deep , sorrowful mortification in other direction . he be greatly pleased at be make a sidesman ; and , in preparation for the further honour of be churchwarden , he go regularly twice a day to church on Sundays . there be enough religious feeling in he to make he disguise the worldly reason for such conduct from himself . he believe that he go because he think it right to attend public worship in the parish church whenever it be offer up ; but it may be question of he , as of many other , how far he would have be as regular in attendance in a place where he be not know . with this , however , we have nothing to do . the fact be that he go regularly to church , and he wish his wife to accompany he to the pew , newly paint , with his name on the door , where he sate in full sight of the clergyman and congregation . 

 Sylvia have never be in the habit of such regular church - going , and she feel it as a hardship , and slip out of the duty as often as ever she could . in her unmarried day , she and her parent have go annually to the mother - church of the parish in which Haytersbank be situate : on the Monday succeed the Sunday next after the Romish Saint 's day , to whom the church be dedicate , there be a great feast or wake hold ; and , on the Sunday , all the parishioner come to church from far and near . frequently , too , in the course of the year , Sylvia would accompany one or other of her parent to Scarby Moorside afternoon service , when the hay be get in , and the corn not ready for cutting , or the cow be dry and there be no afternoon milking . many clergyman be languid in those day , and do not too curiously inquire into the reason which give they such small congregation in country parish . 

 now she be marry , this weekly church - go which Philip seem to expect from she , become a tie and a small hardship , which connect itself with her life of respectability and prosperity . ' a crust of bread and liberty ' be much more accordant to Sylvia 's nature than plenty of creature comfort and many restraint . another wish of Philip 's , against which she say no word , but constantly rebel in thought and deed , be his desire that the servant he have engage during the time of her illness to take charge of the baby , should always carry it whenever it be take out for a walk . Sylvia often feel , now she be strong , as if she would far rather have be without the responsibility of have this nursemaid , of whom she be , in reality , rather afraid . the good side of it be that it set she at liberty to attend to her mother at time when she would have be otherwise occupy with her baby ; but Bell require very little from any one : she be easily pleased , unexacting , and methodical even in her dotage ; preserve the quiet , undemonstrative habit of her early life now that the faculty of reason , which have be at the basis of the formation of such habit , be go . she take great delight in watch the baby , and be pleased to have it in her care for a short time ; but she doze so much that it prevent she have any strong wish on the subject . 

 so Sylvia contrive to get her baby as much as possible to herself , in spite of the nursemaid ; and , above all , she would carry it out , softly cradle in her arm , warm pillow on her breast , and bear it to the freedom and solitude of the sea - shore on the west side of the town where the cliff be not so high , and there be a good space of sand and shingle at all low tide . 

 once here , she be as happy as she ever expect to be in this world . the fresh sea - breeze restore something of the colour of former day to her cheek , the old buoyancy to her spirit ; here she might talk her heart - full of love nonsense to her baby ; here it be all her own ; no father to share in it , no nursemaid to dispute the wisdom of anything she do with it . she sing to it , she toss it ; it crow and it laugh back again , till both be weary ; and then she would sit down on a broken piece of rock , and fall to gaze on the advance wave catch the sunlight on their crest , advance , recede , for ever and for ever , as they have do all her life long as they do when she have walk with they that once by the side of Kinraid ; those cruel wave that , forgetful of the happy lover ' talk by the side of their water , have carry one away , and drown he deep till he be dead . every time she sate down to look at the sea , this process of thought be go through up to this point ; the next step would , she know , bring she to the question she dare not , must not ask . he be dead ; he must be dead ; for be she not Philip 's wife ? then come up the recollection of Philip 's speech , never forget , only bury out of sight : ' what kind of a woman be yo ' to go on dream of another man , and yo ' a wed wife ? ' she use to shudder as if cold steel have be plunge into her warm , live body as she remember these word ; cruel word , harmlessly provoke . they be too much associate with physical pain to be dwell upon ; only their memory be always there . she pay for these happy ramble with her baby by the depression which await she on her re - entrance into the dark , confine house that be her home ; its very fulness of comfort be an oppression . then , when her husband see her pale and fatigue , he be annoy , and sometimes upbraid she for do what be so unnecessary as to load herself with her child . she know full well it be not that that cause her weariness . by - and - by , when he inquire and discover that all these walk be take in one direction , out towards the sea , he grow jealous of her love for the inanimate ocean . be it connect in her mind with the thought of Kinraid ? why do she so perseveringly , in wind or cold , go out to the sea - shore ; the western side , too , where , if she go but far enough , she would come upon the mouth of the Haytersbank gully , the point at which she have last see Kinraid ? such fancy haunt Philip 's mind for hour after she have acknowledge the direction of her walk . but he never say a word that could distinctly tell she he dislike she go to the sea , otherwise she would have obey he in this , as in everything else ; for absolute obedience to her husband seem to be her rule of life at this period obedience to he who would so gladly have obey her small wish have she but express it ! she never know that Philip have any painful association with the particular point on the sea - shore that she instinctively avoid , both from a consciousness of wifely duty , and also because the sight of it bring up so much sharp pain . 

 Philip use to wonder if the dream that precede her illness be the suggestive cause that draw she so often to the shore . her illness consequent upon that dream have fill his mind , so that for many month he himself have have no haunt vision of Kinraid to disturb his slumber . but now the old dream of Kinraid 's actual presence by Philip 's bedside begin to return with fearful vividness . night after night it recur ; each time with some new touch of reality , and close approach ; till it be as if the fate that overtake all man be then , even then , knock at his door . 

 in his business Philip prosper . man praise he because he do well to himself . he have the perseverance , the capability for head - work and calculation , the steadiness and general forethought which might have make he a great merchant if he have live in a large city . without any effort of his own , almost , too , without Coulson 's be aware of it , Philip be now in the position of superior partner ; the one to suggest and arrange , while Coulson only carry out the plan that emanate from Philip . the whole work of life be suit to the man : he do not aspire to any different position , only to the full development of the capability of that which he already hold . he have originate several fresh scheme with regard to the traffic of the shop ; and his old master , with all their love of try way , and distrust of everything new , have be candid enough to confess that their successor ' plan have result in success . ' their successor . ' Philip be content with have the power when the exercise of it be require , and never name his own important share in the new improvement . possibly , if he have , Coulson 's vanity might have take the alarm , and he might not have be so acquiescent for the future . as it be , he forget his own subordinate share , and always use the imperial ' we ' , ' we think ' , ' it strike we , '   c. 




 CHAPTER XXXII 

 rescued from the wave 

 Meanwhile Hester come and go as usual ; in so quiet and methodical a way , with so even and undisturbe a temper , that she be almost forget when everything go well in the shop or household . she be a star , the brightness of which be only recognize in time of darkness . she herself be almost surprised at her own increase regard for Sylvia . she have not think she should ever be able to love the woman who have be such a laggard in acknowledge Philip 's merit ; and from all she have ever hear of Sylvia before she come to know she , from the angry word with which Sylvia have receive she when she have first go to Haytersbank Farm , Hester have intend to remain on friendly term , but to avoid intimacy . but her kindness to Bell Robson have win both the mother 's and daughter 's heart ; and in spite of herself , certainly against her own mother 's advice , she have become the familiar friend and welcome guest of the household . 

 now the very change in Sylvia 's whole manner and way , which grieve and vex Philip , make his wife the more attractive to hester . bring up among Quakers , although not one herself , she admire and respect the staidness and outward peacefulness common amongst the young woman of that sect . Sylvia , whom she have expect to find volatile , talkative , vain , and wilful , be quiet and still , as if she have be bear a friend : she seem to have no will of her own ; she serve her mother and child for love ; she obey her husband in all thing , and never appear to pine after gaiety or pleasure . and yet at time hester thought , or rather a flash come across her mind , as if all thing be not as right as they seem . Philip look old , more care - worn ; nay , even Hester be oblige to allow to herself that she have hear he speak to his wife in sharp , aggrieved tone . innocent Hester ! she could not understand how the very quality she so admire in Sylvia be just what be so foreign to her nature that the husband , who have know she from a child , feel what an unnatural restraint she be put upon herself , and would have hail petulant word or wilful action with an unspeakable thankfulness for relief . 

 one day it be in the spring of 1798 hester be engage to stay to tea with the Hepburns , in order that after that early meal she might set to again in help Philip and Coulson to pack away the winter cloth and flannel , for which there be no long any use . the tea - time be half - past four ; about four o'clock a heavy April shower come on , the hail patter against the window - pane so as to awaken Mrs. Robson from her afternoon 's nap . she come down the corkscrew stair , and find Phoebe in the parlour arrange the tea - thing . 

 Phoebe and Mrs. Robson be well friend than Phoebe and her young mistress ; and so they begin to talk a little together in a comfortable , familiar way . once or twice Philip look in , as if he would be glad to see the tea - table in readiness ; and then Phoebe would put on a spurt of busy bustle , which cease almost as soon as his back be turn , so eager be she to obtain Mrs. Robson 's sympathy in some little dispute that have occur between she and the nurse - maid . the latter have misappropriate some hot water , prepare and require by Phoebe , to the washing of the baby 's clothe ; it be a long story , and would have tire the patience of any one in full possession of their sens ; but the detail be just within poor Bell 's comprehension , and she be listen with the great sympathy . both the woman be unaware of the lapse of time ; but it be of consequence to Philip , as the extra labour be not to be begin until after tea , and the daylight hour be precious . 

 at a quarter to five hester and he come in , and then Phoebe begin to hurry . hester go up to sit by Bell and talk to she . Philip speak to Phoebe in the familiar word of country - folk . indeed , until his marriage , Phoebe have always call he by his christian name , and have find it very difficult to change it into ' master . ' 

 ' where be Sylvie ? ' say he . 

 ' go out wi ' t ' babby , ' reply Phoebe . 

 ' why can not   Nancy carry it out ? ' ask Philip . 

 it be touch on the old grievance : he be tired , and he speak with sharp annoyance . Phoebe might easily have tell he the real state of the case ; Nancy be busy at her washing , which would have be reason enough . but the nursemaid have vex she , and she do not like Philip 's sharpness , so she only say , 

 ' it be noane o ' my business ; it be yo ' t ' look after yo'r own wife and child ; but yo'r but a lad after a ' . ' 

 this be not conciliatory speech , and just put the last stroke to Philip 's fit of ill - temper . 

 ' I be not for my tea to - night , ' say he , to Hester , when all be ready . ' Sylvie be not here , and nothing be nice , or as it should be . I will go and set to on t ' stock - taking . do not   yo ' hurry , Hester ; stop and chat a bit with th ' old lady . ' 

 ' Nay , Philip , ' say hester , ' thou 's sadly tired ; just take this cup o ' tea ; Sylvia will be grieve if yo ' have not   something . ' 

 ' Sylvia do not   care whether I be full or fast , ' reply he , impatiently put aside the cup . ' if she do she would ha ' take care to be in , and ha ' see to thing be as I like they . ' 

 now in general Philip be the least particular of man about meal ; and to do Sylvia justice , she be scrupulously attentive to every household duty in which old Phoebe would allow she to meddle , and always careful to see after her husband 's comfort . but Philip be too vexed at her absence to perceive the injustice of what he be say , nor be he aware how Bell Robson have be attend to what he say . but she be sadly discomfit by it , understand just enough of the grievance in hand to think that her daughter be neglectful of those duty which she herself have always regard as paramount to all other ; nor could hester convince she that Philip have not mean what he say ; neither could she turn the poor old woman 's thought from the word which have cause her distress . 

 presently Sylvia come in , bright and cheerful , although breathless with hurry . 

 ' oh , ' say she , take off her wet shawl , ' we have   have to shelter from such a storm of rain , baby and I but see ! she be none the bad for it , as bonny as iver , bless she . ' 

 hester begin some speech of admiration for the child in order to prevent Bell from deliver the lecture she feel sure be come down on the unsuspecting Sylvia ; but all in vain . 

 ' Philip 's be complain on thee , Sylvie , ' say Bell , in the way in which she have speak to her daughter when she be a little child ; grave and severe in tone and look , more than in word . ' I forget justly what about , but he speak on thy neglect he continual . it be not right , my lass , it be not right ; a woman should but my head 's very tired , and all I can think on to say be , it be not right . ' 

 ' Philip be complain of I , and to mother ! ' say Sylvia , ready to burst into tear , so grieved and angry be she . 

 ' no ! ' say hester , ' thy mother have take it a little too strong ; he be vex like at his tea not be ready . ' 

 Sylvia say no more , but the bright colour fade from her cheek , and the contraction of care return to her brow . she occupy herself with take off her baby 's walk thing . hester linger , anxious to soothe and make peace ; she be look sorrowfully at Sylvia , when she see tear drop on the baby 's cloak , and then it seem as if she must speak a word of comfort before go to the shop - work , where she know she be expect by both Philip and Coulson . she pour out a cup of tea , and come close up to Sylvia , and kneel down by she , she whisper , 

 ' just take he this into t ' ware - room ; it will put all to right if thou'll take it to he wi ' thy own hand . ' 

 Sylvia look up , and hester then more fully see how she have be cry . she whisper in reply , for fear of disturb her mother , 

 ' I do not   mind anything but his speak ill on I to mother . I know I be for iver try and try to be a good wife to he , an ' it be very dull work ; hard than yo ' think on , Hester , an ' I would ha ' be home for tea to - night only I be afeare of baby get wet wi ' t ' storm o ' hail as we have down on t ' shore ; and we shelter under a rock . it be a weary come home to this dark place , and to find my own mother set against I . ' 

 ' take he his tea , like a good lassie . I will answer for it he will be all right . a man take it hardly when he come in tired , a - think his wife will be there to cheer he up a bit , to find she off , and niver know nought of t ' reason why . ' 

 ' I be glad enough I have   getten a baby , ' say Sylvia , ' but for aught else I wish I would niver be marry , I do ! ' 

 ' hush thee , lass ! ' say Hester , rise up indignant ; ' now that be a sin . eh ! if thou only know the lot o ' some folk . but let us talk no more on that , that can not be help ; go , take he his tea , for it be a sad thing to think on he fast all this time . ' 

 hester 's voice be raise by the simple fact of her change of position ; and the word fast catch Mrs. Robson 's ear , as she sate at her knitting by the chimney - corner . 

 ' fast ? he say thou do not   care if he be full or fast . Lassie ! it be not right in thee , I say ; go , take he his tea at once . ' 

 Sylvia rise , and give up the baby , which she have be suckle , to Nancy , who have do her washing , have come for her charge , to put it to bed . Sylvia kiss it fondly , make a little moan of sad , passionate tenderness as she do so . then she take the cup of tea ; but she say , rather defiantly , to hester , 

 ' I will go to he with it , because mother bid I , and it will ease her mind . ' 

 then loud to her mother , she add , 

 ' Mother , I will take he his tea , though I could not   help the be out . ' 

 if the act itself be conciliatory , the spirit in which she be go to do it be the reverse . hester follow she slowly into the ware - room , with intentional delay , think that her presence might be an obstacle to their mutually understand one another . Sylvia hold the cup and plate of bread and butter out to Philip , but avoid meet his eye , and say not a word of explanation , or regret , or self - justification . if she have speak , though ever so crossly , Philip would have be relieve , and would have prefer it to her silence . he want to provoke she to speech , but do not know how to begin . 

 ' Thou be be out again wander on that sea - shore ! ' say he . she do not answer he . ' I can not think what be always take thee there , when one would ha ' think a walk up to Esdale would be far more sheltered , both for thee and baby in such weather as this . Thou'll be have that baby ill some of these day . ' 

 at this , she look up at he , and her lip move as though she be go to say something . oh , how he wish she would , that they might come to a wholesome quarrel , and a make friend again , and a tender kissing , in which he might whisper penitence for all his hasty word , or unreasonable vexation . but she have come resolve not to speak , for fear of show too much passion , too much emotion . only as she be go away she turn and say , 

 ' Philip , mother have not   many more year to live ; dunnot grieve she , and set she again ' I by find fault wi ' I afore she . our be we d be a great mistake ; but before t ' poor old widow woman let we make as if we be happy . ' 

 ' Sylvie ! Sylvie ! ' he call after she . she must have hear , but she do not turn . he go after she , and seize she by the arm rather roughly ; she have sting he to the heart with her calm word , which seem to reveal a long - form conviction . 

 ' Sylvie ! ' say he , almost fiercely , ' what do yo ' mean by what you have   say ? speak ! I will have an answer . ' 

 he almost shake she : she be half frighten by his vehemence of behaviour , which she take for pure anger , while it be the outburst of agonized and unrequited love . 

 ' let I go ! oh , Philip , yo ' hurt I ! ' 

 just at this moment hester come up ; Philip be ashamed of his passionate way in her serene presence , and loosen his grasp of his wife , and she run away ; run into her mother 's empty room , as to a solitary place , and there burst into that sobbing , miserable cry which we instinctively know be too surely lessen the length of our day on earth to be indulge in often . 

 when she have exhaust that first burst and lie weak and quiet for a time , she listen in dread expectation of the sound of his footstep come in search of she to make friend . but he be detain below on business , and never come . instead , her mother come clamber up the stair ; she be now in the habit of go to bed between seven and eight , and to - night she be retire at even an early hour . 

 Sylvia spring up and draw down the window - blind , and make her face and manner as compose as possible , in order to soothe and comfort her mother 's last wake hour . she help she to bed with gentle patience ; the restraint impose upon she by her tender filial love be good for she , though all the time she be long to be alone to have another wild outburst . when her mother be go off to sleep , Sylvia go to look at her baby , also in a soft sleep . then she gaze out at the evening sky , high above the tile roof of the opposite house , and the longing to be out under the peaceful heaven take possession of she once more . 

 ' it be my only comfort , ' say she to herself ; ' and there be no earthly harm in it . I would ha ' be at home to his tea , if I could ; but when he do not   want I , and mother do not   want I , and baby be either in my arm or asleep ; why , I will go any cry my fill out under yon great quiet sky . I can not stay in t ' house to be choke up wi ' my tear , nor yet to have he come about I either for scolding or peace - making . ' 

 so she put on her thing and go out again ; this time along the High Street , and up the long flight of step towards the parish church , and there she stand and think that here she have first meet Kinraid , at Darley 's burying , and she try to recall the very look of all the sad , earnest face round the open grave the whole scene , in fact ; and let herself give way to the miserable regret she have so often try to control . then she walk on , cry bitterly , almost unawares to herself ; on through the high , bleak field at the summit of the cliff ; field bound by loose stone fence , and far from all sight of the habitation of man . but , below , the sea rise and rag ; it be high water at the high tide , and the wind blow gustily from the land , vainly combat the great wave that come invincibly up with a roar and an impotent furious dash against the base of the cliff below . 

 Sylvia hear the sound of the passionate rush and rebound of many water , like the shock of mighty gun , whenever the other sound of the bluster gusty wind be lull for an instant . she be more quiet by this tempest of the element than she would have be have all nature seem as still as she have imagine it to be while she be yet in - door and only see a part of the serene sky . 

 she fix on a certain point , in her own mind , which she would reach , and then turn back again . it be where the outline of the land curve inward , dip into a little bay . here the field - path she have hitherto follow descend somewhat abruptly to a cluster of fisherman 's cottage , hardly large enough to be call a village ; and then the narrow roadway wind up the rise ground till it again reach the summit of the cliff that stretch along the coast for many and many a mile . 

 Sylvia say to herself that she would turn homeward when she come within sight of this cove , Headlington Cove , they call it . all the way along she have meet no one since she have leave the town , but just as she have get over the last stile , or ladder of stepping - stone , into the field from which the path descend , she come upon a number of people quite a crowd , in fact ; man move forward in a steady line , haul at a rope , a chain , or something of that kind ; boy , child , and woman hold baby in their arm , as if all be fain to come out and partake in some general interest . 

 they keep within a certain distance from the edge of the cliff , and Sylvia , advance a little , now see the reason why . the great cable the man hold be attach to some part of a smack , which could now be see by she in the water below , half dismantle , and all but a wreck , yet with her deck cover with live man , as far as the wan light would allow she to see . the vessel strain to get free of the strong guide cable ; the tide be turn , the wind be blow off shore , and Sylvia know without be tell , that almost parallel to this be a line of sink rock that have be fatal to many a ship before now , if she have try to take the inner channel instead of keep out to sea for mile , and then steer in straight for Monkshaven port . and the ship that have be thus lose have be in good plight and order compare to this vessel , which seem nothing but a hull without mast or sail . 

 by this time , the crowd the fisherman from the hamlet down below , with their wife and child all have come but the bedridden have reach the place where Sylvia stand . the woman , in a state of wild excitement , rush on , encourage their husband and son by word , even while they hinder they by action ; and , from time to time , one of they would run to the edge of the cliff and shout out some brave word of hope in her shrill voice to the crew on the deck below . whether these latter hear it or not , no one could tell ; but it seem as if all human voice must be lose in the tempestuous stun and tumult of wind and wave . it be generally a woman with a child in her arm who so employ herself . as the strain upon the cable become great , and the ground on which they strove more uneven , every hand be need to hold and push , and all those woman who be unencumbered hold by the dear rope on which so many life be depend . on they come , a long line of human being , black against the ruddy sunset sky . as they come near Sylvia , a woman cry out , 

 ' Dunnot stand idle , lass , but houd on wi ' us ; there be many a bonny life at stake , and many a mother 's heart a - hangin ' on this bit o ' hemp . tak ' houd , lass , and give a firm grip , and God remember thee I ' thy need . ' 

 Sylvia need no second word ; a place be make for she , and in an instant more the rope be pull against her hand till it seem as though she be hold fire in her bare palm . never a one of they think of let go for an instant , though when all be over many of their hand be raw and bleeding . some strong , experienced fisherman pass a word along the line from time to time , give direction as to how it should be hold accord to vary occasion ; but few among the rest have breath or strength enough to speak . the woman and child that accompany they run on before , break down the loose stone fence , so as to obviate delay or hindrance ; they talk continually , exhort , encouraging , explain . from their many word and fragmentary sentence , Sylvia learn that the vessel be suppose to be a Newcastle smack sailing from London , that have take the dangerous inner channel to save time , and have be catch in the storm , which she be too crazy to withstand ; and that if by some daring contrivance of the fisherman who have first see she the cable have not be get ashore , she would have be cast upon the rock before this , and ' all on board perish ' . 

 ' it be dayleet then , ' quoth one woman ; ' a could see their face , they be so near . they be as pale as dead man , an ' one be prayin ' down on his knee . there be a king 's officer aboard , for I see t ' gowd about he . ' 

 ' he would maybe come from these hom'ard part , and be comin ' to see his own folk ; else it be no common for king 's officer to sail in aught but king 's ship . ' 

 ' eh ! but it be gettin ' dark ! see there be t ' leeght in t ' house in t ' New Town ! T ' grass be crispin ' wi ' t ' white frost under out foot . it will be a hard tug round t ' point , and then she will be gettin ' into still water . ' 

 one more great push and mighty strain , and the danger be past ; the vessel or what remain of she be in the harbour , among the light and cheerful sound of safety . the fisherman spring down the cliff to the quay - side , anxious to see the man whose life they have save ; the woman , weary and over - excite , begin to cry . not Sylvia , however ; her fount of tear have be exhaust early in the day : her principal feeling be of gladness and high rejoicing that they be save who have be so near to death not half an hour before .
