 she would have like to have see the man , and shake hand with they all round . but instead she must go home , and well would it be with she if she be in time for her husband 's supper , and escape any notice of her absence . so she separate herself from the group of woman who sate on the grass in the churchyard , await the return of such of their husband as could resist the fascination of the monkshaven public house . as Sylvia go down the church step , she come upon one of the fisherman who have help to tow the vessel into port . 

 ' there be seventeen man and boy aboard she , and a navy - lieutenant as have come as passenger . it be a good job as we could manage she . good - neet to thee , thou'll sleep all t ' sounder for havin' lend a hand . ' 

 the street air feel hot and close after the sharp keen atmosphere of the height above ; the decent shop and house have all their shutter put up , and be prepare for their early bed - time . already light shine here and there in the upper chamber , and Sylvia scarcely meet any one . 

 she go round up the passage from the quay - side , and in by the private door . all be still ; the basin of bread and milk that she and her husband be in the habit of have for supper stand in the fender before the fire , each with a plate upon they . Nancy have go to bed , Phoebe doze in the kitchen ; Philip be still in the ware - room , arrange good and take stock along with Coulson , for hester have go home to her mother . 

 Sylvia be not willing to go and seek out Philip , after the manner in which they have part . all the despondency of her life become present to she again as she sate down within her home . she have forget it in her interest and excitement , but now it come back again . 

 still she be hungry , and youthful , and tired . she take her basin up , and be eat her supper when she hear a cry of her baby upstairs , and run away to attend to it . when it have be feed and hush away to sleep , she go in to see her mother , attract by some unusual noise in her room . 

 she find Mrs. Robson awake , and restless , and ailing ; dwell much on what Philip have say in his anger against Sylvia . it be really necessary for her daughter to remain with she ; so Sylvia steal out , and go quickly down - stair to Philip now sit tired and wear out , and eat his supper with little or no appetite and tell he she mean to pass the night with her mother . 

 his answer of acquiescence be so short and careless , or so it seem to she , that she do not tell he any more of what she have do or see that evening , or even dwell upon any detail of her mother 's indisposition . 

 as soon as she have leave the room , Philip set down his half - finished basin of bread and milk , and sate long , his face hide in his fold arm . the wick of the candle grow long and black , and fall , and sputter , and gutter ; he sate on , unheede either it or the pale gray fire that be die out dead at last . 




 CHAPTER XXXIII 

 an APPARITION 

 Mrs. Robson be very poorly all night long . uneasy thought seem to haunt and perplex her brain , and she neither sleep nor wake , but be restless and uneasy in her talk and movement . 

 Sylvia lie down by she , but get so little sleep , that at length she prefer sit in the easy - chair by the bedside . here she drop off to slumber in spite of herself ; the scene of the evening before seem to be repeat ; the cry of the many people , the heavy roar and dash of the threaten wave , be repeat in her ear ; and something be say to she through all the conflict noise , what it be she could not catch , though she strain to hear the hoarse murmur that , in her dream , she believe to convey a meaning of the utmost importance to she . 

 this dream , that mysterious , only half - intelligible sound , recur whenever she doze , and her inability to hear the word utter distress she so much , that at length she sate bolt upright , resolve to sleep no more . her mother be talk in a half - conscious way ; Philip 's speech of the evening before be evidently run in her mind . 

 ' Sylvie , if thou're not a good wife to he , it will just break my heart outright . a woman should obey her husband , and not go her own gait . I never leave the house wi'out tell father , and get his leave . ' 

 and then she begin to cry pitifully , and to say unconnected thing , till Sylvia , to soothe she , take her hand , and promise never to leave the house without ask her husband 's permission , though in make this promise , she feel as if she be sacrifice her last pleasure to her mother 's wish ; for she know well enough that Philip would always raise objection to the ramble which remind she of her old free open - air life . 

 but to comfort and cherish her mother she would have do anything ; yet this very morning that be dawn , she must go and ask his permission for a simple errand , or break her word . 

 she know from experience that nothing quiet her mother so well as balm - tea ; it might be that the herb really possess some sedative power ; it might be only early faith , and often repeat experience , but it have always have a tranquillizing effect ; and more than once , during the restless hour of the night , Mrs. Robson have ask for it ; but Sylvia 's stock of last year 's dead leaf be exhaust . still she know where a plant of balm grow in the sheltered corner of Haytersbank Farm garden ; she know that the tenant who have succeed they in the occupation of the farm have have to leave it in consequence of a death , and that the place be unoccupied ; and in the darkness she have plan that if she could leave her mother after the dawn come , and she have attend to her baby , she would walk quickly to the old garden , and gather the tender sprig which she be sure to find there . 

 now she must go and ask Philip ; and till she hold her baby to her breast , she bitterly wish that she be free from the duty and chain of matrimony . but the touch of its waxen finger , the hold of its little mouth , make she relax into docility and gentleness . she give it back to Nancy to be dress , and softly open the door of Philip 's bed - room . 

 ' Philip ! ' say she , gently . ' Philip ! ' 

 he start up from dream of she ; of she , angry . he see she there , rather pale with her night 's watch and anxiety , but look meek , and a little beseech . 

 ' Mother have have such a bad night ! she fancy once as some balm - tea would do she good it allay use to : but my dried balm be all go , and I think there would be sure to be some in t ' old garden at Haytersbank . Feyther plant a bush just for mother , wheere it allay come up early , nigh t ' old elder - tree ; and if yo'd not mind , I could run theere while she sleep , and be back again in an hour , and it be not seven now . ' 

 ' Thou be not wear thyself out with run , Sylvie , ' say Philip , eagerly ; ' I will get up and go myself , or , perhaps , ' continue he , catch the shadow that be come over her face , ' thou'd rather go thyself : it be only that I be so afraid of thy tire thyself . ' 

 ' it will not tire I , ' say Sylvia . ' afore I be marry , I be out often far far than that , afield to fetch up t ' kine , before my breakfast . ' 

 ' well , go if thou will , ' say Philip . ' but get somewhat to eat first , and do not   hurry ; there be no need for that . ' 

 she have get her hat and shawl , and be off before he have finish his last word . 

 the long High Street be almost empty of people at that early hour ; one side be entirely cover by the cool morning shadow which lie on the pavement , and creep up the opposite house till only the topmost story catch the rosy sunlight . up the hill - road , through the gap in the stone wall , across the dewy field , Sylvia go by the very short path she know . 

 she have only once be at Haytersbank since her wedding - day . on that occasion the place have seem strangely and dissonantly change by the numerous child who be divert themselves before the open door , and whose plaything and clothe strew the house - place , and make it one busy scene of confusion and untidiness , more like the Corneys ' kitchen in former time , than her mother 's orderly and quiet abode . those little child be fatherless now ; and the house be shut up , await the entry of some new tenant . there be no shutter to shut ; the long low window be blink in the ray of the morning sun ; the house and cow - house door be closed , and no poultry wander about the field in search of stray grain of corn , or early worm . it be a strange and unfamiliar silence , and strike solemnly on Sylvia 's mind . only a thrush in the old orchard down in the hollow , out of sight , whistle and gurgle with continual shrill melody . 

 Sylvia go slowly past the house and down the path lead to the wild , desert bit of garden . she see that the last tenant have have a pump sink for they , and resent the innovation , as though the well she be pass could feel the insult . over it grow two hawthorn tree ; on the bent trunk of one of they she use to sit , long ago : the charm of the position be enhance by the possible danger of fall into the well and be drown . the rusty unused chain be wind round the windlass ; the bucket be fall to piece from dryness . a lean cat come from some outhouse , and mew pitifully with hunger ; accompany Sylvia to the garden , as if glad of some human companionship , yet refuse to allow itself to be touch . primrose grow in the sheltered place , just as they formerly do ; and make the uncultivated ground seem less deserted than the garden , where the last year 's weed be rot away , and cumber the ground . 

 Sylvia force her way through the berry bush to the herb - plot , and pluck the tender leaf she have come to seek ; sigh a little all the time . then she retrace her step ; pause softly before the house - door , and enter the porch and kiss the senseless wood . 

 she try to tempt the poor gaunt cat into her arm , mean to carry it home and befriend it ; but it be scar by her endeavour and run back to its home in the outhouse , make a green path across the white dew of the meadow . then Sylvia begin to hasten home , thinking , and remember at the stile that lead into the road she be bring short up . 

 some one stand in the lane just on the other side of the gap ; his back be to the morning sun ; all she see at first be the uniform of a naval officer , so well know in Monkshaven in those day . 

 Sylvia go hurry past he , not look again , although her clothe almost brush his , as he stand there still . she have not go a yard no , not half a yard when her heart leap up and fall again dead within she , as if she have be shoot . 

 ' Sylvia ! ' he say , in a voice tremulous with joy and passionate love . ' Sylvia ! ' 

 she look round ; he have turn a little , so that the light fall straight on his face . it be bronze , and the line be strengthen ; but it be the same face she have last see in Haytersbank Gully three long year ago , and have never think to see in life again . 

 he be close to she and hold out his fond arm ; she go flutter towards their embrace , as if draw by the old fascination ; but when she feel they close round she , she start away , and cry out with a great pitiful shriek , and put her hand up to her forehead as if try to clear away some bewilder mist . 

 then she look at he once more , a terrible story in her eye , if he could but have read it . 

 twice she open her stiff lip to speak , and twice the word be overwhelm by the surge of her misery , which bear they back into the depth of her heart . 

 he think that he have come upon she too suddenly , and he attempt to soothe she with soft murmur of love , and to woo she to his outstretched hungry arm once more . but when she see this motion of his , she make a gesture as though push he away ; and with an inarticulate moan of agony she put her hand to her head once more , and turn away begin to run blindly towards the town for protection . 

 for a minute or so he be stun with surprise at her behaviour ; and then he think it account for by the shock of his accost , and that she need time to understand the unexpected joy . so he follow she swiftly , ever keep she in view , but not try to overtake she too speedily . 

 ' I have frighten my poor love , ' he keep think . and by this thought he try to repress his impatience and check the speed he long to use ; yet he be always so near behind that her quickened sense hear his well - know footstep follow , and a mad notion flash across her brain that she would go to the wide full river , and end the hopeless misery she feel enshroud she . there be a sure hiding - place from all human reproach and heavy mortal woe beneath the rushing water bear landward by the morning tide . 

 no one can tell what change her course ; perhaps the thought of her suck child ; perhaps her mother ; perhaps an angel of God ; no one on earth know , but as she run along the quay - side she all at once turn up an entry , and through an open door . 

 he , follow all the time , come into a quiet dark parlour , with a cloth and tea - thing on the table ready for breakfast ; the change from the bright sunny air out of door to the deep shadow of this room make he think for the first moment that she have pass on , and that no one be there , and he stand for an instant baffle , and hear no sound but the beating of his own heart ; but an irrepressible sob gasp make he look round , and there he see she cower behind the door , her face cover tight up , and sharp shudder go through her whole frame . 

 ' my love , my darling ! ' say he , go up to she , and try to raise she , and to loosen her hand away from her face . ' I have   be too sudden for thee : it be thoughtless in I ; but I have so look forward to this time , and see thee come along the field , and go past I , but I should ha ' be more tender and careful of thee . Nay ! let I have another look of thy sweet face . ' 

 all this he whisper in the old tone of manoeuvre love , in that voice she have yearn and hunger to hear in life , and have not hear , for all her longing , save in her dream . 

 she try to crouch more and more into the corner , into the hidden shadow to sink into the ground out of sight . 

 once more he speak , beseech she to lift up her face , to let he hear her speak . 

 but she only moan . 

 ' Sylvia ! ' say he , think he could change his tactic , and pique she into speak , that he would make a pretence of suspicion and offence . 

 ' Sylvia ! one would think you be not   glad to see I back again at length . I only come in late last night , and my first thought on waken be of you ; it have be ever since I leave you . ' 

 Sylvia take her hand away from her face ; it be gray as the face of death ; her awful eye be passionless in her despair . 

 ' where have yo ' be ? ' she ask , in slow , hoarse tone , as if her voice be half strangle within she . 

 ' be ! ' say he , a red light come into his eye , as he bend his look upon she ; now , indeed , a true and not an assumed suspicion enter his mind . 

 ' be ! ' he repeat ; then , come a step near to she , and take her hand , not tenderly this time , but with a resolution to be satisfied . 

 ' do not your cousin Hepburn , I mean do not he tell you ? he see the press - gang seize I , I give he a message to you I bid you keep true to I as I would be to you . ' 

 between every clause of this speech he pause and gasp for her answer ; but none come . her eye dilate and hold his steady gaze prisoner as with a magical charm neither could look away from the other 's wild , search gaze . when he have end , she be silent for a moment , then she cry out , shrill and fierce , 

 ' Philip ! ' no answer . 

 Wilder and shriller still , ' Philip ! ' she cry . 

 he be in the distant ware - room complete the last night 's work before the regular shop hour begin ; before breakfast , also , that his wife might not find he wait and impatient . 

 he hear she cry ; it cut through door , and still air , and great bale of woollen stuff ; he think that she have hurt herself , that her mother be bad , that her baby be ill , and he hasten to the spot whence the cry proceed . 

 on open the door that separate the shop from the sitting - room , he see the back of a naval officer , and his wife on the ground , huddle up in a heap ; when she perceive he come in , she drag herself up by mean of a chair , grope like a blind person , and come and stand face he . 

 the officer turn fiercely round , and would have come towards Philip , who be so bewildered by the scene that even yet he do not understand who the stranger be , do not perceive for an instant that he see the realization of his great dread . 

 but Sylvia lay her hand on Kinraid 's arm , and assume to herself the right of speech . Philip do not know her voice , it be so change . 

 ' Philip , ' she say , ' this be Kinraid come back again to we d I . he be alive ; he have niver be dead , only take by t ' press - gang . and he say yo ' see it , and know it all t ' time . Speak , be it so ? ' 

 Philip know not what to say , whither to turn , under what refuge of word or act to shelter . 

 Sylvia 's influence be keep kinraid silent , but he be rapidly pass beyond it . 

 ' speak ! ' he cry , loosen himself from Sylvia 's light grasp , and come towards Philip , with a threatening gesture . ' do I not bid you tell she how it be ? do I not bid you say how I would be faithful to she , and she be to be faithful to I ? oh ! you damn scoundrel ! have you keep it from she all that time , and let she think I dead , or false ? take that ! ' 

 his closed fist be up to strike the man , who hang his head with bitter shame and miserable self - reproach ; but Sylvia come swift between the blow and its victim . 

 ' Charley , thou shall not   strike he , ' she say . ' he be a damned scoundrel '   this be say in the hard , quiet tone   ' but he be my husband . ' 

 ' oh ! thou false heart ! ' exclaim Kinraid , turn sharp on she . ' if ever I trust woman , I trust you , Sylvia Robson . ' 

 he make as though throw she from he , with a gesture of contempt that sting she to life . 

 ' oh , Charley ! ' she cry , spring to he , ' dunnot cut I to the quick ; have pity on I , though he have none . I do so love thee ; it be my very heart - string as give way when they tell I thou be drown feyther , and th ' Corneys , and all , iverybody . thy hat and t ' bit o ' ribbon I give thee be find drench and dripping wi ' sea - water ; and I go mourn for thee all the day long dunnot turn away from I ; only hearken this once , and then kill I dead , and I will bless yo ' , and have niver be mysel ' since ; niver cease to feel t ' sun grow dark and th ' air chill and dreary when I think on t ' time when thou be alive . I do , my Charley , my own love ! and I think thou be dead for iver , and I wish I be lie beside thee . oh , Charley ! Philip , theere , where he stand , could tell yo ' this be true . Philip , be not   it so ? ' 

 ' would God I be dead ! ' moan forth the unhappy , guilty man . but she have turn to Kinraid , and be speak again to he , and neither of they hear or heed he they be draw close and close together she , with her cheek and eye aflame , talk eagerly . 

 ' and feyther be take up , and all for set some free as t ' press - gang have get by a foul trick ; and he be put I ' York prison , and try , and hung ! hung ! Charley ! good kind feyther be hang on a gallow ; and mother lose her sense and grow silly in grief , and we be like to be turn out on t ' wide world , and poor mother dateless and I think yo ' be dead oh ! I think yo ' be dead , I do oh , Charley , Charley ! ' 

 by this time they be in each other 's arm , she with her head on his shoulder , cry as if her heart would break . 

 Philip come forwards and take hold of she to pull she away ; but Charley hold she tight , mutely defy Philip . unconsciously she be Philip 's protection , in that hour of danger , from a blow which might have be his death if strong will could have aid it to kill . 

 ' Sylvie ! ' say he , grasp she tight . ' listen to I . he do not   love yo ' as I do . he have love other woman . I , yo ' yo ' alone . he love other girl before yo ' , and have leave off love them . I I wish God would free my heart from the pang ; but it will go on till I die , whether yo ' love I or not . and then where be I ? oh ! that very night that he be take , I be a - thinking on yo ' and on he ; and I might ha ' give yo ' his message , but I hear they speak of he as know he well ; talk of his false fickle way . how be I to know he would keep true to thee ? it might be a sin in I , I can not say ; my heart and my sense be go dead within I . I know this , I have   love yo ' as no man but I ever love before . have some pity and forgiveness on I , if it be only because I have   be so tormented with my love . ' 

 he look at she with feverish eager wistfulness ; it fade away into despair as she make no sign of have even hear his word . he let go his hold of she , and his arm fall loosely by his side . 

 ' I may die , ' he say , ' for my life be end ! ' 

 ' Sylvia ! ' speak out Kinraid , bold and fervent , ' your marriage be no marriage . you be trick into it . you be my wife , not his . I be your husband ; we plight each other our troth . see ! here be my half of the sixpence . ' 

 he pull it out from his bosom , tie by a black ribbon round his neck . 

 ' when they strip I and search I in th ' french prison , I manage to keep this . no lie can break the oath we swear to each other . I can get your pretence of a marriage set aside . I be in favour with my admiral , and he will do a deal for I , and back I out . come with I ; your marriage shall be set aside , and we will be marry again , all square and above - board . come away . leave that damn fellow to repent of the trick he play an honest sailor ; we will be true , whatever have come and go . Come , Sylvia . ' 

 his arm be round her waist , and he be draw she towards the door , his face all crimson with eagerness and hope . just then the baby cry . 

 ' Hark ! ' say she , start away from Kinraid , ' baby 's cry for I . his child yes , it be his child I would forget that forget all . I will make my vow now , lest I lose mysel ' again . I will never forgive yon man , nor live with he as his wife again . all that be do and end . he be spoil my life , he be spoil it for as long as iver I live on this earth ; but neither yo ' nor he shall spoil my soul . it go hard wi ' me , Charley , it do indeed . I will just give yo ' one kiss one little kiss and then , so help I God , I will niver see nor hear till no , not that , not that be need I will niver see sure that be enough I will never see yo ' again on this side heaven , so help I God ! I be bind and tie , but I have   swear my oath to he as well as yo ' : there be thing I will do , and there be thing I will not . kiss I once more . God help I , he be go ! ' 




 CHAPTER XXXIV 

 a reckless RECRUIT 

 she lie across a chair , her arm helplessly stretch out , her face unseen . every now and then a thrill run through her body : she be talk to herself all the time with incessant low incontinence of word . 

 Philip stand near she , motionless : he do not know whether she be conscious of his presence ; in fact , he know nothing but that he and she be sunder for ever ; he could only take in that one idea , and it numb all other thought . 

 once more her baby cry for the comfort she alone could give . 

 she rise to her foot , but stagger when she try to walk ; her glaze eye fall upon Philip as he instinctively make a step to hold she steady . no light come into her eye any more than if she have look upon a perfect stranger ; not even be there the contraction of dislike . some other figure fill her mind , and she see he no more than she see the inanimate table . that way of look at he wither he up more than any sign of aversion would have do . 

 he watch she laboriously climb the stair , and vanish out of sight ; and sit down with a sudden feeling of extreme bodily weakness . 

 the door of communication between the parlour and the shop be open . that be the first event of which Philip take note ; but Phoebe have come in unaware to he , with the intention of remove the breakfast thing on her return from market , and see they unused , and know that Sylvia have sate up all night with her mother , she have go back to the kitchen . Philip have neither see nor hear she . 

 now Coulson come in , amazed at Hepburn 's non - appearance in the shop . 

 ' why ! Philip , what be ado ? how ill yo ' look , man ! ' exclaim he , thoroughly alarm by Philip 's ghastly appearance . ' what be the matter ? ' 

 ' I ! ' say Philip , slowly gather his thought . ' why should there be anything the matter ? ' 

 his instinct , quick to act than his reason , make he shrink from his misery be notice , much more make any subject for explanation or sympathy . 

 ' there may be nothing the matter wi ' thee , ' say Coulson , ' but thou be the look of a corpse on thy face . I be afeare something be wrong , for it be half - past nine , and thee so punctual ! ' 

 he almost guard Philip into the shop , and keep furtively watch he , and perplex himself with Philip 's odd , strange way . 

 hester , too , observe the heavy break - down expression on Philip 's ashen face , and her heart ache for he ; but after that first glance , which tell she so much , she avoid all appearance of notice or watch . only a shadow brood over her sweet , calm face , and once or twice she sigh to herself . 

 it be market - day , and people come in and out , bring their store of gossip from the country , or the town from the farm or the quay - side . 

 among the piece of news , the rescue of the smack the night before furnish a large topic ; and by - and - by Philip hear a name that startle he into attention . 

 the landlady of a small public - house much frequent by sailor be talk to Coulson . 

 ' there be a sailor aboard of she as know Kinraid by sight , in Shields , year ago ; and he call he by his name afore they be well out o ' t ' river . and Kinraid be no way set up , for all his lieutenant 's uniform   and eh ! but they say he look handsome in it ! ; but he tell ' m all about it how he be press aboard a man - o'-war , an ' for his good conduct be make a warrant officer , boatswain , or something ! ' 

 all the people in the shop be listen now ; Philip alone seem engross in fold up a piece of cloth , so as to leave no possible chance of crease in it ; yet he lose not a syllable of the good woman 's narration . 

 she , pleased with the enlarged audience her tale have attract , go on with fresh vigour . 

 ' An ' there be a gallant captain , one Sir Sidney Smith , and he have a notion o ' go smack into a french port , an ' carryin ' off a vessel from right under their very nose ; an ' say he , " which of yo ' british sailor will go along with I to death or glory ? " so Kinraid stand up like a man , an ' " I will go with yo ' , captain , " he say . so they , an ' some other as brave , go off , an ' do their work , an ' choose whativer it be , they do it famously ; but they get catch by they French , an ' be clap into prison I ' France for iver so long ; but at last one Philip Philip something   he be a Frenchman , I know   help them to escape , in a fishin'-boat . but they be welcome by th ' whole british squadron as be i ' t ' Channel for t ' piece of dare they would do I ' cuttin ' out t ' ship from a french port ; an ' Captain Sir Sidney Smith be make an admiral , an ' he as we use t ' call Charley Kinraid , the specksioneer , be make a lieutenant , an ' a commission officer I ' t ' King 's service ; and be come to great glory , and slep in my house this very blessed night as be just past ! ' 

 a murmur of applause and interest and rejoice buzz all around Philip . all this be publicly know about Kinraid , and how much more ? all Monkshaven might hear tomorrow nay , to - day of Philip 's treachery to the hero of the hour ; how he have conceal his fate , and supplant he in his love . 

 Philip shrink from the burst of popular indignation which he know must follow . any wrong do to one who stand on the pinnacle of the people 's favour be resent by each individual as a personal injury ; and among a primitive set of country - folk , who recognize the wild passion in love , as it exist untame by the trammel of reason and self - restraint , any story of baulked affection , or treachery in such matter , spread like wildfire . 

 Philip know this quite well ; his doom of disgrace lie plain before he , if only Kinraid speak the word . his head be bent down while he thus listen and reflect . he half resolve on do something ; he lift up his head , catch the reflection of his face in the little strip of glass on the opposite side , in which the woman might look at themselves in their contemplated purchase , and quite resolve . 

 the sight he see in the mirror be his own long , sad , pale face , make plainer and grayer by the heavy pressure of the morning 's event . he see his stoop figure , his rounded shoulder , with something like a feeling of disgust at his personal appearance as he remember the square , upright build of Kinraid ; his fine uniform , with epaulette and sword - belt ; his handsome brown face ; his dark eye , splendid with the fire of passion and indignation ; his white tooth , gleam out with the terrible smile of scorn . 

 the comparison drive Philip from passive hopelessness to active despair . 

 he go abruptly from the crowded shop into the empty parlour , and on into the kitchen , where he take up a piece of bread , and heedless of Phoebe 's look and word , begin to eat it before he even leave the place ; for he need the strength that food would give ; he need it to carry he out of the sight and the knowledge of all who might hear what he have do , and point their finger at he . 

 he pause a moment in the parlour , and then , set his tooth tight together , he go upstairs . 

 first of all he go into the bit of a room opening out of theirs , in which his baby sleep . he dearly love the child , and many a time would run in and play a while with it ; and in such gambol he and Sylvia have pass their happy moment of wed life . 

 the little Bella be have her morning slumber ; Nancy use to tell long afterwards how he kneel down by the side of her cot , and be so strange she think he must have pray , for all it be nigh upon eleven o'clock , and folk in their sens only say their prayer when they get up , and when they go to bed . 

 then he rise , and stoop over , and give the child a long , lingering , soft , fond kiss . and on tip - toe he pass away into the room where his aunt lay ; his aunt who have be so true a friend to he ! he be thankful to know that in her present state she be safe from the knowledge of what be past , safe from the sound of the shame to come . 

 he have not mean to see Sylvia again ; he dread the look of her hatred , her scorn , but there , outside her mother 's bed , she lay , apparently asleep . Mrs. Robson , too , be sleep , her face towards the wall . Philip could not help it ; he go to have one last look at his wife . she be turn towards her mother , her face avert from he ; he could see the tear - stain , the swollen eyelid , the lip yet quiver : he stoop down , and bent to kiss the little hand that lie listless by her side . as his hot breath near that hand it be twitch away , and a shiver run through the whole prostrate body . and then he know that she be not asleep , only wear out by her misery , misery that he have cause . 

 he sigh heavily ; but he go away , down - stair , and away for ever . only as he enter the parlour his eye catch on two silhouette , one of himself , one of Sylvia , do in the first month of their marriage , by some wandering artist , if so he could be call . they be hang against the wall in little oval wooden frame ; black profile , with the light do in gold ; about as poor semblance of humanity as could be conceive ; but Philip go up , and after look for a minute or so at Sylvia 's , he take it down , and button his waistcoat over it . 

 it be the only thing he take away from his home . 

 he go down the entry on to the quay . the river be there , and water , they say , have a luring power , and a weird promise of rest in their perpetual monotony of sound . but many people be there , if such a temptation present itself to Philip 's mind ; the sight of his fellow - townsman , perhaps of his acquaintance , drive he up another entry the town be burrow with such back into the High Street , which he straightway cross into a well - know court , out of which rough step lead to the summit of the hill , and on to the fell and moor beyond . 

 he plunge and pant up this rough ascent . from the top he could look down on the whole town lie below , sever by the bright shin river into two part . to the right lie the sea , shimmering and heaving ; there be the cluster of mast rise out of the little port ; the irregular roof of the house ; which of they , think he , as he carry his eye along the quay - side to the market - place , which of they be his ? and he single it out in its unfamiliar aspect , and see the thin blue smoke rise from the kitchen chimney , where even now Phoebe be cook the household meal that he never more must share . 

 up at that thought and away , he know not nor care not whither . he go through the plough field where the corn be newly springing ; he come down upon the vast sunny sea , and turn his back upon it with loathing ; he make his way inland to the high green pasture ; the short upland turf above which the lark hang poise ' at heaven 's gate ' . he stride along , so straight and heedless of briar and bush , that the wild black cattle cease from graze , and look after he with their great blank puzzled eye . 

 he have pass all enclosure and stone fence now , and be fairly on the desolate brown moor ; through the wither last year 's ling and fern , through the prickly gorse , he tramp , crush down the tender shoot of this year 's growth , and heedless of the startled plover 's cry , goad by the fury . his only relief from thought , from the remembrance of Sylvia 's look and word , be in violent bodily action . 

 so he go on till evening shadow and ruddy evening light come out upon the wild fell . 

 he have cross road and lane , with a bitter avoidance of man 's track ; but now the strong instinct of self - preservation come out , and his ache limb , his weary heart , give great pant and beat for a time , and then cease altogether till a mist swam and quiver before his ache eye , warn he that he must find some shelter and food , or lie down to die . he fall down now , often ; stumble over the slight obstacle . he have pass the cattle pasture ; he be among the black - face sheep ; and they , too , cease nibbling , and look after he , and somehow , in his poor wandering imagination , their silly face turn to likeness of monkshaven people people who ought to be far , far away . 

 ' Thou'll be belate on these fell , if thou do not   tak ' heed , ' shout some one . 

 Philip look abroad to see whence the voice proceed . 

 an old stiff - legged shepherd , in a smock - frock , be within a couple of hundred yard . Philip do not answer , but stagger and stumble towards he . 

 ' good lork ! ' say the man , ' wheere hast ta be ? Thou 's see Oud Harry , I think , thou look so scared . ' 

 Philip rally himself , and try to speak up to the old standard of respectability ; but the effort be pitiful to see , have any one be by , who could have understand the pain it cause to restrain cry of bodily and mental agony . 

 ' I have   lose my way , that be all . ' 

 " twould ha ' be enough , too , I be thinkin ' , if I have not   come out after t ' ewe . there 's t ' Three Griffins near at hand : a sup o ' holland will set thee to reeght . ' 

 Philip follow faintly . he could not see before he , and be guide by the sound of footstep rather than by the sight of the figure move onwards . he keep stumble ; and he know that the old shepherd swear at he ; but he also know such curse proceed from no ill - will , only from annoyance at the delay in go and ' seem ' after t ' ewe . ' but have the man 's word convey the utmost expression of hatred , Philip would neither have wonder at they , nor resent they . 

 they come into a wild mountain road , unfence from the fell . a hundred yard off , and there be a small public - house , with a broad ruddy oblong of firelight shin across the tract . 

 ' theere ! ' say the old man . ' thee can not well miss that . a dunno tho ' , thee bee sich a gawby . ' 

 so he go on , and deliver Philip safely up to the landlord . 

 ' here be a felly as a fund on t ' fell side , just as one as if he be drunk ; but he be sober enough , a reckon , only summat 's wrong I ' his head , a'm thinkin ' . ' 

 ' no ! ' say Philip , sit down on the first chair he come to . ' I be right enough ; just fairly weary out : lose my way , ' and he faint . 

 there be a recruiting sergeant of marine sit in the house - place , drinking . he , too , like Philip , have lose his way ; but be turn his blunder to account by tell all manner of wonderful story to two or three rustic who have come in ready to drink on any pretence ; especially if they could get good liquor without pay for it . 

 the sergeant rise as Philip fall back , and bring up his own mug of beer , into which a noggin of gin have be put   call in Yorkshire ' dog's - nose ' . he partly pour and partly spill some of this beverage on Philip 's face ; some drop go through the pale and part lip , and with a start the wear - out man revive . 

 ' bring he some victual , landlord , ' call out the recruiting sergeant . ' I will stand shot . ' 

 they bring some cold bacon and coarse oat - cake . the sergeant ask for pepper and salt ; mince the food fine and make it savoury , and keep administer it by teaspoonful ; urge Philip to drink from time to time from his own cup of dog's - nose . 

 a burn thirst , which need no stimulant from either pepper or salt , take possession of Philip , and he drink freely , scarcely recognize what he drink . it take effect on one so habitually sober ; and he be soon in that state when the imagination work wildly and freely . 

 he see the sergeant before he , handsome , and bright , and active , in his gay red uniform , without a care , as it seem to Philip , take life lightly ; admire and respect everywhere because of his cloth . 

 if Philip be gay , and brisk , well - dressed like he , return with martial glory to Monkshaven , would not Sylvia love he once more ? could not he win her heart ? he be brave by nature , and the prospect of danger do not daunt he , if ever it present itself to his imagination . 

 he think he be cautious in enter on the subject of enlistment with his new friend , the sergeant ; but the latter be twenty time as cunning as he , and know by experience how to bait his hook . 

 Philip be old by some year than the regulation age ; but , at that time of great demand for man , the question of age be lightly entertain . the sergeant be profuse in statement of the advantage present to a man of education in his branch of the service ; how such a one be sure to rise ; in fact , it would have seem from the sergeant 's account , as though the difficulty consist in remain in the rank . 

 Philip 's dizzy head think the subject over and over again , each time with fail power of reason . 

 at length , almost , as it would seem , by some sleight of hand , he find the fatal shilling in his palm , and have promise to go before the near magistrate to be swear in as one of his Majesty 's marine the next morning . and after that he remember nothing more . 

 he waken up in a little truckle - bed in the same room as the sergeant , who lie sleep the sleep of full contentment ; while gradually , drop by drop , the bitter recollection of the day before came , fill up Philip 's cup of agony . 

 he know that he have receive the bounty - money ; and though he be aware that he have be partly trick into it , and have no hope , no care , indeed , for any of the advantage so liberally promise he the night before , yet he be resign , with utterly despondent passiveness , to the fate to which he have pledge himself . anything be welcome that sever he from his former life , that could make he forget it , if that be possible ; and also welcome anything which increase the chance of death without the sinfulness of his own participation in the act . he find in the dark recess of his mind the dead body of his fancy of the previous night ; that he might come home , handsome and glorious , to win the love that have never be his . 

 but he only sigh over it , and put it aside out of his sight so full of despair be he . he could eat no breakfast , though the sergeant order of the good . the latter keep watch his new recruit out of the corner of his eye , expect a remonstrance , or dread a sudden bolt . 

 but Philip walk with he the two or three mile in the most submissive silence , never utter a syllable of regret or repentance ; and before Justice Cholmley , of Holm - Fell Hall , he be swear into his Majesty 's service , under the name of Stephen Freeman . with a new name , he begin a new life . alas ! the old life live for ever ! 




 CHAPTER XXXV 

 thing unutterable 

 after Philip have pass out of the room , Sylvia lie perfectly still , from very exhaustion . her mother sleep on , happily unconscious of all the turmoil that have take place ; yes , happily , though the heavy sleep be to end in death . but of this her daughter know nothing , imagine that it be refreshing slumber , instead of an ebbing of life . both mother and daughter lie motionless till Phoebe enter the room to tell Sylvia that dinner be on the table . 

 then Sylvia sate up , and put back her hair , bewildered and uncertain as to what be to be do next ; how she should meet the husband to whom she have discard all allegiance , repudiate the solemn promise of love and obedience which she have vow . 

 Phoebe come into the room , with natural interest in the invalid , scarcely old than herself . 

 ' how be t ' old lady ? ' ask she , in a low voice . 

 Sylvia turn her head round to look ; her mother have never move , but be breathe in a loud uncomfortable manner , that make her stoop over she to see the avert face more nearly . 

 ' Phoebe ! ' she cry , ' come here ! she look strange and odd ; her eye be open , but do not   see I . Phoebe ! Phoebe ! ' 

 ' sure enough , she be in a bad way ! ' say Phoebe , climbing stiffly on to the bed to have a near view . ' hold her head a little up t ' ease her breathin ' while I go for master ; he will be for sendin ' for t ' doctor , I will be bind . ' 

 Sylvia take her mother 's head and lay it fondly on her breast , speak to she and try to rouse she ; but it be of no avail : the hard , stertorous breathing grow bad and bad . 

 Sylvia cry out for help ; Nancy come , the baby in her arm . they have be in several time before that morning ; and the child come smile and crow at its mother , who be support her own die parent . 

 ' oh , Nancy ! ' say Sylvia ; ' what be the matter with mother ? yo ' can see her face ; tell I quick ! ' 

 Nancy set the baby on the bed for all reply , and run out of the room , cry out , 

 ' Master ! master ! come quick ! t ' old missus be a - dying ! ' 

 this appear to be no news to Sylvia , and yet the word come on she with a great shock , but for all that she could not cry ; she be surprise herself at her own deadness of feeling . 

 her baby crawl to she , and she have to hold and guard both her mother and her child . it seem a long , long time before any one come , and then she hear muffled voice , and a heavy tramp : it be Phoebe lead the doctor upstairs , and Nancy creep in behind to hear his opinion . 

 he do not ask many question , and Phoebe reply more frequently to his inquiry than do Sylvia , who look into his face with a blank , tearless , speechless despair , that give he more pain than the sight of her die mother . 

 the long decay of Mrs. Robson 's faculty and health , of which he be well aware , have in a certain manner prepare he for some such sudden termination of the life whose duration be hardly desirable , although he give several direction as to her treatment ; but the white , pinch face , the great dilate eye , the slow comprehension of the young woman , strike he with alarm ; and he go on ask for various particular , more with a view of rouse Sylvia , if even it be to tear , than for any other purpose that the information thus obtain could answer . 

 ' you have well have pillow prop up behind she it will not be for long ; she do not know that you be hold she , and it be only tire you to no purpose ! ' 

 Sylvia 's terrible stare continue : he put his advice into action , and gently try to loosen her clasp , and tender hold . this she resist ; lay her cheek against her poor mother 's unconscious face . 

 ' where be hepburn ? ' say he . ' he ought to be here ! ' 

 Phoebe look at Nancy , Nancy at Phoebe . it be the latter who reply , 

 ' he be neither I ' t ' house nor I ' t ' shop . a seed he go past t ' kitchen window well nor an hour ago ; but neither William Coulson or Hester Rose know where he be go to . 

 Dr Morgan 's lip be pucker up into a whistle , but he make no sound . 

 ' give I baby ! ' he say , suddenly . Nancy have take she up off the bed where she have be sit , encircle by her mother 's arm . the nursemaid give she to the doctor . he watch the mother 's eye , it follow her child , and he be rejoice . he give a little pinch to the baby 's soft flesh , and she cry out piteously ; again the same action , the same result . Sylvia lay her mother down , and stretch out her arm for her child , hush it , and moan over it . 

 ' so far so good ! ' say Dr Morgan to himself . ' but where be the husband ? he ought to be here . ' he go down - stair to make inquiry for Philip ; that poor young creature , about whose health he have never feel thoroughly satisfied since the fever after her confinement , be in an anxious condition , and with an inevitable shock await she . her husband ought to be with she , and support she to bear it . 

 Dr Morgan go into the shop . hester alone be there . Coulson have go to his comfortable dinner at his well - order house , with his common - place wife . if he have feel anxious about Philip 's look and strange disappearance , he have also manage to account for they in some indifferent way . 

 hester be alone with the shop - boy ; few people come in during the universal monkshaven dinner - hour . she be rest her head on her hand , and puzzle and distress about many thing all that be imply by the proceeding of the evening before between Philip and Sylvia ; and that be confirm by Philip 's miserable look and strange abstract way to - day . oh ! how easy hester would have find it to make he happy ! not merely how easy , but what happiness it would have be to she to merge she every wish into the one great object of fulfil his will . to she , an on - looker , the course of married life , which should lead to perfect happiness , seem to plain ! alas ! it be often so ! and the resist force which make all such harmony and delight impossible be not recognize by the bystander , hardly by the actor . but if these resist force be only superficial , or constitutional , they be but the necessary discipline here , and do not radically affect the love which will make all thing right in heaven . 

 some glimmering of this latter comfort truth shed its light on Hester 's troubled thought from time to time . but again , how easy would it have be to she to tread the maze that lead to Philip 's happiness ; and how difficult it seem to the wife he have choose ! 

 she be arouse by Dr Morgan 's voice . 

 ' so both Coulson and Hepburn have leave the shop to your care , hester . I want Hepburn , though ; his wife be in a very anxious state . where be he ? can you tell I ? ' 

 ' Sylvia in an anxious state ! I have   not see she to - day , but last night she look as well as could be . ' 

 ' ay , ay ; but many a thing happen in four - and - twenty hour . her mother be die , may be dead by this time ; and her husband should be there with she . can not   you send for he ? ' 

 ' I do not   know where he be , ' say hester . ' he go off from here all on a sudden , when there be all the market - folk in t ' shop ; I think he would maybe go to John Foster 's about th ' money , for they be pay a deal in . I will send there and inquire . ' 

 no ! the messenger bring back word that he have not be see at their bank all morning . further inquiry be make by the anxious hester , by the doctor , by Coulson ; all they could learn be that Phoebe have see he pass the kitchen window about eleven o'clock , when she be peel the potato for dinner ; and two lad play on the quay - side thought they have see he among a group of sailor ; but these latter , as far as they could be identify , have no knowledge of his appearance among they . 

 before night the whole town be excited about his disappearance . before night Bell Robson have go to her long home . and Sylvia still lie quiet and tearless , apparently more unmoved than any other creature by the event of the day , and the strange vanishing of her husband . 

 the only thing she seem to care for be her baby ; she hold it tight in her arm , and Dr Morgan bid they leave it there , its touch might draw the desire tear into her weary , sleepless eye , and charm the aching pain out of they . 

 they be afraid lest she should inquire for her husband , whose non - appearance at such a time of sorrow to his wife must   they think   seem strange to she . and night draw on while they be all in this state . she have go back to her own room without a word when they have desire she to do so ; caress her child in her arm , and sit down on the first chair she come to , with a heavy sigh , as if even this slight bodily exertion have be too much for she . they see her eye turn towards the door every time it be open , and they think it be with anxious expectation of one who could not be find , though many be seek for he in all probable place . 

 when night come some one have to tell she of her husband 's disappearance ; and Dr Morgan be the person who undertake this . 

 he come into her room about nine o'clock ; her baby be sleep in her arm ; she herself pale as death , still silent and tearless , though strangely watchful of gesture and sound , and probably cognizant of more than they imagine . 

 ' well , Mrs. Hepburn , ' say he , as cheerfully as he could , ' I should advise your go to bed early ; for I fancy your husband will not   come home to - night . some journey or other , that perhaps Coulson can explain well than I can , will most likely keep he away till to - morrow . it be very unfortunate that he should be away at such a sad time as this , as I be sure he will feel when he return ; but we must make the good of it . ' 

 he watch she to see the effect of his word . 

 she sigh , that be all . he still remain a little while . she lift her head up a little and ask , 

 ' how long do yo ' think she be unconscious , doctor ? could she hear thing , think yo ' , afore she fall into that strange kind o ' slumber ? ' 

 ' I can not tell , ' say he , shake his head . ' be she breathe in that hard snore kind of way when you leave she this morning ? ' 

 ' yes , I think so ; I can not tell , so much have happen . ' 

 ' when you come back to she , after your breakfast , I think you say she be in much the same position ? ' 

 ' yes , and yet I may be tell yo ' lie ; if I could but think : but it be my head as be ache so ; doctor , I wish yo'd go , for I need be alone , I be so mazed . ' 

 ' good - night , then , for you be a wise woman , I see , and mean to go to bed , and have a good night with baby there . ' 

 but he go down to Phoebe , and tell she to go in from time to time , and see how her mistress be . 

 he find Hester Rose and the old servant together ; both have be cry , both be evidently in great trouble about the death and the mystery of the day . 

 hester ask if she might go up and see Sylvia , and the doctor give his leave , talk meanwhile with Phoebe over the kitchen fire . hester come down again without see Sylvia . the door of the room be bolt , and everything quiet inside . 

 ' do she know where her husband be , think you ? ' ask the doctor at this account of hester 's . ' she be not anxious about he at any rate : or else the shock of her mother 's death have be too much for she . we must hope for some change in the morning ; a good fit of crying , or a fidget about her husband , would be more natural . good - night to you both , ' and off he go . 

 Phoebe and Hester avoid look at each other at these word . both be conscious of the probability of something have go seriously wrong between the husband and wife . hester have the recollection of the previous night , Phoebe the untasted breakfast of to - day to go upon . 

 she speak first . 

 ' a just wish he would come home to still folk ' tongue . it need niver ha ' be know if t ' old lady have not   die this day of all other . it be such a thing for t ' shop t ' have one o ' t ' partner missin ' , an ' no one for t ' know what be come on he . it niver happen I ' foster ' day , that be a ' I know . ' 

 ' he will maybe come back yet , ' say hester . ' it be not so very late . ' 

 ' it be market day , and a ' , ' continue Phoebe , ' just as if iverythe mun go wrong together ; an ' a ' t ' country customers'll go back wi ' fine tale I ' their mouth , as Measter Hepburn be stray an ' missin ' just like a beast o ' some kind . ' 

 ' Hark ! be not   that a step ? ' say hester suddenly , as a footfall sound in the now quiet street ; but it pass the door , and the hope that have arise on its approach fall as the sound die away .

' thou'd good go thy way home ; a shall stay up , for it be not seemly for we a ' t ' go to our bed , an ' a corpse in t ' house ; an ' Nancy , as might ha ' watch , be go to her bed this hour past , like a lazy boot as she be . a can hear , too , if t ' measter do come home ; tho ' a'll be bind he wunnot ; choose wheere he be , he will be I ' bed by now , for it be well on to eleven .  heavily and hopelessly do they all meet in the morning . no news of Philip , no change in Sylvia ; an unceasing flow of angling and conjecture and gossip radiating from the shop into the town . 

 hester could have entreat Coulson on her knee to cease from repeat the detail of a story of which every word touch on a raw place in her sensitive heart ; moreover , when they talk together so eagerly , she could not hear the come footstep on the pavement without . 

 once some one hit very near the truth in a chance remark .

T ' doctor say if she could but cry , she would come to a juster comprehension of thing . ' 

 ' and what do John and Jeremiah Foster say to it all ? ' 

 ' they be down here many a time in t ' day to ask if he be come back , or how she be ; for they make a deal on them both . they be go t ' attend t ' funeral to - morrow , and have give order as t ' shop be to be shut up in t ' morning . ' 

 to the surprise of every one , Sylvia , who have never leave her room since the night of her mother 's death , and be suppose to be almost unconscious of all that be go on in the house , declare her intention of follow her mother to the grave . no one could do more than remonstrate : no one have sufficient authority to interfere with she . Dr Morgan even think that she might possibly be rouse to tear by the occasion ; only he beg Hester to go with she , that she might have the solace of some woman 's company . 

 she go through the great part of the ceremony in the same hard , unmove manner in which she have receive everything for day past . 

 but on look up once , as they form round the open grave , she see Kester , in his Sunday clothe , with a bit of new crape round his hat , cry as if his heart would break over the coffin of his good , kind mistress . 

 his evident distress , the unexpected sight , suddenly loose the fountain of Sylvia 's tear , and her sob grow so terrible that hester fear she would not be able to remain until the end of the funeral . but she struggle hard to stay till the last , and then she make an effort to go round by the place where Kester stand . 

 ' come and see I , ' be all she could say for crying : and Kester only nod his head he could not speak a word . 




 CHAPTER xxxvi 

 MYSTERIOUS tiding 

 that very evening Kester come , humbly knock at the kitchen - door . Phoebe open it . he ask to see Sylvia . 

 ' a know not if she will see thee , ' say Phoebe . ' there be no makin ' she out ; sometimes she be for one thing , sometimes she be for another . ' 

 ' she bid I come and see she , ' say Kester . ' only this mornin ' , at missus ' buryin ' , she tell I to come . ' 

 so Phoebe go off to inform Sylvia that Kester be there ; and return with the desire that he would walk into the parlour . an instant after he be go , Phoebe hear he return , and carefully shut the two door of communication between the kitchen and sitting - room . 

 Sylvia be in the latter when Kester come in , hold her baby close to she ; indeed , she seldom let it go now - a - day to any one else , make Nancy 's place quite a sinecure , much to Phoebe 's indignation . 

 Sylvia 's face be shrink , and white , and thin ; her lovely eye alone retain the youthful , almost childlike , expression . she go up to Kester , and shake his horny hand , she herself tremble all over . 

 ' do not   talk to I of she , ' she say hastily . ' I can not stand it . it be a blessing for she to be go , but , oh ' 

 she begin to cry , and then cheer herself up , and swallow down her sob .

 Kester speak .

 by - and - by Kester come back . it seem as though Sylvia have never stir ; she look eagerly at he , but do not speak . 

 ' he go away I ' Rob Mason 's mail - cart , he as tak 's t ' letter to Hartlepool . T ' lieutenant   as they can ' he down at t ' King 's arm ; they be as proud on his uniform as if it have be a new - paint sign to swing o'er their door , t ' lieutenant have reckon upo ' stayin ' long wi ' them ; but he go out betime o ' Tuesday morn ' , an ' come back a ' ruffle up , an pay his bill pay for his breakfast , though he touch noane on it an ' go off I ' Rob postman 's mail - cart , as start reg'lar at ten o'clock . Corneys have be theere askin ' for he , an ' makin ' a piece o ' work , as he niver go near em ; and they be cousin . niver a one among them know as he be here as far as a could mak ' out . ' 

 ' thank yo ' , Kester , ' say Sylvia , fall back in her chair , as if all the energy that have keep she stiff and upright be go now that her anxiety be relieve . 

 she be silent for a long time ; her eye shut , her cheek lay on her child 's head . Kester speak next . 

 ' a think it be pretty clear as they'n niver meet . but it be a ' t ' more wonder where thy husband 's go to . Thee and he have word about it , and thou tell he thy mind , thou say ? ' 

 ' yes , ' say Sylvia , not move . ' I be afeared lest mother know what I say to he , there , where she be go to I am- ' the tear fill her shut eye , and come softly overflow down her cheek ; ' and yet it be true , what I say , I can not forgive he ; he be just spoil my life , and I be not one - and - twenty yet , and he know how wretched , how very wretched , I be . a word fra ' he would ha ' mend it a ' ; and Charley have bid he speak the word , and give I his faithful love , and Philip see my heart ache day after day , and niver let on as he I be mourn for be alive , and have send I word as he would keep true to I , as I be to do to he . ' 

 ' a wish a'd be theere ; a'd ha ' fell he to t ' ground , ' say Kester , clench his stiff , hard hand with indignation . 

 Sylvia be silent again : pale and weary she sate , her eye still shut . 

 then she say , 

 ' yet he be so good to mother ; and mother love he so . oh , Kester ! ' lift herself up , open her great wistful eye , ' it be well for folk as can die ; they be spar a deal o ' misery . ' 

 ' ay ! ' say he . ' but there be folk as one ' ud like to keep fra ' shirkin ' their misery . think yo ' now as Philip be livin ' ? ' 

 Sylvia shiver all over , and hesitate before she reply . 

 ' I dunnot know . I say such thing ; he deserve them all ' 

 ' well , well , lass ! ' say Kester , sorry that he have ask the question which be produce so much emotion of one kind or another . ' neither thee nor I can tell ; we can neither help nor hinder , seein ' as he be ta'en hissel ' off out on our sight , we would well not think on he . A'll try an ' tell thee some news , if a can think on it wi ' my mind so full . Thou know Haytersbank folk ha ' flitted , and t ' oud place be empty ? ' 

 ' yes ! ' say Sylvia , with the indifference of one weary out with feeling . 

 ' a only tell yo ' t ' account like for I bein ' at a loose end I ' monkshaven . my sister , she as live at Dale End an ' be a widow , have come int ' town to live ; an ' a'm lodge wi ' her , an ' jobbin ' about . A'm gettin ' pretty well to do , an ' a'm noane far t ' seek , an ' a'm go now : only first a just want for t ' say as a'm thy old friend , a reckon , and if a can do a turn for thee , or go an errand , like as a've   do to - day , or if it be any comfort to talk a bit to one who be know thy life from a babby , why yo've   only t ' send for I , an ' a'd come if it be twenty mile . A'm lodgin ' at Peggy Dawson 's , t ' lath and plaster cottage at t ' right hand o ' t ' bridge , a ' among t ' new house , as they be thinkin ' o ' buildin ' near t ' sea : no one can miss it . ' 

 he stand up and shake hand with she . as he do so , he look at her sleep baby . 

 ' she be liker yo ' than he . a think a'll say , God bless she . ' 

 with the heavy sound of his out - go footstep , baby awake . she ought before this time to have be asleep in her bed , and the disturbance make her cry fretfully . 

 ' hush thee , darling , hush thee ! ' murmur her mother ; ' there be no one leave to love I but thee , and I can not stand thy weeping , my pretty one . hush thee , my babe , hush thee ! ' 

 she whisper soft in the little one 's ear as she take she upstairs to bed . 

 about three week after the miserable date of Bell Robson 's death and Philip 's disappearance , Hester Rose receive a letter from he . she know the writing on the address well ; and it make her tremble so much that it be many minute before she dare to open it , and make herself acquainted with the fact it might disclose . 

 but she need not have fear ; there be no fact tell , unless the vague date of ' London ' might be something to learn . even that much might have be find out by the post - mark , only she have be too much take by surprise to examine it . 

 it run as follow : 


 ' dear hester , 

 ' tell those whom it may concern , that I have leave Monkshaven for ever . no one need trouble themselves about I ; I be provide for . please to make my humble apology to my kind friend , the Messrs Foster , and to my partner , William Coulson . please to accept of my love , and to join the same to your mother . please to give my particular and respectful duty and kind love to my aunt Isabella Robson . her daughter Sylvia know what I have always feel , and shall always feel , for she well than I can ever put into language , so I send she no message ; God bless and keep my child . you must all look on I as one dead ; as I be to you , and maybe shall soon be in reality . 

 ' your affectionate and obedient friend to command , 

 ' PHILIP HEPBURN . 

 ' P.S. oh , Hester ! for God 's sake and mine , look after   ' my wife , ' scratch out   Sylvia and my child . I think Jeremiah Foster will help you to be a friend to they . this be the last solemn request of P. H. she be but very young . ' 


 hester read this letter again and again , till her heart catch the echo of its hopelessness , and sink within she . she put it in her pocket , and reflect upon it all the day long as she serve in the shop . 

 the customer find she as gentle , but far more inattentive than usual . she think that in the evening she would go across the bridge , and consult with the two good old brother Foster . but something occur to put off the fulfilment of this plan . 

 that same morning Sylvia have precede she , with no one to consult , because consultation would have require previous confidence , and confidence would have necessitate such a confession about Kinraid as it be most difficult for Sylvia to make . the poor young wife yet feel that some step must be take by she ; and what it be to be she could not imagine . 

 she have no home to go to ; for as Philip be go away , she remain where she be only on sufferance ; she do not know what mean of livelihood she have ; she be willing to work , nay , would be thankful to take up her old life of country labour ; but with her baby , what could she do ? 

 in this dilemma , the recollection of the old man 's kindly speech and offer of assistance , make , it be true , half in joke , at the end of her wedding visit , come into her mind ; and she resolve to go and ask for some of the friendly counsel and assistance then offer . 

 it would be the first time of she go out since her mother 's funeral , and she dread the effort on that account . more even than on that account do she shrink from go into the street again . she could not get over the impression that Kinraid must be linger near ; and she distrust herself so much that it be a positive terror to think of meet he again . she feel as though , if she but catch a sight of he , the glitter of his uniform , or hear his well - know voice in only a distant syllable of talk , her heart would stop , and she should die from very fright of what would come next . or rather so she feel , and so she think before she take her baby in her arm , as Nancy give it to she after put on its out - of - door attire . 

 with it in her arm she be protect , and the whole current of her thought be change . the infant be wail and suffer with its teething , and the mother 's heart be so occupy in soothe and console her moaning child , that the dangerous quay - side and the bridge be pass almost before she be aware ; nor do she notice the eager curiosity and respectful attention of those she meet who recognize she even through the heavy veil which form part of the draping mourning provide for she by Hester and Coulson , in the first unconscious day after her mother 's death . 

 though public opinion as yet reserve its verdict upon Philip 's disappearance warn possibly by Kinraid 's story against hasty decision and judgment in such time as those of war and general disturbance yet every one agree that no more pitiful fate could have befall Philip 's wife . 

 mark out by her strike beauty as an object of admire interest even in those day when she sate in girlhood 's smile peace by her mother at the Market Cross her father have lose his life in a popular cause , and ignominious as the manner of his death might be , he be look upon as a martyr to his zeal in avenge the wrong of his townsman ; Sylvia have marry amongst they too , and her quiet daily life be well known to they ; and now her husband have be carry off from her side just on the very day when she need his comfort most . 

 for the general opinion be that Philip have be ' carry off ' in seaport town such occurrence be not uncommon in those day either by land - crimp or water - crimps . 

 so Sylvia be treat with silent reverence , as one sorely afflict , by all the unheeded people she meet in her faltering walk to Jeremiah Foster 's . 

 she have calculate her time so as to fall in with he at his dinner hour , even though it oblige she to go to his own house rather than to the bank where he and his brother spend all the business hour of the day . 

 Sylvia be so nearly exhausted by the length of her walk and the weight of her baby , that all she could do when the door be open be to totter into the near seat , sit down , and begin to cry . 

 in an instant kind hand be about she , loosen her heavy cloak , offer to relieve she of her child , who cling to she all the more firmly , and some one be press a glass of wine against her lip . 

 ' no , sir , I can not take it ! wine allay give I th ' headache ; if I might have just a drink o ' water . thank you , ma'am '   to the respectable - looking old servant , ' I be well enough now ; and perhaps , sir , I might speak a word with yo ' , for it be that I have   come for . ' 

 ' it be a pity , Sylvia Hepburn , as thee didst not come to I at the bank , for it be be a long toil for thee all this way in the heat , with thy child . but if there be aught I can do or say for thee , thou hast but to name it , I be sure . Martha ! wilt thou relieve she of her child while she come with I into the parlour ? ' 

 but the wilful little Bella stoutly refuse to go to any one , and Sylvia be not willing to part with she , tired though she be . 

 so the baby be carry into the parlour , and much of her after - life depend on this trivial fact . 

 once instal in the easy - chair , and face to face with Jeremiah , Sylvia do not know how to begin . 

 Jeremiah see this , and kindly give her time to recover herself , by pull out his great gold watch , and let the seal dangle before the child 's eye , almost within reach of the child 's eager little finger . 

 ' she favour you a deal , ' say he , at last . ' More than her father , ' he go on , purposely introduce Philip 's name , so as to break the ice ; for he rightly conjecture she have come to speak to he about something connect with her husband . 

 still Sylvia say nothing ; she be choke down tear and shyness , and unwillingness to take as confidant a man of whom she know so little , on such slight ground   as she now feel it to be   as the little kindly speech with which she have be dismiss from that house the last time that she enter it . 

 ' it be no use keep yo ' , sir , ' she break out at last . ' it be about Philip as I come to speak . do yo ' know any thing whatsomever about he ? he niver have a chance o ' say anything , I know ; but maybe he be write ? ' 

 ' not a line , my poor young woman ! ' say Jeremiah , hastily put an end to that vain idea . 

 ' then he be either dead or go away for iver , ' she whisper . ' I mun be both feyther and mother to my child . ' 

 ' oh ! thee must not give it up , ' reply he . ' many a one be carry off to the war , or to the tender o ' man - o'-war ; and then they turn out to be unfit for service , and be send home . Philip will come back before the year 's out ; thee'll see that . ' 

 ' no ; he will niver come back . and I be not sure as I should iver wish he t ' come back , if I could but know what be go wi ' he . Yo ' see , sir , though I be sore set again ' he , I should not   like harm to happen he . ' 

 ' there be something behind all this that I do not understand . can thee tell I what it be ? ' 

 ' I must , sir , if yo're to help I wi ' your counsel ; and I come up here to ask for it . ' 

 another long pause , during which Jeremiah make a feint of play with the child , who dance and shout with tantalized impatience at not be able to obtain possession of the seal , and at length stretch out her soft round little arm to go to the owner of the coveted possession . surprise at this action rouse Sylvia , and she make some comment upon it . 

 ' I niver know her t ' go to any one afore . I hope she will not be troublesome to yo ' , sir ? ' 

 the old man , who have often long for a child of his own in day go by , be highly pleased by this mark of baby 's confidence , and almost forget , in try to strengthen her regard by all the win wile in his power , how her poor mother be still linger over some painful story which she could not bring herself to tell . 

 ' I be afeared of speak wrong again ' any one , sir . and mother be so fond o ' Philip ; but he keep something from I as would ha ' make I a different woman , and some one else , happen , a different man . I be troth - plight wi ' Kinraid the specksioneer , he as be cousin to th ' Corneys o ' Moss Brow , and come back lieutenant I ' t ' navy last Tuesday three week , after ivery one have think he dead and go these three year . ' 

 she pause . 

 ' well ? ' say Jeremiah , with interest ; although his attention appear to be divide between the mother 's story and the eager playfulness of the baby on his knee .

 her white face be redden , her eye flash at this point of her story .

Yo ' know about feyther 's death , and how friendless mother and I be leave ? and so I marry he ; for he be a good friend to we then , and I be daze like wi ' sorrow , and could see naught else to do for mother . he be allay very tender and good to she , for sure . ' 

 again a long pause of silent recollection , break by one or two deep sigh . 

 ' if I go on , sir , now , I mun ask yo ' to promise as yo'll niver tell . I do so need some one to tell I what I ought to do , and I be lead here , like , else I would ha ' die wi ' it all within my tooth . Yo'll promise , sir ? ' 

 Jeremiah Foster look in her face , and see the wistful , eager look , he be touch almost against his judgment into give the promise require ; she go on . 

 ' upon a Tuesday morning , three week ago , I think , tho ' for t ' matter o ' time it might ha ' be three year , Kinraid come home ; come back for t ' claim I as his wife , and I be we d to Philip ! ' 

 she stop speak ; her story seem to she to end there . but her listener say , after a pause , 

 ' it be a cruel wrong , I grant thee that ; but thy oath be a sin , and thy word be evil , my poor lass . what happen next ? ' 

 ' I do not   justly remember , ' she say , wearily . ' Kinraid go away , and mother cry out ; and I go to she . she be asleep , I think , so I lie down by she , to wish I be dead , and to think on what would come on my child if I die ; and Philip come in softly , and I make as if I be asleep ; and that be t ' very last as I have   iver see or heared of he . ' 

 Jeremiah Foster groan as she end her story . then he pull himself up , and say , in a cheerful tone of voice , 

 ' he will come back , Sylvia Hepburn . he will think well of it : never fear ! ' 

 ' I fear his come back ! ' say she . ' that be what I be fear on ; I would wish as I know on his well - do I ' some other place ; but he and I can niver live together again . ' 

 ' Nay , ' plead Jeremiah . ' thee art sorry what thee say ; thee be sore put about , or thee would not   have say it . ' 

 he be try to be a peace - maker , and to heal over conjugal difference ; but he do not go deep enough .

' I be too deeply wrong to be " put about " ; that would go off wi ' a night 's sleep . it be only the thought of mother   she be dead and happy , and know nought of all this , I trust   that come between I and hat Philip . I be not sorry for what I say . ' 

 Jeremiah have never meet with any one so frank and undisguise in expression of wrong feeling , and he scarcely know what to say . 

 he look extremely grieve , and not a little shocked . so pretty and delicate a young creature to use such strong relentless language ! 

 she seem to read his thought , for she make answer to they .

I can not   think o ' that for remember how I have   suffer ; and he know how miserable I be , and might ha ' clear my misery away wi ' a word ; and he hold his peace , and now it be too late !  she be cry before she have end this speech , and see her tear , the child begin to cry too , stretch out its little arm to go back to its mother . the hard stony look on her face melt away into the soft , tender love as she clasp the little one to she , and try to soothe its frightened sob . 

 a bright thought come into the old man 's mind . 

 he have be take a complete dislike to she till her pretty way with her baby show he that she have a heart of flesh within she . 

 ' poor little one ! ' say he , ' thy mother have need love thee , for she be deprive thee of thy father 's love . Thou'rt half - way to be an orphan ; yet I can not call thee one of the fatherless to whom God will be a father . Thou'rt a desolate babe , thou may'st well cry ; thine earthly parent have forsake thee , and I know not if the Lord will take thee up . ' 

 Sylvia look up at he affright ; hold her baby tight to she , she exclaim . 

 ' do not   speak so , sir ! it be cursing , sir ! I have not   forsake she ! oh , sir ! those be awful saying . ' 

 ' Thee hast swear never to forgive thy husband , nor to live with he again . dost thee know that by the law of the land , he may claim his child ; and then thou wilt have to forsake it , or to be forswear ? poor little maiden ! ' continue he , once more lure the baby to he with the temptation of the watch and chain . 

 Sylvia think for a while before speak . then she say , 

 ' I can not tell what way to take . while I think my head be craze . it be a cruel turn he do I ! ' 

 ' it be . I could not   have think he guilty of such baseness . ' 

 this acquiescence , which be perfectly honest on Jeremiah 's part , almost take Sylvia by surprise . why might she not hate one who have be both cruel and base in his treatment of she ? and yet she recoil from the application of such hard term by another to Philip , by a cool - judging and indifferent person , as she esteem Jeremiah to be . from some inscrutable turn in her thought , she begin to defend he , or at least to palliate the harsh judgment which she herself have be the first to pronounce . 

 ' he be so tender to mother ; she be dearly fond on he ; he niver spar aught he could do for she , else I would niver ha ' married he . ' 

 ' he be a good and kind - hearted lad from the time he be fifteen . and I never find he out in any falsehood , no more do my brother . ' 

 ' but it be all the same as a lie , ' say Sylvia , swiftly change her ground , ' to leave I to think as Charley be dead , when he know all t ' time he be alive . ' 

 ' it be . it be a self - seek lie ; put thee to pain to get his own end . and the end of it have be that he be drive forth like Cain . ' 

 ' I niver tell he to go , sir . ' 

 ' but thy word send he forth , Sylvia . ' 

 ' I can not unsay they , sir ; and I believe as I should say they again . ' 

 but she say this as one who rather hop for a contradiction . 

 all Jeremiah reply , however , be , ' poor wee child ! ' in a pitiful tone , address to the baby . 

 Sylvia 's eye fill with tear . 

 ' oh , sir , I will do anything as iver yo ' can tell I for she . that be what I come for t ' ask yo ' . I know I mun not stay theere , and Philip go away ; and I dunnot know what to do : and I will do aught , only I must keep her wi ' I . Whativer can I do , sir ? ' 

 Jeremiah think it over for a minute or two . then he reply , 

 ' I must have time to think . I must talk it over with brother John . ' 

 ' but you have   give I yo'r word , sir ! ' exclaim she . 

 ' I have give thee my word never to tell any one of what have pass between thee and thy husband , but I must take counsel with my brother as to what be to be do with thee and thy child , now that thy husband have leave the shop . ' 

 this be say so gravely as almost to be a reproach , and he get up , as a sign that the interview be end .
