 he give the baby back to its mother ; but not without a solemn blessing , so solemn that , to Sylvia 's superstitious and excited mind , it undo the terror of what she have esteem to be a curse . 

 ' the Lord bless thee and keep thee ! the Lord make his face to shine upon thee ! ' 

 all the way down the hill - side , Sylvia keep kiss the child , and whisper to its unconscious ear , 

 ' I will love thee for both , my treasure , I will . I will hap thee round wi ' my love , so as thou shall niver need a feyther 's . ' 




 CHAPTER XXXVII 

 BEREAVEMENT 

 hester have be prevent by her mother 's indisposition from take Philip 's letter to the Fosters , to hold a consultation with they over its content . 

 Alice Rose be slowly fail , and the long day which she have to spend alone tell much upon her spirit , and consequently upon her health . 

 all this come out in the conversation which ensue after read Hepburn 's letter in the little parlour at the bank on the day after Sylvia have have her confidential interview with Jeremiah Foster . 

 he be a true man of honour , and never so much as allude to her visit to he ; but what she have then tell he influence he very much in the formation of the project which he propose to his brother and hester . 

 he recommend she remain where she be , live still in the house behind the shop ; for he think within himself that she might have exaggerate the effect of her word upon Philip ; that , after all , it might have be some cause totally disconnected with they , which have blot out her husband 's place among the man of Monkshaven ; and that it would be so much easy for both to resume their natural relation , both towards each other and towards the world , if Sylvia remain where her husband have leave she in an expectant attitude , so to speak . 

 Jeremiah Foster question Hester straitly about her letter : whether she have make known its content to any one . no , not to any one . neither to her mother nor to William Coulson ? no , to neither . 

 she look at he as she reply to his inquiry , and he look at she , each wonder if the other could be in the least aware that a conjugal quarrel might be at the root of the dilemma in which they be place by Hepburn 's disappearance . 

 but neither Hester , who have witness the misunderstanding between the husband and wife on the evening , before the morning on which Philip go away , nor Jeremiah Foster , who have learn from Sylvia the true reason of her husband 's disappearance , give the slight reason to the other to think that they each suppose they have a clue to the reason of Hepburn 's sudden departure . 

 what Jeremiah Foster , after a night 's consideration , have to propose be this ; that Hester and her mother should come and occupy the house in the market - place , conjointly with Sylvia and her child . hester 's interest in the shop be by this time acknowledge . Jeremiah have make over to she so much of his share in the business , that she have a right to be consider as a kind of partner ; and she have long be the superintendent of that department of good which be exclusively devoted to woman . so her daily presence be requisite for more reason than one . 

 yet her mother 's health and spirit be such as to render it unadvisable that the old woman should be too much leave alone ; and Sylvia 's devotion to her own mother seem to point she out as the very person who could be a gentle and tender companion to Alice Rose during those hour when her own daughter would necessarily be engage in the shop . 

 many desirable object seem to be gain by this removal of Alice : an occupation be provide for Sylvia , which would detain she in the place where her husband have leave she , and where   Jeremiah Foster fairly expect in spite of his letter   he be likely to come back to find she ; and Alice Rose , the early love of one of the brother , the old friend of the other , would be well care for , and under her daughter 's immediate supervision during the whole of the time that she be occupy in the shop . 

 Philip 's share of the business , augment by the money which he have put in from the legacy of his old Cumberland uncle , would bring in profit enough to support Sylvia and her child in ease and comfort until that time , which they all anticipate , when he should return from his mysterious wander mysterious , whether his go forth have be voluntary or involuntary . 

 thus far be settle ; and Jeremiah Foster go to tell Sylvia of the plan . 

 she be too much a child , too entirely unaccustomed to any independence of action , to do anything but leave herself in his hand . her very confession , make to he the day before , when she seek his counsel , seem to place she at his disposal . otherwise , she have have notion of the possibility of a free country life once more how provide for and arrange she hardly know ; but Haytersbank be to let , and Kester disengage , and it have just seem possible that she might have to return to her early home , and to her old life . she know that it would take much money to stock the farm again , and that her hand be tie from much useful activity by the love and care she owe to her baby . but still , somehow , she hop and she fancy , till Jeremiah Foster 's measure word and carefully - arrange plan make she silently relinquish her green , breezy vision . 

 Hester , too , have her own private rebellion hush into submission by her gentle piety . if Sylvia have be able to make Philip happy , Hester could have feel lovingly and almost gratefully towards she ; but Sylvia have fail in this . 

 Philip have be make unhappy , and be drive forth a wanderer into the wide world never to come back ! and his last word to Hester , the postscript of his letter , contain the very pith of it , be to ask she to take charge and care of the wife whose want of love towards he have uproot he from the place where he be value and honour . 

 it cost Hester many a struggle and many a self - reproach before she could make herself feel what she see all along that in everything Philip treat she like a sister . but even a sister might well be indignant if she see her brother 's love disregard and slight , and his life embitter by the thoughtless conduct of a wife ! still hester fight against herself , and for Philip 's sake she seek to see the good in Sylvia , and she strove to love she as well as to take care of she . 

 with the baby , of course , the case be different . without thought or struggle , or reason , every one love the little girl . Coulson and his buxom wife , who be childless , be never weary of make much of she . hester 's happy hour be spend with that little child . Jeremiah Foster almost look upon she as his own from the day when she honour he by yield to the temptation of the chain and seal , and come to his knee ; not a customer to the shop but know the smile child 's sad history , and many a country - woman would save a rosy - cheeked apple from out her store that autumn to bring it on next market - day for ' Philip Hepburn 's baby , as have lose its father , bless it . ' 

 even stern Alice Rose be graciously inclined towards the little Bella ; and though her idea of the number of the elect be grow narrower and narrower every day , she would have be loth to exclude the innocent little child , that stroke she wrinkle cheek so softly every night in return for her blessing , from the few that should be save . Nay , for the child 's sake , she relent towards the mother ; and strove to have Sylvia rescue from the many castaway with fervent prayer , or , as she phrase it , ' wrestle with the Lord ' . 

 Alice have a sort of instinct that the little child , so tenderly love by , so fondly love , the mother whose ewe - lamb she be , could not be even in heaven without yearn for the creature she have love well on earth ; and the old woman believe that this be the principal reason for her prayer for Sylvia ; but unconsciously to herself , Alice Rose be touch by the filial attention she constantly receive from the young mother , whom she believe to be foredoom to condemnation . 

 Sylvia rarely go to church or chapel , nor do she read she Bible ; for though she speak little of her ignorance , and would fain , for her child 's sake , have remedie it now it be too late , she have lose what little fluency of read she have ever have , and could only make out her word with much spelling and difficulty . so the take her Bible in hand would have be a mere form ; though of this Alice Rose know nothing . 

 no one know much of what be pass in Sylvia ; she do not know herself . sometimes in the night she would waken , cry , with a terrible sense of desolation ; every one who love she , or whom she have love , have vanish out of her life ; every one but her child , who lie in her arm , warm and soft . 

 but then Jeremiah Foster 's word come upon she ; word that she have take for cursing at the time ; and she would so gladly have have some clue by which to penetrate the darkness of the unknown region from whence both blessing and cursing come , and to know if she have indeed do something which should cause her sin to be visit on that soft , sweet , innocent darling . 

 if any one would teach she to read ! if any one would explain to she the hard word she hear in church or chapel , so that she might find out the meaning of sin and godliness ! word that have only pass over the surface of her mind till now ! for her child 's sake she should like to do the will of God , if she only know what that be , and how to be work out in her daily life . 

 but there be no one she dare confess her ignorance to and ask information from . Jeremiah Foster have speak as if her child , sweet little merry Bella , with a love word and a kiss for every one , be to suffer heavily for the just and true word she wronged and indignant mother have speak . Alice always speak as if there be no hope for she ; and blame she , nevertheless , for not use the mean of grace that it be not in her power to avail herself of . 

 and Hester , that Sylvia would fain have love for her uniform gentleness and patience with all around she , seem so cold in her unruffled and undemonstrative behaviour ; and moreover , Sylvia feel that hester blame her perpetual silence regard Philip 's absence without know how bitter a cause Sylvia have for cast he off . 

 the only person who seem to have pity upon she be Kester ; and his pity be show in look rather than word ; for when he come to see she , which he do from time to time , by a kind of mutual tacit consent , they speak but little of former day . 

 he be still lodge with his sister , widow Dobson , work at odd job , some of which take he into the country for week at a time . but on his return to monkshaven he be sure to come and see she and the little Bella ; indeed , when his employment be in the immediate neighbourhood of the town , he never allow a week to pass away without a visit . 

 there be not much conversation between he and Sylvia at such time . they skim over the surface of the small event in which both take an interest ; only now and then a sudden glance , a checked speech , tell each that there be deep not forget , although they be never mention . 

 twice Sylvia below her breath have ask Kester , just as she be hold the door open for his departure , if anything have ever be hear of Kinraid since his one night 's visit to monkshaven : each time   and there be an interval of some month between the inquiry   the answer have be simply , no . 

 to no one else would Sylvia ever have name his name . but indeed she have not the chance , have she wish it ever so much , of ask any question about he from any one likely to know . the Corneys have leave Moss Brow at Martinmas , and go many mile away towards Horncastle . Bessy Corney , it be true be marry and leave behind in the neighbourhood ; but with her Sylvia have never be intimate ; and what girlish friendship there might have be between they have cool very much at the time of Kinraid 's suppose death three year before . 

 one day before Christmas in this year , 1798 , Sylvia be call into the shop by Coulson , who , with his assistant , be busy undo the bale of winter good supply to they from the West Riding , and other place . he be look at a fine irish poplin dress - piece when Sylvia answer to his call . 

 ' here ! do you know this again ? ' ask he , in the cheerful tone of one sure of give pleasure . 

 ' no ! have I iver see it afore ? ' 

 ' not this , but one for all t ' world like it . ' 

 she do not rouse up to much interest , but look at it as if try to recollect where she could have see its like . 

 ' my missus have one on at th ' party at John Foster 's last March , and yo ' admire it a deal . and Philip , he think o ' nothing but how he could get yo ' just such another , and he set a vast o ' folk agait for to meet wi ' its marrow ; and what he do just the very day afore he go away so mysterious be to write through Dawson Brothers , o ' Wakefield , to Dublin , and order that one should be weave for yo ' . Jemima have to cut a bit off her for to give he t ' exact colour . ' 

 Sylvia do not say anything but that it be very pretty , in a low voice , and then she quickly leave the shop , much to Coulson 's displeasure . 

 all the afternoon she be unusually quiet and depressed . 

 Alice Rose , sit helpless in her chair , watch she with keen eye . 

 at length , after one of Sylvia 's deep , unconscious sigh , the old woman speak : 

 ' it be religion as must comfort thee , child , as it be do many a one afore thee . ' 

 ' how ? ' say Sylvia , look up , startle to find herself an object of notice . 

 ' how ? '   the answer be not quite so ready as the precept have be .   ' read thy Bible , and thou wilt learn . ' 

 ' but I can not read , ' say Sylvia , too desperate any long to conceal her ignorance . 

 ' not read ! and thee Philip 's wife as be such a great scholar ! of a surety the way o ' this life be crook ! there be our hester , as can read as well as any minister , and Philip pass over she to go and choose a young lass as can not read her Bible . ' 

 ' be Philip and Hester ' 

 Sylvia pause , for though a new curiosity have dawn upon she , she do not know how to word her question . 

 ' many a time and oft have I see hester take comfort in her Bible when Philip be follow after thee . she know where to go for consolation . ' 

 ' I would fain read , ' say Sylvia , humbly , ' if anybody would learn I ; for perhaps it might do I good ; I be noane so happy . ' 

 her eye , as she look up at Alice 's stern countenance , be full of tear . 

 the old woman see it , and be touch , although she do not immediately show her sympathy . but she take her own time , and make no reply . 

 the next day , however , she bid Sylvia come to she , and then and there , as if her pupil have be a little child , she begin to teach Sylvia to read the first chapter of Genesis ; for all other reading but the scripture be as vanity to she , and she would not condescend to the weakness of other book . Sylvia be now , as ever , slow at book - learning ; but she be meek and desirous to be teach , and her willingness in this respect please Alice , and draw she singularly towards one who , from be a pupil , might become a convert . 

 all this time Sylvia never lose the curiosity that have be excite by the few word Alice have let drop about Hester and Philip , and by degree she approach the subject again , and have the idea then start confirm by Alice , who have no scruple in use the past experience of her own , of her daughter 's , or of any one 's life , as an instrument to prove the vanity of set the heart on anything earthly . 

 this knowledge , unsuspecte before , sink deep into Sylvia 's thought , and give she a strange interest in Hester poor hester , whose life she have so cross and blight , even by the very blighting of her own . she give Hester her own former passionate feeling for Kinraid , and wonder how she herself should have feel towards any one who have come between she and he , and wile his love away . when she remember hester 's unfailing sweetness and kindness towards herself from the very first , she could well bear the comparative coldness of her present behaviour . 

 she try , indeed , hard to win back the favour she have lose ; but the very mean she take be blunder , and only make it seem to she as if she could never again do right in hester 's eye . 

 for instance , she beg she to accept and wear the pretty poplin gown which have be Philip 's especial choice ; feel within herself as if she should never wish to put it on , and as if the good thing she could do with it be to offer it to hester . but Hester reject the proffered gift with as much hardness of manner as she be capable of assume ; and Sylvia have to carry it upstairs and lie it by for the little daughter , who , Hester say , might perhaps learn to value thing that her father have give especial thought to . 

 yet Sylvia go on try to win hester to like she once more ; it be one of her great labour , and learn to read from Hester 's mother be another . 

 Alice , indeed , in her solemn way , be become quite fond of Sylvia ; if she could not read or write , she have a deftness and gentleness of motion , a capacity for the household matter which fall into her department , that have a great effect on the old woman , and for her dear mother 's sake Sylvia have a stock of patient love ready in her heart for all the aged and infirm that fall in her way . she never think of seek they out , as she know that hester do ; but then she look up to hester as some one very remarkable for her goodness . if only she could have like she ! 

 hester try to do all she could for Sylvia ; Philip have tell she to take care of his wife and child ; but she have the conviction that Sylvia have so materially fail in her duty as to have make her husband an exile from his home a penniless wanderer , wifeless and childless , in some strange country , whose very aspect be friendless , while the cause of all live on in the comfortable home where he have place she , want for nothing an object of interest and regard to many friend with a lovely little child to give her joy for the present , and hope for the future ; while he , the poor outcast , might even lie dead by the wayside . how could Hester love Sylvia ? 

 yet they be frequent companion that ensue spring . hester be not well ; and the doctor say that the constant occupation in the shop be too much for she , and that she must , for a time at least , take daily walk into the country . 

 Sylvia use to beg to accompany she ; she and the little girl often go with hester up the valley of the river to some of the nestling farm that be hide in the more sheltered nook for hester be bid to drink milk warm from the cow ; and to go into the familiar haunt about a farm be one of the few thing in which Sylvia seem to take much pleasure . she would let little Bella toddle about while hester sate and rest : and she herself would beg to milk the cow destine to give the invalid her draught . 

 one May evening the three have be out on some such expedition ; the country side still look gray and bare , though the leaf be show on the willow and blackthorn and sloe , and by the tinkle runnel , make hidden music along the copse side , the pale delicate primrose bud be show amid their fresh , green , crinkle leaf . the lark have be sing all the afternoon , but be now drop down into their nest in the pasture field ; the air have just the sharpness in it which go along with a cloudless evening sky at that time of the year . 

 but Hester walk homeward slowly and languidly , speak no word . Sylvia notice this at first without venture to speak , for hester be one who dislike have her ailment notice . but after a while hester stand still in a sort of weary dreamy abstraction ; and Sylvia say to she , 

 ' I be afeare yo're sadly tired . maybe we have   be too far . ' 

 hester almost start . 

 ' no ! ' say she , ' it be only my headache which be bad to - night . it have be bad all day ; but since I come out it have feel just as if there be great gun booming , till I could almost pray them to be quiet . I be so weary o ' th ' sound . ' 

 she step out quickly towards home after she have say this , as if she wish for neither pity nor comment on what she have say . 




 CHAPTER XXXVIII 

 the RECOGNITION 

 far away , over sea and land , over sunny sea again , great gun be boom on that 7th of May , 1799 . 

 the Mediterranean come up with a long roar on a beach glitter white with snowy sand , and the fragment of innumerable sea - shell , delicate and shin as porcelain . look at that shore from the sea , a long ridge of upland ground , begin from an inland depth , stretch far away into the ocean on the right , till it end in a great mountainous bluff , crown with the white building of a convent slop rapidly down into the blue water at its base . 

 in the clear eastern air , the different character of the foliage that clothe the side of that sea - wash mountain might be discern from a long distance by the naked eye ; the silver gray of the olive - tree near its summit ; the heavy green and bossy form of the sycamore lower down ; break here and there by a solitary terebinth or ilex tree , of a deep green and a wide spread ; till the eye fall below on the maritime plain , edge with the white seaboard and the sandy hillock ; with here and there feathery palm - tree , either isolated or in group motionless and distinct against the hot purple air . 

 look again ; a little to the left on the sea - shore there be the white wall of a fortified town , glitter in sunlight , or black in shadow . 

 the fortification themselves run out into the sea , form a port and a haven against the wild Levantine storm ; and a lighthouse rise out of the wave to guide mariner into safety . 

 beyond this walled city , and far away to the left still , there be the same wide plain shut in by the distant rise ground , till the upland circuit come close in to the north , and the great white rock meet the deep tideless ocean with its intensity of blue colour . 

 above , the sky be literally purple with heat ; and the pitiless light smite the gazer 's weary eye as it come back from the white shore . nor do the plain country in that land offer the refuge and rest of our own soft green . the limestone rock underlie the vegetation , and give a glittering , ashen hue to all the bare patch , and even to the cultivate part which be burn up early in the year . in spring - time alone do the country look rich and fruitful ; then the corn - field of the plain show their capability of bear , ' some fifty , some an hundred fold ' ; down by the brook Kishon , flow not far from the base of the mountainous promontory to the south , there grow the broad green fig - tree , cool and fresh to look upon ; the orchard be full of glossy - leave cherry - tree ; the tall amarylli put forth crimson and yellow glory in the field , rival the pomp of King Solomon ; the daisy and the hyacinth spread their myriad flower ; the anemone , scarlet as blood , run hither and thither over the ground like dazzle flame of fire . 

 a spicy odour linger in the heated air ; it come from the multitude of aromatic flower that blossom in the early spring . later on they will have wither and fade , and the corn will have be gather , and the deep green of the eastern foliage will have assume a kind of gray - bleach tint . 

 even now in May , the hot sparkle of the everlasting sea , the terribly clear outline of all object , whether near or distant , the fierce sun right overhead , the dazzle air around , be inexpressibly weary to the english eye that keep their skilled watch , day and night , on the strongly - fortify coast - town that lie out a little to the northward of where the british ship be anchor . 

 they have keep up a flanking fire for many day in aid of those besiege in St Jean d'Acre ; and at interval have listen , impatient , to the sound of the heavy siege gun , or the sharp rattle of the french musketry . 

 in the morning , on the 7th of May , a man at the masthead of the Tigre sing out that he see ship in the offing ; and in reply to the signal that be hastily run up , he see the distant vessel hoist friendly flag . that May morning be a busy time . the besiege Turks take heart of grace ; the french outside , under the command of their great general , make hasty preparation for a more vigorous assault than all many , both vigorous and bloody , that have go before   for the siege be now at its fifty - first day , in hope of carry the town by storm before the reinforcement come by sea could arrive ; and Sir Sidney Smith , aware of Buonaparte 's desperate intention , order all the man , both sailor and marine , that could be spar from the necessity of keep up a continual flanking fire from the ship upon the French , to land , and assist the Turks and the british force already there in the defence of the old historic city . 

 Lieutenant Kinraid , who have share his captain 's daring adventure off the coast of France three year before , who have be a prisoner with he and Westley Wright , in the Temple at Paris , and have escape with they , and , through Sir Sidney 's earnest recommendation , be promote from be a warrant officer to the rank of lieutenant , receive on this day the honour from his admiral of be appoint to an especial post of danger . his heart be like a war - horse , and say , ha , ha ! as the boat bound over the wave that be to land he under the ancient machicolate wall where the Crusaders make their last stand in the Holy Land . not that Kinraid know or care one jot about those gallant knight of old : all he know be , that the French , under Boney , be try to take the town from the Turks , and that his admiral say they must not , and so they should not . 

 he and his man land on that sandy shore , and enter the town by the water - port gate ; he be sing to himself his own country song , 

 Weel may the keel row , the keel row ,   C. 

 and his man , with sailor ' aptitude for music , catch up the air , and join in the burden with inarticulate sound . 

 so , with merry heart , they thread the narrow street of Acre , hem in on either side by the white wall of turkish house , with small grated opening high up , above all chance of peep intrusion . 

 here and there they meet an ample - rob and turbane Turk go along with as much haste as his stately self - possession would allow . but the majority of the male inhabitant be gather together to defend the breach , where the french gun thunder out far above the head of the sailor . 

 they go along none the less merrily for the sound to Djezzar Pacha 's garden , where the old Turk sate on his carpet , beneath the shade of a great terebinth tree , listen to the interpreter , who make known to he the meaning of the eager speech of Sir Sidney Smith and the colonel of the marine . 

 as soon as the admiral see the gallant sailor of H.M.S. Tigre , he interrupt the council of war without much ceremony , and go to Kinraid , he despatch they , as before arrange , to the North Ravelin , show they the way with rapid , clear direction . 

 out of respect to he , they have keep silent while in the strange , desolate garden ; but once more in the street , the old Newcastle song rise up again till the man be , perforce , silence by the haste with which they go to the post of danger . 

 it be three o'clock in the afternoon . for many a day these very man have be swear at the terrific heat at this hour even when at sea , fan by the soft breeze ; but now , in the midst of hot smoke , with former carnage taint the air , and with the rush and whizz of death perpetually whistle in their ear , they be uncomplaine and light - hearted . many an old joke , and some new one , come brave and hearty , on their cheerful voice , even though the speaker be veil from sight in great cloud of smoke , cloven only by the bright flame of death . 

 a sudden message come ; as many of the crew of the Tigre as be under Lieutenant Kinraid 's command be to go down to the Mole , to assist the new reinforcement   see by the sailor from the masthead at day - dawn , under command of Hassan Bey , to land at the Mole , where Sir Sidney then be . 

 off they go , almost as bright and thoughtless as before , though two of their number lie silent for ever at the North Ravelin silence in that one little half - hour . and one go along with the rest , swear lustily at his ill - luck in have his right arm break , but ready to do good business with his left . 

 they help the turkish troop to land more with good - will than tenderness ; and then , lead by Sir Sidney , they go under the shelter of english gun to the fatal breach , so often assail , so gallantly defend , but never so fiercely contest as on this burn afternoon . the ruin of the massive wall that here have be break down by the French , be use by they as stepping stone to get on a level with the besiege , and so to escape the heavy stone which the latter hurl down ; nay , even the dead body of the morning 's comrade be make into ghastly stair . 

 when Djezzar Pacha hear that the british sailor be defend the breach , head by Sir Sidney Smith , he leave his station in the palace garden , gather up his robe in haste , and hurry to the breach ; where , with his own hand , and with right hearty good - will , he pull the sailor down from the post of danger , say that if he lose his english friend he lose all ! 

 but little recke the crew of the Tigre of the one old man Pacha or otherwise who try to hold they back from the fight ; they be up and at the french assailant clamber over the breach in an instant ; and so they go on , as if it be some game at play instead of a deadly combat , until Kinraid and his man be call off by Sir Sidney , as the reinforcement of turkish troop under Hassan Bey be now sufficient for the defence of that old breach in the wall , which be no long the principal object of the french attack ; for the besieger have make a new and more formidable breach by their incessant fire , knock down whole street of the city wall . 

 ' fight your good kinraid ! ' say Sir Sidney ; ' for there be Boney on yonder hill look at you . ' 

 and sure enough , on a rise ground , call Richard Coeur de Lion 's Mount , there be a half - circle of french general , on horseback , all deferentially attend to the motion , and apparently to the word , of a little man in their centre ; at whose bid the aide - de - camp gallop swift with message to the more distant french camp . 

 the two ravelin which Kinraid and his man have to occupy , for the purpose of send a flanking fire upon the enemy , be not ten yard from that enemy 's van . 

 but at length there be a sudden rush of the French to that part of the wall where they imagine they could enter unopposed . 

 surprise at this movement , Kinraid venture out of the shelter of the ravelin to ascertain the cause ; he , safe and untouched during that long afternoon of carnage , fall now , under a stray musket - shot , and lie helpless and expose upon the ground undiscerne by his man , who be recall to help in the hot reception which have be plan for the French ; who , descend the city wall into the Pacha 's garden , be attack with sabre and dagger , and lie headless corps under the flower rose - bush , and by the fountain side . 

 Kinraid lie beyond the ravelin , many yard outside the city wall . 

 he be utterly helpless , for the shot have break his leg . dead body of Frenchmen lie strew around he ; no Englishman have venture out so far . 

 all the wounded man that he could see be french ; and many of these , furious with pain , gnash their tooth at he , and curse he aloud , till he think that his good course be to assume the semblance of death ; for some among these man be still capable of drag themselves up to he , and by concentrate all their fail energy into one blow , put he to a speedy end . 

 the outlying picket of the french army be within easy rifle shoot ; and his uniform , although less conspicuous in colour than that of the marine , by whose side he have be fight , would make he a sure mark if he so much as move his arm . yet how he long to turn , if ever so slightly , so that the cruel slanting sun might not beat full into his ache eye . Fever , too , be come upon he ; the pain in his leg be every moment grow more severe ; the terrible thirst of the wound , add to the heat and fatigue of the day , make his lip and tongue feel baked and dry , and his whole throat seem parch and wooden . thought of other day , of cool Greenland sea , where ice abound , of grassy english home , begin to make the past more real than the present . 

 with a great effort he bring his wandering sens back ; he know where he be now , and could weigh the chance of his life , which be but small ; the unwonted tear come to his eye as he think of the newly - make wife in her english home , who might never know how he die think of she . 

 suddenly he see a party of English marine advance , under shelter of the ravelin , to pick up the wounded , and bear they within the wall for surgical help . they be so near he could see their face , could hear they speak ; yet he durst not make any sign to they when he lie within range of the french picket 's fire . 

 for one moment he could not resist raise his head , to give himself a chance for life ; before the unclean creature that infest a camp come round in the darkness of the night to strip and insult the dead body , and to put to death such as have yet the breath of life within they . but the set sun come full into his face , and he see nothing of what he long to see . 

 he fall back in despair ; he lie there to die . 

 that strong clear sunbeam have work his salvation . 

 he have be recognize as man be recognize when they stand in the red glare of a house on fire ; the same despair of help , of hopeless farewell to life , stamp on their face in blood - red light . 

 one man leave his fellow , and come run forwards , forwards in among the enemy 's wound , within range of their gun ; he bend down over Kinraid ; he seem to understand without a word ; he lift he up , carry he like a child ; and with the vehement energy that be more from the force of will than the strength of body , he bear he back to within the shelter of the ravelin not without many shot be aim at they , one of which hit Kinraid in the fleshy part of his arm . 

 Kinraid be rack with agony from his dangle broken leg , and his very life seem leave he ; yet he remember afterwards how the marine recall his fellow , and how , in the pause before they return , his face become like one formerly know to the sick sens of Kinraid ; yet it be too like a dream , too utterly improbable to be real . 

 yet the few word this man say , as he stand breathless and alone by the faint Kinraid , fit in well with the belief conjure up by his personal appearance . he pant out , 

 ' I niver think you would ha ' keep true to she ! ' 

 and then the other come up ; and while they be make a sling of their belt , Kinraid faint utterly away , and the next time that he be fully conscious , he be lie in his berth in the Tigre , with the ship surgeon set his leg . after that he be too feverish for several day to collect his sens . when he could first remember , and form a judgment upon his recollection , he call the man especially charge to attend upon he , and bid he go and make inquiry in every possible manner for a marine name Philip Hepburn , and , when he be find , to entreat he to come and see Kinraid . 

 the sailor be away the great part of the day , and return unsuccessful in his search ; he have be from ship to ship , hither and thither ; he have question all the marine he have meet with , no one know anything of any Philip Hepburn . 

 Kinraid pass a miserably feverish night , and when the doctor exclaim the next morning at his retrogression , he tell he , with some irritation , of the ill - success of his servant ; he accuse the man of stupidity , and wish fervently that he be able to go himself . 

 partly to soothe he , the doctor promise that he would undertake the search for Hepburn , and he engage faithfully to follow all Kinraid 's eager direction ; not to be satisfied with man 's careless word , but to look over muster - roll and ship ' book . 

 he , too , bring the same answer , however unwillingly give . 

 he have set out upon the search so confident of success , that he feel doubly discomfited by failure . however , he have persuade himself that the lieutenant have be partially delirious from the effect of his wound , and the power of the sun shin down just where he lay . there have , indeed , be slight symptom of Kinraid 's have receive a sun - stroke ; and the doctor dwelt largely on these in his endeavour to persuade his patient that it be his imagination which have endue a stranger with the lineament of some former friend . 

 Kinraid throw his arm out of bed with impatience at all this plausible talk , which be even more irritate than the fact that Hepburn be still undiscovered . 

 ' the man be no friend of mine ; I be like to have kill he when last I see he . he be a shopkeeper in a country town in England . I have see little enough of he ; but enough to make I able to swear to he anywhere , even in a marine 's uniform , and in this swelter country . ' 

 ' face once see , especially in excitement , be apt to return upon the memory in case of fever , ' quoth the doctor , sententiously . 

 the attendant sailor , reinstall to some complacency by the failure of another in the search in which he himself have be unsuccessful , now put in his explanation . 

 ' maybe it be a spirit . it be not th ' first time as I have   heared of a spirit come upon earth to save a man 's life I ' time o ' need . my father have an uncle , a west - country grazier . he be a - come over Dartmoor in Devonshire one moonlight night with a power o ' money as he would get for his sheep at t ' fair . it be stow I ' leather bag under th ' seat o ' th ' gig . it be a rough kind o ' road , both as a road and in character , for there would be many robbery there of late , and th ' great rock stand convenient for hiding - place . all at once father 's uncle feel as if some one be sit beside he on th ' empty seat ; and he turn his head and look , and there he see his brother sit his brother as have be dead twelve year and more . so he turn his head back again , eye right , and never say a word , but wonder what it all mean . all of a sudden two fellow come out upo ' th ' white road from some black shadow , and they look , and they let th ' gig go past , father 's uncle drive hard , I will warrant he . but for all that he hear one say to t ' other , " by , there be two on them ! " straight on he drive fast than ever , till he see th ' far light of some town or other . I forget its name , though I have   hear it many a time ; and then he draw a long breath , and turn his head to look at his brother , and ask he how he have manage to come out of his grave I ' Barum churchyard , and th ' seat be as empty as it have be when he set out ; and then he know that it be a spirit come to help he against th ' man who think to rob he , and would likely enough ha ' murder he . ' 

 Kinraid have keep quiet through this story . but when the sailor begin to draw the moral , and to say , ' and I think I may make bold to say , sir , as th ' marine who carry you out o ' th ' Frenchy 's gun - shot be just a spirit come to help you , ' he exclaim impatiently , swear a great oath as he do so , ' it be no spirit , I tell you ; and I be in my full sens . it be a man name Philip Hepburn . he say word to I , or over I , as none but himself would have say . yet we hat each other like poison ; and I can not   make out why he should be there and put himself in danger to save I . but so it be ; and as you can not   find he , let I hear no more of your nonsense . it be he , and not my fancy , doctor . it be flesh and blood , and not a spirit , Jack . so get along with you , and leave I quiet . ' 

 all this time Stephen Freeman lie friendless , sick , and shatter , on board the Theseus . 

 he have be about his duty close to some shell that be place on her deck ; a gay young midshipman be thoughtlessly strive to get the fusee out of one of these by a mallet and spike - nail that lie close at hand ; and a fearful explosion ensue , in which the poor marine , clean his bayonet near , be shockingly burn and disfigure , the very skin of all the low part of his face be utterly destroy by gunpowder . they say it be a mercy that his eye be spar ; but he could hardly feel anything to be a mercy , as he lie tossing in agony , burn by the explosion , wound by splinter , and feel that he be disabled for life , if life itself be preserve . of all that suffer by that fearful accident   and they be many   none be so forsaken , so hopeless , so desolate , as the Philip Hepburn about whom such anxious inquiry be be make at that very time . 




 CHAPTER XXXIX 

 confidences 

 it be a little later on in that same summer that Mrs. Brunton come to visit her sister Bessy . 

 Bessy be married to a tolerably well - to - do farmer who live at an almost equal distance between Monkshaven and Hartswell ; but from old habit and convenience the latter be regard as the dawson ' market - town ; so Bessy seldom or never see her old friend in Monkshaven . 

 but Mrs. Brunton be far too flourish a person not to speak out her wish , and have her own way . she have no notion , she say , of come such a long journey only to see Bessy and her husband , and not to have a sight of her former acquaintance at Monkshaven . she might have add , that her new bonnet and cloak would be as good as lose if it be not display among those who , know she as Molly Corney , and be less fortunate in matrimony than she be , would look upon it with wonder admiration , if not with envy . 

 so one day farmer Dawson 's market - cart deposit Mrs. Brunton in all her bravery at the shop in the market - place , over which Hepburn and Coulson 's name still flourish in joint partnership . 

 after a few word of brisk recognition to Coulson and Hester , Mrs Brunton pass on into the parlour and greet Sylvia with boisterous heartiness . 

 it be now four year and more since the friend have meet ; and each secretly wonder how they have ever come to be friend . Sylvia have a country , raw , spiritless look to Mrs. Brunton 's eye ; Molly be loud and talkative , and altogether distasteful to Sylvia , train in daily companionship with hester to appreciate soft slow speech , and grave thoughtful way . 

 however , they keep up the form of their old friendship , though their heart have drift far apart . they sit hand in hand while each look at the other with eye inquisitive as to the change which time have make . molly be the first to speak . 

 ' well , to be sure ! how thin and pale yo've   grown , Sylvia ! Matrimony have not   agree wi ' yo ' as well as it be do wi I . Brunton be allay say   yo ' know what a man he be for his joke   that if he would ha ' know how many yard o ' silk I should ha ' ta'en for a gown , he would ha ' think twice afore he would ha ' marry I . why , I have   gain a matter o ' thirty pound o ' flesh sin ' I be marry ! ' 

 ' yo ' do look brave and hearty ! ' say Sylvia , put her sense of her companion 's capacious size and high colour into the prettiest word she could . 

 ' eh ! Sylvia ! but I know what it be , ' say Molly , shake her head . ' it be just because o ' that husband o ' thine as have go and leave thee ; thou 's pining after he , and he be not worth it . Brunton say , when he hear on it I mind he be smoke at t ' time , and he take his pipe out of his mouth , and shake out t ' ash as grave as any judge " the man , " say he , " as can desert a wife like Sylvia Robson as be , deserve hang ! " that be what he say ! eh ! Sylvia , but speakin ' o ' hang I be so grieve for yo ' when I hear of yo'r poor feyther ! such an end for a decent man to come to ! many a one come an ' call on I o ' purpose to hear all I could tell them about he ! ' 

 ' please do not   speak on it ! ' say Sylvia , tremble all over . 

 ' well , poor creature , I wunnot . it be hard on thee , I grant . but to give t ' devil his due , it be good I ' hepburn to marry thee , and so soon after there be a ' that talk about thy feyther . many a man would ha ' draw back , choose howiver far they would go . I be noane so sure about Charley Kinraid . eh , Sylvia ! only think on his be alive after all . I doubt if our Bessy would ha ' we d Frank Dawson if she have know as he be not   drown . but it be as well she do , for Dawson 's a man o ' property , and have getten twelve cow in his cow - house , beside three right down good horse ; and Kinraid be allay a fellow wi ' two string to his bow . I have   allay say and do maintain , that he go on pretty strong wi ' yo ' , Sylvie ; and I will say I think he care more for yo ' than for our Bessy , though it be only yesterday at e'en she be stand out that he like she well than yo ' . Yo'll ha ' heared on his grand marriage ? ' 

 ' no ! ' say Sylvia , with eager painful curiosity . 

 ' no ! it be in all t ' paper ! I wonder as yo ' do not   see it . wait a minute ! I cut it out o ' t ' Gentleman 's Magazine , as Brunton buy o ' purpose , and put it I ' my pocket - book when I be a - come here : I know I have   get it somewheere . ' 

 she take out her smart crimson pocket - book , and rummage in the pocket until she produce a little crumpled bit of print paper , from which she read aloud , 

 ' on January the third , at St Mary Redcliffe , Bristol , Charles Kinraid , Esq . , lieutenant Royal Navy , to Miss Clarinda Jackson , with a fortune of 10,000l . ' 

 ' theere ! ' say she , triumphantly , ' it be something as Brunton say , to be cousin to that . ' 

 ' would yo ' let I see it ? ' say Sylvia , timidly . 

 Mrs. Brunton graciously consent ; and Sylvia bring she newly acquire reading - knowledge , hitherto principally exercise on the Old Testament , to bear on these word . 

 there be nothing wonderful in they , nothing that she might not have expect ; and yet the surprise turn her giddy for a moment or two . she never think of see he again , never . but to think of his caring for another woman as much as he have do for she , nay , perhaps more ! 

 the idea be irresistibly force upon she that Philip would not have act so ; it would have take long year before he could have be induce to put another on the throne she have once occupy . for the first time in her life she seem to recognize the real nature of Philip 's love . 

 but she say nothing but ' thank yo ' , ' when she give the scrap of paper back to Molly Brunton . and the latter continue give her information about Kinraid 's marriage . 

 ' he be down in t ' west , Plymouth or somewheere , when he meet wi ' she . she be no feyther ; he would be in t ' sugar - bake business ; but from what Kinraid write to old Turner , th ' uncle as bring he up at Cullercoats , she be have t ' good of edication : can play on t ' instrument and dance t ' shawl dance ; and Kinraid have all her money settle on she , though she say she would rayther give it all to he , which I must say , be his cousin , be very pretty on she . he be leave she now , have to go off in t ' Tigre , as be his ship , to t ' Mediterranean sea ; and she be write to offer to come and see old Turner , and make friend with his relation , and Brunton be go to gi'e I a crimson satin as soon as we know for certain when she be come , for we be sure to be ask out to Cullercoats . ' 

 ' I wonder if she be very pretty ? ' ask Sylvia , faintly , in the first pause in this torrent of talk . 

 ' oh ! she be a perfect beauty , as I understand . there be a traveller as come to our shop as have be at York , and know some of her cousin theere that be in t ' grocery line her mother be a York lady and they say she be just a picture of a woman , and iver so many gentleman have be wantin ' to marry she , but she just wait for Charley Kinraid , yo ' see ! ' 

 ' well , I hope they will be happy ; I be sure I do ! ' say Sylvia . 

 ' that be just luck . some folk be happy I ' marriage , and some be not . it be just luck , and there be no forecasting it . man be such unaccountable animal , there be no prophesyin ' upon them . who would ha ' thought of yo'r husband , he as be so slow and sure steady Philip , as we lasse use to can ' he makin ' a moonlight flittin ' , and leavin ' yo ' to be a widow bewitch ? ' 

 ' he do not   go at night , ' say Sylvia , take the word ' moonlight flitting ' in their literal sense . 

 ' no ! well , I only say " moonlight flittin ' " just because it come uppermost and I know no well . tell I all about it , Sylvie , for I can not   mak ' it out from what Bessy say . have he and yo ' have word ? but in course yo ' have . ' 

 at this moment hester come into the room ; and Sylvia joyfully avail herself of the pretext for break off the conversation that have reach this painful and awkward point . she detain hester in the room for fear lest Mrs. Brunton should repeat her inquiry as to how it all happen that Philip have go away ; but the presence of a third person seem as though it would be but little restraint upon the inquisitive Molly , who repeatedly bear down upon the same question till she nearly drive Sylvia distract , between her astonishment at the news of Kinraid 's marriage ; her wish to be alone and quiet , so as to realize the full meaning of that piece of intelligence ; her desire to retain hester in the conversation ; her effort to prevent Molly 's recurrence to the circumstance of Philip 's disappearance , and the longing more vehement every minute for her visitor to go away and leave she in peace . she become so disturbed with all these thought and feeling that she hardly know what she be say , and assent or dissent to speech without there be either any reason or truth in her word . 

 Mrs. Brunton have arrange to remain with Sylvia while the horse rest , and have no compunction about the length of her visit . she expect to be ask to tea , as Sylvia find out at last , and this she feel would be the bad of all , as Alice Rose be not one to tolerate the coarse , careless talk of such a woman as Mrs. Brunton without uplift her voice in many a testimony against it . Sylvia sate hold hester 's gown tight in order to prevent she leave the room , and try to arrange her little plan so that too much discordance should not arise to the surface . just then the door open , and little Bella come in from the kitchen in all the pretty , sturdy dignity of two year old , Alice follow she with careful step , and protect , outstretched arm , a slow smile soften the sternness of her grave face ; for the child be the unconscious darling of the household , and all eye soften into love as they look on she . she make straight for her mother with something grasp in her little dimpled fist ; but half - way across the room she seem to have become suddenly aware of the presence of a stranger , and she stop short , fix her serious eye full on Mrs. Brunton , as if to take in her appearance , nay , as if to penetrate down into her very real self , and then , stretch out her disengage hand , the baby speak out the word that have be hover about her mother 's lip for an hour past . 

 ' do away ! ' say Bella , decisively . 

 ' what a perfect love ! ' say Mrs. Brunton , half in real admiration , half in patronage . as she speak , she get up and go towards the child , as if to take she up . 

 ' do away ! do away ! ' cry Bella , in shrill affright at this movement . 

 ' Dunnot , ' say Sylvia ; ' she be shy ; she do not   know stranger . ' 

 but Mrs. Brunton have grasp the struggling , kick child by this time , and her reward for this be a vehement little slap in the face . 

 ' Yo ' naughty little spoilt thing ! ' say she , set Bella down in a hurry . ' Yo ' deserve a good whipping , yo ' do , and if yo ' be mine yo ' should have it . ' 

 Sylvia have no need to stand up for the baby who have run to her arm , and be soothe herself with sob on her mother 's breast ; for Alice take up the defence . 

 ' the child say , as plain as word could say , " go away , " and if thou wouldst follow thine own will instead of heed her wish , thou mun put up with the wilfulness of the old Adam , of which it seem to I thee hast getten thy share at thirty as well as little Bella at two . ' 

 ' thirty ! ' say Mrs. Brunton , now fairly affront . ' thirty ! why , Sylvia , yo ' know I be but two year old than yo ' ; speak to that woman an ' tell she as I be only four - and - twenty . thirty , indeed ! ' 

 ' Molly 's but four - and - twenty , ' say Sylvia , in a pacificatory tone . 

 ' whether she be twenty , or thirty , or forty , be alike to I , ' say Alice . ' I mean no harm . I mean but for t ' say as her angry word to the child bespoke she to be one of the foolish . I know not who she be , nor what her age may be . ' 

 ' she be an old friend of mine , ' say Sylvia . ' she be Mrs. Brunton now , but when I know she she be Molly Corney . ' 

 ' ay ! and yo ' be Sylvia Robson , and as bonny and light - hearte a lass as any in a ' t ' riding , though now yo're a poor widow bewitch , leave wi ' a child as I must not   speak a word about , an ' live wi ' folk as talk about t ' old Adam as if he be not   dead and do wi ' long ago ! it be a change , Sylvia , as make my heart ache for yo ' , to think on they old day when yo ' be so thought on yo ' might have have any man , as Brunton often say ; it be a great mistake as yo ' iver take up wi ' yon man as have run away . but seven year will soon be past fro ' t ' time he go off , and yo'll only be six - and - twenty then ; and there will be a chance of a well husband for yo ' after all , so keep up yo'r heart , Sylvia . ' 

 Molly Brunton have put as much venom as she know how into this speech , mean it as a vengeful payment for the supposition of she be thirty , even more than for the reproof for her angry word about the child . she think that Alice Rose must be either mother or aunt to Philip , from the serious cast of countenance that be remarkable in both ; and she rather exult in the allusion to a happy second marriage for Sylvia , with which she have conclude her speech . it rouse Alice , however , as effectually as if she have be really a blood relation to Philip ; but for a different reason . she be not slow to detect the intentional offensiveness to herself in what have be say ; she be indignant at Sylvia for suffer the word speak to pass unanswered ; but in truth they be too much in keep with Molly Brunton 's character to make as much impression on Sylvia as they do on a stranger ; and besides , she feel as if the less reply molly receive , the less likely would it be that she would go on in the same strain . so she coax and chatter to her child and behave like a little coward in try to draw out of the conversation , while at the same time listen attentively . 

 ' as for Sylvia Hepburn as be Sylvia Robson , she know my mind , ' say Alice , in grim indignation . ' she be humble herself now , I trust and pray , but she be light - minded and full of vanity when Philip marry she , and it might ha ' be a lift towards her salvation in one way ; but it please the Lord to work in a different way , and she mun wear her sackcloth and ash in patience . so I will say naught more about she . but for he as be absent , as thee hast speak on so lightly and reproachfully , I would have thee to know he be one of a different kind to any thee ever know , I reckon . if he be lead away by a pretty face to slight one as be fitter for he , and who have love he as the apple of her eye , it be he as be suffer for it , inasmuch as he be a wanderer from his home , and an outcast from wife and child . ' 

 to the surprise of all , Molly 's word of reply be cut short even when they be on her lip , by Sylvia . Pale , fire - eye , and excited , with Philip 's child on one arm , and the other stretch out , she say , 

 ' Noane can tell noane know . no one shall speak a judgment ' twixt Philip and I . he act cruel and wrong by I . but I have   say my word to he hissel ' , and I be noane go to make any plaint to other ; only they as know should judge . and it be not fitting , it be not '   almost sob , ' to go on wi ' talk like this afore I . ' 

 the two for Hester , who be aware that her presence have only be desire by Sylvia as a check to an unpleasant tete - a - tete conversation , have slip back to her business as soon as her mother come in the two look with surprise at Sylvia ; her word , her whole manner , belong to a phase of her character which seldom come uppermost , and which have not be perceive by either of they before . 

 Alice Rose , though astonish , rather approve of Sylvia 's speech ; it show that she have more serious thought and feel on the subject than the old woman have give her credit for ; her general silence respect her husband 's disappearance have lead Alice to think that she be too childish to have receive any deep impression from the event . molly Brunton give vent to her opinion on Sylvia 's speech in the following word : 

 ' Hoighty - toighty ! that tell tale , lass . if yo ' treat steady Philip to many such look an ' speech as yo'n give we now , it be easy t ' see why he take hisself off . why , Sylvia , I niver see it in yo ' when yo ' be a girl ; yo're grow into a regular little vixen , theere wheere yo ' stand ! ' 

 indeed she do look defiant , with the swift colour flush her cheek to crimson on its return , and the fire in her eye not yet die away . but at Molly 's jesting word she sink back into her usual look and manner , only say quietly , 

 ' it be for noane to say whether I be vixen or not , as do not   know th ' past thing as be bury in my heart . but I can not hold they as my friend as go on talk on either my husband or I before my very face . what he be , I know ; and what I be , I reckon he know . and now I will go hurry tea , for yo'll be need it , molly ! ' 

 the last clause of this speech be mean to make peace ; but molly be in twenty mind as to whether she should accept the olive - branch or not . her temper , however , be of that obtuse kind which be not easily ruffle ; her mind , stagnant in itself , enjoy excitement from without ; and her appetite be invariably good , so she stay , in spite of the inevitable tete - a - tete with Alice . the latter , however , refuse to be draw into conversation again ; reply to Mrs. Brunton 's speech with a curt yes or no , when , indeed , she reply at all . 

 when all be gather at tea , Sylvia be quite calm again ; rather pale than usual , and very attentive and subduce in her behaviour to Alice ; she would evidently fain have be silent , but as Molly be her own especial guest , that could not be , so all her endeavour go towards steer the conversation away from any awkward point . but each of the four , let alone little Bella , be thankful when the market - cart draw up at the shop door , that be to take Mrs. Brunton back to her sister 's house . 

 when she be fairly off , Alice Rose open her mouth in strong condemnation ; wind up with 

 ' and if aught in my word give thee cause for offence , Sylvia , it be because my heart rise within I at the kind of talk thee and she have be have about Philip ; and her evil and light - minded counsel to thee about wait seven year , and then wed another . ' 

 hard as these word may seem when repeat , there be something of a near approach to an apology in Mrs. Rose 's manner than Sylvia have ever see in it before . she be silent for a few moment , then she say , 

 ' I ha ' often think of tell yo ' and Hester , special - like , when yo've   be so kind to my little Bella , that Philip an ' I could niver come together again ; no , not if he come home this very night ' 

 she would have go on speak , but hester interrupt she with a low cry of dismay . 

 Alice say , 

 ' hush thee , hester . it be no business o ' thine . Sylvia Hepburn , thou'rt speak like a silly child . ' 

 ' no . I be speak like a woman ; like a woman as find out she be be cheat by man as she trust , and as have no help for it . I be noane go to say any more about it . it be I as have be wrong , and as have to bear it : only I think I would tell yo ' both this much , that yo ' might know somewhat why he go away , and how I say my last word about it . ' 

 so indeed it seem . to all question and remonstrance from Alice , Sylvia turn a deaf ear . she avert her face from Hester 's sad , wistful look ; only when they be part for the night , at the top of the little staircase , she turn , and put her arm round Hester 's neck she lay her head on her neck , and whisper , 

 ' poor hester poor , poor hester ! if yo ' an ' he have but be marry together , what a deal o ' sorrow would ha ' be spar to we all ! ' 

 hester push she away as she finish these word ; look searchingly into her face , her eye , and then follow Sylvia into her room , where Bella lie sleep , shut the door , and almost kneel down at Sylvia 's foot , clasp she , and hide her face in the fold of the other 's gown . 

 ' Sylvia , Sylvia , ' she murmur , ' some one have tell you I think no one know it be no sin it be do away with now indeed it be it be long ago before yo ' be marry ; but I can not forget . it be a shame , perhaps , to have think on it iver , when he niver think o ' I ; but I niver believe as any one could ha ' find it out . I be just fit to sink into t ' ground , what wi ' my sorrow and my shame . ' 

 hester be stop by her own rise sob , immediately she be in Sylvia 's arm . Sylvia be sit on the ground hold she , and soothe she with caress and broken word . 

 ' I be allay say t ' wrong thing , ' say she . ' it seem as if I be all upset to - day ; and indeed I be ; ' she add , allude to the news of Kinraid 's marriage she have yet to think upon . 

 ' but it be not   yo ' , hester : it be nothing yo ' iver say , or do , or look , for that matter . it be yo'r mother as let it out . ' 

 ' oh , mother ! mother ! ' wail out hester ; ' I niver think as any one but God would ha ' know that I have iver for a day thought on his be more to I than a brother . ' 

 Sylvia make no reply , only go on stroke Hester 's smooth brown hair , off which her cap have fall . Sylvia be think how strange life be , and how love seem to go all at cross purpose ; and be lose herself in bewilderment at the mystery of the world ; she be almost startled when Hester rise up , and take Sylvia 's hand in both of her , and look solemnly at she , say , 

 ' Sylvia , yo ' know what have be my trouble and my shame , and I be sure yo're sorry for I for I will humble myself to yo ' , and own that for many month before yo ' be marry , I feel my disappointment like a heavy burden lay on I by day and by night ; but now I ask yo ' , if yo've   any pity for I for what I go through , or if yo've   any love for I because of yo'r dead mother 's love for I , or because of any fellowship , or daily breadliness between we two , put the hard thought of Philip away from out yo'r heart ; he may ha ' do yo ' wrong , anyway yo ' think that he have ; I niver know he aught but kind and good ; but if he come back from wheriver in th ' wide world he be go to   and there be not a night but I pray God to keep he , and send he safe back , yo ' put away the memory of past injury , and forgive it all , and be , what yo ' can be , Sylvia , if you have   a mind to , just the kind , good wife he ought to have . ' 

 ' I can not ; yo ' know nothing about it , Hester . ' 

 ' tell I , then , ' plead hester . 

 ' no ! ' say Sylvia , after a moment 's hesitation ; ' I would do a deal for yo ' , I would , but I dare not   forgive Philip , even if I could ; I take a great oath again ' he . ay , yo ' may look shocked at I , but it be he as yo ' ought for to be shock at if yo ' know all . I say I would niver forgive he ; I shall keep to my word . ' 

 ' I think I would well pray for his death , then , ' say hester , hopelessly , and almost bitterly , loose her hold of Sylvia 's hand . 

 ' if it be not   for baby theere , I could think as it be my death as ' ud be good . they as one think t ' most on , forget one soon . ' 

 it be Kinraid to whom she be allude ; but hester do not understand she ; and after stand for a moment in silence , she kiss she , and leave she for the night . 




 CHAPTER XL 

 an unexpected messenger 

 after this agitation , and these partial confidence , no more be say on the subject of Philip for many week . they avoid even the slight allusion to he ; and none of they know how seldom or how often he might be present in the mind of the other . 

 one day the little Bella be unusually fractious with some slight childish indisposition , and Sylvia be oblige to have recourse to a never - fail piece of amusement ; namely , to take the child into the shop , when the number of new , bright - colour article be sure to beguile the little girl out of her fretfulness . she be walk along the high terrace of the counter , keep steady by her mother 's hand , when Mr. Dawson 's market - cart once more stop before the door . but it be not Mrs. Brunton who alight now ; it be a very smartly - dress , very pretty young lady , who put one dainty foot before the other with care , as if descend from such a primitive vehicle be a new occurrence in her life . then she look up at the name above the shop - door , and after ascertain that this be indeed the place she desire to find , she come in blushing . 

 ' be Mrs. Hepburn at home ? ' she ask of Hester , whose position in the shop bring she forward to receive the customer , while Sylvia draw Bella out of sight behind some great bale of red flannel . 

 ' can I see she ? ' the sweet , south - country voice go on , still address hester . Sylvia hear the inquiry , and come forwards , with a little rustic awkwardness , feel both shy and curious . 

 ' will yo ' please walk this way , ma'am ? ' say she , lead her visitor back into her own dominion of the parlour , and leave Bella to Hester 's willing care . 

 ' you do not   know I ! ' say the pretty young lady , joyously . ' but I think you know my husband . I be Mrs. Kinraid ! ' 

 a sob of surprise rise to Sylvia 's lip she choke it down , however , and try to conceal any emotion she might feel , in place a chair for her visitor , and try to make she feel welcome , although , if the truth must be tell , Sylvia be wonder all the time why her visitor come , and how soon she would go . 

 ' you know Captain Kinraid , do you not ? ' say the young lady , with innocent inquiry ; to which Sylvia 's lip form the answer , ' yes , ' but no clear sound issue therefrom . 

 ' but I know your husband know the captain ; be he at home yet ? can I speak to he ? I do so want to see he . ' 

 Sylvia be utterly bewilder ; Mrs. Kinraid , this pretty , joyous , prosperous little bird of a woman , Philip , Charley 's wife , what could they have in common ? what could they know of each other ? all she could say in answer to Mrs. Kinraid 's eager question , and still more eager look , be , that her husband be from home , have be long from home : she do not know where he be , she do not know when he would come back . 

 Mrs. Kinraid 's face fall a little , partly from her own real disappointment , partly out of sympathy with the hopeless , indifferent tone of Sylvia 's reply . 

 ' Mrs. Dawson tell I he have go away rather suddenly a year ago , but I think he might be come home by now . I be expect the captain early next month . oh ! how I should have like to see Mr. Hepburn , and to thank he for save the captain 's life ! ' 

 ' what do yo ' mean ? ' ask Sylvia , stir out of all assumed indifference . ' the captain ! be that '   not ' Charley ' , she could not use that familiar name to the pretty young wife before her   ' yo'r husband ? ' 

 ' yes , you know he , do not   you ? when he use to be stay with Mr Corney , his uncle ? ' 

 ' yes , I know he ; but I do not   understand . will yo ' please to tell I all about it , ma'am ? ' say Sylvia , faintly . 

 ' I think your husband would have tell you all about it ; I hardly know where to begin . you know my husband be a sailor ? ' 

 Sylvia nod assent , listen greedily , her heart beat thick all the time . 

 ' and he be now a Commander in the Royal Navy , all earn by his own bravery ! oh ! I be so proud of he ! ' 

 so could Sylvia have be if she have be his wife ; as it be , she think how often she have feel sure that he would be a great man some day . 

 ' and he have be at the siege of Acre . ' 

 Sylvia look perplex at these strange word , and Mrs. Kinraid catch the look . 

 ' St Jean d'Acre , you know though it be fine say " you know " , when I do not   know a bit about it myself till the captain 's ship be order there , though I be the head girl at Miss Dobbin 's in the geography class acre be a seaport town , not far from Jaffa , which be the modern name for Joppa , where St Paul go to long ago ; you have   read of that , I be sure , and Mount Carmel , where the prophet Elijah be once , all in Palestine , you know , only the Turks have get it now ? ' 

 ' but I do not   understand yet , ' say Sylvia , plaintively ; ' I daresay it be all very true about St Paul , but please , ma'am , will yo ' tell I about yo'r husband and mine have they meet again ? ' 

 ' yes , at Acre , I tell you , ' say Mrs. Kinraid , with pretty petulance . ' the Turks hold the town , and the French want to take it ; and we , that be the British Fleet , would not   let they . so Sir Sidney Smith , a commodore and a great friend of the captain 's , land in order to fight the French ; and the captain and many of the sailor land with he ; and it be burn hot ; and the poor captain be wound , and lie a - dying of pain and thirst within the enemy 's that be the french fire ; so that they be ready to shoot any one of his own side who come near he . they think he be dead himself , you see , as he be very near ; and would have be too , if your husband have not come out of shelter , and take he up in his arm or on his back   I could not   make out which , and carry he safe within the wall . ' 

 ' it could not   have be Philip , ' say Sylvia , dubiously . 

 ' but it be . the captain say so ; and he be not a man to be mistake . I think I would get his letter with I ; and I would have read you a part of it , but I leave it at Mrs. Dawson 's in my desk ; and I can not   send it to you , ' blushing as she remember certain passage in which ' the captain ' write very much like a lover , ' or else I would . but you may be quite sure it be your husband that venture into all that danger to save his old friend 's life , or the captain would not have say so . ' 

 ' but they be not   they be not   not to call great friend . ' 

 ' I wish I would get the letter here ; I can not   think how I could be so stupid ; I think I can almost remember the very word , though I have   read they over so often . he say , " just as I give up all hope , I see one Philip Hepburn , a man whom I have know at Monkshaven , and whom I have some reason to remember well "   I be sure he say so " remember well " , " he see I too , and come at the risk of his life to where I lay . I fully expect he would be shoot down ; and I shut my eye not to see the end of my last chance . the shot rain about he , and I think he be hit ; but he take I up and carry I under cover . " I be sure he say that , I have   read it over so often ; and he go on and say how he hunt for Mr. Hepburn all through the ship , as soon as ever he could ; but he could hear nothing of he , either alive or dead . do not   go so white , for pity 's sake ! ' say she , suddenly startle by Sylvia 's blanch colour . ' you see , because he could not   find he alive be no reason for give he up as dead ; because his name be not   to be find on any of the ship ' book ; so the captain think he must have be know by a different name to his real one . only he say he should like to have see he to have thank he ; and he say he would give a deal to know what have become of he ; and as I be stay two day at Mrs. Dawson 's , I tell they I must come over to Monkshaven , if only for five minute , just to hear if your good husband be come home , and to shake his hand , that help to save my own dear captain . ' 

 ' I do not   think it could have be Philip , ' reiterate Sylvia . 

 ' why not ? ' ask her visitor ; ' you say you do not   know where he be ; why might not   he have be there where the captain say he be ? ' 

 ' but he be not   a sailor , nor yet a soldier . ' 

 ' oh ! but he be . I think somewhere the captain call he a marine ; that be neither one nor the other , but a little of both . he will be come home some day soon ; and then you will see ! ' 

 Alice Rose come in at this minute , and Mrs. Kinraid jump to the conclusion that she be Sylvia 's mother , and in her overflowing gratitude and friendliness to all the family of he who have ' save the captain ' she go forward , and shake the old woman 's hand in that pleasant confiding way that win all heart . 

 ' here be your daughter , ma'am ! ' say she to the half - astonish , half - pleased Alice . ' I be Mrs. Kinraid , the wife of the captain that use to be in these part , and I be come to bring her news of her husband , and she do not   half believe I , though it be all to his credit , I be sure . ' 

 Alice look so perplexed that Sylvia feel herself bind to explain . 

 ' she say he be either a soldier or a sailor , and a long way off at some place name in t ' Bible . ' 

 ' Philip Hepburn lead away to be a soldier ! ' say she , ' who have once be a Quaker ? ' 

 ' yes , and a very brave one too , and one that it would do my heart good to look upon , ' exclaim Mrs. Kinraid . ' he be be save my husband 's life in the Holy Land , where Jerusalem be , you know . ' 

 ' Nay ! ' say Alice , a little scornfully . ' I can forgive Sylvia for not be over keen to credit thy news . her man of peace become a man of war ; and suffer to enter Jerusalem , which be a heavenly and a typical city at this time ; while I , as be one of the elect , be oblige to go on dwell in Monkshaven , just like any other body . ' 

 ' Nay , but , ' say Mrs. Kinraid , gently , see she be touch on delicate ground , ' I do not say he have go to Jerusalem , but my husband see he in those part , and he be do his duty like a brave , good man ; ay , and more than his duty ; and , you may take my word for it , he will be at home some day soon , and all I beg be that you will let the captain and I know , for I be sure if we can , we will both come and pay our respect to he . and I be very glad I have   see you , ' say she , rise to go , and put out her hand to shake that of Sylvia ; ' for , besides be Hepburn 's wife , I be pretty sure I have   hear the captain speak of you ; and if ever you come to Bristol I hope you will come and see we on Clifton Downs . ' 

 she go away , leave Sylvia almost stunned by the new idea present to she . Philip a soldier ! Philip in a battle , risk his life . most strange of all , Charley and Philip once more meeting together , not as rival or as foe , but as saviour and save ! add to all this the conviction , strengthen by every word that happy , love wife have utter , that Kinraid 's old , passionate love for herself have fade away and vanish utterly : its very existence apparently blot out of his memory . she have tear up her love for he by the root , but she feel as if she could never forget that it have be . 

 hester bring back Bella to her mother . she have not like to interrupt the conversation with the strange lady before ; and now she find her mother in an obvious state of excitement ; Sylvia quieter than usual . 

 ' that be Kinraid 's wife , hester ! he that be th ' specksioneer as make such a noise about t ' place at the time of Darley 's death . he be now a captain a navy captain , accord to what she say . and she would fain have we believe that Philip be abide in all manner of scripture place ; place as have be long do away with , but the similitude whereof be in the heaven , where the elect shall one day see they . and she say Philip be there , and a soldier , and that he save her husband 's life , and be come home soon . I wonder what John and Jeremiah will say to his soldiering then ? it will noane be to their taste , I be think . ' 

 this be all very unintelligible to hester , and she would dearly have like to question Sylvia ; but Sylvia sate a little apart , with Bella on her knee , her cheek rest on her child 's golden curl , and her eye fix and almost trance - like , as if she be see thing not present . 

 so hester have to be content with ask her mother as many elucidatory question as she could ; and after all do not gain a very clear idea of what have really be say by Mrs. Kinraid , as her mother be more full of the apparent injustice of Philip 's be allow the privilege of treading on holy ground if , indeed , that holy ground exist on this side heaven , which she be inclined to dispute than to confine herself to the repetition of word , or narration of fact . 

 suddenly Sylvia rouse herself to a sense of hester 's deep interest and balked inquiry , and she go over the ground rapidly . 

 ' Yo'r mother say right she be his wife . and he be away fight ; and get too near t ' French as be shoot and fire all round he ; and just then , accord to her story , Philip see he , and go straight into t ' midst o ' t ' shot , and fetch he out o ' danger . that be what she say , and uphold . ' 

 ' and why should it not be ? ' ask Hester , her cheek flush . 

 but Sylvia only shake her head , and say , 

 ' I can not tell . it may be so . but they would little cause to be friend , and it seem all so strange Philip a soldier , and they meet theere after all ! ' 

 hester lay the story of Philip 's bravery to her heart she fully believe in it . Sylvia ponder it more deeply still ; the cause for her disbelief , or , at any rate , for her wonder , be unknown to hester ! many a time she sink to sleep with the picture of the event narrate by Mrs. Kinraid as present to her mind as her imagination or experience could make it : first one figure prominent , then another . many a morning she waken up , her heart beat wildly , why , she know not , till she shudder at the remembrance of the scene that have pass in her dream : scene that might be act in reality that very day ; for Philip might come back , and then ? 

 and where be Philip all this time , these many week , these heavily pass month ? 




 CHAPTER XLI 

 the bedesman of ST SEPULCHRE
