 Philip lie long ill on board the hospital ship . if his heart have be light , he might have rally soon ; but he be so depressed he do not care to live . his shattered jaw - bone , his burn and blacken face , his many injury of body , be torture to both his physical frame , and his sick , weary heart . no more chance for he , if indeed there ever have be any , of return gay and gallant , and thus regain his wife 's love . this have be his poor , foolish vision in the first hour of his enlistment ; and the vain dream have recur more than once in the feverish stage of excitement which the new scene into which he have be hurry as a recruit have call forth . but that be all over now . he know that it be the most unlikely thing in the world to have come to pass ; and yet those be happy day when he could think of it as barely possible . now all he could look forward to be disfigurement , feebleness , and the bare pittance that keep pensioner from absolute want . 

 those around he be kind enough to he in their fashion , and attend to his bodily requirement ; but they have no notion of listen to any revelation of unhappiness , if Philip have be the man to make confidence of that kind . as it be , he lie very still in his berth , seldom ask for anything , and always say he be well , when the ship - surgeon come round with his daily inquiry . but he do not care to rally , and be rather sorry to find that his case be consider so interesting in a surgical point of view , that he be likely to receive a good deal more than the average amount of attention . perhaps it be owe to this that he recover at all . the doctor say it be the heat that make he languid , for that his wound and burn be all do well at last ; and by - and - by they tell he they have order he ' home ' . his pulse sink under the surgeon 's finger at the mention of the word ; but he do not say a word . he be too indifferent to life and the world to have a will ; otherwise they might have keep their pet patient a little long where he be . 

 slowly pass from ship to ship as occasion serve ; rest here and there in garrison hospital , Philip at length reach Portsmouth on the evening of a September day in 1799 . the transport - ship in which he be , be load with wound and invalid soldier and sailor ; all who could manage it in any way struggle on deck to catch the first view of the white coast of England . one man lift his arm , take off his cap , and feebly wave it aloft , cry , ' Old England for ever ! ' in a faint shrill voice , and then burst into tear and sob aloud . other try to pipe up ' rule Britannia ' , while more sate , weak and motionless , look towards the shore that once , not so long ago , they never think to see again . Philip be one of these ; his place a little apart from the other man . he be muffle up in a great military cloak that have be give he by one of his officer ; he feel the September breeze chill after his sojourn in a warm climate , and in his shattered state of health . 

 as the ship come in sight of Portsmouth harbour , the signal flag run up the rope ; the beloved Union Jack float triumphantly over all . return signal be make from the harbour ; on board all become bustle and preparation for landing ; while on shore there be the evident movement of expectation , and man in uniform be see press their way to the front , as if to they belong the right of reception . they be the man from the barrack hospital , that have be signal for , come down with ambulance litter and other mark of forethought for the sick and wound , who be return to the country for which they have fight and suffer . 

 with a dash and a great rock swing the vessel come up to her appoint place , and be safely moor . Philip sit still , almost as if he have no part in the cry of welcome , the bustling care , the loud direction that cut the air around he , and pierce his nerve through and through . but one in authority give the order ; and Philip , discipline to obedience , rise to find his knapsack and leave the ship . Passive as he seem to be , he have his liking for particular comrade ; there be one especially , a man as different from Philip as well could be , to whom the latter have always attach himself ; a merry fellow from Somersetshire , who be almost always cheerful and bright , though Philip have overhear the doctor say he would never be the man he be before he have that shot through the side . this marine would often sit make his fellow laugh , and laugh himself at his own good - humour joke , till so terrible a fit of cough come on that those around he fear he would die in the paroxysm . after one of these fit he have gasp out some word , which lead Philip to question he a little ; and it turn out that in the quiet little village of Potterne , far inland , nestle beneath the high stretch of Salisbury Plain , he have a wife and a child , a little girl , just the same age even to a week as Philip 's own little Bella . it be this that draw Philip towards the man ; and this that make Philip wait and go ashore along with the poor consumptive marine . 

 the litter have move off towards the hospital , the sergeant in charge have give his word of command to the remain invalid , who try to obey they to the good of their power , fall into something like military order for their march ; but soon , very soon , the weak broke step , and lag behind ; and feel as if the rough welcome and rude expression of sympathy from the crowd around be almost too much for they . Philip and his companion be about midway , when suddenly a young woman with a child in her arm force herself through the people , between the soldier who keep press on either side , and throw herself on the neck of Philip 's friend . 

 ' oh , Jem ! ' she sob , ' I have   walk all the road from Potterne . I have   never stop but for food and rest for Nelly , and now I have   get you once again , I have   get you once again , bless God for it ! ' 

 she do not seem to see the deadly change that have come over her husband since she part with he a ruddy young labourer ; she have get he once again , as she phrase it , and that be enough for she ; she kiss his face , his hand , his very coat , nor would she be repulse from walk beside he and hold his hand , while her little girl run along scared by the voice and the strange face , and cling to her mammy 's gown . 

 Jem cough , poor fellow ! he cough his churchyard cough ; and Philip bitterly envy he envy his life , envy his approach death ; for be he not wrap round with that woman 's tender love , and be not such love strong than death ? Philip have feel as if his own heart be grow numb , and as though it have change to a cold heavy stone . but at the contrast of this man 's lot to his own , he feel that he have yet the power of suffering leave to he . 

 the road they have to go be full of people , keep off in some measure by the guard of soldier . all sort of kindly speech , and many a curious question , be address to the poor invalid as they walk along . Philip 's jaw , and the low part of his face , be bandage up ; his cap be slouch down ; he hold his cloak about he , and shiver within its fold . 

 they come to a standstill from some slight obstacle at the corner of a street . down the causeway of this street a naval officer with a lady on his arm be walk briskly , with a step that tell of health and a light heart . he stay his progress though , when he see the convoy of maim and wound man ; he say something , of which Philip only catch the word , ' same uniform , ' ' for his sake , ' to the young lady , whose cheek blanch a little , but whose eye kindle . then leave she for an instant , he press forward ; he be close to Philip , poor sad Philip absorb in his own thought , so absorb that he notice nothing till he hear a voice at his ear , have the northumbrian burr , the Newcastle inflection which he know of old , and that be to he like the sick memory of a deadly illness ; and then he turn his muffled face to the speaker , though he know well enough who it be , and avert his eye after one sight of the handsome , happy man , the man whose life he have save once , and would save again , at the risk of his own , but whom , for all that , he pray that he might never meet more on earth . 

 ' here , my fine fellow , take this , ' force a crown piece into Philip 's hand . ' I wish it be more ; I would give you a pound if I have it with I . ' 

 Philip mutter something , and hold out the coin to Captain Kinraid , of course in vain ; nor be there time to urge it back upon the giver , for the obstacle to their progress be suddenly remove , the crowd press upon the captain and his wife , the procession move on , and Philip along with it , hold the piece in his hand , and long to throw it far away . indeed he be on the point of drop it , hop to do so unperceived , when he bethink he of give it to Jem 's wife , the footsore woman , limp happily along by her husband 's side . they thank he , and speak in his praise more than he could well bear . it be no credit to he to give that away which burn his finger as long as he keep it . 

 Philip know that the injury he have receive in the explosion on board the Theseus would oblige he to leave the service . he also believe that they would entitle he to a pension . but he have little interest in his future life ; he be without hope , and in a depressed state of health . he remain for some little time stationary , and then go through all the form of dismissal on account of wound receive in service , and be turn out loose upon the world , uncertain where to go , indifferent as to what become of he . 

 it be fine , warm October weather as he turn his back upon the coast , and set off on his walk northward . green leaf be yet upon the tree ; the hedge be one flush of foliage and the wild rough - flavour fruit of different kind ; the field be tawny with the uncleared - off stubble , or emerald green with the growth of the aftermath . the roadside cottage garden be gay with hollyhock and Michaelmas daisy and marigold , and the bright pane of the window glitter through a veil of China rose . 

 the war be a popular one , and , as a natural consequence , soldier and sailor be hero everywhere . Philip 's long droop form , his arm hang in a sling , his face scar and blacken , his jaw bind up with a black silk handkerchief ; these mark of active service be reverence by the rustic cottager as though they have be crown and sceptre . many a hard - handed labourer leave his seat by the chimney corner , and come to his door to have a look at one who have be fight the French , and push forward to have a grasp of the stranger 's hand as he give back the empty cup into the good wife 's keeping , for the kind homely woman be ever ready with milk or homebrewe to slake the feverish traveller 's thirst when he stop at their door and ask for a drink of water . 

 at the village public - house he have have a welcome of a more interested character , for the landlord know full well that his circle of customer would be large that night , if it be only know that he have within his door a soldier or a sailor who have see service . the rustic politician would gather round Philip , and smoke and drink , and then question and discuss till they be drouthy again ; and in their sturdy obtuse mind they set down the extra glass and the supernumerary pipe to the score of patriotism . 

 altogether human nature turn its sunny side out to Philip just now ; and not before he need the warmth of brotherly kindness to cheer his shivering soul . day after day he drift northward , make but the slow progress of a feeble man , and yet this short daily walk tired he so much that he long for rest for the morning to come when he need not to feel that in the course of an hour or two he must be up and away . 

 he be toil on with this longing at his heart when he see that he be draw near a stately city , with a great old cathedral in the centre keep solemn guard . this place might be yet two or three mile distant ; he be on a rise ground look down upon it . a labour man pass by , observe his pallid look and his languid attitude , and tell he for his comfort , that if he turn down a lane to the left a few step far on , he would find himself at the Hospital of St Sepulchre , where bread and beer be give to all comer , and where he might sit he down and rest awhile on the old stone bench within the shadow of the gateway . obey these direction , Philip come upon a building which date from the time of Henry the Fifth . some knight who have fight in the french war of that time , and have survive his battle and come home to his old hall , have be stir up by his conscience , or by what be equivalent in those day , his confessor , to build and endow a hospital for twelve decayed soldier , and a chapel wherein they be to attend the daily mass he ordain to be say till the end of all time   which eternity last rather more than a century , pretty well for an eternity bespeak by a man , for his soul and the soul of those whom he have slay . there be a large division of the quadrangular building set apart for the priest who be to say these mass ; and to watch over the well - being of the bedesman . in process of year the origin and primary purpose of the hospital have be forget by all except the local antiquary ; and the place itself come to be regard as a very pleasant quaint set of almshouse ; and the warden 's office   he who should have say or sing his daily mass be now call the warden , and read daily prayer and preach a sermon on Sundays   an agreeable sinecure . 

 another legacy of old Sir Simon Bray be that of a small croft of land , the rent or profit of which be to go towards give to all who ask for it a manchet of bread and a cup of good beer . this beer be , so Sir Simon ordain , to be make after a certain receipt which he leave , in which ground ivy take the place of hop . but the receipt , as well as the mass , be modernize accord to the progress of time . 

 Philip stand under a great broad stone archway ; the back - door into the warden 's house be on the right side ; a kind of buttery - hatch be place by the porter 's door on the opposite side . after some consideration , Philip knock at the closed shutter , and the signal seem to be well understand . he hear a movement within ; the hatch be draw aside , and his bread and beer be hand to he by a pleasant - look old man , who prove himself not at all disinclined for conversation . 

 ' you may sit down on yonder bench , ' say he . ' nay , man ! sit I ' the sun , for it be a chilly place , this , and then you can look through the grate and watch th ' old fellow toddle about in th ' quad . ' 

 Philip sit down where the warm October sun slant upon he , and look through the iron railing at the peaceful sight . 

 a great square of velvet lawn , intersect diagonally with broad flag - pave walk , the same kind of walk go all round the quadrangle ; low two - storied brick house , tint gray and yellow by age , and in many place almost cover with vine , virginian creeper , and monthly rose ; before each house a little plot of garden ground , bright with flower , and evidently tend with the utmost care ; on the farth side the massive chapel ; here and there an old or infirm man sun himself , or leisurely do a bit of gardening , or talk to one of his comrade the place look as if care and want , and even sorrow , be lock out and exclude by the ponderous gate through which Philip be gaze . 

 ' it be a nice enough place , bean't   it ? ' say the porter , interpret Philip 's look pretty accurately . ' leastway , for they as like it . I have   get a bit weary on it myself ; it be so far from th ' world , as a man may say ; not a decent public within a mile and a half , where one can hear a bit o ' news of an evening . ' 

 ' I think I could make myself very content here , ' reply Philip . ' that be to say , if one be easy in one 's mind . ' 

 ' ay , ay , my man . that be it everywhere . why , I do not   think that I could enjoy myself not even at th ' White Hart , where they give you as good a glass of ale for twopence as anywhere I ' th ' four kingdom I could not , to say , flavour my ale even there , if my old woman lie a - dying ; which be a sign as it be the heart , and not the ale , as make the drink . ' 

 just then the warden 's back - door open , and out come the warden himself , dress in full clerical costume . 

 he be go into the neighbour city , but he stop to speak to Philip , the wounded soldier ; and all the more readily because his old faded uniform tell the warden 's experienced eye that he have belong to the Marines . 

 ' I hope you enjoy the victual provide for you by the founder of St Sepulchre , ' say he , kindly . ' you look but poorly , my good fellow , and as if a slice of good cold meat would help your bread down . ' 

 ' thank you , sir ! ' say Philip . ' I be not hungry , only weary , and glad of a draught of beer . ' 

 ' you have   be in the Marines , I see . where have you be serve ? ' 

 ' I be at the siege of Acre , last May , sir . ' 

 ' at acre ! be you , indeed ? then perhaps you know my boy Harry ? he be in the th . ' 

 ' it be my company , ' say Philip , warm up a little . look back upon his soldier 's life , it seem to he to have many charm , because it be so full of small daily interest . 

 ' then , do you know my son , Lieutenant Pennington ? ' 

 ' it be he that give I this cloak , sir , when they be send I back to England . I have be his servant for a short time before I be wound by the explosion on board the Theseus , and he say I should feel the cold of the voyage . he be very kind ; and I have   hear say he promise to be a first - rate officer . ' 

 ' you shall have a slice of roast beef , whether you want it or not , ' say the warden , ring the bell at his own back - door . ' I recognize the cloak now the young scamp ! how soon he have make it shabby , though , ' he continue , take up a corner where there be an immense tear not too well botch up . ' and so you be on board the Theseus at the time of the explosion ? bring some cold meat here for the good man or stay ! come in with I , and then you can tell Mrs. Pennington and the young lady all you know about Harry , and the siege , and the explosion . ' 

 so Philip be usher into the warden 's house and make to eat roast beef almost against his will ; and he be question and cross - question by three eager lady , all at the same time , as it seem to he . he have give all possible detail on the subject about which they be curious ; and be begin to consider how he could well make his retreat , when the young Miss Pennington go up to her father who have all this time stand , with his hat on , hold his coat - tail over his arm , with his back to the fire . he bend his ear down a very little to hear some whisper suggestion of his daughter 's , nod his head , and then go on question Philip , with kindly inquisitiveness and patronage , as the rich do question the poor . 

 ' and where be you go to now ? ' 

 Philip do not answer directly . he wonder in his own mind where he be go . at length he say , 

 ' Northwards , I believe . but perhaps I shall never reach there . ' 

 ' have not   you friend ? be not   you go to they ? ' 

 there be again a pause ; a cloud come over Philip 's countenance . he say , 

 ' no ! I be not go to my friend . I do not   know that I have   get any left . ' 

 they interpret his look and this speech to mean that he have either lose his friend by death , or offend they by enlist . 

 the warden go on , 

 ' I ask , because we have   get a cottage vacant in the mead . old Dobson , who be with General Wolfe at the taking of Quebec , die a fortnight ago . with such injury as your , I fear you will never be able to work again . but we require strict testimonial as to character , ' he add , with as penetrate a look as he could summon up at Philip . 

 Philip look unmoved , either by the offer of the cottage , or the illusion to the possibility of his character not be satisfactory . he be grateful enough in reality , but too heavy at heart to care very much what become of he . 

 the warden and his family , who be accustomed to consider a settlement at St Sepulchre 's as the sum of all good to a wear - out soldier , be a little annoyed at Philip 's cool way of receive the proposition . the warden go on to name the contingent advantage . 

 ' besides the cottage , you would have a load of wood for fire on all saint ' , on Christmas , and on Candlemas day a blue gown and suit of clothe to match every Michaelmas , and a shilling a day to keep yourself in all other thing . your dinner you would have with the other man , in hall . ' 

 ' the warden himself go into hall every day , and see that everything be comfortable , and say grace , ' add the warden 's lady . 

 ' I know I seem stupid , ' say Philip , almost humbly , ' not to be more grateful , for it be far beyond what I iver expect or think for again , and it be a great temptation , for I be just wear out with fatigue . several time I have   think I must lie down under a hedge , and just die for very weariness . but once I have a wife and a child up in the north , ' he stop . 

 ' and be they dead ? ' ask one of the young lady in a soft sympathize tone . her eye meet Philip 's , full of dumb woe . he try to speak ; he want to explain more fully , yet not to reveal the truth . 

 ' well ! ' say the warden , think he perceive the real state of thing , ' what I propose be this . you shall go into old Dobson 's house at once , as a kind of probationary bedesman . I will write to Harry , and get your character from he . Stephen Freeman I think you say your name be ? before I can receive his reply you will have be able to tell how you would like the kind of life ; and at any rate you will have the rest you seem to require in the meantime . you see , I take Harry 's have give you that cloak as a kind of character , ' add he , smile kindly . ' of course you will have to conform to rule just like all the rest , chapel at eight , dinner at twelve , light out at nine ; but I will tell you the remainder of our regulation as we walk across quad to your new quarter . ' 

 and thus Philip , almost in spite of himself , become instal in a bedesman 's house at St Sepulchre . 




 CHAPTER XLII 

 a fable at fault 

 Philip take possession of the two room which have belong to the dead Sergeant Dobson . they be furnish sufficiently for every comfort by the trustee of the hospital . some little fragment of ornament , some small article pick up in distant country , a few tattered book , remain in the room as legacy from their former occupant . 

 at first the repose of the life and the place be inexpressibly grateful to Philip . he have always shrink from encounter stranger , and display his blacken and scarred countenance to they , even where such disfigurement be most regard as a mark of honour . in St Sepulchre 's he meet none but the same set day after day , and when he have once tell the tale of how it happen and submit to their gaze , it be over for ever , if he so minded . the slight employment his garden give he there be a kitchen - garden behind each house , as well as the flower - plot in front and the daily arrangement of his parlour and chamber be , at the beginning of his time of occupation , as much bodily labour as he could manage . there be something stately and utterly remove from all Philip 's previous existence in the form observe at every day 's dinner , when the twelve bedesman meet in the large quaint hall , and the warden come in his college - cap and gown to say the long latin grace which wind up with something very like a prayer for the soul of Sir Simon Bray . it take some time to get a reply to ship letter in those time when no one could exactly say where the fleet might be find . 

 and before Dr Pennington have receive the excellent character of Stephen Freeman , which his son gladly send in answer to his father 's inquiry , Philip have become restless and uneasy in the midst of all this peace and comfort . 

 sit alone over his fire in the long winter evening , the scene of his past life rise before he ; his childhood ; his aunt Robson 's care of he ; his first go to Foster 's shop in Monkshaven ; Haytersbank Farm , and the spelling lesson in the bright warm kitchen there ; Kinraid 's appearance ; the miserable night of the Corneys ' party ; the farewell he have witness on Monkshaven sand ; the press - gang , and all the long consequence of that act of concealment ; poor Daniel Robson 's trial and execution ; his own marriage ; his child 's birth ; and then he come to that last day at Monkshaven : and he go over and over again the torture detail , the look of contempt and anger , the word of loathe indignation , till he almost bring himself , out of his extreme sympathy with Sylvia , to believe that he be indeed the wretch she have consider he to be . 

 he forget his own excuse for have act as he have do ; though these excuse have at one time seem to he to wear the garb of reason . after long thought and bitter memory come some wonder . what be Sylvia do now ? where be she ? what be his child like his child as well as her ? and then he remember the poor footsore wife and the little girl she carry in her arm , that be just the age of Bella ; he wish he have notice that child more , that a clear vision of it might rise up when he want to picture Bella . 

 one night he have go round this mill - wheel circle of idea till he be weary to the very marrow of his bone . to shake off the monotonous impression he rise to look for a book amongst the old tattered volume , hop that he might find something that would sufficiently lay hold of he to change the current of his thought . there be an old volume of Peregrine Pickle ; a book of sermon ; half an army list of 1774 , and the Seven Champions of Christendom . Philip take up this last , which he have never see before . in it he read how Sir Guy , Earl of Warwick , go to fight the Paynim in his own country , and be away for seven long year ; and when he come back his own wife Phillis , the countess in her castle , do not know the poor travel - wear hermit , who come daily to seek his dole of bread at her hand along with many beggar and much poor . but at last , when he lie a - die in his cave in the rock , he send for she by a secret sign know but to they twain . and she come with great speed , for she know it be her lord who have send for she ; and they have many sweet and holy word together before he give up the ghost , his head lie on her bosom . 

 the old story know to most people from their childhood be all new and fresh to Philip . he do not quite believe in the truth of it , because the fictitious nature of the history of some of the other Champions of Christendom be too patent . but he could not help think that this one might be true ; and that Guy and Phillis might have be as real flesh and blood , long , long ago , as he and Sylvia have even be . the old room , the quiet moonlit quadrangle into which the cross - barred casement look , the quaint aspect of everything that he have see for week and week ; all this predispose Philip to dwell upon the story he have just be read as a faithful legend of two lover whose bone be long since dust . he think that if he could thus see Sylvia , himself unknown , unseen could live at her gate , so to speak , and gaze upon she and his child some day too , when he lie a - dying , he might send for she , and in soft word of mutual forgiveness breathe his life away in her arm . or perhaps and so he lose himself , and from think , pass on to dream . all night long Guy and Phillis , Sylvia and his child , pass in and out of his vision ; it be impossible to make the fragment of his dream cohere ; but the impression make upon he by they be not the less strong for this . he feel as if he be call to Monkshaven , want at Monkshaven , and to Monkshaven he resolve to go ; although when his reason overtake his feeling , he know perfectly how unwise it be to leave a home of peace and tranquillity and surround friendliness , to go to a place where nothing but want and wretchedness await he unless he make himself know ; and if he do , a deep want , a more woeful wretchedness , would in all probability be his portion . 

 in the small oblong of look - glass hang against the wall , Philip catch the reflection of his own face , and laugh scornfully at the sight . the thin hair lie upon his temple in the flake that betoken long ill - health ; his eye be the same as ever , and they have always be consider the good feature in his face ; but they be sink in their orbit , and look hollow and gloomy . as for the low part of his face , blacken , contract , draw away from his tooth , the outline entirely change by the breakage of his jaw - bone , he be indeed a fool if he think himself fit to go forth to win back that love which Sylvia have forswear . as a hermit and a beggar , he must return to Monkshaven , and fall perforce into the same position which Guy of Warwick have only assume . but still he should see his Phillis , and might feast his sad hopeless eye from time to time with the sight of his child . his small pension of sixpence a day would keep he from absolute want of necessary . 

 so that very day he go to the warden and tell he he think of give up his share in the bequest of Sir Simon Bray . such a relinquishment have never occur before in all the warden 's experience ; and he be very much inclined to be offend . 

 ' I must say that for a man not to be satisfied as a bedesman of St Sepulchre 's argue a very wrong state of mind , and a very ungrateful heart . ' 

 ' I be sure , sir , it be not from any ingratitude , for I can hardly feel thankful to you and to Sir Simon , and to madam , and the young lady , and all my comrade in the hospital , and I niver expect to be either so comfortable or so peaceful again , but ' 

 ' but ? what can you have to say against the place , then ? not but what there be always plenty of applicant for every vacancy ; only I think I be do a kindness to a man out of Harry 's company . and you will not see Harry either ; he be get his leave in March ! ' 

 ' I be very sorry . I should like to have see the lieutenant again . but I can not rest any long so far away from people I once know . ' 

 ' ten to one they be dead , or remove , or something or other by this time ; and it will serve you right if they be . mind ! no one can be choose twice to be a bedesman of St Sepulchre 's . ' 

 the warden turn away ; and Philip , uneasy at stay , dishearten at leave , go to make his few preparation for set out once more on his journey northward . he have to give notice of his change of residence to the local distributor of pension ; and one or two farewell have to be take , with more than usual sadness at the necessity ; for Philip , under his name of Stephen Freeman , have attach some of the old bedesman a good deal to he , from his unselfishness , his willingness to read to they , and to render they many little service , and , perhaps , as much as anything , by his habitual silence , which make he a convenient recipient of all their garrulousness . so before the time for his departure come , he have the opportunity of one more interview with the warden , of a more friendly character than that in which he give up his bedesmanship . and so far it be well ; and Philip turn his back upon St Sepulchre 's with his sore heart partly heal by his four month ' residence there . 

 he be strong , too , in body , more capable of the day - after - day walk that be require of he . he have save some money from his allowance as bedesman and from his pension , and might occasionally have take an outside place on a coach , have it not be that he shrink from the first look of every stranger upon his disfigured face . yet the gentle , wistful eye , and the white and faultless tooth always do away with the first impression as soon as people become a little acquainted with his appearance . 

 it be February when Philip leave St Sepulchre 's . it be the first week in April when he begin to recognize the familiar object between York and Monkshaven . and now he begin to hang back , and to question the wisdom of what he have do just as the warden have prophesy that he would . the last night of his two hundred mile walk he sleep at the little inn at which he have be enlist nearly two year before . it be by no intention of his that he rest at that identical place . night be draw on ; and , in make , as he think , a short cut , he have miss his way , and be fain to seek shelter where he might find it . but it bring he very straight face to face with his life at that time , and ever since . his mad , wild hope half the result of intoxication , as he now know all dead and go ; the career then freshly opening shut up against he now ; his youthful strength and health change into premature infirmity , and the home and the love that should have open wide its door to console he for all , why in two year death might have be busy , and take away from he his last feeble chance of the faint happiness of see his beloved without be see or know of she . all that night and all the next day , the fear of Sylvia 's possible death overcloud his heart . it be strange that he have hardly ever think of this before ; so strange , that now , when the terror come , it take possession of he , and he could almost have swear that she must be lie dead in Monkshaven churchyard . or be it little Bella , that blooming , lovely babe , whom he be never to see again ? there be the tolling of mournful bell in the distant air to his disturbed fancy , and the cry of the happy bird , the plaintive bleating of the new - drop lamb , be all omen of evil import to he . 

 as well as he could , he find his way back to Monkshaven , over the wild height and moor he have cross on that black day of misery ; why he should have choose that path he could not tell it be as if he be lead , and have no free will of his own . 

 the soft clear evening be draw on , and his heart beat thick , and then stop , only to start again with fresh violence . there he be , at the top of the long , steep lane that be in some part a literal staircase lead down from the hill - top into the High Street , through the very entry up which he have pass when he shrink away from his former and his then present life . there he stand , look down once more at the numerous irregular roof , the many stack of chimney below he , seek out that which have once be his own dwelling who dwelt there now ? 

 the yellower gleam grow narrower ; the evening shadow broader , and Philip creep down the lane a weary , woeful man . at every gap in the close - pack building he hear the merry music of a band , the cheerful sound of excited voice . still he descend slowly , scarcely wonder what it could be , for it be not associate in his mind with the one pervading thought of Sylvia . 

 when he come to the angle of junction between the lane and the High Street , he seem plunge all at once into the very centre of the bustle , and he draw himself up into a corner of deep shadow , from whence he could look out upon the street . 

 a circus be make its grand entry into Monkshaven , with all the pomp of colour and of noise that it could muster . trumpeter in parti - colour clothe ride first , blare out triumphant discord . next come a gold - and - scarlet chariot draw by six piebald horse , and the winding of this team through the tortuous narrow street be pretty enough to look upon . in the chariot sate king and queen , hero and heroine , or what be mean for such ; all the little boy and girl run alongside of the chariot envy they ; but they themselves be very much tired , and shiver with cold in their heroic pomp of classic clothing . all this Philip might have see ; do see , in fact ; but heed not one jot . almost opposite to he , not ten yard apart , stand on the raise step at the well - know shop door , be Sylvia , hold a child , a merry dancing child , up in her arm to see the show . she too , Sylvia , be laugh for pleasure , and for sympathy with pleasure . she hold the little Bella aloft that the child might see the gaudy procession the well and the long , look at it herself with red lip apart and white tooth glance through ; then she turn to speak to some one behind her Coulson , as Philip see the moment afterwards ; his answer make her laugh once again . Philip see it all ; her bonny careless look , her pretty matronly form , her evident ease of mind and prosperous outward circumstance . the year that he have spend in gloomy sorrow , amongst wild scene , on land or by sea , his life in frequent peril of a bloody end , have go by with she like sunny day ; all the more sunny because he be not there . so bitterly think the poor disabled marine , as , weary and despairing , he stand in the cold shadow and look upon the home that should have be his haven , the wife that should have welcome he , the child that should have be his comfort . he have banish himself from his home ; his wife have forswear he ; his child be blossom into intelligence unwitting of any father . Wife , and child , and home , be all do well without he ; what madness have tempt he thither ? an hour ago , like a fanciful fool , he have think she might be dead dead with sad penitence for her cruel word at her heart with mournful wonder at the unaccounted - for absence of her child 's father preying on her spirit , and in some measure cause the death he have apprehend . but to look at she there where she stand , it do not seem as if she have have an hour 's painful thought in all her bloom life . 

 ay ! go in to the warm hearth , mother and child , now the gay cavalcade have go out of sight , and the chill of night have succeed to the sun 's setting . husband and father , steal out into the cold dark street , and seek some poor cheap lodging where you may rest your weary bone , and cheat your more weary heart into forgetfulness in sleep . the pretty story of the Countess Phillis , who mourn for her husband 's absence so long , be a fable of old time ; or rather say Earl Guy never wed his wife , know that one she love well than he be alive all the time she have believe he to be dead . 




 CHAPTER XLIII 

 the unknown 

 a few day before that on which Philip arrive at Monkshaven , Kester have come to pay Sylvia a visit . as the early friend she have , and also as one who know the real secret of her life , Sylvia always give he the warm welcome , the cordial word , and the sweet look in which the old man delight . he have a sort of delicacy of his own which keep he from go to see she too often , even when he be stationary at Monkshaven ; but he look forward to the time when he allow himself this pleasure as a child at school look forward to its holiday . the time of his service at Haytersbank have , on the whole , be the happy in all his long monotonous year of daily labour . Sylvia 's father have always treat he with the rough kindness of fellowship ; Sylvia 's mother have never stint he in his meat or grudge he his share of the good that be go ; and once , when he be ill for a few day in the loft above the cow - house , she have make he posset , and nurse he with the same tenderness which he remember his mother show to he when he be a little child , but which he have never experience since then . he have know Sylvia herself , as bud , and sweet promise of blossom ; and just as she be open into the full - blown rose , and , if she have be happy and prosperous , might have pass out of the narrow circle of Kester 's interest , one sorrow after another come down upon her pretty innocent head , and Kester 's period of service to Daniel Robson , her father , be tragically cut short . all this make Sylvia the great centre of the faithful herdsman 's affection ; and Bella , who remind he of what Sylvia be when first Kester know she , only occupy the second place in his heart , although to the child he be much more demonstrative of his regard than to the mother . 

 he have dress himself in his Sunday good , and although it be only Thursday , have forestall his Saturday 's shaving ; he have provide himself with a paper of humbug for the child ' humbug ' be the north - country term for certain lump of toffy , well - flavour with peppermint and now he sit in the accustomed chair , as near to the door as might be , in Sylvia 's presence , coax the little one , who be not quite sure of his identity , to come to he , by open the paper parcel , and let its sweet content be see . 

 ' she be like thee and yet she favour her feyther , ' say he ; and the moment he have utter the incautious word he look up to see how Sylvia have take the unpremeditated , unusual reference to her husband . his stealthy glance do not meet her eye ; but though he think she have colour a little , she do not seem offend as he have fear . it be true that Bella have her father 's grave , thoughtful , dark eye , instead of her mother 's gray one , out of which the childlike expression of wonder would never entirely pass away . and as Bella slowly and half distrustfully make her way towards the temptation offer she , she look at Kester with just her father 's look . 

 Sylvia say nothing in direct reply ; Kester almost think she could not have hear he . but , by - and - by , she say , 

 ' Yo'll have hear how Kinraid who be a captain now , and a grand officer have go and get marry . ' 

 ' Nay ! ' say Kester , in genuine surprise . ' he niver have , for sure ! ' 

 ' ay , but he have , ' say Sylvia . ' and I be sure I dunnot see why he should not . ' 

 ' well , well ! ' say Kester , not look up at she , for he catch the inflection in the tone of her voice . ' he be a fine stirrin ' chap , yon ; an ' he be allay for do summut ; an ' when he fund as he could not   ha ' one thing as he would set his mind on , a reckon he think he mun put up wi ' another . ' 

 ' it ' ud be no " put up , " ' say Sylvia . ' she be stay at Bessy Dawson 's , and she come here to see I she be as pretty a young lady as yo'd see on a summer 's day ; and a real lady , too , wi ' a fortune . she do not   speak two word wi'out bring in her husband 's name , " the captain " , as she call he . ' 

 ' An ' she come to see thee ? ' say Kester , cock his eye at Sylvia with the old shrewd look . ' that be summut queer , be not   it ? ' 

 Sylvia redden a good deal . 

 ' he be too fause to have speak to she on I , in t ' old way , as he use for t ' speak to I . I be nought to she but Philip 's wife . ' 

 ' An ' what t ' dickin have she to do wi ' Philip ? ' ask Kester , in intense surprise ; and so absorb in curiosity that he let the humbug all fall out of the paper upon the floor , and the little Bella sit down , plump , in the midst of treasure as great as those fabled to exist on Tom Tiddler 's ground . 

 Sylvia be again silent ; but Kester , know she well , be sure that she be struggle to speak , and bid his time without repeat his question . 

 ' she say and I think her tale be true , though I can not get to t ' right on it , think on it as I will as Philip save her husband 's life somewheere nearabout to Jerusalem . she would have it that t ' captain for I think I will niver can ' he Kinraid again be in a great battle , and be near upon be shoot by t ' French , when Philip our Philip come up and go right into t ' fire o ' t ' gun , and save her husband 's life . and she speak as if both she and t ' captain be more beholden to Philip than word could tell . and she come to see I , to try and get news on he . 

 ' it be a queer kind o ' story , ' say Kester , meditatively . ' a should ha ' thought as Philip be more likely to ha ' gi'en he a shove into t ' thick on it , than t ' help he out o ' t ' scrape . ' 

 ' Nay ! ' say Sylvia , suddenly look straight at Kester ; ' yo're out theere . Philip have a deal o ' good in he . and I dunnot think as he would ha ' go and marry another woman so soon , if he have be I ' Kinraid 's place . ' 

 ' an ' yo've   niver heared on Philip sin ' he leave ? ' ask Kester , after a while . 

 ' Niver ; nought but what she tell I . and she say that t ' captain make inquiry for he right and leave , as soon after that happen as might be , and could hear niver a word about he . no one have see he , or know his name . ' 

 ' yo ' niver heared of his go for t ' be a soldier ? ' persevere Kester . 

 ' Niver . I have   tell yo ' once . it be unlike Philip to think o ' such a thing . ' 

 ' but thou mun ha ' be thinkin ' on he at time I ' a ' these year . bad as he have behave hissel ' , he be t ' feyther o ' thy little un . what do ta think he have be agait on when he leave here ? ' 

 ' I do not   know . I be noane so keen a - thinking on he at first . I try to put he out o ' my thought a'together , for it make I like mad to think how he have stand between I and that other . but I would begin to wonder and to wonder about he , and to think I should like to hear as he be do well . I reckon I think he be I ' London , wheere he have be that time afore , yo ' know , and have allay speak as if he have enjoy hissel ' tolerable ; and then Molly Brunton tell I on t ' other one 's marriage ; and , somehow , it give I a shake in my heart , and I begin for to wish I have not   say all they word I ' my passion ; and then that fine young lady come wi ' her story and I have   think a deal on it since , and my mind have come out clear . Philip 's dead , and it be his spirit as come to t ' other 's help in his time o ' need . I have   hear feyther say as spirit can not rest I ' their graf for try to undo t ' wrong they have   do I ' their body . ' 

 ' they be my conclusion , ' say Kester , solemnly . ' a be fain for to hear what be yo'r judgment first ; but they be the conclusion I come to as soon as I hear t ' tale . ' 

 ' let alone that one thing , ' say Sylvia , ' he be a kind , good man . ' 

 ' it be a big deal on a " one thing " , though , ' say Kester . ' it just spoil yo'r life , my poor lass ; an ' might ha ' go near to spoilin ' Charley Kinraid 's too . ' 

 ' man take a deal more nor woman to spoil their life , ' say Sylvia , bitterly . 

 ' not a ' mak ' o ' man . I reckon , lass , Philip 's life be pretty well on for bein ' spoilt at after he leave here ; and it be , mebbe , a good thing he get rid on it so soon . ' 

 ' I wish I would just have a few kind word wi ' he , I do , ' say Sylvia , almost on the point of cry . 

 ' come , lass , it be as ill moanin ' after what be past as it ' ud be for I t ' fill my eye wi ' weepin ' after t ' humbug as this little wench o ' thine have grub up whilst we'n be talkin ' . why , there be not one on them leave ! ' 

 ' she be a sad spoilt little puss ! ' say Sylvia , hold out her arm to the child , who run into they , and begin pat her mother 's cheek , and pull at the soft brown curl tuck away beneath the matronly cap . ' Mammy spoil she , and hester spoil she ' 

 ' Granny Rose do not   spoil I , ' say the child , with quick , intelligent discrimination , interrupt her mother 's list . 

 ' no ; but Jeremiah Foster do above a bit . he will come in fro ' t ' Bank , Kester , and ask for she , a'most ivery day . and he will bring her thing in his pocket ; and she be so fause , she allay go straight to peep in , and then he shift t ' apple or t ' toy into another . eh ! but she be a little fause one , ' half devour the child with her kiss . ' and he come and take she a walk oftentime , and he go as slow as if he be quite an old man , to keep pace wi ' Bella 's step . I often run upstairs and watch them out o ' t ' window ; he do not   care to have I with them , he be so fain t ' have t ' child all to hisself . ' 

 ' she be a bonny un , for sure , ' say Kester ; ' but not so pretty as thou be , Sylvie . A've   niver tell'd thee what a come for tho ' , and it be about time for I t ' be go . A'm off to t ' cheviot to - morrow morn t ' fetch home some sheep as Jonas Blundell have purchase . it will be a job o ' well nor two month a reckon . ' 

 ' it will be a nice time o ' year , ' say Sylvia , a little surprised at Kester 's evident discouragement at the prospect of the journey or absence ; he have often be away from Monkshaven for a long time without seem to care so much about it . 

 ' well , yo ' see it be a bit hard upon I for t ' leave my sister she as be t ' widow - woman , wheere a put up when a'm at home . thing be main an ' dear ; four - pound loaf be at sixteenpence ; an ' there be a deal o ' talk on a famine I ' t ' land ; an ' whaten a pay for my victual an ' t ' bed I ' t ' lean - to help t ' oud woman a bit , an ' she be sadly down I ' t ' mouth , for she can not hear on a lodger for t ' tak ' my place , for a ' she be move o'er to t ' other side o ' t ' bridge for t ' be near t ' new building , an ' t ' grand new walk they be makin ' round t ' cliff , thinkin ' she would be likeli t ' pick up a labourer as would be glad on a bed near his work . A'd ha ' like to ha ' set her agait wi ' a ' sponsible lodger afore a'd ha ' left , for she be just so soft - hearted , any scamp may put upon she if he nobbut get houd on her blind side . ' 

 ' can I help she ? ' say Sylvia , in her eager way . ' I should be so glad ; and I have   a deal of money by I - ' 

 ' Nay , my lass , ' say Kester , ' thou munnot go off so fast ; it be just what I be fear on i ' tellin ' thee . I have   leave she a bit o ' money , and I will mak ' shift to send she more ; it be just a kind word , t ' keep up her heart when I be go , as I want . if thou'd step in and see her fra ' time to time , and cheer she up a bit wi ' talkin ' to she on I , I would tak ' it very kind , and I would go off wi ' a light heart . ' 

 ' then I be sure I will do it for yo ' , Kester . I niver justly feel like mysel ' when yo're away , for I be lonesome enough at time . she and I will talk a ' t ' well about yo ' for both on we grieve after yo ' . ' 

 so Kester take his leave , his mind set at ease by Sylvia 's promise to go and see his sister pretty often during his absence in the North . 

 but Sylvia 's habit be change since she , as a girl at Haytersbank , like to spend half her time in the open air , run out perpetually without anything on to scatter crumb to the poultry , or to take a piece of bread to the old cart - horse , to go up to the garden for a handful of herb , or to clamber to the high point around to blow the horn which summon her father and Kester home to dinner . live in a town where it be necessary to put on hat and cloak before go out into the street , and then to walk in a steady and decorous fashion , she have only care to escape down to the freedom of the sea - shore until Philip go away ; and after that time she have learn so to fear observation as a deserted wife , that nothing but Bella 's health would have be a sufficient motive to take she out of door . and , as she have tell Kester , the necessity of give the little girl a daily walk be very much lighten by the great love and affection which Jeremiah Foster now bear to the child . ever since the day when the baby have come to his knee , allure by the temptation of his watch , he have apparently consider she as in some sort belong to he ; and now he have almost come to think that he have a right to claim she as his companion in his walk back from the Bank to his early dinner , where a high chair be always place ready for the chance of she come to share his meal . on these occasion he generally bring she back to the shop - door when he return to his afternoon 's work at the Bank . sometimes , however , he would leave word that she be to be send for from his house in the New Town , as his business at the Bank for that day be end . then Sylvia be compel to put on her thing , and fetch back her darling ; and except for this errand she seldom go out at all on week - day . 

 about a fortnight after Kester 's farewell call , this need for her visit to Jeremiah Foster 's arise ; and it seem to Sylvia that there could not be a well opportunity of fulfil her promise and go to see the widow Dobson , whose cottage be on the other side of the river , low down on the cliff - side , just at the bend and rush of the full stream into the open sea . she set off pretty early in order to go there first . she find the widow with her house - place tidy up after the midday meal , and busy knitting at the open door not look at her rapid - click needle , but gaze at the rush and recession of the wave before she ; yet not see they either , rather see day long past . 

 she start into active civility as soon as she recognize Sylvia , who be to she as a great lady , never have know Sylvia Robson in her wild childish day . Widow Dobson be always a little scandalize at her brother Christopher 's familiarity with Mrs. Hepburn . 

 she dust a chair which need no dusting , and place it for Sylvia , sit down herself on a three - legged stool to mark her sense of the difference in their condition , for there be another chair or two in the humble dwelling ; and then the two fall into talk first about Kester , whom his sister would persist in call Christopher , as if his dignity as her eld brother be compromise by any familiar abbreviation ; and by - and - by she open her heart a little more . 

 ' a could wish as a'd learn write - of - hand , ' say she ; ' for a've   that for to tell Christopher as might set his mind at ease . but yo ' see , if a write he a letter he could not   read it ; so a just comfort mysel ' wi ' thinkin ' nobody need learn writin ' unless they'n get friend as can read . but a reckon he would ha ' be glad to hear as a've   getten a lodger . ' here she nod her head in the direction of the door open out of the house - place into the ' lean - to ' , which Sylvia have observe on draw near the cottage , and the recollection of the mention of which by Kester have enable she to identify widow Dobson 's dwelling . ' he be a - bed yonder , ' the latter continue , drop her voice . ' he be a queer - lookin ' tyke , but a do not   think as he be a bad un . ' 

 ' when do he come ? ' say Sylvia , remember Kester 's account of his sister 's character , and feeling as though it behove she , as Kester 's confidante on this head , to give cautious and prudent advice . 

 ' eh ! a matter of a s'ennight ago . a'm noane good at mindin ' time ; he be pay I his rent twice , but then he be keen to pay aforehand . he have come in one night , an ' sate he down afore he could speak , he be so do up ; he would be on tramp this many a day , a reckon . " can yo ' give I a bed ? " say he , pant like , after a bit . " a chap as a met near here say as yo've   a lodging for t ' let . " " ay , " say a , " a ha ' that ; but yo ' mun pay I a shilling a week for ' t. " then my mind misgive I , for a thought he have not   a shilling i ' t ' world , an ' yet if he have not , a should just ha ' gi'en he t ' bed a ' t ' same : a'm not one as can turn a dog out if he come t ' I weary o ' his life . so he out wi ' a shillin ' , an ' lay it down on t ' table , 'bout a word . " a'll not trouble yo ' long , " say he . " A'm one as be good out o ' t ' world , " he say . then a thought as a'd be a bit hard upon he . an ' say I , " A'm a widow - woman , and one as have getten but few friend : " for yo ' see a be low about our Christopher 's go away north ; ' so a'm force - like to speak hard to folk ; but a've   make mysel ' some stirabout for my supper ; and if yo'd like t ' share an ' share about wi ' I , it be but puttin ' a sup more watter to ' t , and God 's blessing will be on ' t , just as same as if ' t be meal . " so he up wi ' his hand afore his e'en , and say not a word . at last he say , " Missus , " say he , " can God 's blessing be share by a sinner one o ' t ' devil 's child ? " say he . " for the Scriptur ' say he be t ' father o ' lie . " so a be puzzle - like ; an ' at length a say , " thou mun ask t ' parson that ; a'm but a poor faint - hearted widow - woman ; but a've   allay have God 's blessing somehow , now a bethink I , an ' a'll share it wi ' thee as far as my will go . " so he raxe his hand across t ' table , an ' mutter summat , as he grip mine . a think it be Scriptur ' as he say , but a'd need a ' my strength just then for t ' lift t ' pot off t ' fire it be t ' first vittle a'd taste sin ' morn , for t ' famine come down like stone on t ' head o ' us poor folk : an ' a ' a said be just " Coom along , chap , an ' fa ' to ; an ' God 's blessing be on he as eat most . " an ' sin ' that day he and I be be as thick as thief , only he be niver tell I nought of who he be , or wheere he come fra ' . but a think he be one o ' they poor collier , as have getten brunt I ' t ' coal - pit ; for , t ' be sure , his face be a ' black wi ' fire - mark ; an ' o ' late day he be ta'en t ' his bed , an ' just lie there sigh , for one can hear he plain as dayleet thro ' t ' bit partition wa ' . ' 

 as a proof of this , a sigh almost a groan startle the two woman at this very moment . 

 ' poor fellow ! ' say Sylvia , in a soft whisper . ' there be more sore heart I ' t ' world than one reckon for ! ' but after a while , she bethink she again of Kester 's account of his sister 's ' softness ' ; and she think that it behove she to give some good advice . so she add , in a sterner , hard tone ' still , yo ' say yo ' know nought about he ; and tramp be tramp a ' t ' world over ; and yo're a widow , and it behove yo ' to be careful . I think I would just send he off as soon as he be a bit rest . Yo ' say he be plenty o ' money ? ' 

 ' Nay ! a never say that . a know nought about it . he pay I aforehand ; an ' he pay I down for whativer a've   getten for he ; but that be but little ; he be noane up t ' his vittle , though a've   make he some broth as good as a could make them . ' 

 ' I would not   send he away till he be well again , if I be yo ; but I think yo'd be well rid on he , ' say Sylvia . ' it would be different if yo'r brother be in Monkshaven . ' as she speak she rise to go . 

 Widow Dobson hold her hand in hers for a minute , then the humble woman say , 

 ' Yo'll noane be vex wi ' me , missus , if a can not find I ' my heart t ' turn he out till he want to go hissel ' ? for a would not   like to vex yo ' , for Christopher 's sake ; but a know what it be for t ' feel for friendless folk , an ' choose what may come on it , I can not send he away . ' 

 ' no ! ' say Sylvia . ' why should I be vex ? it be no business o ' mine . only I should send he away if I be yo ' . he might go lodge wheere there be man - folk , who know t ' way o ' tramp , and be up to they . ' 

 into the sunshine go Sylvia . in the cold shadow the miserable tramp lay sighing . she do not know that she have be so near to he towards whom her heart be soften , day by day . 




 CHAPTER XLIV 

 first word 

 it be the spring of 1800 . old people yet can tell of the hard famine of that year . the harvest of the autumn before have fail ; the war and the corn law have bring the price of corn up to a famine rate ; and much of what come into the market be unsound , and consequently unfit for food , yet hungry creature buy it eagerly , and try to cheat disease by mix the damp , sweet , clammy flour with rice or potato meal . rich family deny themselves pastry and all unnecessary and luxurious us of wheat in any shape ; the duty on hair - powder be increase ; and all these palliative be but as drop in the ocean of the great want of the people . 

 Philip , in spite of himself , recover and grow strong ; and as he grow strong hunger take the place of loathe dislike to food . but his money be all spend ; and what be his poor pension of sixpence a day in that terrible year of famine ? many a summer 's night he walk for hour and hour round the house which once be his , which might be his now , with all its homely , blessed comfort , could he but go and assert his right to it . but to go with authority , and in his poor , maim guise assert that right , he have need be other than Philip Hepburn . so he stand in the old shelter of the steep , crooked lane opening on to the hill out of the market - place , and watch the soft fading of the summer 's eve into night ; the closing of the once familiar shop ; the exit of good , comfortable William Coulson , go to his own home , his own wife , his comfortable , plentiful supper . then Philip there be no police in those day , and scarcely an old watchman in that primitive little town would go round on the shady side of street , and , quickly glance about he , cross the bridge , look on the quiet , ripple stream , the gray shimmer foretell the come dawn over the sea , the black mast and rigging of the still vessel against the sky ; he could see with his wistful , eager eye the shape of the window the window of the very room in which his wife and child slept , unheede of he , the hungry , break - hearted outcast . he would go back to his lodging , and softly lift the latch of the door ; still more softly , but never without an unspoken , grateful prayer , pass by the poor sleep woman who have give he a shelter and her share of God 's blessing she who , like he , know not the feeling of satisfied hunger ; and then he lay he down on the narrow pallet in the lean - to , and again give Sylvia happy lesson in the kitchen at Haytersbank , and the dead be alive ; and Charley Kinraid , the specksioneer , have never come to trouble the hopeful , gentle peace . 

 for widow Dobson have never take Sylvia 's advice . the tramp know to she by the name of Freeman that in which he receive his pension lodge with she still , and pay his meagre shilling in advance , weekly . a shilling be meagre in those hard day of scarcity . a hungry man might easily eat the produce of a shilling in a day . 

 Widow Dobson plead this to Sylvia as an excuse for keep her lodger on ; to a more calculate head it might have seem a reason for send he away . 

 ' Yo ' see , missus , ' say she , apologetically , to Sylvia , one evening , as the latter call upon the poor widow before go to fetch little Bella   it be now too hot for the child to cross the bridge in the full heat of the summer sun , and Jeremiah would take she up to her supper instead   ' yo ' see , missus , there be not a many as ' ud take he in for a shillin ' when it go so little way ; or if they do , they would take it out on he some other way , an ' he be not getten much else , a reckon . he can be I granny , but a'm vast mista'en if he be ten year young nor I ; but he be getten a fine appetite of his own , choose how young he may be ; an ' a can see as he could eat a deal more nor he be getten money to buy , an ' it be few as can mak ' victual go far nor I . eh , missus , but yo ' may trust I a'll send he off when time be well ; but just now it would be sendin ' he to his death ; for a ha ' plenty and to spare , thank be to God an ' yo'r bonny face . ' 

 so Sylvia have to be content with the knowledge that the money she gladly give to Kester 's sister go partly to feed the lodger who be neither labourer nor neighbour , but only just a tramp , who , she fear , be prey on the good old woman . still the cruel famine cut sharp enough to penetrate all heart ; and Sylvia , an hour after the conversation record above , be much touch , on her return from Jeremiah Foster 's with the little merry , chatter Bella , at see the feeble step of one , whom she know by description must be widow Dobson 's lodger , turn up from the newly - cut road which be to lead to the terrace walk around the North Cliff , a road which lead to no dwelling but widow Dobson 's . tramp , and vagrant , he might be in the eye of the law ; but , whatever his character , Sylvia could see he before she in the soft dusk , creep along , over the bridge , often stop to rest and hold by some support , and then go on again towards the town , to which she and happy little Bella be wend . 

 a thought come over she : she have always fancy that this unknown man be some fierce vagabond , and have dread lest in the lonely bit of road between widow Dobson 's cottage and the peopled highway , he should fall upon she and rob she if he learn that she have money with she ; and several time she have go away without leave the little gift she have intend , because she imagine that she have see the door of the small chamber in the ' lean - to ' open softly while she be there , as if the occupant   whom widow Dobson speak of as never leave the house before dusk , except once a week   be listen for the chink of the coin in her little leathern purse . now that she see he walk before she with heavy languid step , this fear give place to pity ; she remember her mother 's gentle superstition which have prevent she from ever send the hungry empty away , for fear lest she herself should come to need bread . 

 ' Lassie , ' say she to little Bella , who hold a cake which Jeremiah 's housekeeper have give she tight in her hand , ' yon poor man theere be hungry ; will Bella give he her cake , and mother will make she another to - morrow twice as big ? ' 

 for this consideration , and with the feeling of satisfaction which a good supper not an hour ago give even to the hungry stomach of a child of three year old , Bella , after some thought , graciously assent to the sacrifice . 

 Sylvia stop , the cake in her hand , and turn she back to the town , and to the slow wayfarer in front . under the cover of her shawl she slip a half - crown deep into the crumb of the cake , and then restore it to little Bella , she give she her direction . 

 ' Mammy will carry Bella ; and when Bella go past the poor man , she shall give he the cake over mammy 's shoulder . poor man be so hungry ; and Bella and mammy have plenty to eat , and to spare . ' 

 the child 's heart be touch by the idea of hunger , and her little arm be outstretche ready for the moment her mother 's hurried step take her brushing past the startle , tremble Philip . 

 ' poor man , eat this ; Bella not hungry . ' 

 they be the first word he have ever hear his child utter . the echo of they rang in his ear as he stand endeavour to hide his disfigured face by look over the parapet of the bridge down upon the stream run away towards the ocean , into which his hot tear slowly fall , unheede by the weeper . then he change the intention with which he have set out upon his nightly walk , and turn back to his lodging . 

 of course the case be different with Sylvia ; she would have forget the whole affair very speedily , if it have not be for little Bella 's frequent recurrence to the story of the hungry man , which have touch her small sympathy with the sense of an intelligible misfortune . she like to act the dropping of the bun into the poor man 's hand as she go past he , and would take up any article near she in order to illustrate the gesture she have use . one day she get hold of hester 's watch for this purpose , as be of the same round shape as the cake ; and though hester , for whose benefit the child be repeat the story in her broken language for the third or fourth time , try to catch the watch as it be intend that she should   she be the representative of the ' hungry man ' for the time be , it go to the ground with a smash that frighten the little girl , and she begin to cry at the mischief she have do . 

 ' do not   cry , Bella , ' say hester . ' niver play with watch again . I do not   see thee at mine , or I would ha ' stop thee in time . but I will take it to old Darley 's on th ' quay - side , and maybe he will soon set it to right again . only Bella must niver play with watch again . ' 

 ' Niver no more ! ' promise the little sob child . and that evening Hester take she watch down to old Darley 's . 

 this William Darley be the brother of the gardener at the rectory ; the uncle to the sailor who have be shoot by the press - gang year before , and to his bed - ride sister . he be a clever mechanician , and his skill as a repairer of watch and chronometer be great among the sailor , with whom he do a very irregular sort of traffic , conduct , often without much use of money , but rather on the principle of barter , they bring he foreign coin and odd curiosity pick up on their travel in exchange for his service to their nautical instrument or their watch . if he have ever have capital to extend his business , he might have be a rich man ; but it be to be doubt whether he would have be as happy as he be now in his queer little habitation of two room , the front one be both shop and workshop , the other serve the double purpose of bedroom and museum . 

 the skill of this odd - temper , shabby old man be sometimes seek by the jeweller who keep the more ostentatious shop in the High Street ; but before Darley would undertake any ' tickle ' piece of delicate workmanship for the other , he sneer at his ignorance , and taunt and abuse he well . yet he have soft place in his heart , and Hester Rose have find her way to one by her patient , endure kindness to his bed - ride niece . he never snarl at she as he do at too many ; and on the few occasion when she have ask he to do anything for she , he have seem as if she be confer the favour on he , not he on she , and only make the small possible charge . 

 she find he now sit where he could catch the most light for his work , spectacle on nose , and microscope in hand . 

 he take her watch , and examine it carefully without a word in reply to she . then he begin to open it and take it to piece , in order to ascertain the nature of the mischief . 

 suddenly he hear she catch her breath with a check sound of surprise . he look at she from above his spectacle ; she be hold a watch in her hand which she have just take up off the counter . 

 ' what be amiss wi ' thee now ? ' say Darley . ' hast ta niver see a watch o ' that mak ' afore ? or be it they letter on t ' back , as be so wonderful ? ' 

 yes , it be those letter that interlace , old - fashioned cipher . that Z. H. that she know of old stand for Zachary Hepburn , Philip 's father . she know how Philip value this watch . she remember have see it in his hand the very day before his disappearance , when he be look at the time in his annoyance at Sylvia 's detention in her walk with baby . hester have no doubt that he have take this watch as a matter of course away with he . she feel sure that he would not part with this relic of his dead father on any slight necessity . where , then , be Philip ? by what chance of life or death have this , his value property , find its way once more to monkshaven ? 

 ' where do yo ' get this ? ' she ask , in as quiet a manner as she could assume , sick with eagerness as she be . 

 to no one else would Darley have answer such a question . he make a mystery of most of his dealing ; not that he have anything to conceal , but simply because he delight in concealment . he take it out of her hand , look at the number mark inside , and the maker 's name ' Natteau Gent , York ' and then reply , 

 ' a man bring it I yesterday , at nightfall , for t ' sell it . it be a matter o ' forty year old . Natteau Gent have be dead and in his grave pretty nigh as long as that . but he do his work well when he be alive ; and so I give he as bring it for t ' sell about as much as it be worth , I ' good coin . a try he first I ' t ' bartering line , but he would not   bite ; like enough he want food , many a one do now - a - day . ' 

 ' who be he ? ' gasp Hester . 

 ' Bless t ' woman ! how should I know ? ' 

 ' what be he like ? how old ? tell I . ' 

 ' my lass , a've   summut else to do wi ' my eye than go peer into man 's face I ' t ' dusk light . ' 

 ' but yo ' must have have light for t ' judge about the watch . ' 

 ' eh ! how sharp we be ! A'd a candle close to my nose . but a do not   tak ' it up for to gaze int ' his face . that would not   be manner , to my thinking . ' 

 hester be silent . then Darley 's heart relent . 

 ' if yo're so set upo ' know who t ' fellow be , a could , mebbe , put yo ' on his track . ' 

 ' how ? ' say hester , eagerly . ' I do want to know . I want to know very much , and for a good reason . ' 

 ' well , then , a'll tell yo ' . he be a queer tyke , that one be . A'll be bind he be sore press for t ' brass ; yet he out 's wi ' a good half - crown , all wrap up I ' paper , and he ax I t ' make a hole in it . say I , " it be mar good king 's coin , at after a've   make a hole in't , it will never pass current again . " so he mumble , and mumble , but for a ' that it must need be do ; and he be leave it here , and be t ' call for ' t to - morrow at e'en . ' 

 ' oh , William Darley ! ' say Hester , clasp her hand tight together . ' find out who he be , where he be anything everything about he and I will so bless yo ' . ' 

 Darley look at she sharply , but with some sign of sympathy on his grave face . ' my woman , ' he say ' a could ha ' wish as you would niver see t ' watch . it be poor , thankless work think too much on one o ' God 's creature . but a'll do thy bidding , ' he continue , in a light and different tone . ' A'm a ' cute old badger when need be . come for thy watch in a couple o ' day , and a'll tell yo ' all as a've   learn . ' 

 so Hester go away , her heart beat with the promise of know something about Philip , how much , how little , in these first moment , she dare not say even to herself . some sailor newly land from distant sea might have become possess of Philip 's watch in far - off latitude ; in which case , Philip would be dead . that might be . she try to think that this be the most probable way of accounting for the watch . she could be certain as to the positive identity of the watch be in William Darley 's possession . again , it might be that Philip himself be near at hand be here in this very place starve , as too many be , for insufficiency of mean to buy the high - price food . and then her heart burn within she as she think of the succulent , comfortable meal which Sylvia provide every day nay , three time a day for the household in the market - place , at the head of which Philip ought to have be ; but his place know he not . for Sylvia have inherit her mother 's talent for housekeeping , and on she , in Alice 's decrepitude and hester 's other occupation in the shop , devolve the care of due provision for the somewhat heterogeneous family . 

 and Sylvia ! hester groan in heart over the remembrance of Sylvia 's word , ' I can niver forgive he the wrong he do to I , ' that night when Hester have come , and clung to she , make the sad , shameful confession of her unreturned love . 

 what could ever bring these two together again ? could hester herself ignorant of the strange mystery of Sylvia 's heart , as those who be guide solely by obedience to principle must ever be of the clue to the action of those who be lead by the passionate ebb and flow of impulse ? could hester herself ? oh ! how should she speak , how should she act , if Philip be near if Philip be sad and in miserable estate ? her own misery at this contemplation of the case be too great to bear ; and she seek her usual refuge in the thought of some text , some promise of Scripture , which should strengthen her faith . 

 ' with God all thing be possible , ' say she , repeat the word as though to lull her anxiety to rest . 

 yes ; with God all thing be possible . but ofttime he do his work with awful instrument . there be a peacemaker whose name be death . 




 CHAPTER XLV 

 SAVED and lose 

 Hester go out on the evening of the day after that on which the unknown owner of the half - crown have appoint to call for it again at William Darley 's . she have school herself to believe that time and patience would serve she good . her plan be to obtain all the knowledge about Philip that she could in the first instance ; and then , if circumstance allow it , as in all probability they would , to let drop by drop of healing , peacemake word and thought fall on Sylvia 's obdurate , unforgive heart . so hester put on her thing , and go out down towards the old quay - side on that evening after the shop be close . 

 Poor little Sylvia ! she be unforgiving , but not obdurate to the full extent of what hester believe . many a time since Philip go away have she unconsciously miss his protect love ; when folk speak shortly to she , when Alice scold she as one of the non - elect , when Hester 's gentle gravity have something of severity in it ; when her own heart fail she as to whether her mother would have judge that she have do well , could that mother have know all , as possibly she do by this time . Philip have never speak otherwise than tenderly to she during the eighteen month of their married life , except on the two occasion before record : once when she refer to her dream of Kinraid 's possible return , and once again on the evening of the day before her discovery of his concealment of the secret of Kinraid 's involuntary disappearance . 

 after she have learn that Kinraid be marry , her heart have still more strongly turn to Philip ; she think that he have judge rightly in what he have give as the excuse for his double dealing ; she be even more indignant at Kinraid 's fickleness than she have any reason to be ; and she begin to learn the value of such endure love as Philip 's have be last ever since the day when she first begin to fancy what a man 's love for a woman should be , when she have first shrink from the tone of tenderness he put into his especial term for she , a girl of twelve ' little lassie , ' as he be will not to call she . 

 but across all this relenting come the shadow of her vow like the chill of a great cloud pass over a sunny plain . how should she decide ? what would be her duty , if he come again , and once more call she ' wife ' ? she shrink from such a possibility with all the weakness and superstition of her nature ; and this it be which make she strengthen herself with the re - utterance of unforgive word ; and shun all recurrence to the subject on the rare occasion when Hester have try to bring it back , with a hope of soften the heart which to she appear altogether harden on this one point . 

 now , on this bright summer evening , while Hester have go down to the quay - side , Sylvia stand with her out - of - door thing on in the parlour , rather impatiently watch the sky , full of hurry cloud , and flush with the warm tint of the approach sunset . she could not leave Alice : the old woman have grow so infirm that she be never leave by her daughter and Sylvia at the same time ; yet Sylvia have to fetch her little girl from the New Town , where she have be to her supper at Jeremiah Foster 's . hester have say that she should not be away more than a quarter of an hour ; and hester be generally so punctual that any failure of her , in this respect , appear almost in the light of an injury on those who have learn to rely upon she . Sylvia want to go and see widow Dobson , and learn when Kester might be expect home . his two month be long past ; and Sylvia have hear through the Fosters of some suitable and profitable employment for he , of which she think he would be glad to know as soon as possible . it be now some time since she have be able to get so far as across the bridge ; and , for aught she know , Kester might already be come back from his expedition to the Cheviots .
