Kester be come back . scarce five minute have elapse after these thought have pass through her mind before his hasty hand lift the latch of the kitchen - door , his hurried step bring he face to face with she . the smile of greeting be arrest on her lip by one look at he : his eye star wide , the expression on his face wild , and yet pitiful . 

 ' that 's reet , ' say he , see that her thing be already on . ' thou're want sore . come along . ' 

 ' oh ! dear God ! my child ! ' cry Sylvia , clutch at the chair near she ; but recover she eddying sens with the strong fact before she that whatever the terror be , she be need to combat it . 

 ' ay ; thy child ! ' say Kester , take she almost roughly by the arm , and draw she away with he out through the open door on to the quay - side . 

 ' tell I ! ' say Sylvia , faintly , ' be she dead ? ' 

 ' she be safe now , ' say Kester . ' it be not she it be he as save she as need yo ' , if iver husband need a wife . ' 

 ' he ? who ? o Philip ! Philip ! be it yo ' at last ? ' 

 unheede what spectator might see her movement , she throw up her arm and stagger against the parapet of the bridge they be then cross . 

 ' he ! Philip ! save Bella ? Bella , our little Bella , as get her dinner by my side , and go out wi ' Jeremiah , as well as could be . I can not take it in ; tell I , Kester . ' she keep tremble so much in voice and in body , that he see she could not stir without danger of fall until she be calm ; as it be , her eye become filmy from time to time , and she draw her breath in great heavy pant , lean all the while against the wall of the bridge . 

 ' it be no illness , ' Kester begin . ' T ' little un have go for a walk wi ' Jeremiah Foster , an ' he be draw for to go round t ' edge o ' t ' cliff , wheere they be makin ' t ' new walk reet o'er t ' sea . but it be but a bit on a pathway now ; an ' t ' one be too oud , an ' t ' other too young for t ' see t ' water comin ' along wi ' great leap ; it be allay for comin ' high up again ' t ' cliff , an ' this spring - tide it be comin ' in I ' terrible big wave . some one say as they pass t ' man a - sittin ' on a bit on a rock up above a dunnot know , a only know as a heared a great fearful screech I ' t ' air . a be just a - restin ' I at after a'd come in , not half an hour I ' t ' place . A've   walk well nor a dozen mile to - day ; an ' a run out , an ' a looked , an ' just on t ' walk , at t ' turn , be t ' swish of a wave runnin ' back as quick as t ' mischief int ' t ' sea , an ' oud Jeremiah standin ' like one crazy , lookin ' o'er int ' t ' watter ; an ' like a stroke o ' leeghtnin ' come a man , an ' int ' t ' very midst o ' t ' great wave like a shot ; an ' then a knowed summut be in t ' watter as be nearer death than life ; an ' a seem to misdoubt I that it be our Bella ; an ' a shout an ' a cry for help , an ' a go mysel ' to t ' very edge o ' t ' cliff , an ' a bid oud Jeremiah , as be like one beside hissel ' , houd tight on I , for he be good for nought else ; an ' a bide my time , an ' when a see two arm houdin ' out a little drippin ' streamin ' child , a clutch she by her waist - band , an ' haul she to land . she 's noane t ' bad for her bath , a'll be bind . '

 he let go , and she stand still , suddenly feel herself too weak to stir .

 ' niver think on what thou do say ; think on what thou will say to he now , for he lie a - dyin ' ! he be dash again t ' cliff an ' bruised sore in his innard afore t ' man as come wi ' a boat could pick he up . ' 

 she do not speak ; she do not even tremble now ; she set her tooth together , and , hold tight by Kester , she urge he on ; but when they come to the end of the bridge , she seem uncertain which way to turn . 

 ' this way , ' say Kester . ' he be be lodgin ' wi ' Sally this nine week , an ' niver a one about t ' place as know he ; he be be I ' t ' war an ' getten his face brunt . ' 

 ' and he be short o ' food , ' moan Sylvia , ' and we have plenty , and I try to make yo'r sister turn he out , and send he away . oh ! will God iver forgive I ? ' 

 mutter to herself , break her muttering with sharp cry of pain , Sylvia , with Kester 's help , reach widow Dobson 's house . it be no long a quiet , lonely dwelling . several sailor stand about the door , await , in silent anxiety , for the verdict of the doctor , who be even now examine Philip 's injury . two or three woman stand talk eagerly , in low voice , in the doorway . 

 but when Sylvia draw near the man fall back ; and the woman move aside as though to allow she to pass , all look upon she with a certain amount of sympathy , but perhaps with rather more of antagonistic wonder as to how she be take it she who have be live in ease and comfort while her husband 's shelter be little well than a hovel , her husband 's daily life a struggle with starvation ; for so much of the lodger at widow Dobson 's be popularly know ; and any distrust of he as a stranger and a tramp be quite forget now . 

 Sylvia feel the hardness of their look , the hardness of their silence ; but it be as nothing to she . if such thing could have touch she at this moment , she would not have stand still right in the midst of their avert heart , and murmur something to Kester . he could not hear the word utter by that hoarse choke voice , until he have stoop down and bring his ear to the level of her mouth . 

 ' we would well wait for t ' doctor to come out , ' she say again . she stand by the door , shiver all over , almost face the people in the road , but with her face turn a little to the right , so that they think she be look at the pathway on the cliff - side , a hundred yard or so distant , below which the hungry wave still lash themselves into high ascending spray ; while near to the cottage , where their force be break by the bar at the entrance to the river , they come softly lap up the shelve shore . 

 Sylvia see nothing of all this , though it be straight before her eye . she only see a blurred mist ; she hear no sound of water , though it fill the ear of those around . instead she hear low whisper pronounce Philip 's earthly doom . 

 for the doctor be both agree ; his internal injury be of a mortal kind , although , as the spine be severely injure above the seat of the fatal bruise , he have no pain in the low half of his body . 

 they have speak in so low a tone that John Foster , stand only a foot or so away , have not be able to hear their word . but Sylvia hear each syllable there where she stand outside , shiver all over in the sultry summer evening . she turn round to Kester . 

 ' I mun go to he , Kester ; thou'll see that noane come in to we , when t ' doctor come out . ' 

 she speak in a soft , calm voice ; and he , not know what she have hear , make some easy conditional promise . then those opposite to the cottage door fall back , for they could see the grave doctor come out , and John Foster , graver , sadder still , follow they . without a word to they , without a word even of inquiry which many outside thought and speak of as strange white - face , dry - eyed Sylvia slip into the house out of their sight . 

 and the wave keep lap on the shelve shore . 

 the room inside be dark , all except the little halo or circle of light make by a dip candle . Widow Dobson have she back to the bed her bed on to which Philip have be bear in the hurry of terror as to whether he be alive or whether he be dead . she be cry cry quietly , but the tear down - fall fast , as , with she back to the lowly bed , she be gather up the dripping clothe cut off from the poor maim body by the doctor ' order . she only shake her head as she see Sylvia , spirit - like , steal in white , noiseless , and upborne from earth . 

 but noiseless as her step might be , he hear , he recognize , and with a sigh he turn his poor disfigured face to the wall , hide it in the shadow . 

 he know that she be by he ; that she have kneel down by his bed ; that she be kiss his hand , over which the languor of approach death be steal . but no one speak . 

 at length he say , his face still avert , speak with an effort . 

 ' little lassie , forgive I now ! I can not live to see the morn ! ' 

 there be no answer , only a long miserable sigh , and he feel her soft cheek lay upon his hand , and the quiver that run through her whole body . 

 ' I do thee a cruel wrong , ' he say , at length . ' I see it now . but I be a die man . I think that God will forgive I and I have   sin against he ; try , lassie try , my Sylvie will not thou forgive I ? ' 

 he listen intently for a moment . he hear through the open window the wave lap on the shelve shore . but there come no word from she ; only that same long shiver , miserable sigh break from her lip at length . 

 ' child , ' say he , once more . ' I ha ' make thee my idol ; and if I could live my life o'er again I would love my God more , and thee less ; and then I should not   ha ' sin this sin against thee . but speak one word of love to I one little word , that I may know I have thy pardon . ' 

 ' oh , Philip ! Philip ! ' she moan , thus adjure . 

 then she lift her head , and say , 

 ' they be wicked , wicked word , as I say ; and a wicked vow as I vow ; and Lord God Almighty have ta'en I at my word . I be sorely punish , Philip , I be indeed . ' 

 he press her hand , he stroke her cheek . but he ask for yet another word . 

 ' I do thee a wrong . in my lie heart I forget to do to thee as I would have have thee to do to I . and I judge Kinraid in my heart . ' 

 ' Thou think as he be faithless and fickle , ' she answer quickly ; ' and so he be . he be married to another woman not so many week at after thou go away . oh , Philip , Philip ! and now I have thee back , and ' 

 ' die ' be the word she would have say , but first the dread of tell he what she believe he do not know , and next her passionate sob , choke she . 

 ' I know , ' say he , once more stroke her cheek , and soothe she with gentle , caress hand . ' little lassie ! ' he say , after a while when she be quiet from very exhaustion , ' I niver think to be so happy again . God be very merciful . ' 

 she lift up her head , and ask wildly , ' will he iver forgive I , think yo ' ? I drive yo ' out fra ' yo'r home , and send yo ' away to t ' war , wheere yo ' might ha ' getten yo'r death ; and when yo ' come back , poor and lone , and weary , I tell she for t ' turn yo ' out , for a ' I know yo ' must be starve in these famine time . I think I shall go about among they as gnash their tooth for iver , while yo ' be wheere all tear be wipe away . ' 

 ' no ! ' say Philip , turn round his face , forgetful of himself in his desire to comfort she . ' God pity we as a father pity his poor wandering child ; the nearer I come to death the clear I see he . but you and I have do wrong to each other ; yet we can see now how we be lead to it ; we can pity and forgive one another . I be get low and faint , lassie ; but thou must remember this : God know more , and be more forgiving than either you to I , or I to you . I think and do believe as we shall meet together before his face ; but then I shall ha ' learn to love thee second to he ; not first , as I have do here upon the earth . ' 

 then he be silent very still . Sylvia know widow Dobson have bring it in that there be some kind of medicine , send by the hopeless doctor , lie upon the table hard by , and she softly rise and pour it out and drop it into the half - open mouth . then she kneel down again , hold the hand feebly stretch out to she , and watch the faint light in the wistful love eye . and in the stillness she hear the ceaseless wave lap against the shelve shore . 

 something like an hour before this time , which be the deep midnight of the summer 's night , Hester Rose have come hurry up the road to where Kester and his sister sate outside the open door , keep their watch under the star - light sky , all other have go away , one by one , even John and Jeremiah Foster have return to their own house , where the little Bella lay , sleep a sound and healthy slumber after her perilous adventure . 

 hester have hear but little from William Darley as to the owner of the watch and the half - crown ; but he be chagrin at the failure of all his skilful interrogation to elicit the truth , and promise her further information in a few day , with all the more vehemence because he be unaccustomed to be baffle . and Hester have again whisper to herself ' Patience ! patience ! ' and have slowly return back to her home to find that Sylvia have leave it , why she do not at once discover . but , grow uneasy as the advance hour neither bring Sylvia nor little Bella to their home , she have set out for Jeremiah Foster 's as soon as she have see her mother comfortably asleep in her bed ; and then she have learn the whole story , bit by bit , as each person who speak break in upon the previous narration with some new particular . but from no one do she clearly learn whether Sylvia be with her husband , or not ; and so she come speed along the road , breathless , to where Kester sate in wakeful , mournful silence , his sister 's sleep head lie on his shoulder , the cottage door open , both for air and that there might be help within call if need ; and the dim slanting oblong of the interior light lie across the road . 

 hester come pant up , too agitated and breathless to ask how much be truth of the fatal , hopeless tale which she have hear . Kester look at she without a word . through this solemn momentary silence the lapping of the ceaseless wave be hear , as they come up close on the shelve shore . 

 ' he ? Philip ? ' say she . Kester shake his head sadly . 

 ' and his wife Sylvia ? ' say hester . 

 ' in there with he , alone , ' whisper Kester . 

 hester turn away , and wrung her hand together . 

 ' oh , Lord God Almighty ! ' say she , ' be I not even worthy to bring they together at last ? ' and she go away slowly and heavily back to the side of her sleep mother . but ' Thy will be do ' be on her quivering lip before she lie down to her rest . 

 the soft gray dawn lighten the darkness of a midsummer night soon after two o'clock . Philip watch it come , know that it be his last sight of day , as we reckon day on earth . 

 he have be often near death as a soldier ; once or twice , as when he rush into fire to save Kinraid , his chance of life have be as one to a hundred ; but yet he have have a chance . but now there be the new feeling the last new feeling which we shall any of we experience in this world that death be not only close at hand , but inevitable . 

 he feel its numbness steal up he steal up he . but the head be clear , the brain more than commonly active in produce vivid impression . 

 it seem but yesterday since he be a little boy at his mother 's knee , wish with all the earnestness of his childish heart to be like Abraham , who be call the friend of God , or David , who be say to be the man after God 's own heart , or St John , who be call ' the Beloved . ' as very present seem the day on which he make resolution of try to be like they ; it be in the spring , and some one have bring in cowslip ; and the scent of those flower be in his nostril now , as he lie a - die his life end , his battle fight , his time for ' be good ' over and go the opportunity , once give in all eternity , past . 

 all the temptation that have beset he rise clearly before he ; the scene themselves stand up in their solid materialism he could have touch the place ; the people , the thought , the argument that Satan have urge in behalf of sin , be reproduce with the vividness of a present time . and he know that the thought be illusion , the argument false and hollow ; for in that hour come the perfect vision of the perfect truth : he see the ' way to escape ' which have come along with the temptation ; now , the strong resolve of an ardent boyhood , with all a life before it to show the world ' what a Christian might be ' ; and then the swift , terrible now , when his naked , guilty soul shrink into the shadow of God 's mercy - seat , out of the blaze of his anger against all those who act a lie . 

 his mind be wander , and he pluck it back . be this death in very deed ? he try to grasp at the present , the earthly present , fade quick away . he lie there on the bed on Sally Dobson 's bed in the house - place , not on his accustomed pallet in the lean - to . he know that much . and the door be open into the still , dusk night ; and through the open casement he could hear the lapping of the wave on the shelve shore , could see the soft gray dawn over the sea he know it be over the sea he see what lie unseen behind the poor wall of the cottage . and it be Sylvia who hold his hand tight in her warm , living grasp ; it be his wife whose arm be throw around he , whose sobbing sigh shake his numbed frame from time to time . 

 ' God bless and comfort my darling , ' he say to himself . ' she know I now . all will be right in heaven in the light of God 's mercy . ' 

 and then he try to remember all that he have ever read about , God , and all that the bless Christ that bringeth glad tiding of great joy unto all people , have say of the Father , from whom he come . those saying drop like balm down upon his troubled heart and brain . he remember his mother , and how she have love he ; and he be go to a love wiser , tenderer , deep than her . 

 as he think this , he move his hand as if to pray ; but Sylvia clench her hold , and he lie still , pray all the same for she , for his child , and for himself . then he see the sky redden with the first flush of dawn ; he hear Kester 's long - draw sigh of weariness outside the open door . 

 he have see widow Dobson pass through long before to keep the remainder of her watch on the bed in the lean - to , which have be his for many and many a sleepless and tearful night . those night be over he should never see that poor chamber again , though it be scarce two foot distant . he begin to lose all sense of the comparative duration of time : it seem as long since kind Sally Dobson have bend over he with soft , lingering look , before go into the humble sleeping - room as long as it be since his boyhood , when he stand by his mother dream of the life that should be his , with the scent of the cowslip tempt he to be off to the woodland where they grow . then there come a rush and an eddying through his brain his soul try her wing for the long flight . again he be in the present : he hear the wave lap against the shelve shore once again . 

 and now his thought come back to Sylvia . once more he speak aloud , in a strange and terrible voice , which be not his . every sound come with effort that be new to he . 

 ' my wife ! Sylvie ! once more forgive I all . ' 

 she spring up , she kiss his poor burn lip ; she hold he in her arm , she moan , and say , 

 ' oh , wicke I ! forgive I me Philip ! ' 

 then he speak , and say , ' Lord , forgive we our trespass as we forgive each other ! ' and after that the power of speech be conquer by the come death . he lie very still , his consciousness fast fade away , yet come back in throb , so that he know it be Sylvia who touch his lip with cordial , and that it be Sylvia who murmur word of love in his ear . he seem to sleep at last , and so he do a kind of sleep , but the light of the red morning sun fall on his eye , and with one strong effort he rise up , and turn so as once more to see his wife 's pale face of misery . 

 ' in heaven , ' he cry , and a bright smile come on his face , as he fall back on his pillow . 

 not long after hester come , the little Bella scarce awake in her arm , with the purpose of bring his child to see he ere yet he pass away . hester have watch and pray through the livelong night . and now she find he dead , and Sylvia , tearless and almost unconscious , lie by he , her hand hold his , her other throw around he . 

 Kester , poor old man , be sob bitterly ; but she not at all . 

 then hester bear her child to she , and Sylvia open wide her miserable eye , and only star , as if all sense be go from she . but Bella suddenly rouse up at the sight of the poor , scarred , peaceful face , cry out , 

 ' poor man who be so hungry . be he not hungry now ? ' 

 ' no , ' say hester , softly . ' the former thing be pass away and he be go where there be no more sorrow , and no more pain . ' 

 but then she break down into weeping and cry . Sylvia sit up and look at she . 

 ' why do yo ' cry , hester ? ' she say . ' Yo ' niver say that yo ' would not   forgive he as long as yo ' live . Yo ' niver break the heart of he that love yo ' , and let he almost starve at yo'r very door . oh , Philip ! my Philip , tender and true . ' 

 then Hester come round and close the sad half - open eye ; kiss the calm brow with a long farewell kiss . as she do so , her eye fall on a black ribbon round his neck . she partly lift it out ; to it be hang a half - crown piece . 

 ' this be the piece he leave at William Darley 's to be bore , ' say she , ' not many day ago . ' 

 Bella have creep to her mother 's arm as a known haven in this strange place ; and the touch of his child loosen the fountain of her tear . she stretch out her hand for the black ribbon , put it round her own neck ; after a while she say , 

 ' if I live very long , and try hard to be very good all that time , do yo ' think , Hester , as God will let I to he where he be ? ' 

 Monkshaven be alter now into a rise bathing place . yet , stand near the site of widow Dobson 's house on a summer 's night , at the ebb of a spring - tide , you may hear the wave come lap up the shelve shore with the same ceaseless , ever - recurrent sound as that which Philip listen to in the pause between life and death . 

 and so it will be until ' there shall be no more sea ' . 

 but the memory of man fade away . a few old people can still tell you the tradition of the man who die in a cottage somewhere about this spot , die of starvation while his wife live in hard - hearte plenty not two good stone - throw away . this be the form into which popular feeling , and ignorance of the real fact , have mould the story . not long since a lady go to the ' Public Baths ' , a handsome stone building erect on the very site of widow Dobson 's cottage , and find all the room engage she sit down and have some talk with the bath woman ; and , as it chance , the conversation fall on Philip Hepburn and the legend of his fate . 

 ' I know an old man when I be a girl , ' say the bathing woman , ' as could niver abide to hear t ' wife blame . he would say nothing again ' th ' husband ; he use to say as it be not fit for man to be judge ; that she have have her sore trial , as well as Hepburn hisself . ' 

 the lady ask , ' what become of the wife ? ' 

 ' she be a pale , sad woman , allay dress in black . I can just remember she when I be a little child , but she die before her daughter be well grow up ; and Miss Rose take t ' lassie , as have always be like her own . ' 

 ' Miss Rose ? ' 

 ' hester Rose ! have yo ' niver heared of Hester Rose , she as found t ' alm - house for poor disabled sailor and soldier on t ' Horncastle road ? there be a piece o ' stone in front to say that " this building be erect in memory of P. H. " and some folk will have it P. H. stand for t ' name o ' th ' man as be starve to death . ' 

 ' and the daughter ? ' 

 ' one o ' th ' Fosters , they as found t ' Old Bank , leave she a vast o ' money ; and she be married to distant cousin of theirs , and go off to settle in America many and many a year ago . ' 

 the END .
